Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics

Series Editor

John M. Walker

Preface

The recent wave in AI is to replace the task-specific models with foundation models that are trained on a broad set of unlabeled data that can be used for different tasks with minimal fine-tuning. These models are called foundation models as they serve as the foundation for many applications of the AI model. Large Language Models (LLMs) are a class of foundation models that are (pre)trained on enormous amounts of data to provide the foundational capabilities needed to drive multiple use cases and applications. LLMs are typically based on a transformer architecture and involve training on a massive corpus of data (e.g., text). The transformer architecture of LLMs allows LLMs to effectively handle long context and sequential information. LLMs represent a significant breakthrough in natural language processing (NLP) and are designed to understand and generate texts/contents. LLMs have found application in text/content generation, content summarization, AI assistants, code generation, and language translation, among others.

LLMs have shown significant promise in various research fields including protein bioinformatics. Thanks to advances in LLMs, the field of protein bioinformatics has also witnessed a lot of advances in various areas including but not limited to protein structure prediction, protein function prediction, and others. Starting with training of Protein Language Models (PLMs, LLMs that are trained on protein sequence/structure) and the subsequent application of these PLMs, the field has seen a plethora of approaches for various protein bioinformatics tasks.

The application of LLMs in protein bioinformatics can be broadly classified into two categories: representation (understanding) and protein design/engineering (generation). In the representation category, the protein language model is used to extract embeddings typically from the last layer and then these embeddings are used for downstream prediction/classification tasks. Most people refer to this as static, pretrained embeddings, and it has been one the most common approaches in protein bioinformatics. Recently, in NLP, we have seen a few works that use task-specific supervised fine-tuning and training for both the PLM encoder and the prediction head. The field of LLMs is developing at a much faster pace, and new topics like AI agents, etc. are already becoming more popular. We hope to see the application of AI agents and other new emerging themes in the area of protein bioinformatics as well. Additionally, there are newer concepts like large context models (LCMs), and we also expect to see their application in the field.

The following chapters are included in this volume of Methods in Molecular Biology. The book begins (Chap. 1) by setting up the premise for the development of LLM-based approaches in protein bioinformatics, specifically, surveying recent pretrained protein language models. This chapter gives an excellent overview of various types of protein language models based on architecture, aka encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only. The chapter also briefly summarizes the most recent trends in the field: fine-tuning protein language models and multimodal protein language models, among other things.

In Chap. 2, Dong Xu’s group from the University of Missouri-Columbia describes S-PLM, a 3D structure-aware protein language model. Motivated by the recent advances in protein structure prediction approaches, S-PLM allows one to obtain structural embeddings by leveraging a Geometric Vector Perceptron (GVP) model to process the 3D coordinates of protein.

Protein language models have high demand for computational resources. In Chap. 3, Yan Wang, Zhidong Xue, and colleagues describe their lightweight protein language model called ProtFlash. ProtFlash uses several key technological breakthroughs including mixedchunk attention, among other things. The authors also describe step-by-step instructions for utilizing the ProtFlash library.

Bonvin’s group from Utrecht University describes an approach called DeepRank-GNNesm based on protein language models for protein-protein interaction prediction to rank protein-protein models (scoring problem) in Chap. 4. As the ranking of good models from the large pool of generated models in protein-protein interaction prediction is an important step, this chapter describes the use of protein language model (ESM-2) features to improve protein-protein interaction prediction.

In Chap. 5, Daisuke Kihara from Purdue University and colleagues describe GO2Sum, gene ontology (GO) terms summarizer that uses a protein-language model. Essentially, GO2Sum takes a list of GO terms as input and converts them into a summary that describes various GO aspects of a protein. The chapter also describes the web server of GO2Sum.

Jinalin Cheng’s group from University of Missouri-Columbia describes their recently developed protein function annotation tool called TransFun in Chap. 6. Recognizing the lack of functional annotations for many proteins, TransFun leverages embeddings from ESM-1b and predicted structures from AlphaFold to predict function for a given protein. The authors also describe in detail how to get started with TransFun.

In Chap. 7, Lydia Fredollino’s group at University of Michigan describes InterLabelGO +, a top-performing model to predict GO term in CAFA5. InterLabelGO+ is an approach for prediction of protein functions in the form of gene ontology that uses the ESM2 protein language model to extract sequence features. Additionally, the group also describes the procedure to perform protein GO term prediction with InterLabelGO+ webserver and the standalone package in details.

In Chap. 8, Ana Rojas and collaborators from Centro Andaluz De Biologia Del Desarrollo discuss the application of a protein language model (ProtTrans) for protein function annotation. Additionally, they also describe the FANTASIA tool for large-scale annotation of uncharacterized proteomes.

Debswapna Bhattacharya from Virginia Tech and collaborators summarize the recent advances in protein-nucleic acid binding site prediction approaches that harness protein language models in Chap. 9. Additionally, the chapter also presents their own approach called EquiPNAS that integrates pLM with equivariant deep graph neural networks for protein-DNA and protein-RNA binding site prediction.

In Chap. 10, Henrik Nielsen from the Technical University of Denmark describes three important tools that his group recently developed related to what proteins belong to which compartments, making use of protein language models. Specifically, the chapter describes SignalP6.0 for prediction of signal peptides, DeepLoc2.1 for prediction of subcellular location and membrane association in eukaryotes, and DeepLocPro1.0 for prediction of subcellular location in prokaryotes.

Iman Dehzangi and colleagues from Rutgers University-Camden discuss their tool CNN-Meth for predicting lysine methylation sites that uses evolutionary information and structural features in Chap. 11. Although their method does not directly use a protein language model, the Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM) features in their approach can readily be replaced by protein language model-based embeddings.

Pier Luigi Martelli and colleagues from the University of Bologna describe their approach for characterizing proteins and for predicting the pathogenicity of human protein

variants. Their approach uses embeddings from protein language models. Additionally, they describe their Bioinform atics Sweeties, a web portal, that has a list of bioinformatics tools for characterizing proteins and different aspects of pathogenic variants with examples in Chap. 12.

In Chap. 13, Shandar Ahmad’s group from Jawaharlal Nehru University discusses various existing approaches for prediction of biological function that leverages protein language models and NLP-based techniques. Additionally, the survey highlights the major advances in the field and possible future directions for the research in the field.

Shanfeng Zhu and Jianyi Yang’s group discusses their recent approach, in Chap. 14, for homologous protein search and sequence alignment that uses protein language models. PLMSearch is their protein language model-based tools for searching homologous sequences, and PLMAlign is their tool for aligning remote homologous sequences. The chapter also describes in detail how to use these tools.

In Chap. 15, Siwei Chen’s group at the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard summarizes the recent advances in protein-protein interaction analysis that leverages protein language models. Essentially, the computational tools for predicting protein-protein interactions and protein-protein interaction site prediction are discussed in detail. The chapter also highlights some of the other promising areas of PPI prediction, including PPI hotspots among others.

Identifying protein-peptide binding residue is important for understanding the mechanisms of protein functions and drug discovery. In Chap. 16, Leyi Wei and colleagues describe their PepBCL tool to predict protein-peptide binding site. PepBCL uses a pretrained BERT model called ProtBert-BFD to generate the encoding vector.

Bioactive peptide discovery is another important field across food, nutraceuticals, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals. In Chap. 17, Yonghui Li and colleagues from Kansas State University describe their tool that leverages a protein language model for predicting peptide bioactivity. Their approach, called UniDL4BioPep, uses the ESM protein language model.

In Chap. 18, Boxue Tian’s group at Tsinghua University discusses a new tool called CLAPE for the prediction of protein-ligand binding site. CLAPE uses contrastive learning and the pretrained protein language model ProtBERT. The authors describe in detail the architecture, model performance, and datasets utilized in the training of CLAPE as well as how to use CLAPE.

Finally, in Chap. 19, My group from the Rochester Institute of Technology focus our chapter on a survey of recent advances in the prediction of post-translational modification sites in proteins that leverage large language models. We also identify emerging trends in the field and outline some of the challenges and future research directions in the field.

I hope readers receive this book as a comprehensive collection of methods, resources, and studies that use LLMs in protein bioinformatics. In addition to the description of these approaches, I believe the book will also serve as a practical guide for using these LLM-based tools in relation to various protein bioinformatics tasks. I am hopeful that this book exhibits a state of the art of the current research field in the arena and provides future trends in the field regarding the use of LLMs for protein bioinformatics.

This book would not have been possible without the generous contributions from all the contributing authors in this book. I sincerely thank them for their contributions. I am also thankful to the series editor, Dr. John M. Walker, for patiently working with me

throughout the process. In addition, I would like to thank all the lab members of my lab, my collaborators and my c olleagues in the Department of Computer Science at Rochester Institute of Technology (RIT). Most importantly, I would like to sincerely acknowledge the inspiration, encour agement, and guidance I received from my mentors Dr. Tatsuya Akutsu, Dr. Dennis Liv esay, and Dr. Ilya Vakser, among others.

Rochester, NY, USA

Dukka B. KC

A Survey of Pretrained Protein Language Models

Suresh Pokharel, Pawel Pratyush, Meenal Chaudhari, Michael Heinzinger, Doina Caragea, Hiroto Saigo, and Dukka B. KC

Abstract

Inspired by the transformative success of large language models (LLMs) in natural language processing (NLP), numerous protein language models (PLMs) have recently emerged, revolutionizing the field of protein bioinformatics. PLMs have demonstrated remarkable achievements in representing proteins and designing new ones, capturing intrinsic structural and functional information trained on vast datasets of proteins, PLMs have demonstrated exceptional performance across a variety of bioinformatics tasks, including classification, function prediction, and de novo protein design. This chapter explores the evolution of PLMs, tracing their origins from NLP-based transformers and large language models (LLMs). A comprehensive summary of notable PLMs is presented, with a particular focus on encoder-only, encoder– decoder, and decoder-only architectures. Additionally, we delve into cutting-edge trends in PLM applications, such as fine-tuning methods, multimodal architectures, and the use of reduced alphabets. These innovations underscore the growing potential of PLMs to tackle complex biological problems and drive future breakthroughs in the field.

Key words Protein language models, Transformers in bioinformatics, Encoder-only, Encoder, Decoder, Decoder-only models

1 Introduction

Proteins are fundamental to life, acting as the building blocks and functional machinery of living organisms. These biomolecules are composed of one or multiple long chains of amino acids called polypeptides. Amino acids are organic molecules with a central carbon atom bonded to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen atom, and a variable side chain. Proteins fold into three-dimensional (3D) structures that determine their roles in cellular processes that form structural components like collagen, function as enzymes, hormones, or antibodies, and regulate various biological processes [1]. Proteins make up about \(4 2 \%\) of the human body’s dry weight and are essential to nearly every biological function [2, 3]. Scientists study proteins at many levels including the

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_1, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

linear amino acid sequence (primary structure) to folded patterns like alpha helices and beta sheets (secondary structure) and complex 3D shapes (tertiary structure) to understand molecular mechanisms of life [3].

Recent studies follow an intriguing connection between proteins and natural language processing (NLP) [4–7], drawing an analogy between protein sequences and human language. Protein sequences, akin to “sentences” composed of amino acid “words,” have paved the way for leveraging NLP techniques in protein bioinformatics. This analogy has facilitated groundbreaking advancements of NLP techniques to achieve remarkable achievements in protein bioinformatics, uncovering complex patterns in proteins, predicting structures, identifying functions, and even designing de novo proteins.

On one hand, recent advancements in transformer-based applications, particularly large language models (LLMs), have significantly revolutionized the NLP field [8]. Their ability to process sequential data efficiently makes them well suited for understanding complex relationships within text and for text generation. Similarly, protein language models (PLMs) are trained using large protein sequence databases, much like LLMs are trained using vast text corpora. PLMs have demonstrated remarkable achievements in representing proteins and designing new ones, capturing intrinsic structural and functional information [4, 9, 10]. This innovative application of cutting-edge LLM-driven approaches has demonstrated breakthroughs in protein bioinformatics with transformative applications in areas like drug discovery and protein engineering.

In this chapter, we start with providing a summary of transformers and describing various types of transformers architecture by examining the core principles behind LLMs and tracing the progression of various pretrained protein language models. Then, we delve into the various types of protein language models (PLMs) especially based on their architectures. Subsequently, we briefly describe the three major types of PLMS: encoder only, encoder– decoder, and decoder only architectures. We then briefly describe some applications of PLMs in protein bioinformatics and then finally summarize by outlining the recent trends in PLMs.

2 Transformers: A Historical Perspective and Types of Transformers

Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and their variants, such as long short-term memory networks (LSTMs) and gated recurrent units (GRUs), were long considered the gold standard for natural language sequence modeling tasks (predicting the next element in a data sequence). These architectures excelled at processing sequential data by maintaining hidden states to encode information from

previous time steps. However, these RNNs and their variants have critical limitations. First, RNNs process inputs sequentially, which hinders parallelization and makes training computationally expensive. Second, as sequence lengths increase, gradients can suffer from the problem of vanishing or exploding, hindering the process of learning. Even though LSTMs and GRUs addressed some of these challenges, they still had difficulty capturing long-range dependencies. Their sequential processing nature often caused information from earlier inputs to be forgotten in long sequences.

In 2017, researchers at Google Brain and Google Research introduced the transformer architecture [11], fundamentally changing sequence modeling tasks by moving away from traditional recurrent neural networks. The key innovation was the use of a selfattention mechanism instead of recurrent structures, allowing the model to assess the importance of each token relative to all others in a sequence. Unlike recurrent models that process data step by step, transformers handle all tokens simultaneously. They compute attention scores between every pair of elements in parallel, effectively capturing long-range dependencies without sequential processing. To incorporate sequence order information, positional encoding was added to the model. By eliminating recurrence, transformers can compute contextual relationships in a single operation through parallel processing, which significantly accelerates training and inference times. This approach also overcomes the limitations of recurrent models, such as inefficiency and challenges with long-range dependencies. The ability to process entire sequences at once enhances the scalability of transformers, especially with longer sequences, establishing them as a groundbreaking architecture for sequence modeling tasks. The transformer model outperformed existing architectures in machine translation tasks, setting new records and demonstrating the power of attention mechanisms.

The introduction of transformers sparked a surge of research and development across various domains, particularly in natural language processing (NLP). Notable early implementations include bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) [12], developed by Google in 2018, which utilized the encoder part of the transformer architecture to create deep bidirectional representations with the help of masked language modeling (MLM). During MLM, the model receives partially corrupted inputs which it tries to correct in the output. To do well in this task, the model has to learn to gather information from a non-corrupted context. This enabled BERT to excel in understanding the context of words with their surroundings, reaching state-ofthe-art results across numerous NLP tasks. Central to BERT’s success was pretraining on a large corpora of unlabeled data obtained from Wikipedia and BooksCourpus, followed by finetuning on specific tasks. This two-stage approach allowed the

models to learn a general understanding of language during pretraining, which could then be adapted to downstream tasks with minimal labeled data. Another prominent architecture, OpenAI’s generative pretrained transformer (GPT) [13] models, leveraged the decoder part of the transformer for language generation. GPT-2 and GPT-3, released in 2019 and 2020, respectively, showcased exceptional abilities in generating coherent and contextually relevant text, pushing the boundaries of language modeling. Another notable advancement was Transformer-XL, which addressed the fixed-length context limitation of standard transformers by incorporating recurrence mechanisms, enabling the model to capture longer-term dependencies and improving performance on tasks requiring extensive contextual understanding. Following these groundbreaking models, several modifications and improvements were proposed by researchers. Meta AI’s RoBERTa refined BERT by optimizing training strategies, such as dynamic masking and longer training with larger batches, leading to improved performance. To tackle increasing computational costs, ALBERT introduced parameter reduction techniques, making models more efficient without a significant loss in accuracy. Google’s T5 (text-totext transfer transformer) [14] unified NLP tasks by framing them as a text-to-text problem, achieving state-of-the-art results across numerous benchmarks. XLNet proposed a permutation-based training objective that integrated the benefits of autoregressive and autoencoding models, surpassing BERT in several NLP tasks. These advancements underscored the adaptability of the transformer architecture, solidifying its position as a foundation for modern NLP systems.

Transformers soon surpassed their initial application in NLP, demonstrating their versatility in other domains. Vision transformers (ViT) marked a major breakthrough in image recognition by adapting transformer architectures to process images. ViT models split images into patches and treated them similarly to word tokens, achieving competitive results compared to traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs). Multimodal transformers, such as DALL·E and CLIP, combined textual and visual data, enabling tasks like image generation from text descriptions and understanding relationships between images and text. In speech processing, transformers have been employed in speech recognition and synthesis, enhancing models’ ability to understand and generate human speech with higher fidelity. These diverse applications highlighted the transformative impact of the architecture across multiple disciplines, making transformers a cornerstone of modern machine learning.

Until now, we briefly discussed the history and evolution of transformers. Now, let us examine the types of transformers. Transformers can be classified based on various aspects. One such aspect is architectural modifications, which involve changes to the original

Table 1 Overview of transformer types based on pretraining method

Type Context directionality Pretraining objective Pretraining task Examples
Encoder only Bidirectional Masked language modeling (MLM) Understanding/ encoding tasks BERT, RoBERTa, ALBERT
Decoder only Unidirectional (autoregressive) Causal language modeling (CLM) Text generation tasks GPT series, Transformer-XL, Megatron-LM
Encoder-decoder Bidirectional + autoregressive Denoising, Seq2Seq generation Input-to-output transformations T5, BART

transformer structure, such as sparse attention mechanisms, linearized attention, and hierarchical designs, aimed at improving efficiency, scalability, or adaptability. Another classification is based on pretraining methods, where transformers differ in their learning strategies. They can also be categorized by their applications, as they are tailored for diverse fields such as NLP, computer vision, and audio processing, each requiring unique adaptations. We recommend referring to the paper by Lin et al. [15] for detailed information on these classifications. In this chapter, we focus on the classification based on pretraining methods, as discussed below. Additionally, we have summarized this classification in Table 1.

2.1 Encoder-Only Architectures

Encoder-only transformers utilize the encoder component of the original transformer architecture. These models are pretrained using masked language modeling (MLM), where a portion of the input tokens are masked, and the model learns to predict these masked tokens by leveraging bidirectional context. The architecture features self-attention mechanisms, allowing each token to attend to every other token in the sequence regardless of positional distance, with positional encodings incorporated to provide ordering information. By stacking multiple layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, these models gain depth and enhance their ability to recognize complex patterns in the data. Encoder-only models are particularly suited for tasks requiring a deep understanding of the input, such as text classification, named entity recognition (NER), question answering, and semantic similarity tasks. Examples include BERT, which introduced bidirectional training for nuanced language understanding; RoBERTa, an optimized version of BERT with improved training strategies; and ALBERT, which reduces model size while maintaining high performance for efficient deployment.

2.2 Encoder– Decoder Architectures

Encoder–decoder transformers integrate both encoder and decoder components to convert an input sequence into a corresponding output sequence, making them well suited for sequence-to-sequence tasks, such as translation, where the output differs from the input. These models are typically pretrained using objectives like denoising autoencoding, where the input sequence is corrupted (e.g., with masking, shuffling, or deletion), and the model learns to reconstruct the original sequence or sequence-tosequence generation, where the decoder predicts the output sequence based on the encoder’s output. The encoder transforms the input sequence into continuous representations using layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, while the decoder generates the output sequence by focusing on both the encoder’s output and its own previously generated tokens. Masked selfattention ensures predictions depend only on preceding tokens, and encoder–decoder attention allows the decoder to focus on the most relevant parts of the input sequence. These models excel in tasks like machine translation, text summarization, paraphrasing, and question generation. Notable examples include the original transformer, which revolutionized neural machine translation; T5 (text-to-text transfer transformer), which reformulates all NLP tasks as text-to-text problems; and BART, which combines bidirectional encoding with autoregressive decoding for summarization and generation tasks.

2.3 Decoder–Only Architectures

Decoder-only transformers consist solely of the decoder component and are designed for sequence generation tasks. These models are pretrained using causal language modeling (CLM), where the model predicts the next token in a sequence based on all previous tokens, enforcing a unidirectional (left-to-right) structure. Masked self-attention ensures that the model does not have access to future tokens during training, while positional encodings provide token position information. This approach makes decoder-only transformers particularly effective for generative tasks such as language modeling, text completion, creative writing, and dialogue systems. Examples include the GPT series, known for generating human-like text; Transformer-XL, which addresses fixed-length context limitations by introducing recurrence mechanisms for long-term dependency modeling; and Megatron-LM, designed for scalable training of transformer models with billions of parameters.

In the field of protein bioinformatics, researchers have adopted transformer architectures by treating individual amino acids as “words” and entire protein sequences as “sentences” [7]. This allows transformer models to examine protein sequences in a manner analogous to processing natural language. As a result, transformer-based architectures can be pretrained on extensive protein sequence datasets, such as UniRef50 and UniRef100 [16], enabling them to learn the “grammar” of the protein

language, including the structural and functional context of each amino acid with respect to the overall sequence. Language models pretrained in this way on protein sequences (and/or structures) are collectively referred to as protein language models (PLM). In the next section, we will discuss about PLMs in detail.

3 Protein Language Models

Large language models were developed to process human language, learning to predict word sequences and comprehend syntax and semantics using vast amounts of text data. These large language models (LLMs) like GPT and BERT [12, 13, 17, 18] revolutionized natural language processing by utilizing deep learning techniques and transformer architectures, which allowed for the modeling of long-range dependencies and the context in text sequences. Drawing inspiration from the success of human language-based large language models (LLMs) in understanding linguistic structures, protein language models (PLMs) [19] have emerged as powerful tools in bioinformatics. The evolution from human language-based models to protein language models (PLMs) [4, 20–26] capitalized on this foundation, applying similar methods to protein sequences. Proteins, like language, are composed of sequences of amino acids in place of words in sentences. Researchers realized that proteins exhibit structural and functional motifs, much like how human language has grammatical rules. By training models on a large corpus of protein sequences and protein structures, (pretrained) PLMs learn to represent this biological information in a high-dimensional space, capturing evolutionary, structural, and functional information about proteins [9, 27, 28]. PLMs have been primarily used in protein bioinformatics for two major tasks: protein representation and protein generation/ design. In protein representation, pretrained protein language models are used to extract numerical vector representations (aka embeddings) for the sequence in hand, and in protein generation/ design tasks, these pretrained PLMs are used to generate/design proteins with certain properties.

This adaptation to biological data has been transformative, as PLMs have demonstrated the ability to predict various protein functions and structures, understand sequence-function relationships, and perform multiple protein downstream tasks including protein classification [29–32] and function prediction [30, 33– 37]. Their ability to generalize across diverse protein families highlights their potential to address complex problems in genomics and proteomics. Protein language models (PLMs) have been developed to learn meaningful representations of proteins, with distinct models focusing on different data types such as protein sequences, 3D structures, or a combination of both.


Protein Language Models (pLMs)
Fig. 1 Categorization of protein language models (PLMs) based on architectural design into encoder-only, Categorization of protein language m–

Sequence-based PLMs are trained solely on amino acid sequences, learning patterns, motifs, and evolutionary relationships within proteins. By learning rich representations from the protein sequences, PLMs provide a gateway to various applications including protein classification, structure prediction, and protein function prediction. Similarly, structure-based PLMs are trained on information derived from 3D protein structures capturing the spatial arrangements and interactions of amino acids. Furthermore, some of the PLMs are trained by integrating both AA sequence and 3D structure information to generate potentially better representations to improve performance in various tasks providing both sequencebased and structural insights. In the reminder of this section, we explore a comprehensive list of the latest PLMs (as illustrated in Fig. 1) pretrained using encoder-only, encoder–decoder, and decoder-only architecture protein sequence data, 3D structural information, and a combination of both.

3.1 Types of PLMS Based on Pretraining

We focus on three key transformer architecture types for the PLMs: encoder–only, encoder–decoder, and decoder-only models including their applications to the field of protein bioinformatics, as described in the following subsections. Below, we describe these three types of PLMs.

Table 2 Summary of encoder-only PLMs with training datasets and architecture details

PLM Dataset Model architecture
ProteinBert [38] Pretrained on 106M protein sequences, obtained from UniProtKB/UniRef90 Bert
ProtTrans [23] ProtElectra UniRef50, UniRef100 BFD
ProtAlbert UniRef100
ProtBert UniRef100, BFD 100
ProtXLNet UniRef100
ESM (v1/v2) [25] UniRef50, UniRef90, UniRef100 Standard transformer architecture
Other variants: ESM-1b, ESM-MSA-1, ESM-MSA-1b, ESM-1v, ESM-IF1
S-PLM [39] ~500K sequences from Swiss-Prot and AlphaFold2-predicted Cα contact maps Transformer-based sequence encoder and a ResNet50 structure encoder integrated with multi-view contrastive learning
SaProt [26] ~40 million protein sequences and AlphaFold2-predicted structures Similar to ESM-2 architecture with an expanded embedding layer
PST [40] ~500K structures AlphaFold DB (SwissProt subset) ESM-2 architecture With GNN-based structure extractors
DeProt [41] ~18.8 million AlphaFoldDB's 3D structures Hybrid transformer with a GNN-based structure encoder and disentangled attention to integrate sequence and structural information
ESM-3 [10] ~3 billion protein sequences, 236 million structures, 539 million annotated proteins Bidirectional transformer integrating sequence, structure, and function through tokenized multimodal representation with geometric attention

3.1.1 Encoder–Only PLMs

Encoder-only PLMs are designed to extract contextual representations of protein sequences, making them particularly well suited for classification tasks in various protein-related tasks. These models use the encoder component of the transformer architecture to process input sequences and capture their complex relationships and features. We describe some of the encoder-only PLMs that are used to generate information-rich protein representation useful in training models for various protein-related downstream tasks, following the summary of each model. The list of encoder-only PLMs described in the chapter is shown in Table 2.

ProteinBert

Built upon BERT [12] architecture, ProteinBERT [38] is a PLM trained on a large protein sequence dataset obtained from Uni-ProtKB/UniRef90. The model is based on denoising autoencoder architecture, pretraining on two tasks of bidirectional language modeling of protein sequences and GO (Gene Ontology) annotations simultaneously, extracting both the local-level and globallevel representations. The architecture comprises multiple transformer blocks with skip connections and layer normalizations. The model is evaluated on nine benchmark datasets on the tasks of protein structure, posttranslational modifications, and biophysical properties, through fine-tuning methodology. Depending on the target variable of the benchmark tasks, local or global features were used, and supervised fine-tuning has almost outperformed other state-of-the-art models in the respective protein benchmarks. Further, the ProteinBERT model is not dependent on the sequence length and thus generalizes well over different sequence lengths. Compared to other protein language models, ProteinBERT is smaller in size, having only 16 M parameters, making it less resource-extensive for inference.

ProtTrans Models (ProtElectra, ProtAlbert, ProtBert)

ProtTrans [23] utilizes high-performance computing to understand the language of life as defined by amino acid sequences, by training language models on billions of protein sequences leading to protein language models. The authors pretrain two autoregressive models; Transformer-XL and XL-Net, and four autoencoder models; BERT, Albert, Electra, and T5 on protein sequence datasets of UniRef50, UniRef100, and BFD. The Prot-Trans models take as input the tokenized amino acids and pretrain them using either CLM (auto-regressive models) or MLM (autoencoder models). The model utilizes the transformer architecture and the self-attention mechanism to obtain context-aware representations at both residue or amino acid level and protein or global level. The resulting embeddings, i.e., high-dimensional vector representations, have been shown to capture the biophysical features, protein structural class, and lineage of kingdoms through tSNE projections. Depending on the task, a CNN can be trained on residue-level (embeddings of individual amino acids) for tokenlevel classification such as binding residue prediction. These embeddings can be concatenated and average-pooled to obtain global context-aware embeddings of fixed-length vectors, irrespective of the input sequence length. Afterward, protein-level embeddings can be used to train, e.g., feedforward neural networks for protein-level classifications such as subcellular localization prediction. ProtTrans has been used in diverse bioinformatics applications such as signal peptides classification [42], prediction of antibody

structure [43], and glutarylation sites [44]. ProtBERT has been used in predicting intrinsic disorder proteins/regions [45] and protein DNA binding [31, 46].

ESM \(( \mathbf { v } \mathbf { 1 } / \mathbf { v } 2 )\)

The ESM family of models was developed to apply natural language techniques to protein modeling. ESM 1b [47] is a transformer model, trained on masked language modeling objectives. It was trained on UniParc and UniRef databases [16]. Different datasets were developed with the objective of training, measuring generalization, and tuning toward a diverse set of sequences. The models were compared with each other on the metric of exponential crossentropy, which measured the mean uncertainty of prediction. The ESM 1b architecture features a 34-layer transformer model with 670 million parameters, pretrained on diverse protein sequence datasets. These models were developed to question whether natural language techniques could be transferred to the protein domain. The protein sequences were considered as sentences and amino acids are characters, training in an unsupervised way to obtain latent representations of protein sequences. The learned embeddings thus obtained, are shown to encode biochemical properties, remote homology, and alignment within a protein family, and applied for the prediction of secondary structure and tertiary contacts.

ESM 2 [48] is an enhanced model, where an attention mechanism is used to boost performance. It is a self-attention transformer, trained on a UniRef database in an unsupervised setting. The model was evaluated on the metric of perplexity that measures the prediction in relation to the context. The work establishes a strong link between language modeling perplexity and the accuracy of structural prediction. With a 15B parameter model, it was the largest model to train and showed the ability to capture atomic resolution prediction. Further, it was found that the size of the model increases the ability to capture structural information. The latent representations obtained from ESM 2 were applied to develop ESMfold, which predicts secondary structure faster than AlphaFold, using only protein sequences. These variants of ESM models have been used by other researchers in various applications such as genome-wide disease variant effects [49], cleavage site prediction [50, 51], predicting intrinsically disordered prediction [52], protein-protein interaction (PPI) [53], and secondary structure prediction [30].

S-PLM

The S-PLM [39] was trained on a protein dataset from the Swiss-Prot library, with structural data derived from AlphaFold2- predicted 3D structures represented as Cα contact maps. The architecture integrates two encoders: a transformer-based sequence encoder adapted from ESM 2 for sequence embeddings and a

ResNet50-based structure encoder for contact map embeddings. While S-PLM incorporates both sequence and 3D structure information, it primarily emphasizes the benefits of encoding protein sequences and leverages structural data to enhance sequence-based prediction tasks. The training of S-PLM is based on a multi-view contrastive learning framework, aligning sequence and structure embeddings of the same protein. This approach employs a contrastive loss function based on normalized temperature-scaled crossentropy (NT-Xent). The model has been applied to tasks such as protein function annotation, where the alignment of sequence and structure information improves the prediction accuracy of active protein sites and functional roles.

SaProt

The SaProt [26] uses a dataset of \({ \sim } 4 0\) million protein sequences and their corresponding AlphaFold2-predicted 3D protein structures. This data was processed using Foldseek [54] to encode structural information into 3Di tokens, combined with residue tokens to create a novel structure-aware (SA) vocabulary. SAPROT uses the ESM-2 architecture with an expanded embedding layer to handle the SA tokens, enabling it to incorporate geometric protein structure data alongside sequence information. The model was trained using a masked language modeling (MLM) objective, focusing on predicting residue tokens while leveraging structure information as context. The training involved masking \(1 5 \%\) o f SA tokens per batch. This approach enabled SaProt to excel in tasks requiring comprehensive protein representation, including structure and function prediction. The SaProt model has been applied in secondary structure prediction, protein cleavage site prediction, and protein-protein interaction (PPI) prediction [55, 56].

PST

The protein structure transformer (PST) [40] was trained on a curated subset of AlphaFold’s database containing \({ \sim } 5 0 0 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }\) predicted protein structures. It builds on the ESM-2 language model which is used to extract embeddings for each protein sequence. Subsequently, the protein genome position and coding strand information are concatenated with ESM-2 embeddings and passed through a PST encoder that consists of multi-head attention layers. In contrast to the commonly used training objective in the masked language model (MLM), this uses triplet loss, conditioned on the relationship between proteins and genomes for improved capture of patterns in genetic diversity. The triplet loss objective ensures that the positive genome is closer to the anchor genome than the negative genome, maintaining a separation defined by a tunable margin. Finally, the PST embeddings are obtained that are further used as protein representation which is used to train downstream classification models.

DeProt

Disentangled protein sequence-structure model (DeProt) [41] was pretrained on \({ \sim } 1 9\) million protein sequences and high-quality protein structures from AlphaFoldDB, filtered for quality using pLDDT scores, and clustered to reduce redundancy. It incorporates both amino acid information and 3D structure information using techniques called structure quantization and sequence-structure disentangled attention. Specifically, 3D protein structures are represented using a graph \(G = ( V , E )\) , where \(V\) and \(E\) represent the residue-level nodes and edges, respectively. The features of the edges include the angles and distances between two residues, as well as positional encoding. Structure quantization transforms the graph representation of protein structures into discrete tokens for better integration with sequence data. The disentangled attention mechanism explicitly models the relationships between residues, structures, and their relative positions. Pretraining employed a masked language modeling objective with structure-conditioned masking, enabling the model to predict masked residues using both sequence and structural context.

ESM-3

Evolutionary scale modeling-3 (ESM-3) [10] is a multimodal generative language model trained on protein sequences and structures enabling the design of novel proteins. It uses a bidirectional transformer architecture with up to 98 billion parameters and integrates discrete token representations of sequence, structure, and function into a shared latent space. Trained on 3.15 billion sequences, 236 million structures, and 539 million annotated proteins (totaling 771 billion unique tokens), ESM-3 uses a generative MLM objective with variable masking rates to balance representation learning and generative capability. Its structural tokenization and geometric attention mechanisms allow efficient processing of 3D protein structures.

3.1.2 Encoder–Decoder PLMs

Encoder–decoder PLMs are designed to solve sequence-tosequence tasks in protein biology. These models excel in various tasks, such as protein sequence generation and engineering, by combining an encoder that transforms input protein sequences into contextual representations with a decoder that generates corresponding output sequences. The encoder component of the encoder–decoder PLMs is often used for protein representation (aka as a feature extractor) and the decoder component can be used for protein generation. This architecture is used by models like ProtT5 and ProstT5, to capture the complex relationships in protein sequences/structures. The list of encoder–decoder PLMs is presented in Table 3.

Table 3 Summary of encoder–decoder PLMs with training datasets and architecture details

PLM Dataset Model architecture
ProtT5 [Variants: T5-XL and T5-XXL] [23] BFD, UniRef50 T5-XL and T5-XXL model, 24-layer encoder-decoder, ~3B parameters
ProteinLM [57] PFAM BERT-based model (48 transformer layers)
CaLM [58] Pretrained on ~9 million nonredundant coding sequences from whole-genome sequencing data Transformer 12 encoder layers, 12 attention heads)
Ankh [24] T5 architecture (similar to ProtT5 framework with hyperparameter tuning) UniRef50
ProstT5 [59] 17 million sequences, AlphaFold predicted 3D structures T5 (fine-tuned ProtT5 for both sequence-structure translations)
ss-PLM [60] UniRef50 (only 1.7 Million Sequences) Encoder-decoder transformer
ProteinDT [61] SwissProtCLAP with 441K text-protein pairs from SwissProt 3 component architecture: ProteinCLAP (contrastive learning for aligning text and protein representations), ProteinFacilitator (generates protein sequence representations from text), and a decoder (produces protein sequences from representations)
LM-Design [62] CATH 4.2/4.3, AlphaFold-2 predicted structures Enhanced PLMs using a lightweight structural adapter Integrates geometric information from structure encoders like ProteinMPNN
xTrimoPGLM [63] ~940 million unique protein sequences from multiple sources General language model (GLM) Architecture

ProtT5 (ProtTrans)

ProtT5-XL-UniRef50 [23] is based on Google’s T5 [14] architecture, specifically the T5-3B architecture, which consists of a 24-layer encoder–decoder with around 2.8 billion trainable parameters. Unlike the original T5, which uses a span denoising objective, ProtT5 is pretrained using a BERT-like MLM objective, where \({ \sim } 1 5 \%\) of amino acids are randomly masked in the input sequences given to the encoder and the decoder is asked to output the non-corrupted counterpart. This approach allows the model to predict the missing tokens, enhancing its ability to learn the biophysical and fundamental characteristics of the protein sequences. It is pretrained in a self-supervised fashion on two large datasets: the

Big Fantastic Database (BFD) and UniRef50, the latter comprising about 45 million protein sequences. The model has been extensively used for embedding extractions that have been effective in multiple downstream tasks capturing key biophysical properties in protein sequences such as PTM prediction [33, 34, 37], secondary structure prediction [30], and DNA-binding protein prediction [31, 64, 65].

ProteinLM

ProteinLM [57] is a large-scale PLM that captures the complex evolutionary and biological patterns from the protein sequences. The model is pretrained on the BERT architecture, specifically adapting its masked language modeling (MLM) approach to proteins with slight modifications to its loss function and model structure. Unlike BERT, ProteinLM does not employ next-sentence prediction (NSP), inferring that protein sequences lack the semantic continuity characteristic of natural language. The training data is derived from the PFAM dataset, which consists of over 32 million protein sequences clustered into evolutionarily related families. It is trained unsupervised by randomly masking \(1 5 \%\) of amino acid tokens and training the model to predict these masked tokens, focusing solely on the MLM loss. For efficient training at this scale, ProteinLM utilizes the Megatron-LM framework, optimizing the model with techniques like model parallelism and memory optimization. ProteinLM demonstrates its capacity to capture local and global sequence dependencies, achieving superior performance in various downstream tasks, such as secondary structure prediction, remote homology detection, contact prediction, fluorescence prediction, and stability assessment.

CaLM

Codon adaptation language model (CaLM) [58] is built upon the ESM-2 framework that uses an architecture comprising 12 encoder layers, each with 12 attention heads, resulting in a model with 86 million parameters. Unlike other protein language models that utilize amino acid sequences, CaLM is trained on codon sequences (cDNA) derived from approximately nine million nonredundant coding sequences obtained through whole-genome sequencing. The model uses a masked language modeling (MLM) objective during pretraining with a denoising objective that enables CaLM to learn contextual representations of codons, capturing patterns of synonymous codon usage and the underlying biochemical features of codon sequences. Despite its relatively smaller parameter count compared to larger models like ESMs or ProtTrans, CaLM’s focus on codon sequences tends to provide a unique advantage in capturing evolutionary signals from codon-level representation that are not present in amino acid-based models. Recently, CaLMPhosKAN

[66], a phosphorylation site prediction tool that has been developed by integrating CaLM along with ProtT5, has been proposed to enhance phosphorylation site prediction.

Ankh

Ankh [24] is an optimized PLM for protein sequence modeling, based on an encoder–decoder transformer architecture developed for machine translation. Unlike the recent trends of training models, which focus mostly on increasing the model size, Ankh focuses on protein-specific optimizations to improve performance efficiently. The model has two variants: Ankh_base with 450 million parameters and a 768-dimensional embedding space, and Ankh_large, with 1.15 billion parameters and a 1536-dimensional embedding space. It has an optimized architecture with a 48-layer encoder and a 24-layer decoder, optimized for various tasks of protein modeling. Based on the ProtT5 [23] framework, Ankh achieves competitive performance on most benchmarks for protein structure, function, and generation with drastically reduced computational requirements. This design prioritizes both performance and accessibility, making Ankh a versatile model for diverse downstream protein tasks such as PTM prediction [34] and protein solubility prediction [67].

ProstT5

ProstT5 [59] extends ProtT5, a model that was originally trained using only amino acid sequences. ProstT5 includes both protein sequence-amino acids and protein structure information, encoded as 3Di tokens [54]. The dataset for fine-tuning consists of 17 million high-quality, nonredundant protein sequences with predicted 3D structures from AlphaFoldDB [68], which were converted into 1D 3Di-token representations using Foldseek [54]. The training objective includes both the original span-based denoising objective on both sequences (amino acids and 3Di) to prevent catastrophic forgetting and a bi-directional translation task, where the model learns to translate between amino acid sequences and 3Di structure representations. This training technique is applied to capture rich features from both protein sequences and their 3D structural representations. ProstT5 excels in downstream protein tasks such as protein-binding site prediction [69], PTM prediction [70], and peptide bioactivity predictors [71].

SS-PLM

The Small-scale protein language model (SS-PLM) [60] is a comparatively small encoder–decoder architecture trained with selfattention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies within protein sequences. The model is trained based on the causal language model approach with data corruption where the objective is

to predict the next amino acid in a sequence. The SS-PLM was first pretrained on a subset of the UniRef50 dataset, specifically selected to focus on enzymatic sequences, reducing the dataset size to 1.7 million representative sequences from 45 million sequences. These major contributions of SS-PLM are aiming to maximize performance while reducing computational overhead, by optimizing training and architectural design considerations, making protein language modeling more accessible and efficient.

ProteinDT

ProteinDT [61] is a multimodal framework that bridges the gap between textual descriptions and protein design, incorporating both textual and protein sequence data. The architecture consists of three key components: ProteinCLAP, ProteinFacilitator, and a decoder. ProteinCLAP is the pretraining component that uses contrastive learning to align the representation spaces of text and protein sequences. It uses the SwissProtCLAP dataset that contains text and protein pairs which is the foundation for training the model by ensuring that both modalities (text and protein sequences) are represented in a shared latent space. Second, ProteinFacilitator generates protein sequence representations from text input, enabling the third component (i.e., decoder) to generate the corresponding protein sequences. The model is trained to maximize the alignment between textual descriptions of protein functions and the generated protein sequences, improving the framework’s ability to perform text-guided protein generation.

LM-Design

LM-Design [62] transforms existing protein language models (PLMs) like ESMs into protein design tools by incorporating a lightweight structural adapter that introduces structural awareness into the sequence-based model. Using a framework called LM-Design, the adapter integrates geometric information from external structure encoders such as ProteinMPNN to utilize both sequential and structural knowledge effectively. It uses conditional masked language modeling (CMLM) training objectives where random amino acid residues are masked and the model learns to predict them using the surrounding sequence context and structure information. Multiple datasets including CATH \(4 . 2 / 4 . 3\) benchmarks are used to evaluate and compare the performance against other tools.

xTrimoPGLM

xTrimoPGLM [63] is a large protein language model built on the general language model (GLM) architecture, which uniquely combines bidirectional and autoregressive modeling capabilities to address protein understanding and generation tasks. It is trained

using a dual approach incorporating the MLM and the GLM objectives that enable simultaneous optimization of token prediction in masked sequences and autoregressive sequence generation. The model was pretrained on a large dataset of ~940 million unique protein sequences containing around 200 billion residues, using ~100 billion parameter model processing over 1 trillion tokens. The overall training process was structured in two phases: an initial pretraining with the MLM objective to boost understanding capabilities, followed by a merged training phase with a \(2 0 \%\) MLM and \(8 0 \%\) GLM ratio to refine representation and generative abilities. Despite xTrimoPGLM having great potential to better represent proteins, its massive scale of 100 billion parameters often poses challenges for inference, particularly for small-scale downstream tasks in resource-constrained settings.

3.1.3 Decoder-Only PLMs

Pure decoder-only models, like ProtGPT2 [72], are designed for autoregressive tasks, focusing on predicting sequences from prior inputs. Models built using only the decoder component predict the next amino acid in a sequence given all the previous ones. Decoderonly PLMs have been shown to excel in de novo protein sequence generation and further protein engineering and drug discovery by designing novel functional proteins. The list of decoder-only PLMs described in this chapter are presented in Table 4.

ProGen2

ProGen2 [73] is an autoregressive transformer model for protein design which is trained using next-token prediction language modeling learning objective. The architecture is a decoder-only transformer with left-to-right causal masking with rotary positional encoding. A mixture of Uniref90 and BFD30 databases are trained. It has small-to-large versions ranging from \(1 . 5 \mathrm { ~ M ~ }\) to 6.5B parameters. ProGen2 is used in various protein sequence design applications [75].

Table 4 Summary of decoder-only PLMs with training datasets, and architecture details

PLM Dataset Model architecture
ProGen2 [73] Uniref90, BFD30 Decoder-only transformer with next-token prediction and left-to-right causal masking, using rotary positional encoding
ProtGPT2 [72] UniRef50 Decoder-only transformer (36 layers)
ProLLaMA [74] UniRef50, InterPro (property texts from) LLaMA2 Multitask PLP with instruction tuning
ProseLM [75] CATH 4.2, SAbDab Causal encoder for structural context + ProGen2 for structure-conditioned protein sequence design

ProtGPT2

ProtGPT2 [72] is a decoder-only protein language model, closely resembling the GPT-2 large model with 36 layers. The model consists of 738 million parameters trained on the UniRef50 dataset using a self-attention mechanism optimized for generating protein sequences in an autoregressive manner. For tokenization, ProtGPT2 employs Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), creating a vocabulary of 50,256 tokens, where each token represents an average of four amino acids. This large-scale training setup is considered to enable ProtGPT2 to learn the underlying structure of protein sequences efficiently, positioning it to generate novel, diverse, and potentially functional protein sequences.

ProseLM

ProseLM [75] is a protein sequence design model that adapts protein language models with structural and functional context. Built on ProGen2, it integrates structural information using parameter-efficient adapters to combine embeddings from a causal encoder with each layer of the pretrained language model. The causal encoder extracts structural features and atomic context graphs to predict sequences based on protein backbones. ProseLM was trained on diverse datasets, including single-chain proteins, multi-chain complexes, and antibody structures, with frozen language model parameters updating the adapters and casual encoder.

ProLLaMA

Using a two-stage training approach, the ProLLaMA [74] framework introduces a protein large language model (ProLLM) for multitask protein language processing (PLP) by adapting general LLMs, such as LLaMA2 [76]. The model is trained using causal language modeling on the UniRef50 dataset and protein property texts from InterPro, with continual learning to instruct protein language while preserving natural language capabilities. ProL-LaMA uses Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to train only a fraction of the parameters, reducing computational costs and preventing catastrophic forgetting. The architecture integrates LoRA adapters into LLaMA2’s decoder layers to effectively distinguish protein from natural language.

4 Application of PLMs

Protein language models (PLMs) have become essential tools in protein bioinformatics, demonstrating exceptional efficiency across various protein-related applications. These models can mainly be used for two major tasks: protein representation and protein

design/engineering. Encoder-only and encoder–decoder models are particularly effective at extracting protein representations, which are crucial for classification tasks. These models have shown high accuracy in tasks such as structure prediction, disorder region identification, posttranslational modification (PTM) prediction, protein-ligand binding site identification, protein-protein interaction prediction, and cleavage site prediction. Furthermore, encoder–decoder and decoder-only PLMs are highly suited for applications in protein engineering and design [77]. Specifically, encoder–decoder models excel in sequence-to-sequence prediction tasks, such as designing protein variants or aligning sequences to specific structural templates whereas decoder-only models are highly effective in autoregressive tasks such as generation of novel protein sequences [78]. These capabilities are critical for designing de novo proteins with desired properties. Please refer to the work of Ruffolo and Madani [79] for review on designing proteins using language models. Some downstream protein bioinformatics tasks and the corresponding tools that leverage PLMs for these tasks are presented in Table 5.

Similarly, Table 6 highlights some of the exciting application of protein language models (PLMs) in protein engineering and design. These models leverage sequence and structure-based protein language models in tasks such as designing novel protein sequences, optimizing structures for specific functions, and solving inverse folding challenges.

Table 5 Overview of recent applications of protein language models (PLMs) for various downstream tasks

Task Applications
PTM prediction LMSuccSite [33], LM-OGlcNAc-Site [34], PLMSNOSite [80], LMCrot [37], LMNGlyPred [81], LMPhosSite [35], CalmPhosKAN [66], DeepPTM, PTMGPT2 [82]
Nucleic acid protein-binding site prediction/DNA-binding sites prediction PDNAPred [64], CLAPE [46], bindEmbed21 [83], DeepProSite[84], EquiPNAS [85], CrossBind [86]
DNA-binding protein prediction PLM-DBPs [31], PreDBP-PLMs [32], ESM-DBP [87]
Protein-protein interaction (PPI) ESMDNN-PPI [88], KSGPPI [89]
Protein function annotation/prediction DeepGO [90], DeepFRi [91], GAT-GO [92], SPROF-GO [93]
Protein cleavage site prediction DeepNeuropePred [50]
Disorder region prediction SETH [94], IDP-ELM [95], DisPredict3.0 [52], IDP-LM [45]
Protein structure prediction ESMFold [25], AlphaFold [68], OmegaFold [96], RoseTTA Fold [97], EMBER2 [98]HelixFold-Single [99], RGN2 [100]

Table 6 Recent applications of protein language models (PLMs) in protein engineering and design

Task Application
Protein engineering/design (sequence or structure) RFDiffusion [101], Prot-VAE [78], ProGen [102] Verkuil et al. [103]
Protein sequence prediction (inverse folding) ESM-IF1 [104], ProteinMPNN [105], MIF-ST [106], ESM-Design [107], EvoDiff [108]

5 Conclusion and Discussion

The last couple of years has seen tremendous advancements in the field of protein language models. The two most important uses of PLMs have been the protein understanding task aka in the downstream classification task and protein generation task—aka engineering and design of proteins/antibodies/biologics. Although there has been a tremendous improvement in the field of protein language models based on protein sequences, the same is not yet true for protein language models based on protein structures. With the availability of predicted structures for all the proteins, courtesy of AlphaFold, we are likely to see more developments in proteinstructure-based language models. Moreover, the field of LLMs in NLP is developing at an alarming rate and these advances are likely to be incorporated into the PLMs. One challenge in the field has been the lack of interpretability of the embeddings generated from these models. Below we summarize some emerging trends in the field.

5.1 (Scaling) Small Protein Language Models

There is no doubt that PLMs have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in understanding contextual relationships, outperforming traditional approaches in downstream tasks in protein bioinformatics. More recently, following the same trend as in natural language processing (NLP), protein language models have continuously been scaled up. One thing to note is that the training of these PLMs poses a significant challenge. In this regard, there have been some studies to explore small-scale protein language models [60] which perform similarly to the larger models that are computationally more demanding. Additionally, there are also efforts [109] to make an efficient codebase, design a modern architecture, and remove data quality concerns. Amplify [109] is a newer model which is less expensive to train and deploy than other existing protein language models.

5.2 Multimodal Protein Language Models

As discussed above, most of the PLMs are trained on protein sequences. One new trend that has been observed in the field is the development of multimodal protein language models trained

on multiple modes of information (protein sequence, structure, text, etc.). ProteinChat [110] is a multimodal protein language model that is trained on protein sequences and text. Unlike existing PLMs, this PLM takes an amino acid sequence and a (text) prompt as inputs, and generates a detailed textual prediction of protein function, unlike discrete categories as in GO. Additionally, the PST protein structure transformer is a new PLM [40] that is trained on protein structure databases. One thing to note here is that somehow the field is yet to come up with an efficient approach to tokenize structure as protein structure language models did not improve over PLMs to an extent that would amortize the computational overhead of having to predict structures first.

5.3 Enhancing Efficient Training of PLM

There have also been some works in the field to deal with difficulties in training a PLM in problems that lack large amounts of data. One of the recent works, Few-Shot Learning for Protein Fitness Prediction (FSFP) [111] uses a training strategy that can effectively optimize protein language models for protein fitness prediction under these data scarcity conditions by leveraging meta-transfer learning, learning to rank and parameter efficient fine-tuning. Additionally, ESM-DBP [87] uses domain-adaptive pretraining of the general PLM and DNA-binding protein repertoire to improve DNA-binding protein prediction.

5.4 Emergence of Domain-Specific PLM

In addition to general purpose PLM (a language model trained on a vast dataset of protein sequences, allowing it to analyze and predict various properties of proteins across a wide range of protein families, without being specifically optimized for a single protein type or function; essentially, it acts as a versatile tool for understanding and manipulating protein sequences in diverse biological contexts, similar to how a general purpose natural language model can handle various text-based tasks), one of the other trends that have been observed in the field is the development of domainspecific PLM. DR-BERT is a domain-specific PLM that is trained to predict disorder regions [112]. IgLM [113] is a generative immunoglobulin language model to generate/design antibodies. Another example is the several PLMs trained on immune-specific protein sequences (such as AbLang, Antibert, and AbMAP). Additionally, there have been studies also to assess the usefulness of domain-specific PLMs vs. general purpose PLMs [114]. At least, this study shows that it is not always true that “domain-specific models outperform general models” and also highlights the importance of selecting the appropriate layer for extracting embeddings in optimizing performance on downstream tasks.

5.5 PLM Trained on the Reduced Alphabet

Reduced amino acid alphabets have been of interest in the field due to their ability to simplify the protein sequence space. Ieremie et al. [115] explored the training of PLM on reduced amino acid alphabets, and as expected the PLM trained on reduced amino acid alphabets does not perform as well as amino acid alphabets due to loss of protein sequence information and due to the subsequent loss on learned representation.

5.6 Advanced Use of PLMs for Protein Design—AI Agents

Designing de novo proteins beyond those found in nature holds significant promise for advancements in both scientific and engineering applications. The use of emerging themes like AI agents and others has started to show their usage in the field of protein bioinformatics. ProtAgents [116] is a platform for protein design based on LLMs where multiple AI agents with distinct capabilities collaboratively address information retrieval, protein structure analysis, physics-based simulation, and results analysis.

5.7 Fine-Tuning and Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning of PLMs

Until recently, most protein language model-based protein predictions just use raw embeddings from the protein language model and do not back-propagate to the language model. In NLP, finetuning large language models in a supervised manner to adapt to the subsequent downstream task has become the de facto standard [30]. However, the computational resources needed for finetuning large PLMS pose a significant challenge. Borrowing from NLP, we are observing approaches for efficient fine-tuning like parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) approaches like LoRA, DoRA, qLORA, etc. Recently, Bonnie Berger’s group at MIT [117] proposed PEFT approaches for the prediction of proteinprotein interaction and homo-oligomer symmetry. Similarly, Rost’s group also proposed PEFT on several downstream tasks [30]. One of the main observations of these approaches is that task-specific fine-tuning is likely to improve the downstream performance and the required computational resources are always fewer than full fine-tuning.

5.8 Prompt-Based Fine-Tuning of PLM

We have started seeing the application of prompt-based fine-tuning of PLMs. PTMGPT2 [118] is a prompt-based fine-tuning of ProtGPT2 [72] (a GPT2 model based on protein sequences) model that fine-tunes ProtGTP2 for generating classification labels for PTMs.
