Exploring ProtFlash: An Efficient Approach to Protein Data Analysis

Lei Wang, Zhidong Xue, and Yan Wang

Abstract

This chapter introduces ProtFlash, a novel lightweight protein language model that represents a significant advancement in the field of bioinformatics. By addressing the computational challenges inherent in protein sequence analysis, ProtFlash offers an efficient and accessible solution that combines innovative architectural design with practical applicability. The model incorporates several key technological breakthroughs, including a mixed-chunk attention mechanism that integrates both global and local attention patterns, and the implementation of RoPE position encoding for handling protein sequences of arbitrary lengths. This comprehensive guide details the model’s development, implementation, and practical applications in protein downstream tasks. It provides step-by-step instructions for utilizing the ProtFlash library (https://github.com/ISYSLAB-HUST/ProtFlash), including advanced techniques such as protein embedding generation and model fine-tuning using low-rank adaptation (LoRA) methodology. The chapter serves as both a theoretical foundation and practical handbook for researchers and practitioners in the field of protein sequence analysis and bioinformatics.

Key words ProtFlash, Protein language model, Protein embedding, LoRA, t-SNE

1 Introduction

Proteins are fundamental macromolecules of life and play a crucial role in maintaining human health and in the treatment of diseases. These biomolecules are involved in almost every cellular process, including enzyme catalysis, signal transduction, immune responses, and cellular structure maintenance. As such, understanding the properties and functions of proteins is paramount in biomedical research, drug design, and precision medicine [1]. The sequence information of proteins—comprising chains of amino acids—is critical for determining their three-dimensional structure and biological functions. This knowledge is essential for decoding the “biological language” of proteins, which is key to understanding

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_3, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

complex biological systems and disease mechanisms [2]. With the rapid advancement of sequencing technologies, enormous volumes of protein sequence data have been generated. Large-scale databases such as UniProt [3] and Pfam [4] have been established to store and organize this data, providing essential resources for scientists. These databases serve as valuable repositories of protein sequences, annotations, and functional information. Researchers can now tap into these datasets to develop sophisticated computational models, particularly deep learning models, for a variety of protein-related tasks.

Simultaneously, artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, particularly large language models (LLMs), have revolutionized natural language processing (NLP) and many other fields [5]. A prominent example of this is ChatGPT, a conversational model that has set new benchmarks in human–computer interaction. Inspired by the success of LLMs in NLP, protein language models have emerged as a powerful tool for representing and learning from protein sequence data. These models, which aim to capture the “semantic” meaning of proteins, are quickly becoming the standard approach for protein feature representation and have shown promise in outperforming traditional methods for protein sequence analysis [6– 8]. Protein language models operate by learning distributed representations of protein sequences through deep neural networks. These models are typically trained in an unsupervised manner, meaning they can learn from large, unannotated datasets. This approach enables them to capture the complex relationships and hidden patterns embedded in protein sequences. The result is a model that can “understand” the sequence in much the same way that large language models understand human languages. As a consequence, these models have made groundbreaking strides in a variety of downstream tasks, such as predicting protein structures [9] and functional annotation [10].

However, despite the impressive progress made, several challenges remain in the development and application of protein language models. One of the most significant challenges is the inherent complexity of protein sequences. The length of proteins varies greatly, ranging from approximately 30 to over 33,000 amino acid residues. This is much larger than typical sequences in natural languages, where sentences usually contain no more than 15–30 words. The sheer length of protein sequences poses a significant challenge for computational models, particularly those based on the transformer architecture, which is widely used in protein language models [11], which has a computational complexity that scales quadratically with the input sequence length. Therefore, the high demand for computational resources has become a bottleneck

for most academic communities with limited resources. A common approach is to truncate protein sequences to reduce computational complexity; however, this may compromise the semantic information of the protein sequences.

To address the computational intensity of existing protein language models, we have developed a lightweight protein language model called ProtFlash [12]. Its lightweight characteristics are mainly reflected in three aspects: linear complexity, lower memory consumption, and relatively fewer parameters. The model employs a hybrid chunked attention mechanism, incorporating both global and local attention, to meet the linear complexity requirements of attention-based models. Unlike learnable position encodings, Prot-Flash integrates the latest RoPE position encoding method [13], allowing it to encode protein sequences of arbitrary lengths. Another key feature of ProtFlash is its lightweight architecture, which is reflected in its relatively small number of parameters. With only 174 million parameters, ProtFlash is substantially smaller than many of the larger protein language models available today. Despite its smaller size, ProtFlash has shown excellent performance in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Specifically, the model can be trained efficiently on just eight NVIDIA A40 Graphics Processing Units (GPUs), a significant reduction in computational resource requirements compared to larger models. Additionally, ProtFlash’s memory consumption increases more slowly as the protein sequence length grows. For example, for sequences ranging from 1024 to 4096 residues, the memory consumption of ProtFlash increases by approximately 3.4 times, which is a substantial improvement over existing attention-based protein models that experience much larger increases in memory usage. Given these advantages, ProtFlash is well suited for use by researchers with limited access to high-performance computing resources. By lowering the computational requirements, ProtFlash opens up new possibilities for protein sequence analysis in academic and smaller-scale research settings.

In this chapter, we will provide a comprehensive introduction to using the ProtFlash library. We will guide readers through its installation and use in Python environments, such as IPython notebooks or Python scripts. Through step-by-step examples, we will demonstrate how to apply ProtFlash for various tasks, including generating protein embeddings and fine-tuning the model using techniques like low-rank adaptation (LoRA), which helps improve model performance with fewer parameters. These examples will showcase how ProtFlash can be effectively integrated into research workflows, offering a practical tool for protein sequence analysis.

2 Materials

To use ProtFlash, you will need the following resources:

  1. CPU: Intel Core i9 or AMD Ryzen 9 series, supporting fast processing of large datasets and complex computational tasks.
  2. Memory (RAM): At least 32 GB; 64 GB or higher is recommended for handling large datasets and performing large-scale model training.
  3. GPU: NVIDIA RTX 3080 or higher model, providing strong computing power and high memory bandwidth suitable for deep learning training.
  4. Storage: At least 1 TB SSD; using NVMe SSD can enhance read/write speeds, and consider additional HDD for data backup.
  5. Network: High-speed internet connection, suitable for users frequently downloading large datasets or synchronizing with remote servers.

Datasets:

Protein sequence data can be downloaded from public databases such as UniProt (https://www.uniprot.org/) and Pfam (http://pfam.xfam.org/).

3 Methods

This section is the core of this chapter and will detail how to use ProtFlash for protein sequence analysis. We will introduce it, stepby-step.

3.1 Installing ProtFlash

You can customize the command based on your system configuration (e.g., CUDA version):

conda install pytorch torchvision torchaudio -c pytorch

You can use this one-liner for installation, using the latest release version:

# latest version
pip install git+https://github.com/isyslab-hust/ProtFlash
# stable version
pip install ProtFlash

3.2 Data Preparation

Unlike traditional bioinformatics software that directly uses the fasta format, ProtFlash parses sequences directly from dictionaries. This usually involves converting the sequence to a specific format that ProtFlash can handle. First, download protein sequence data from the UniProt database. Format the data as follows to fit the input requirements of ProtFlash:

data = [  
("protein1", "MKTVRQERLKSIVRILERSKEPVSGAQLAEELSVSRRQVIVQD IAYLRSLG"),  
("protein2", "KALTARQQEVFDLIRDHISQTGMPPTRAEIAQRLGFRSPNAA EEHLKAL"),  
]

3.3 Generating Protein Embeddings

This code snippet demonstrates how to generate protein embeddings using the ProtFlash library. It begins by importing the necessary functions, load_prot_flash_base for loading the pretrained ProtFlash model and batchConverter for preparing the input data. A list of protein identifiers and their corresponding amino acid sequences is defined. The batchConverter processes this data, returning the tokenized sequences and their lengths. After loading the ProtFlash model, embeddings for the sequences are generated without tracking gradients, which saves memory and computational resources. Finally, the code computes average representations for each protein sequence by averaging the token embeddings, excluding padding tokens, and stores these representations for further analysis. This setup facilitates various tasks in protein sequence analysis, such as classification or clustering.

from ProtFlash.pretrain import load_protflash_base
from ProtFlash.utilis import batchConverter
ids, batch_token, lengths = batchConverter(data)
model \(=\) load_protflash_base()
with torch.no_grad(): token_embedding \(\equiv\) model(batch_token, lengths)
# Generate per-sequence representations via averaging
representations \(=\) []
for i, (_, seq) in enumerate(data): representations.append(token_embedding[i, 0: len(seq) + 1].mean(0))

3.4 t-SNE Visualization of Protein Embeddings: Uncovering Hidden Patterns

This code snippet demonstrates how to visualize protein sequence representations using t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) [ ]. It begins by importing the necessary libraries:14 numpy [ ] for array manipulation, matplotlib.pyplot for plotting,15 and sklearn.manifold.TSNE for dimensionality reduction. The averaged embeddings from the previous step are converted to a NumPy array. t-SNE is then applied to reduce the dimensionality of the embeddings to 2D, suitable for visualization. A scatter plot is created to display the t-SNE results, with each point representing a protein sequence, annotated with its identifier for clarity. This visualization helps to illustrate the relationships between different protein sequences in the embedding space.

import numpy as np   
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt   
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE   
# Assuming 'sequence Representations' contains the averaged embeddings from the previous code   
# Convert the list to a NumPy array for t-SNE   
embeddings_array = np.array(representations)

Apply t-SNE to reduce dimensions to 2D for visualization
tsne \(=\) TSNE(n_components=2, random_state \(\coloneqq\) 42)
tsne_results \(=\) tsne.fit_transform(embeddings_array)
# Plot the t-SNE results
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
pltscatter(tsne_results[.,0],tsne_results[.,1],marker \(\coloneqq\) ‘o’)
#Annotate points with protein identifiers
for i,(protein_id,_in enumerate(data): plt. annotate(protein_id,(tsne_results[i,0],tsne_results[i,1]),fontsize \(\coloneqq\) 9)
plt.title(“t-SNE Visualization of Protein Sequence Representations”)
plt.xlabel(“t-SNE Component 1”)
pltilated(“t-SNE Component 2”)
plt.grid()
plt.show()

3.5 Initializing ProtFlash with Custom Model Parameters

The provided code snippet demonstrates how to load custom weights into a ProtFlash model using PyTorch. It begins by importing the necessary libraries, including torch and FLASHTransformer from ProtFlash. The code then loads the model’s weights and hyperparameters from a specified file using torch.load(), extracting the relevant hyperparameters required for model initialization. The FLASHTransformer model is instantiated with these hyperparameters, including dimensions, number of tokens, layers, and other configuration settings. Finally, the pretrained weights are loaded into the model using the load_state_dict() method, enabling the use of customized weights for specific tasks while maintaining the model’s architecture.

import torch

from ProtFlash.model import FLASHTransformer

Load the model data from the specified file

model_data \(=\) torch.load(“your_parameters_file”) # Replace with your actual file path

Extract hyperparameters

hyper_parameter \(=\) model_data[“hyper_parameters”]

Initialize the FLASHTransformer model

model \(=\) FLASHTransformer(

hyper_parameter[‘dim’],

hyper_parameter[‘num_tokens’],

hyper_parameter[‘num_layers’],

group_size=hyper_parameter[‘num_tokens’],

query_key_dim=hyper_parameter[‘qk_dim’],

max_rel_dist=hyper_parameter[‘max_rel_dist’],

expansion_factor=hyper_parameter[‘expansion_factor’]

Load the model state dictionary

model.load_state_dict(model_data[‘state_dict’])

3.6 Model Optimization

3.6.1 Introduction to LoRA

Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) [16] is a parameter-efficient finetuning technique for neural networks. It significantly reduces the number of parameters that need to be trained during fine-tuning by applying a low-rank decomposition to the pretrained model’s weights, improving training efficiency, especially in downstream tasks.

LoRA works by decomposing the weight update matrix ΔW in neural networks into the product of two smaller matrices: \(\Delta { \cal W } = { \cal A } \times { \cal B }\) , where \(A \in R ^ { d ^ { \star } \times \ r }\) and \(\boldsymbol { B } \in \boldsymbol { R } ^ { r \times \textit { k } }\) , with \(r\) being the low-rank dimension (typically much smaller than \(d\) and \(k\) ). During fine-tuning, the original weight matrix W is frozen, and only the low-rank matrices \(A\) and \(B\) are trained. This reduces the number of trainable parameters from \(d \times k\) to \(( d \times \boldsymbol { r } ) + ( \boldsymbol { r } \times \boldsymbol { k } ) .\) , making finetuning more efficient. The final adapted weight becomes \(W + \Delta W = W + A \times B\) , allowing the model to maintain pretrained knowledge while adapting to new tasks with fewer parameters.

3.6.2 Installing the minLoRA Library

minLoRA (https://github.com/cccntu/minlora) is a compact yet fully functional PyTorch library for reimplementing LoRA, allowing it to be directly applied to any model that inherits from torch. nn.Module without modifying the model definition. It leverages PyTorch’s torch.nn.utils.parametrize for parameterization, making it easy to extend and enabling users to add custom LoRA parameterization methods. With just around 100 lines of code, minLoRA supports the training and inference of various LoRA models, offering a simple yet powerful solution for LoRA integration. To install the minLoRA library, you can follow these steps:

git clone https://github.com/cccntu/minLoRA.git

cd minLoRA

pip install -e .

3.6.3 Applying LoRA in ProtFlash

This code snippet demonstrates how to integrate the LoRA module into the ProtFlash model and optimize the LoRA parameters. The specific steps are as follows:

  1. Define LoRA Configuration: Use the LoRAParametrization. from_linear function to define the LoRA weight parameterization for torch.nn.Linear layers. The rank \(= 3\) indicates that the rank of the low-rank approximation is 3.

  2. Load ProtFlash Model: Use load_prot_flash_base() to load the original ProtFlash model.

  3. Add LoRA to the ProtFlash Model: Integrate the LoRA module into the ProtFlash model using the add_lora function and specify which layers use LoRA through lora_config.

  4. Move Model to Appropriate Device: Move the model to either GPU or CPU for computation based on the availability of a GPU.

  5. Obtain LoRA Parameters for Optimization: Use the get_lora_params method to retrieve the LoRA parameters and optimize these parameters with the Adam optimizer, setting the learning rate to 3e-4.

from minlora import add_lora, get_lora.params, get_lora_state_dict, LoRAPa  
rametrization  
from functools import partial  
# LoRA configuration  
lora_config = {torch(nn.Linear: {"weight": partial(LoRParametrization.from_LINEAR, rank=3),},}  
# Load the original ProtFlash model  
model = load_protflash_base()  
# Add LoRA to the ProtFlash model  
add_lora(model, lora_config=lora_config)

Move model to the appropriate device
device \(=\) torch_device(‘cuda:0’ if torch.cuda.is-available() else ‘cpu’)
model \(=\) model.to(device)
#Example of obtaining LoRA parameters for optimization
optimizer \(=\) torch.optim.Adam(get_lora.params(model),lr=3e-4)

4 Conclusion

This chapter serves as a comprehensive tutorial, detailing ProtFlash, a groundbreaking lightweight protein language model designed to overcome the computational limitations of traditional models. Characterized by linear complexity, low memory consumption, and a smaller number of parameters, ProtFlash is well suited for environments with limited resources. Through step-by-step guidance, this chapter demonstrates how to use ProtFlash within IPython notebooks or Python scripts, covering installation, data preparation, generation of protein embedding information, and model optimization using LoRA techniques. Additionally, we provide t-SNE visualization methods to reveal the relationships between protein sequences in a reduced-dimensional space.

In summary, this chapter offers detailed instructions for using ProtFlash and showcases its effective application in protein sequence analysis. Whether you are new to the field of bioinformatics or an expert, these guidelines will help you quickly become proficient and make the most of ProtFlash’s powerful capabilities.

Acknowledgments

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 62172172, the Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF under Grant Number GZC20240545, and the Scientific Research Start-up Foundation of Binzhou Medical University under Grant BY2020KYQD01.

  7. Lin Z, Akin H, Rao R, Hie B, Zhu Z, Lu W, Smetanin N, Verkuil R, Kabeli O, Shmueli Y (2023) Evolutionary-scale prediction of atomic-level protein structure with a language model. Science 379(6637):1123–1130

  8. Elnaggar A, Heinzinger M, Dallago C, Rehawi G, Wang Y, Jones L, Gibbs T, Feher T, Angerer C, Steinegger M (2021) Prottrans: toward understanding the language of life through self-supervised learning. IEEE Trans Pattern Anal Mach Intell 44(10): 7112–7127

  11. Lin T, Wang Y, Liu X, Qiu X (2022) A survey of transformers. AI Open 3:111–132

  12. Wang L, Zhang H, Xu W, Xue Z, Wang Y (2023) Deciphering the protein landscape with ProtFlash, a lightweight language model. Cell Rep Phys Sci 4(10):101600

  13. Su J, Ahmed M, Lu Y, Pan S, Bo W, Liu Y (2024) Roformer: enhanced transformer with rotary position embedding. Neurocomputing 568:127063

  14. Van der Maaten L, Hinton G (2008) Visualizing data using t-SNE. J Mach Learn Res 9(11): 2579

  15. Harris CR, Millman KJ, Van Der Walt SJ, Gommers R, Virtanen P, Cournapeau D, Wieser E, Taylor J, Berg S, Smith N (2020) Array programming with NumPy. Nature 585(7825):357–362

  16. Hu EJ, Shen Y, Wallis P, Allen-Zhu Z, Li Y, Wang S, Wang L, Chen W (2021) Lora: low-rank adaptation of large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:09685

Ranking Protein–Protein Models with Large Language Models and Graph Neural Networks

Xiaotong Xu and Alexandre M. J. J. Bonvin

Abstract

Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) are associated with various diseases, including cancer, infections, and neurodegenerative disorders. Obtaining three-dimensional structural information on these PPIs serves as a foundation to interfere with those or to guide drug design. Various strategies can be followed to model those complexes, all typically resulting in a large number of models. A challenging step in this process is the identification of good models (near-native PPI conformations) from the large pool of generated models. To address this challenge, we previously developed DeepRank-GNN-esm, a graph-based deep learning algorithm for ranking modeled PPI structures harnessing the power of protein language models. In this chapter, we detail the use of our software with examples. DeepRank-GNN-esm is freely available at https://github. com/haddocking/DeepRank-GNN-esm.

Key words Protein–protein scoring, Graph neural network, Protein language model, DeepRank-GNN-esm

1 Introduction

Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental for driving various biological processes within cells, such as cell growth, metabolism, and regulatory pathways. Obtaining three-dimensional (3D) structural insights into these PPIs is vital for understanding both normal physiological processes and their disruption in case of disease, guiding efforts toward the development of drugs to restore a normal functioning. Next to experimental structural methods such as cryo-electron microscopy and X-ray crystallography, various computational techniques have emerged as alternatives, ranging from integrative modeling [1–3] to artificial intelligence (AI)- based methods [4–6].

Despite recent progress in predicting the structure of biomolecular complexes, accurately identifying near-native conformations (good models) from the large pool of generated models, which is

known as the “scoring problem,” remains challenging as demonstrated, for example, in the critical assessment of predicted interactions (CAPRI) [7, 8]. To address this issue, we have previously developed DeepRank-GNN [9], a deep learning architecture that enables analysis of PPI interfaces using graph neural networks. The workflow of DeepRank-GNN is as follows: Given an input PPI 3D model, it converts the interaction interface into a residue graph where nodes represent protein residues at the interface and edges denote interactions between them. All graphs are stored and handled using the HDF5 format [10] to ensure smooth I/O operations and efficient training. The generated graph is then processed through a pretrained graph neural network and the model quality is predicted in terms of \(f _ { n a t } ,\) a metric between 0 and 1 reporting the fraction of native intermolecular contacts with respects to the ground truth (which would be the experimental structure of the complex if available). The network has been trained on large datasets alongside their corresponding \(f _ { n a t }\) values, learning to rationalize between 3D structure, sequences, and interface quality. In the original DeepRank-GNN approach, five node features are used to capture various aspects of protein–protein interaction interfaces. These include residue type, polarity, buried surface area, and charge, which aims to characterize the physical properties of the interface. Additionally, we integrate evolutionary information through position-specific scoring matrix (PSSM) profiles. PSSM features have been shown to play a crucial role in improving the predictive performance of DeepRank-GNN [9]. They are, however, quite computational demanding to generate in terms of time and resources, requiring typically large sequence alignments to extract the evolutionary information.

The field of natural language processing has witnessed remarkable advancements, largely driven by the escalating scale of large language models (LLMs) [11]. This progression has extended to the realm of protein science with the development of sophisticated protein language models such as ProteinBERT [12], ProGen [13], evolutionary scale modeling-2/3 (ESM-2/EMS-3) [6, 14]. ESM-2 has been trained to predict the identity of randomly masked amino acids in a protein sequence. By solving billions of sequence puzzles, ESM-2 effectively captured evolutionary insights spanning across sequences. Consequently, sequence embeddings extracted from ESM-2 models could provide valuable features for down-stream PPI-related tasks such as protein structure prediction [15], functional annotation [16], or generating novel sequences for protein families [17]. Recognizing the great potential of such large language models, we have replaced the PSSM features with embeddings from the ESM-2 protein language model, resulting in a new version of our software called DeepRank-GNN-esm [18]. This improvement greatly reduced the computation time at no cost in-, or even better performance than the original PSSM-based

model on two applications: (1) Discriminating physiological from non-physiological interfaces and (2) Ranking docked PPI models.

In this chapter, we provide comprehensive instructions for the use of DeepRank-GNN-esm, focusing on its application for scoring PPI conformations. We introduce two execution modes for the algorithm with clear guidance on their implementations. Additionally, we dive into the process of customizing the deep learning architecture to meet individual requirements, ensuring flexibility and adaptability to diverse research needs.

2 Materials

The following resources are required to use our deep learning tools.

2.1 Software

  1. ESMFold [6] software package (available at https://github. com/facebookresearch/esm): Code and pretrained weights for protein language model embedding calculation.
  2. DeepRank-GNN-esm [18] software package (https://github. com/haddocking/DeepRank-GNN-esm): Code and pretrained model weights for \(f _ { n a t }\) computation.
  3. Python package PDB-tools (version 2.5.0 https://github. com/haddocking/pdb-tools): A set of useful tools for pre-processing input coordinate files from the Protein Data Bank (PDB) [19].
  4. Conda (https://conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index. html): An open-source software environment manager.
  5. Jupyter Notebook (https://jupyter.org/) (optional): A web-based interactive computing platform.

2.2 Hardware

We have tested the installation on CentOS 7 (with CUDA 11.6 and GeForce GTX 1080 Ti card), RockyLinux (with CUDA 12.5 and GeForce GTX 1080 Ti card), Ubuntu 20.04 LTS (with CUDA 12.4 and A100 GPU card), and openSUSE tumbleweed (with CUDA 12.3 and A4500 GPU card). A Linux computer with multiple CPUs and/or GPU is preferable.

2.3 Data

Structural models of protein–protein complexes in PDB format.

3 Methods

In this section, we outline the process for evaluating the quality of PPI conformations using the DeepRank-GNN-esm algorithm. Additionally, we offer instructions on how our deep learning architecture can be retrained and/or re-designed for new applications.

To rank PPI conformations with DeepRank-GNN-esm, the following steps should be followed:

  1. Install the required software packages.
  2. Prepare and preprocess the input data (the models to be scored).
  3. \(\mathrm { R u n } f _ { n a t }\) \(f _ { n a t }\) prediction using the command line interface tool from a terminal or the DeepRank-GNN-esm package at python level.
  4. Analyze results.

In the following, all those steps are described in details and illustrated with two examples using different settings, highlighting the versatility and efficacy of each approach.

3.1 Installation of the Required Software Packages

DeepRank-GNN-esm can be used either as a command-line tool or a python package. Installation instructions for both settings are provided below.

  1. Download and install Anaconda (https://docs.anaconda.com/ free/anaconda/install/index.html).
  2. Open a Linux terminal and clone the DeepRank-GNN-esm Github repository
> git clone https://github.com/haddocking/DeepRank-GNN-esm  
> cd DeepRank-GNN-esm
  1. Install the CPU or GPU version of DeepRank-GNN-esm depending on the hardware available in your system via conda: CPU version: Create and activate the conda environment.
> conda env create -f environment-cpu.yml  
> conda activate deeprank-gnn-esm-cpu

GPU version: Create and activate the conda environment.

> conda env create -f environment-gpu.yml  
> conda activate deepprank-gnn-esm-gpu
  1. Install the command line tool
> pip install.
  1. In the same installation directory, clone the ESMFold github repository (see Note 1)
> git clone https://github.com/facebookresearch/esm.git

6. Install pdb-tools

pip install pdb-tools

7. Install Jupyer Notebook (Optional)

pip install notebook

Note that once you have installed all software, before using it in a new terminal session, you will have to activate the conda environment again with:

conda activate deeprank-gnn-esm-gpu.

3.2 Preprocessing of the Input Data

Deeprank-GNN-esm is primarily tailored for interactions involving two chains. To run computation for complexes with more than two chains, refer to Note 2. Prior to utilizing the tool, renumbering the residue indexes for every chain in the PDB file is required (see Note 2). For this purpose, we recommend to use the provided script “pdb_renumber.py”: For example, for one input PDB file (e.g., input_protein.pdb), the script will renumber all the chains in the protein starting from residue “1” and generate a new PDB file in the user-defined output directory (note that if the output directory already exists it will be overwritten). To run it type:

\(>\) python scripts/pdb_renumber.py input_protein.pdb 1 output_- dir

3.3 Run DeepRank-GNN-Esm Computation

3.3.1 Run Computation with Command Line Interface Tool

We use one example input to demonstrate the usage of the tool.

  1. First create a separate ‘tutorial’ directory where we will run this example:

mkdir tutorial
\(>\) cd tutorial

  1. Download an example PDB file from the PDB (see Note 3)

pdb_fetch 1B6C > 1B6C.pdb
3. Prepare the input structure. This python command will overwrite the original PDB file.

python ../scripts/pdb_renumber.py 1B6C.pdb 1.

4. Run \(f _ { n a t }\) computation:

To assess the quality of the protein–protein interface between chain “A” and chain “B,” run the following command (see Note 4). This command requires four inputs from the user: the PDB file of interest (“1B6C.pdb”), the identifiers of the two chains you wish to assess (chain_id1 “A” and chain_id2 “B”), and the number of cores you want to allocate for the prediction. In this case, we use \(^ { \mathfrak { c } } 1 ^ { \mathfrak { s } }\) core. If you wish to score PDB ensembles, the tool can be used in exactly the same way; simply replace the single PDB input with the ensemble input file.

deeprank-gnn-esm-predict 1B6C.pdb A B 1

For a description of the resulting output refer to Subheading 3.4.

When ranking a small number of PDB structures \(( < 1 0 0 )\) , we recommend using the command–line interface tool. This tool consolidates all functionalities into one user-friendly command. However, the process may be relatively slow because it handles only one input PDB at a time and running the predictions directly from Python (see Subheading 3.3.2) might be more efficient.

3.3.2

Computations in Python Using the Deep Learning Architecture

DeepRank-GNN-esm can also be used as a python deep learning architecture. We will use as an example (PDBID:1ATN) to demonstrate the process. By running parallel predictions harvesting multiple CPUs for data loading, we can accelerate the process compared to the command-line tool version.

Below are the steps for using the Deeprank-GNN-esm architecture for making \(f _ { n a t }\) predictions with example PDB files (see Note 5, for naming requirement for the PDB files) as input. For the convenience for the reader, we also provide a Jupyter notebook version of all the steps (see Note 6).

  1. From within the directory where you installed the software, create a new directory for the tutorial and copy all example PDB files to the new directory:
> cd ../  
> mkdir tutorial.python  
> cp -r example/data/pdb/1ATN tutorial.python  
> cd tutorial.python
  1. Prepare all input PDBs in the folder: Inside the “tutorial_python” directory, in bash shell, run:
> for mdb_file in 1ATN/\*.pdb; do python ../scripts/pdb_re-number.py "$pdb_file" 1 1ATN/; done
  1. All input PDBs have been renumbered and organized in the folder “1ATN”. To compute ESM-2 embeddings for them, we begin by extracting sequences from the PDBs (see Note 7). Open a terminal, activate the deeprank-gnn-esm conda environment (see above) and then type:

python ../scripts/get_fasta.py 1ATN/ A B

This command generates a fasta file (“1ATN.fasta”) from chain “A” and chain “B” for this complex. For other chain IDs, replace “A” and “B” with specific chain identifiers. To calculate the ESM-2 embeddings from the input fasta file, as described in the supplementary material in our original publication [18], we recommend using the script provided by ESM-Fold [6] (see Note 8 ):

> mkdir embedding  
> python ../esm/scripts/extract.py esm2_t33_650M_UR50D 1ATN.  
fasta embedding --repr_layers 33 --include mean per_token

The command generates ESM-2 embeddings for all sequences in “1ATN.fasta” with pretrained ESM-2 model “esm2_t33_650- M_UR50D” and stores them in the “embedding” directory.

  1. To convert the input PDBs into interface graphs that can be processed and handled by the network, in the current directory, create a python file named ‘prepare_HDF5.py’, copy the following python code snippet into the file:
from deeprank_gnn.GraphGenMP import GraphHDF5  
pdb_path = "1ATN"  
embedding_path = "embedding"  
nproc = 20  
outfile = "1ATN=residue.hdf5"  
GraphHDF5(pdb_path = pdb_path, embedding_path = embedding_path, graph_type = "residue",  
outfile = outfile,  
nproc = nproc,  
tmpdir="/tmpdir")

Run the python file in the “tutorial_python” directory with:

python ./prepare_HDF5.py

This code will generate the input interface graphs with ESM-2 embeddings as one of their node features for all PDB files in the directory and compact them into the output HDF5 file format using 20 CPU cores.

  1. To use pre-trained model to rank the input conformations in HDF5 format, create a new python file named “predict.py’ in the current directory and add the following code to the file:

from deeprank_gnn.ginet import GINet
from deeprank_gnn.NeuralNet import NeuralNet as NN
database_test \(=\) “1ATN_residue.hdf5”
gnn \(=\) GINet
target \(=\) “fnat”
edge_feature \(= [{}^{\prime \prime}\) dist”]
node_features \(=\) [“type”, “polarity”, “bsa”, “charge”, “embed- ding”]
threshold \(= 0.3\) pretrained_model \(= "\) ./paper_pretrained_models/scoring_of_
docking_models/gnn_ism/treg_yfnat_b64_e20_lr0.001_foldal- l_ism.pth.tar”
device_name \(=\) “cuda:0”
num_workers \(= 10\) model \(=\) NN(
database_test,
gnn,
device_name \(=\) device_name,
edge_feature \(=\) edge_feature,
node_feature \(=\) node_features,
target \(=\) target,
num_workers \(=\) num_workers,
pretrained_model \(\equiv\) pretrained_model,
threshold \(=\) threshold)
model.test(hdf5 \(=\) “GNN_ism_prediction.hdf5”)

Run the python file in the “tutorial_python” directory with:

> python ./predict.py

As outlined in the original publication, the available node features include “type,” “polarity,” “charge,” “bsa,” and “embedding.” It is important to note that since the model is trained with all these features, we highly recommend using all of them during inference time for optimal performance.

3.4 Analyzing the Results

3.4.1 Analyzing the Output from the Command Line Interface Tool

The command line tool generates a result folder within the current working directory. The structure of this folder is as follows:

1B6C-gnn_esign_pred_A_B  
1B6C.pdb  
all.fasta  
1B6C.A.pt 
1B6C.B.pt graph.hdf5 GNN_ese_m_prediction.hdf5 GNN_ese_m_prediction.csv

The output folder “1B6C-gnn_esm_pred_A_B” contains the pre-processed input protein (“1B6C.pdb”), the extracted protein sequences (“all.fasta”), the calculated ESM-2 embeddings for protein chain \(^ { \mathfrak { s } } \mathrm { A } ^ { \mathfrak { s } }\) and chain “B” (“1B6C.A.pt”, “1B6C.B.pt”), the generated interface graph (“graph.hdf5”) and the prediction output files (“GNN_esm_prediction.hdf5” and “GNN_esm_prediction.csv”) with predicted \(f _ { n a t }\) values for the interface. In our paper [18], we use a \(f _ { n a t }\) cutoff of 0.3 to differentiate between good and bad models, with a predicted \(\ " { f _ { n a t } } \ "\) value higher than 0.3 indicating a good interface conformation and vice versa.

To access the predicted output, simply open the “GNN_esm_- prediction.csv” with a txt editor. To read node features and edge features of the generated interface graph (“graph.hdf5”), use to the following python code:

import h5py  
f = h5py.File("1B6C-gnn-esm_pred_A_B/graph.hdf5")  
#view all node features  
node_features = f["1B6C"]["node_data"].keys()  
print(node_features)  
#view node feature embeddings  
embeddings = f["1B6C"},{"node_data"},{"embedding"},{"dis"]}  
print(embeddings)  
#view edge feature distance  
distance = f["1B6C"},{"edge_data"},{"dis"]}  
print(distance)

3.4.2 Analyzing the Output from the Python Deeprank-GNN-Esm Network Architecture

The provided code snippet above generates an output HDF5 file named “GNN_esm_prediction.hdf5”. Similar to the outputs from the command line tool, you can access predicted \(f _ { n a t }\) through python code:

import h5py
f = h5py.File(“GNN_ism_prediction.hdf5”,“r+”)
mol_names = f[“epoch_0000”][“test”][“mol”][“（）]
fnats \(=\) f[“epoch_0000”][“test”][“outputs”},{“）]
for mol_name，fnat in zip(mol_names，fnats):
print(mol_name.decode()，fnat)

3.5 Customizing the Deep Learning Network

DeepRank-GNN-esm is designed to facilitate easy adaptation for your own applications. To re-train it for customized tasks, follow these steps:

  1. Construct and format training and evaluation data sets.
  2. Define the training tasks. Two training modes are supported: regression (“reg”) or classification (“class”).
  3. Define the learning parameters: Determine “batch_size,” learning rate “lr” based on your system setup and hardware capacity (see Note 9).
  4. Load the network.
  5. Retrain the network

We use the following code example to demonstrate the process:

from deeprank_gnn.NeuralNet import NeuralNet
from deeprank_gnn.ginet import GINet
database_train \(=\) ‘train.hdf5’
database_eval \(=\) ‘eval.hdf5’
task \(\equiv\) reg
batch_size=64
lr \(= 0.001\) node_feature \(=\) [‘type’, ‘polarity’, ‘bsa’, ‘charge’, ‘embed- ding’]
edge_feature \(=\) [‘dist’]
model \(=\) NeuralNet(databaseentence,
GINet,
node_feature \(\equiv\) node_feature,
edge_feature \(\equiv\) edge_feature,
target \(\equiv\) target,
task \(\equiv\) task,
lr \(\equiv\) lr,
batch_size \(\equiv\) batch_size,
database_eval \(=\) database_eval)
model.train(nepoch \(\coloneqq\) 50,
validate \(\equiv\) True,
save_model \(\equiv\) ‘best’,
hdf5 \(\equiv\) ‘output.hdf5’)

Graphs, neural network layers and training target could also be customized. For advanced users, we refer you to the example here https://deeprank-gnn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial. advanced.html.

4 Notes

  1. The environment required for ESMFold is integrated into the conda environment for DeepRank-GNN-esm. Users only need to clone the repository to access the helper scripts necessary for embeddings calculation.

  2. To ensure correct matching between the calculated ESM-2 embeddings (shaped [AA_len, 1280]) and their corresponding residue nodes in the graph, we employ residue renumbering prior to computations. This approach will ensure the residue numbering in all chains is continuous and starts from residue “1.” As a result, the ith element in the embeddings corresponds to the ith residue in the sequence. It is worth noting that gaps in the structure do not affect this process, as long as the sequence order in the PDB file matches the sequence provided to ESM-2.

  3. To use our scoring function with files in the default CIF format from the Protein Data Bank, you need to first convert them to the PDB format. We recommend using the “pdb_fromcif” command from PDB tools for this conversion.

  4. Deeprank-GNN-esm is primarily tailored for interactions involving two chains. While our method may not directly evaluate the overall quality of complexes with more than two chains, it can still be effectively applied to assess interactions between specific pairs of chains within such complexes. In such cases, providing the two relevant chain IDs will enable our method to function probably.

  5. All input PDB file should be named according to the following format: PDBID (four-letter code) followed by any combination of characters of any length and ending with the “.pdb” extension.

  6. A Jupyter notebook version of the tutorial (“tutorial.ipynb”) is distributed with the package. To run the tutorial, first install the conda environment and Jupyter Notebook. Then initiate Jupyter Notebook with command “jupyter notebook” and run the tutorial inside the DeepRank-GNN-esm installation folder.

  7. We assume that all input PDB conformations share the same fasta sequence, which is typically the case in scoring scenarios in which the models are derived from one specific docking software. All modeled conformations for one complex should be stored in one directory. In such scenarios, it suffices to calculate the fasta sequence for only one PDB. However, if the input PDB conformations possess different sequences, it is necessary to generate fasta sequences for each of them.

  8. Refer to the ESM-Fold repository or the Supplementary Material of our original DeepRank-GNN-esm publication for detailed explanations of each input parameter.

  9. As an example of learning parameters, for a GPU GeForce GTX 1080 Ti card (11G memory), we use “batch _size” \(\scriptstyle \mathbf { \bar { \Gamma } } = 6 4\) and \(\cdot \mathrm { < l r } ^ { \mathfrak { N } } { = } 0 . 0 0 1\) .

Acknowledgments

This work was supported by the European Union Horizon 2020, project BioExcel [823830]. X. Xu acknowledges financial support from the China Scholarship Council, Grant 202208310024.

References

  1. Koukos PI, Bonvin AMJJ (2020) Integrative modelling of biomolecular complexes. J Mol Biol 432:2861–2881

  2. van Noort CW, Honorato RV, Bonvin AMJJ (2021) Information-driven modeling of biomolecular complexes. Curr Opin Struct Biol 70:70–77

  3. Braberg H, Echeverria I, Kaake RM, Sali A, Krogan NJ (2022) From systems to structure — using genetic data to model protein structures. Nat Rev Genet 23:342–354

  4. Translating a GO Term List to Human Readable Function Description Using GO2Sum

Swagarika J. Giri, Udayan Pandey, Joon Hong Park, and Daisuke Kihara

Abstract

Understanding the functions of proteins is one of the most important challenges in modern biology. Typically, protein function prediction methods generate a list of gene ontology (GO) terms, sometimes consisting of 50–100 functional terms. While GO serves the purpose of standardizing terms, interpreting a long list of GO terms is difficult for biologists. To address this challenge, we developed Gene Ontology terms Summarizer (GO2Sum), a language model–based summarizer that takes a list of GO terms as input and converts them into a concise, free-text summary describing the protein’s function, subunit structure, and pathway information. GO2Sum was fine-tuned on GO term assignments and free-text function descriptions from UniProt entries. We built a Web server of \(\mathrm { G O } 2 \mathrm { S u m }\) , which offers an easy use of GO2Sum for biology users.

Key words GO2Sum, Protein function, Gene ontology, Summarization, Large language model, LLM, Function prediction

1 Introduction

Elucidating the biological function of proteins is a central challenge in modern biology. While protein functions are ultimately determined through wet lab experiments, computational methods play a crucial role in hypothesizing potential functions beforehand [1]. Usually, protein function prediction outputs possible functions that a protein may perform in terms of a list of gene ontology (GO) terms. The gene ontology (GO) [2] is a standardized vocabulary, which facilitate description of protein function by computer programs. GO categorizes protein functions into three sub-ontologies: biological process (BP), referring to biological programs accomplished by multiple molecular activities; cellular component (CC), specifying the location in the cell where the gene product is active; and molecular function (MF), focusing on

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_5, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

the biochemical activity of the protein. Despite its convenience for computational analysis, understanding a list of GO terms is challenging for researchers.

This chapter explains Gene Ontology terms Summarizer (GO2Sum) [3], a novel tool that converts a list of GO terms into human-readable function descriptions in free text. GO2Sum uses a large language model (LLM), which has been extensively studied in the natural language processing (NLP) area in recent years. GO2Sum uses text summarization technique [3] with LLM. GO2Sum processes GO term lists, which are often generated by protein function prediction methods [4–6] or obtained from databases like UniProt [7] and InterPro [8]. It then converts these lists into three functional descriptions, Function, Subunit Structure, and Pathway, which are presented in UniProt entries. The Function description provides the overall role of the protein, Subunit Structure details molecular interactions with other proteins, and a Pathway paragraph explains the metabolic pathways associated with the protein. In this chapter, we first explain the workflow of GO2Sum and then discuss evaluation results for GO2Sum with examples of paragraphs generated by GO2Sum. Finally, we will introduce the GO2Sum Web server, which provides an easy use of GO2Sum to researchers.

2 Workflow of GO2Sum

The GO2Sum workflow is summarized in Fig. 1. GO2Sum takes a list of GO terms as input. These GO terms are annotations of the protein, generated either from protein function databases like Uni-Prot [7] and InterPro [8], or through experimental methods or computational protein function prediction tools [1, 4–6, 9– 13]. Each GO term is associated with a text description in the Gene Ontology Consortium (GOC) [2] database. For instance, GO: 0034498 is associated with the description “The directed movement of substances from early endosomes to the Golgi.” These descriptions are concatenated into a single document, which serves as input for the summarization module. In GO2Sum, text-to-text transfer transformer (T5) model [14] was used to summarize functional descriptions from a list of GO terms. T5 uses an encoder–decoder deep learning architecture, where the encoder is assigned to extract a rich latent representation of the text, irrespective of the task, and the decoder aims at generating task-specific output based on the encoder embeddings. T5 was originally trained on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4) [15], which combines data from Wikipedia and Web scraping and achieved state-of-the-art results across various text-to-text tasks, including translation, summarization, question answering, and classification.


Fig. 1 The GO2Sum workflow. UniProt entry A1A4S6, “Rho GTPase-activating The GO2Sum workflow. Uni

In \(\mathrm { G O } 2 \mathrm { S u m }\) , we downloaded the entire SwissProt portion from UniProt (Release: February 11, 2022). After applying filtration to remove redundancy, the dataset was reduced to 109,658 proteins from an initial 542,953. The original paper of GO2Sum provides a detailed discussion on preprocessing. For each protein, we gathered three different paragraphs, Function, Subunit Structure, and Pathway, from the UniProt database. As an entry often does not have all three functional descriptions of Function, Subunit Structure, and Pathway, we constructed three separate datasets for

each. The dataset for Function, Subunit Structure, and Pathway had 97,600, 62,340, and 14,600 proteins, respectively. We finetuned three separate T5 models on GO assignments, each designed to produce paragraphs for the Function, Subunit Structure, and Pathway sections in UniProt, respectively.

3 Evaluation of GO2Sum

In this section, we briefly summarize the benchmark study we did to evaluate the performance of GO2Sum. More results are available in the original paper of GO2Sum [3]. We evaluated paragraphs generated by GO2Sum in comparison with the ground-truth paragraphs in UniProt using scoring functions used in text comparison in NLP. Scores used are three embedding-based metrics, BERT [16], MiniLM [17], and BioSentVec [18], and additional three sentence-mover-based metrics, SMS, WMS, and S + WMS [19]. The embedding-based metrics represent paragraphs as embeddings, numerical vectors, from the deep learning models for NLP. These vectors capture the semantic meaning of the text by encoding it into a high dimensional space, where similar texts locate close to each other. We used the Cosine similarity between an embedding of the ground-truth UniProt description and that of the description generated by GO2Sum to quantify the agreement between them. In sentence-mover-based metrics, sentences are treated as collections of words. We represented words and sentences of both the ground truth and the descriptions generated by GO2Sum in the embedding space. Next, we solve a linear optimization problem to minimize the cost of transforming the ground truth into the descriptions generated by \(\mathrm { G O } 2 \mathrm { S u m }\) , and the cost is considered as the distance between the two descriptions.

We compared the paragraphs generated by GO2Sum with those produced by the vanilla (original) T5 model and SciFive [20], which is specifically pretrained LLM on extensive biomedical literature. In Fig. 2a–c, we compared the evaluation scores of GO2Sum with SciFive using the average of the three embeddingbased scores, BERT, MiniLM, and BioSentVec. As shown, GO2Sum demonstrated superior performance, achieving higher scores than SciFive in \(9 7 . 6 0 \%\) of cases for Function, \(9 8 . 8 9 \%\) for Subunit Structure, and \(9 9 . 7 9 \%\) for Pathway descriptions, respectively (Fig. 2a–c). In Fig. 2d, we used the three mover-distancebased similarity scores to compare GO2Sum and the vanilla T5. The results show that the summary by GO2Sum was more similar to the ground truth in UniProt than vanilla T5 for almost all UniProt entries, \(9 5 . 3 \%\) , \(9 7 . 3 \%\) , and \(9 7 . 0 \%\) , of the cases evaluated by WMS, SMS, and \(S + \mathrm { W M S }\) , respectively.


Fig. 2 Evaluation results for G02Sum. (ac) Comparison of G02Sum and SciFive using average scores of three Evaluation results for GO2Sum. ( ) Comparison of GO2Sum and SciFive using average scores of three a b embedding scores, BERT, MiniLM, and BioSentVec. ( ) Benchmarked on the UniProt Function description. ( ) c d Benchmarked on UniProt Subunit Structure description. ( ) Benchmarked on UniProt Pathway description. ( ) Comparison between GO2Sum and vanilla (original) T5 using three Sentence Mover’s similarity-based e metrics. ( ) The distribution of the confidence score for entries of Function. Entries were classified into – f three classes based on average embedding score: high, [0.8 1.0]; moderate, [0.5,0,8); and low, [0,0.5). ( ) The correlation between evaluation by human biology exp

Figure 2e shows the confidence score of generated description by GO2Sum. GO2Sum employs beam search, which generates many different paragraphs with a probability value assigned. We used this probability value as a confidence score, which can indicate the quality of the generated paragraph. In Fig. 2e, the distribution of confidence scores for generated paragraphs for UniProt Function section is shown. Generated paragraphs were categorized into three accuracy classes based on the average embedding scores: high (0.8 to 1.0), moderate (0.5 to 0.8), and low (below 0.5). The plot shows that confidence scores for highly accurate summaries tend to be high: \(8 2 . 6 \%\) of paragraphs in the high-scoring class have confidence scores above 0.9, while this proportion drops to \(4 5 . 0 \%\) fo r the moderate class and \(3 6 . 4 \%\) for the low-scoring class, indicating that the confidence score corresponds well to their accuracy.

In Fig. 2f, we evaluated the alignment between our average embedding score we used for evaluation with assessments by human experts to confirm that the automatic evaluation we performed is reasonable. We sampled 100 paragraphs generated by GO2Sum and asked six human evaluators to give a score from 1 (the worst) to 4 (the best) by comparing them with the ground

truth paragraphs in UniProt. The y-axis shows the average score from the six evaluators. The correlation between the human evaluators’ score and the average embedding score we used 0.804, indicating that overall the average embedding score agrees well with human evaluation. Particularly, high-scoring summaries generated by GO2Sum are deemed highly reliable, as confirmed by the human evaluators.

4 GO Summary for Function

In this section, we show examples of descriptions generated by GO2Sum for UniProt Function paragraph. We used UniProt entries taken from its latest release (April 2024). GO terms are taken from the top page of entries (but not from a complete list of GO terms on QuickGO that is linked from each entry page). In Table 1, the first protein, Q9LNA5 (ACT domain-containing protein ACR8), is associated with two GO term

Table 1 Examples of Function description generated by GO2Sum

Protein GO IDS GO2Sum generated summary UniProt description
Q9LNA5 GO:0016597;GO:0009737 May bind amino acids May bind amino acids
Q9ZUL7 GO:0005576;GO:0005739; GO:0006952; GO:0009506; GO:0031640; GO:0050832 Confers broad-spectrum resistance to pathogens Confers broad-spectrum resistance to pathogens
Q51472 GO:0000976;GO:0001216; GO:0003677; GO:0005829; GO:0006310; GO:0006417; GO:0030527; GO:0032993; GO:0045893 This protein is one of the two subunits of integration host factor, a specific DNA-binding protein that functions in genetic recombination as well as in transcriptional and translational control This protein is one of the two subunits of integration host factor, a specific DNA-binding protein that functions in genetic recombination as well as in transcriptional and translational control
Q41062 GO:0005524;GO:0005829; GO:0006605; GO:0006886; GO:0008320; GO:0009535; GO:0009570; GO:0009941; GO:0016464; GO:0017038; GO:0071806 Has a central role in coupling the hydrolysis of ATP to the transfer of proteins across the thylakoid Has a central role in coupling the hydrolysis of ATP to the transfer of proteins across the thylakoid membrane. Facilitates the transport of precursor proteins from the chloroplast stroma to thylakoid lumen

annotations, GO:0016597 and GO:0009737, in the UniProt entry. The term GO:0016597 describes its function as “binding to an amino acid or organic acids containing one or more amino substituents” and the second one, GO:0009737 is for “response to abscisic acid.” UniProt has a brief function description for this entry, “may bind amino acids,” which perfectly agrees with the output from GO2Sum.

The second protein, Q9ZUL7 (Defensin-like protein 1), is associated with GO terms, including GO:0006952 (defense response), GO:0050832 (defense response to fungus), and GO:0031640 (killing of cells of another organism), which are linked to pathogen resistance. The remaining three GO terms (GO:0005576, GO:0005739, GO:0005739) are related to the cellular location of the protein in the extracellular region, mitochondria, and plasmodesma, respectively. Again, for this example, GO2Sum outputs the identical function description as UniProt Function, “Confers broad-spectrum resistance to pathogens.”

The third example, Q51472 (integration host factor subunit alpha) is annotated with nine different terms, including GO:0032993 (protein–DNA complex), GO:0003677 (DNA binding), GO:0001216 (DNA-binding transcription activator activity), GO:0045893 (positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription), GO:0000976 (transcription cis-regulatory region binding), GO:0006310 (DNA recombination), GO:0006417 (regulation of translation), GO:0005829 (cytosol), and GO:0030527 (structural constituent of chromatin). Function summarizer correctly choose the seven GO terms related to Function (GO:0032993; GO:0003677; GO:0001216; GO:0045893; G-O:0000976; GO:0006417; and GO:0006310) and was able to produce identical annotations as UniProt.

The fourth example, Q41062 (protein translocase subunit SecA, chloroplastic), is annotated with 11 different terms: GO:0005524 (ATP binding), GO:0005829 (cytosol), GO:0006605 (protein targeting), GO:0006886 (intracellular protein transport), GO:0008320 (protein transmembrane transporter activity), GO:0009535 (chloroplast thylakoid membrane), GO:0009570 (chloroplast stroma), GO:0009941 (chloroplast stroma), GO:0016464 (chloroplast proteintransporting ATPase activity), GO:0017038 (protein import), and GO:0071806 (protein transmembrane transport). The function description of this protein has two sentences: coupling ATP hydrolysis to the transfer of proteins across the thylakoid membrane and facilitating the transport of precursor proteins from the chloroplast stroma to the thylakoid lumen. GO2Sum successfully reproduced the first sentence, but did not use the GO term of chloroplast storma and reproduced the second sentence.

5 GO Summary for Subunit Structure

We showed examples of Subunit Structure paragraphs generated by GO2Sum in Table 2. Compared to Function description, Subunit Structure descriptions in UniProt are usually short, typically consisting of a few words. The first example, P41912 (Ras modification protein ERF4), functions as part of the ERF2–SHR5 complex and is a palmitoyltransferase specific for Ras proteins. GO2Sum correctly generated its Subunit Structure as “Interacts with ERF2,” reproducing the description in UniProt. All the four GO terms GO:0006612 (protein targeting to membrane), GO:0005789 (endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane), GO:0031211 (ER palmitoyltransferase complex), and GO:0005783 (ER) contributed to this accurate output by GO2Sum. While these GO terms do not contain the name ERF2 as its interacting partner, they provide functional and locational clues. GO:0031211 (ER palmitoyltransferase complex) GO term seems to be a key to produce the correct output, as ERF2 is known to be a subunit of this complex.

The next example is Q752A3, topoisomerase 1–associated factor 1 (TOF1). It plays a crucial role in forming a fork protection complex (FPC) with CSM3. This complex is essential for chromosome segregation during meiosis and for the repair of DNA

Table 2 Examples of Subunit Structure description generated by GO2Sum

Protein GO IDS GO2Sum generated summary UniProt description
P41912 GO:0005783;GO:0005789; GO:0006612;GO:0031211 Interacts with ERF2 Interacts with ERF2
Q752A3 GO:0000076;GO:0003677; GO:0005829;GO:0006281; GO:0007064;GO:0031298; GO:0043111;GO:0043570; GO:0051321 Component of the fork protection complex (FPC) consisting of TOF1 and CSM3 Component of the fork protection complex (FPC) consisting of TOF1 and CSM3
P63031 GO:0005743;GO:0006086; GO:0006850;GO:0050833; GO:1990830 The functional 150 kDa pyruvate import complex is a heteromer of MPC1 and MPC2 Homodimer. Forms heterodimer with MPC2. The heterodimer is the more stable and dominant form
Q8BM54 GO:0004842; GO:0005737; GO:0005856; GO:0005886; GO:0006511; GO:0007399; GO:0008092; GO:0010977; GO:0010989; GO:0016567; GO:0031648; GO:0032803; GO:0042632; GO:0045732; GO:0046872; GO:0061630; GO:0071404 Homodimer. Interacts with the E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme, UBE2D1 Homodimer. Interacts with the E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme, UBE2D1 (via RING-type zinc finger). Interacts with myosin regulatory light chain (MRLC) and TMEM4

damage. This protein is annotated with nine GO terms, and among them two GO terms, GO:0031298 (replication fork protection complex) and GO:0043111 (replication fork arrest) are related to Subunit Structure. GO2Sum generated a summary, which did not include the abbreviation “(FPC)” in UniProt but besides this, it has a perfect match.

The next protein, P63031, mitochondrial pyruvate carrier 1 (MPC1), is responsible for transporting pyruvate into the mitochondria. This protein is annotated with five GO terms, two of which, GO:0006850 (mitochondrial pyruvate transmembrane transport) and GO:0050833 (pyruvate transmembrane transporter activity), are closely related to the subunit structure predicted by GO2Sum. Both GO2Sum and UniProt agree on the formation of the heterodimer (MPC1 and MPC2), but UniProt provides additional detail about the homodimeric potential of MPC1, as well as a clearer statement on the stability and dominance of the heterodimer over the homodimer. The size of the complex, \(1 5 0 \mathrm { k D a }\) is not information included in these five GO terms, but it probably came from the same protein from a different organism (P53157), which has a similar set of GO terms in its annotation and has the identical the subunit description as this entry.

The fourth protein, Q8BM54 (E3 ubiquitin–protein ligase MYLIP), has 17 GO term annotations, four of which are related to ubiquitin. These include GO:0016567 (protein ubiquitination), GO:0061630 (ubiquitin–protein ligase activity), GO:0004842 (ubiquitin–protein transferase activity), and GO:0016567 (protein ubiquitination). These annotations align with the Subunit Structure paragraph generated by GO2Sum and highlight the protein’s interaction with the E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme, UBE2D1. In this case, GO2Sum was successful in capturing a part of interaction information, missing the specific interaction between the myosin regulatory light chain (MRLC) and TMEM4. Interaction with myosin could be inferred from other GO terms pointing the localization at cytoskeleton. But according to UniProt, this Subunit Structure description is annotated by similarity, thus not all the information are embedded in GO term annotations.

6 GO Summary for Pathway

Table 3 lists three examples for pathway paragraphs from GO2Sum. Protein Q8LPK5 (cellulose synthase A catalytic subunit 8) which is essential for glycan metabolism and cellulose biosynthesis. The GO2Sum output “glycan metabolism; plant cellulose biosynthesis,” seems determined mainly from two terms GO:0016760 (cellulose synthase (UDP-forming) activity) and GO:0030244 (cellulose biosynthetic process). The rest of GO terms are primarily related to the protein’s role in stress response and structural integrity.

Table 3 Examples of Pathway description generated by GO2Sum

Protein GO IDS G02Sum generated summary UniProt description
Q8LPK5 GO:0005886;GO:0006970; GO:0009414;GO:0009834; GO:0016760;GO:0030244; GO:0042742;GO:0046872; GO:0050832;GO:0071555 Glycan metabolism; plant cellulose biosynthesis Glycan metabolism; plant cellulose biosynthesis
P21177 GO:0003857;GO:0004165; GO:0004300;GO:0006635; GO:0008692;GO:0016509; GO:0036125;GO:0070403 Lipid metabolism; fatty acid beta-oxidation Lipid metabolism; fatty acid beta-oxidation
Q8YA44 GO:0000287;GO:0004789; GO:0005737;GO:0009228; GO:0009229 Cofactor biosynthesis; thiamine diphosphate biosynthesis Cofactor biosynthesis; thiamine diphosphate biosynthesis; thiamine phosphate from 4-amino-2-methyl-5-diphosphomethylpyrimidine and 4-methyl-5-(2-phosphoethyl)-thiazole: step 1/1

The next protein, P21177 (fatty acid oxidation complex subunit alpha), is integral to lipid metabolism, particularly in fatty acid beta-oxidation. The associated GO terms, GO:0006635 (fatty acid beta-oxidation) and GO:0036125 (fatty acid beta-oxidation multienzyme complex), highlight its participation in the multienzyme complex that facilitates the breakdown of fatty acids. A supporting publication further validates the pathway summary generated by GO2Sum [21].

For the last example in this table, Q8YA44 (thiamine–phosphate synthase), GO2Sum successfully captures the pathway information, “cofactor biosynthesis; thiamine diphosphate biosynthesis,” but did not provide more detailed chemical reaction that was included in UniProt. The specific reaction information is included in GO:0004789, but GO2Sum did not take information from it.

7 GO2Sum Code and Web Server

The source code of GO2Sum can be downloaded from Github at https://github.com/kiharalab/GO2Sum under the terms of GNU Lesser General Public License Ver. 3.0. We also developed a Web server of GO2Sum that provides an easy access. The GO2Sum server is freely accessed at https://kiharalab.org/go2sum_server/. It is a Web application and does not require any platform other than Web browser.

This server takes a list of GO terms for protein(s) and outputs a summary paragraph in Function, Pathway, and Subunit categories of the protein(s). Figure 3 shows the front page of the GO2Sum server. The input file should have two columns: a protein name and

G02Sum


Fig. 3 Screenshot of the G02Sum server. The server can take a list of GO terms or take protein and their G0 Screenshot of the GO2Sum server. The server can take a list of GO terms or take protein and their GO term annotations for multiple proteins. For a single protein, GO term annotations can be selected from the is a checkbox for selecting summary categories to generate,either Function,Subunit Structure or Patwa. is a checkbox for selecting summary categories to generate, either Function, Subunit Structure, or Pathwa

a list of GO term IDs for each row. The column headers should be “Protein” and “GO_IDS.” A space is used to separate the columns, and a semicolon is used to separate multiple GO terms in the list. A sample input file can be downloaded to help users create input files for multiple proteins.

Users can choose GO terms from a drop-down menu instead of uploading a file. When a GO term ID is typed, GO2Sum automatically recognizes and displays matching options in a pull-down list, allowing for easy selection. For instance, typing \({ } ^ { \mathfrak { a } } 5 7 3 7 { } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }\) will display “GO:0005737” as a selectable option. At the end of the page, there are three checkboxes, one for each summary type, which the server will output: Function, Subunit Structure, and Pathway. Users can select one or more checkboxes to generate summaries. The GO2Sum server also offers an option to receive descriptions via email, which is particularly useful for large datasets.

An example of output is shown in Fig. 4. We present the functional output for two proteins, O00237 and O00462. The input windows of Option 1 shows GO terms input for these two proteins. For example, protein O00237 (E3 ubiquitin–protein ligase RNF103) is annotated with eight GO terms: GO:0004842 (ubiquitin–protein transferase activity); GO:0005783 (endoplasmic reticulum); GO:0005789 (endoplasmic reticulum membrane); GO:0007417 (central nervous system development); GO:0016567 (protein ubiquitination); GO:0036503 (ERAD pathway); GO:0046872 (metal ion binding); and GO:0061630 (ubiquitin–protein ligase activity). The output is displayed in a table format with three columns at the bottom of the page: the first column contains the protein name, the second describes the summary type, and the third provides the GO2Sum summary. GO2Sum correctly predicts the function of the protein as “Acts as an E2-dependent E3 ubiquitin– protein ligase, probably involved in the ER-associated protein degradation pathway.” The pathway is predicted as “protein modification; protein ubiquitination,” and the subunit structure as “Interacts with DERL1 and VCP.”

8 Conclusion

In this chapter, we discussed GO2Sum, an LLM-based summarizer that takes a list of GO terms as input and generates a concise, freetext summary detailing the protein’s functional descriptions, including its function, subunit structure, and pathway information. The summaries generated by GO2Sum demonstrate a substantially higher level of agreement with UniProt data when compared to the results obtained using the standard T5 and SciFive.

For many years, protein function prediction has relied on GO term annotations to describe protein functions. However, recent

advancements in NLP, particularly the development of LLM technologies, have enabled us to translate these predictions into concise, human-readable text that describes these functions. We believe

that GO2Sum significantly enhances the interpretability of GO term predictions, especially in supporting biologists and medical scientists in their research endeavors.
