TransFun: A Tool of Integrating Large Language Models, Transformers, and Equivariant Graph Neural Networks to Predict Protein Function

Frimpong Boadu, Ahhyun Lee, and Jianlin Cheng

Abstract

Experimentally determining the functions of proteins is a complex and time-consuming process. This challenge contributes to a gap, where many proteins have known sequences, predicted structures, and other crucial information, yet lack functional annotations. This gap underscores the critical importance of automated function prediction (AFP) methods, which aim to develop computational techniques dedicated to predicting protein functions. Most AFP methods leverage the wealth of diverse protein information available, such as sequences, structures, protein-protein interactions, and domain characteristics. These methods often utilize individual features or integrate multiple features to enhance the accuracy of function prediction. In this chapter, we focus on TransFun, a structure-based protein function prediction technique. TransFun leverages the embeddings provided by the ESM-1b pretrained protein language models to distill intricate sequence features and combines them with AlphaFold’s predicted structures to predict protein functions. Availability: https://github.com/jianlin-cheng/TransFun

Key words Protein function prediction, Gene ontology, Transformer, Graph neural networks, Equivariant graph neural networks, GO terms, Deep learning

1 Introduction

In this section, we delve into the automated function prediction (AFP) [3, 11, 19] of proteins problem, a crucial endeavor aimed at elucidating the functions of proteins. Understanding protein functions is pivotal for comprehending biological systems and manipulating biological processes, thus holding significant implications for biomedical research and biotechnology development. We discuss the problem in detail and introduce TransFun [4], a method for protein function prediction that operates on both sequence and structural information. Leveraging the ESM-1B [12] pretrained protein language model, TransFun extracts representative embeddings from protein sequences. These embeddings are then

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_6, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

combined with predicted protein structures to make predictions on protein functions.

To streamline the organization and understanding of protein functions, they are structured into ontologies, which categorize functions into terms with defined relationships, capturing the intricate connections among various functional terms. This structured approach not only provides a clear understanding of functional terms but also facilitates knowledge exchange, enabling efficient analysis and interpretation of protein functions.

In the realm of protein function prediction, many methods focus on the biomedical ontologies developed by the Gene Ontology (GO) Consortium [1, 2, 7, 15]. These ontologies consist of three main groups: Molecular Function Ontology (MFO), Biological Process Ontology (BPO), and Cellular Component Ontology (CCO), each represented as a directed acyclic graph.

The function prediction problem can be succinctly defined as follows: Given a set of proteins \({ \cal D } _ { l } = \{ ( p _ { 1 } , O _ { 1 } ) , ( p _ { 2 } , O _ { 2 } ) , . . . , ( p _ { n } , O _ { n } ) \}\) , where \(\mathbf { \nabla } _ { \mathbf { \mathit { P } } _ { i } }\) denotes the ith protein and \(O _ { i }\) represents its true functional annotation labels (specifically, a set of Gene Ontology terms), the objective is to accurately predict \(O _ { i }\) for any given protein \(\mathbf { \nabla } _ { \mathbf { \mathit { p } } _ { i } }\) . Since annotations are organized hierarchically, with a general root term at the apex, when a GO term \(_ x\) is linked to protein \(\mathbf { \nabla } _ { \mathbf { \mathit { p } } _ { i } }\) , all ancestor terms of \(_ x\) in the GO graph are also attributed to \(\mathbf { \nabla } _ { \mathbf { \mathit { p } } _ { i } }\) . Hence, we aim to predict the subgraph \(\mathcal { J }\) within the GO graph \(G\) , comprising all GO terms associated with the protein, as shown in Fig. 1 [6].

As previously mentioned, proteins possess essential features or properties—such as sequence, structure, domain, and interaction— that distinguish one protein from another. Experiments have demonstrated that protein function is closely related to these properties, and inference about protein function can be derived from them.

In TransFun [4], we leverage two fundamental features— sequence and structure information—to predict protein functions. Utilizing representative embeddings derived from the ESM-1B [12] pretrained protein language model, TransFun generates feature representations from protein sequences. These embeddings are then seamlessly integrated with AlphaFold’s [10, 18] predicted protein structures, effectively combining the insights from both sequence and 3D structural data to predict protein functions accurately. This combined approach not only enhances the predictive power of TransFun but also underscores the importance of leveraging multiple sources of information for comprehensive protein function prediction. To incorporate the intricate 3D features, we employ the equivariant neural network (EGNN) [13] to accurately model the protein’s three-dimensional structures. In the next section, we discuss the data collection process, delve into the TransFun

Fg 1 Theimage illustrates how Gene Ontology ) terms are structured hierarchically. For example, a The image illustrates how Gene Ontology (GO) terms are structured hierarchically. For example, if a protein is annotated with the specific GO term “GO:0000978,” it automatically includes broader terms like “GO:0000977” and “GO:0003676.” However, a higher-level annotation like “GO:0003676” does not guarantee the specific function “GO:0000978.” This hierarchy is key in predicting rel

QuickGO - https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO

methodology—including the data preprocessing steps that involve leveraging the ESM-1B language model and EGNN network—and elucidate the integration of these preprocessed features. Furthermore, we explore how to use the tool to predict protein functions.

1.1 Dataset

In our study, we curated protein sequences with functional annotations from the UniProt/Swiss-Prot database [16], with release date of 23 February 2022. Additionally, we obtained predicted protein structures from the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (Alpha-FoldDB) [18], with release date January 2022. To ensure data consistency, we cross-referenced the sequences extracted from the predicted structures with their corresponding entries in UniProt. We then filtered the dataset to include only proteins with sequence lengths ranging from 100 to 1022 residues for our training and validation sets. The upper limit of 1022 residues corresponds to the maximum sequence length accepted by the pretrained ESM model used for generating sequence embeddings. Functional annotations for the proteins were retrieved from UniProt, while ontology graph data were sourced from the Open Biological and Biomedical Ontology (OBO) Foundry data repository [7]. Protein functions were collected from the Gene Ontology (GO), which organizes relationships between GO terms in directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Adhering to CAFA standards [20], we exclusively considered terms curated under specific evidence codes (EXP, IDA, IPI, IMP, IGI, IEP, TAS, IC, HTP, HDA, HMP, HGI, HEP). To mitigate the inclusion of rare GO terms, we retained only those terms associated with at least 60 proteins for both training and testing purposes. Additionally, to investigate how sequence identity influences the accuracy of protein function prediction, we used MMseqs2 [14] to cluster the proteins in our curated dataset at sequence identity thresholds of \(3 0 \%\) , \(5 0 \%\) , \(9 0 \%\) , and \(9 5 \%\) . This resulted in the following data statistics (Table 1):

Table 1 The statistics of the curated protein function prediction dataset

Ontology No. of Proteins No. of GO terms Sequence identity threshold
0.3 0.5 0.9
MF 35,507 600 14,667 19,512 26,876
CC 50,340 547 20,679 26,808 36,721
BP 50,320 3,774 20,180 26,647 37,536

a The first three columns are the GO category, the total number of proteins in each category, and the number of GO terms in each category. The remaining four columns list the number of protein clusters at each sequence identity threshold (0.3, 0.5, 0.9, 0.95)

1.2 Data Preprocessing

Each protein is represented as a graph, with the extraction process from the predicted structure following a systematic approach. For every protein, we construct a graph encapsulated by an adjacency matrix \(\varkappa \times \varkappa\) , where n denotes the number of residues within the protein. This graph, denoted as \(G = \left( V , E , X \right)\) , comprises a vertex set \(V = \{ \boldsymbol { p } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { p } _ { 2 } , . . . , \boldsymbol { p } _ { n } \}\) , an edge set \(E\) Þ, and a node feature matrix \(\ b { X } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { m \times \vert V \vert }\) g, where m represents the dimension of the feature and \(\vert V \vert\) denotes the number of vertices (or residues).

The establishment of edges between residues is computed using the K-nearest neighbor (KNN) method. Specifically, for the KNN approach, a node \(\mathcal { V }\) is included in the neighborhood \(N _ { k }\) if it is among the \(K\) nearest neighbors of a given node u. Here, \(K\) is determined as \(\sqrt [ 3 ] { n }\) , where n signifies the number of residues. Alternatively, we explored two additional methods for graph generation: setting \(K\) to \(\sqrt { n }\) and employing a distance threshold approach. In the latter method, edges are established based on the Euclidean distance between carbon alpha atoms, with five distinct distance thresholds examined: \(4 \ \mathring { \mathrm { A } } , \ \mathring { 6 } \ \mathring { \mathrm { A } } , \ 8 \ \mathring { \mathrm { A } } , \ 1 0 \ \mathring { \mathrm { A } } .\) , and \(1 2 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }\) .

To extract sequence features, we utilize the ESM-1B pretrained protein language model. This model operates at the residue level, generating embeddings for each residue in the protein sequence. These embeddings form a tensor of dimensions \(l \times d\) , where $ { l }$ represents the sequence length and \(d\) denotes the embedding dimension (1280). To generate embeddings for a given sequence, we adopt a mean aggregation approach over the residue-level embeddings. Furthermore, the ESM-1B transformer model produces embeddings at multiple layers, and for our purposes, we specifically use embeddings from the last (33rd) layer [12].

1.3 Method

The architecture of TransFun [4] is designed with blocks of rotation- and translation-equivariant graph neural networks, leveraging the equivariant graph neural network (EGNN) [13] as the foundation. Our design comprises four EGNN blocks, each interspersed with a rectified linear unit (ReLU) activation function and a batch normalization layer.

The equivariant graph neural network (EGNN) [13] embodies key principles of equivariance, including translation, rotation, reflection equivariance (E(n)), and permutation equivariance. Permutation equivariance ensures that the architecture respects input symmetries under permutations, maintaining invariance to changes in element order. Similarly, translation equivariance preserves structural consistency by ensuring that network output remains unaffected by translations of the input data. Rotation (and reflection) equivariance enhances robustness to variations in position and orientation, guaranteeing that the model’s predictions remain consistent under rotations or reflections of the input data.

In essence, the EGNN achieves these equivariances by operating on relative positions and orientations within the graph. This means that alterations to the graph, such as translations or rotations, maintain the relative positions and orientations between nodes and edges. This preservation of relative information ensures the integrity of the data during message passing and contributes to the model’s robustness and effectiveness in processing graphstructured data.

In our model, each EGNN block accepts a graph as input to update its features. Initially, we incorporate node features, including per-residue embeddings and \(x , y , z\) coordinates of each residue. We experimented with optional edge features such as the distance between nodes and a binary indicator for adjacent residues connected by a peptide bond. However, these edge features did not yield improvements in prediction accuracy beyond node features and were consequently excluded from the final model.

1.4 Getting Started with TransFun

TransFun is an open-source tool for predicting protein functions, accessible to all individuals through GitHub. TransFun is available via the following GitHub link: https://github.com/jianlin-cheng/ TransFun. The repository contains a comprehensive toolkit for working with protein structure prediction, including model training, data preprocessing, and utilities for managing and transforming datasets.

1.5 Installation Instructions

First, to install TransFun on your local computer, clone the project from GitHub at your desired directory on your computer, as shown below:

1.5.1 Clone the Repository

git clone https://github.com/jianlin-cheng/ TransFun.git   
cd TransFun/

This command clones the repository and then changes the directory to the newly created local repository folder.

1.5.2 Downloading Necessary Data

TransFun uses pretrained models and additional data files that are essential for its operation. These need to be downloaded separately.

curl https://calla.rnet.missouri.edu/rnami-ner/transfun/data
--output data.zip unzip data

These commands download the required data and model files into a zip archive and then unzip them in the current directory.

1.5.3 Setting Up the Conda Environment

To ensure that all dependencies required by TransFun are installed and managed properly, a Conda environment is used. This isolates the software’s dependencies from other Python environments on your system.

conda env create -f environment.yml conda activate transfun

These commands create a new Conda environment based on the specifications in the environment.yml file and then activate this environment. Make sure Conda is installed before executing these commands.

1.6 Using TransFun: Step-by-Step Guide

TransFun allows prediction of protein functions with either protein structures in PDB format or protein sequences in FASTA format combined with structures.

1.6.1 Predicting Protein Functions Using PDB Files

To predict the function of proteins based on their threedimensional structures provided in PDB format. The software automatically extracts sequences from these PDB files.

python predict.py --data-path path\to\store  
\intermediate  
\files --ontology GO\function\category --input-type  
pdb --pdb-path data\/alphafold --output output  
\file  
--cut-off probability\threshold

1.6.2 Predicting Protein Functions Using FASTA and PDB Files

For cases where protein sequences are available separately from structures, TransFun can utilize both FASTA-formatted sequences and PDB files for a more comprehensive analysis.

python predict.py --data-path path\to\store \_intermediate \_files --ontology GO\function\category --input-type fasta --pdb-path data\alphafold --fasta-path path 
\_to\_a\_fasta  
\_file--output result.txt --cut-off probabilit-ity\_\threshold

1.6.3 Command-Line Options Explained

--data-path: Specifies where to store intermediate data files. --ontology: Indicates the GO function category (e.g., cellular\_component, molecular\_function). --input-type: Type of input (pdb or fasta). --pdb-path: Directory path containing PDB files. --fasta-path: Path to a FASTA file (used only if input-type is fasta). --output: Path for the output file where results will be saved. --cut-off: Probability threshold for predictions.

Here, we present examples for two proteins—A0A5S9MMK5 and E9Q0S6—focusing on molecular function and cellular component, respectively. Using the commands provided below, each taking an input PDB file, we display the results. Specifically, we show the correct predictions (true positives) from TransFun along with their respective probability scores, the incorrect predictions (false positives with their corresponding scores), and the missed predictions (false negatives) in Table 2.

font, false positives in red font, and false negatives in orange font Predictions for protein A0A5S9MMK5 (molecular function) and protein E9Q0S6 (cellular component) are shown. True positives are indicated in blue able 2

  1. Molecular function (AOA5S9MMK5)

GO:0005622 (0.901) GO:0015629 (0.129) GO:0043226 (0.571) GO:0005856 (0.129) GO:0043229 (0.519) GO:0043232 (0.167)
GO:0070013 (0.127) GO:0043231 (0.364) GO:0030054 (0.225) GO:0071944 (0.354) GO:0005654 (0.127) GO:0043233 (0.127)

python predict.py --data-path data --ontology molecular  
\_function --cut-off 0.01 --input-type pdb --pdb-path  
test/pdbss/ --output result.txt  
python predict.py --data-path data  
--ontology cellular\component --cut-off 0.1  
--input-type pdb --pdb-path test/pdbss/  
--output result.txt

2 Conclusion

In this chapter, we present a book chapter detailing our research on protein function prediction (AFP) using TransFun [4]. TransFun is a structure-based method that leverages embeddings from the ESM-1B [12] pretrained protein language model and AlphaFold’s predicted structures [10] to predict protein functions.

For future work, we aim to decompose and better understand the individual contributions of sequence and structural features. To the best of our knowledge, most structure-based AFP methods also incorporate sequence features. This is largely due to the widespread use of graph neural networks (GNNs) [5, 8], which typically require node-level features where sequence-derived features are prevalent. As a result, distinguishing the specific contributions of structural features has proven challenging.

To address this, we plan to utilize methods that extract features exclusively from protein structures, such as the \(3 D i\) alphabet [9, 17] or other structural modules, to isolate and assess the impact of structural information alone. Additionally, we aim to enhance prediction accuracy by integrating multiple modalities, including textual data and InterPro annotations, through advanced multimodal approaches and large language models.

Acknowledgements

This work was supported in part by a grant from the US National Science Foundation (NSF grant numbers: DBI2308699, CCF2343612).

Using InterLabelGO+ for Accurate Protein Language Model-Based Function Prediction

Chengxin Zhang, Quancheng Liu, and Lydia Freddolino

Abstract

The recent availability of large language models for protein sequences has spurred the development of deep learning models for prediction of protein functions, mainly in the form of gene ontology (GO) terms. We developed InterLabelGO+, a top performing deep learning–based protein GO term prediction model in the recent fifth Critical Assessment of Function Annotation (CAFA5) challenge. InterLabelGO+ uses the ESM2 protein language model to extract sequence features, which are then used as inputs to a deep learning model that was trained under a loss function that considers the potentially complex relations between different GO terms. The deep learning–predicted GO terms are then combined with GO terms from sequence homology search to derive consensus predictions. InterLabelGO+ is available at https://seq2fun. dcmb.med.umich.edu/InterLabelGO/. In this chapter, we demonstrate procedures to perform protein GO term prediction with the InterLabelGO+ webserver and the standalone package, as well as how to retrain the model with up-to-date training data.

Key words Protein function prediction, Gene ontology, Protein language model, Deep learning, Sequence homology

1 Introduction

Proteins are the main biomacromolecules performing biological functions in living cells. As the most popular standardized vocabulary for protein functions, gene ontology (GO) terms categorize functions into three aspects: molecular function (MF), Biological Process (BP) and Cellular Component (CC). Since predicting protein functions, especially GO terms, is of great significance to the biological and biomedical community, many template-based [1–5] and machine learning–based [6–8] approaches have been developed for this purpose. With recent advances in artificial intelligence and natural language processing, almost all recently published

protein GO term predictors [9–11] are at least partly based on deep learning, often making use of large language models (LLMs) such as ESM [12], ProtT5 [13], and ProteinBert [14] that are trained on self-supervised learning of protein sequences.

A typical deep learning–based protein GO prediction model [9–11] use an LLM to extract features from the query amino acid sequence. These sequence features are then input to a neural network to predict the GO term labels. The prediction of each GO term is treated as a binary classification problem, typically through the cross-entropy loss function during neural network training, having made use of a dedicated protein training set with previously known functions to use as ground truths.

We recently developed InterLabelGO+, a hybrid pipeline for accurately predicting protein GO terms by combining a deep learning model (InterLabelGO) and sequence homolog search method (AlignmentKNN) (Fig. 1) [15]. There are two main differences between InterLabelGO and most existing deep learning– based protein GO predictors. First, instead of simply using the final output of an LLM for sequence feature generation, InterLabelGO extracts sequence features from the last three layers of the ESM2 LLM to harvest richer and more informative sequence data from the embedding. Second, rather than using a loss function that considers prediction of each GO terms separately (such as the crossentropy loss that is nearly ubiquitous in the function prediction field), InterLabelGO uses a combinatory loss function (the F1 loss


Fig. 1 \(\mathsf { G O + }\) , which combines protein G0 term predictions from a deep learning—based component (InterLabelGO, blue) and a sequence homolog search-based component (AlignmentkNN, red) to component (InterLabelGO, blu

plus the ZLPR loss [16] to consider correlation among different GO annotations as well as the information contents of each GO terms). Details of the architecture design and benchmark performance of InterLabelGO+ are reported elsewhere [15]. In this chapter, we focus on how to run the InterLabelGO+ program through the online web-server and through a standalone package.

2 Materials

The InterLabelGO+ web server can be run through most major web browsers, including Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, and Microsoft Edge. An academic email address is needed to receive notification upon job completion.

To run the standalone package of InterLabelGO+ locally, either a Linux operating system or Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) is needed. While predicting GO terms using the pretrained Inter-LabelGO model can be performed on either Central Processing Unit (CPU) or Graphics Processing Unit (GPU), Compute Unified Device Architecture (CUDA)-capable GPU is required if the user wishes to retrain the standalone InterLabelGO program. Python libraries required by InterlabelGO+ (Table 1) can be setup by conda, which is available either through Anaconda (https://www.anaconda.com/download) or Miniconda (https:// docs.anaconda.com/miniconda/).

3 Methods

3.1 Using the InterLabelGO+ Webserver

3.1.1 Webserver Input

The InterLabelGO+ webserver is designed for academic users who needs to perform protein function predictions for a small number of proteins. Here, we will use the Cyclin-H protein from fruit fly (UniProt accession: O76513) as an example (Fig. 2). First, the user may submit the protein of interest via https://seq2fun.dcmb.med. umich.edu/InterLabelGO/ (Fig. 2a), where the user should provide the amino acid sequence (either in FASTA format or just the sequence without the header), an academic email (Hotmail, Gmail, Yahoo, or other commercial emails are not acceptable) to track the job status, and the name of the protein. If the name of the protein is not provided, the protein name will be “protein” by default.

Upon successful job submission, a webpage link in the format of https://seq2fun.dcmb.med.umich.edu/InterLabelGO/out put/IL******** is provided by the webserver (Fig. 2b). Here, \(\langle \epsilon \star \star \star \star \star \star \star \star \rangle \nmid\) stands for an eight-digit number. This webpage link will be the place where the user may check the job status and view the final result. If this link does not lead to an active webpage, just check back in a few minutes. The webpage link is also sent to the user via email (Fig. 2c).

Table 1 Python packages required to run the InterLabe \(\mathbf { 6 0 + }\) standalone package (note that this information is also available in the requirements.txt file of the standalone package)

(continued)

Table 1 (continued)

Package Version
torch 2.1.0+cu121 (from https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121)
torchaudio 2.1.0+cu121
torchvision 0.16.0+cu121

3.1.2 Webserver Output

A prediction job takes about 15 min. This wait time depends heavily on our server load and can be significantly longer if there are many prediction jobs in queue. The actual time to run the prediction is much shorter (see Subheading 3.2.3).

Once the prediction job is finished, the user will be notified by email of job completion (Fig. 2d). The full result will be available at the webpage link provided by Fig. 2b, d. The result webpage (Fig. 3) starts with the input sequence, followed by predicted GO terms. Predicted terms for molecular function (MF), biological process (BP), and cellular components (CC) aspects are shown separately.

For each GO aspect, we list the predicted GO terms, the InterLabelGO \(^ +\) prediction scores for those terms, and the full names of the GO term in a table (Fig. 3). If both the child term and its parent term are predicted, and the parent term does not have a higher predicted scores than the child term, the parent term will not be shown in the table. Here, the InterLabelGO+ prediction score, also known as C-score in the webserver output, ranges between 0 and 1. A higher C-score indicates a higher precision, i.e., posterior probability of the predicted GO term being correct. C-scores of 0.166, 0.755, and 0.139 correspond to a precision of 0.5 for MF, BP, and CC, respectively (see calibration curves in Fig. 4).

Apart from the table listing predicted GO terms, we also use Graphviz [17] to draw directed acyclic graphs for the predicted terms (colored) and their parent terms (white). The predicted terms are colored in red, orange, yellow, green, cyan, blue, or purple in descending order of the prediction score.

In another table, we list the top 20 sequence homologs identified by DIAMOND in the AlignmentKNN component (Fig. 3, right side). Note that the sequence templates for different aspects may be different, e.g., in the case that a sequence template has GO annotations for the MF aspect but not the BP or CC aspect in the database. For each sequence template, we list its ranking, UniProt accession, alignment bit-score, sequence identity of the aligned residues, E-value, sequence length, global sequence identity normalized by the query length, and global sequence identity normalized by the template length.


Fig. 2 \(^ { 6 0 + }\) webserver. (a) Submitting a protein sequence (cyclin-H from fruit fly) to the Th \(\mathsf { G O + }\) erLabelGO+ webserver. ( Submitting a protein sequence (cyclin-H from fruit fly) to the b) c InterLabelGO+ website. ( Webpage shown after successful job submissiod


Fig. 3 \(\mathsf { I G O + }\) webserver prediction for fruit fly cyclin-H. While the full prediction result Excerpt of InterLabelGO+ webserver prediction for fruit fly cyclin-H. While the full prediction result contains f


Fig. 4 Calibration curves (red) between the InterLabe \(\mathsf { I G O + }\) prediction score (i.e., C-score) and the precision of Calibration curves (red) between the InterLabelGO+ prediction score (i.e., C-score) and the precision of GO term predictions for the three aspects (MF, BP, and CC) of GO terms. These data are derived from 2041

3.2 Using the InterLabelGO+ Standalone Package

Although the InterLabelGO+ webserver is user friendly, it has several limitations; thus, we also provide an InterLabelGO+ standalone package which can be applied by users whose needs exceed the usage cases supported by the webserver. The limitations of the webserver include first, the webserver requires an academic email, while the standalone package can be used by any users, including commercial ones. Second, while the webserver only process protein sequences with up to 1500 amino acids, the standalone package can work with arbitrary protein sequence lengths so long as there is sufficient physical memory. Third, the webserver only processes one sequence at a time, while the standalone package can perform bulk prediction of thousands of proteins in a single run, making the latter more useful for proteome-wide function annotation. Fourth, the standalone package allows the user to retrain the model parameters using the most recent protein function databases, or a specific version of protein function database to enable fair comparison with other programs. This retraining is not available for the user of the webserver. In the following sections, we will discuss how to run the standalone package to deploy, train, and test InterLabelGO+.

3.2.1 Installing the InterLabelGO+ Standalone Package

  1. Download and extract the InterLabelGO \(^ +\) package:
$ wget https://seq2fun.dcbmb.med.umich.edu/InterLabelGO/InterLabelGO+.tar.xz
$ tar -xvf InterLabelGO+.tar.xz
$ cd InterLabelGO

The tar.xz compressed package is approximately 7 GB. While we also make the source code for the InterLabelGO package available at https://github.com/QuanEvans/InterLabelGO, this GitHub site does not include pretrained model parameters, which are over the file size limits of GitHub. Therefore, we recommend downloading the tar.xz compressed package instead. The files in the folder are explained by Table 2.

  1. Install Python dependencies required by InterLabelGO+. Although these dependencies (Table 1) can be directly installed using pip (https://pypi.org/project/pip/), we strongly recommend running pip within a dedicated environment setup with the conda command:
$ conda create -n InterLabelGO python=3.11.5 -y
$ conda activate InterLabelGO
$ pip install -r requirements.txt

Table 2 Files in the InterLabe \(\mathbf { 6 0 + }\) standalone package

Filename Description
README.md Readme file for how to install and use InterLabelGO+
LICENSE MIT license
predict.py Inference script to perform InterLabelGO+ prediction
alignment_knn.py Script to perform AlignmentKNN prediction
plm.py Script to convert FASTA sequence into ESM2 embedding
train_InterLabelGO.py Training script
prepare_data.py Convert data format for the training data
update_data.py Download the latest GOA database and convert it into the required format for retraining the model
settings.py Configuration file for paths of data files
requirements.txt PIP script to specify python packages used by InterLabelGO+
setup_env.sh Script to help set up dependencies
Network/ Scripts to define InterLabelGO model architecture, loss functions, and data loading functions
example/ Folder for example files
models/ZLPR_PTF1_GOF1/ InterLabelGO pretrained model parameters
Data/alignment_database/ DIAMOND format sequence databases for AlignmentKNN
Data/alignment_labels/ GO terms for sequence databases for AlignmentKNN
Data/esm_models/ The ESM2 protein language model
Data/cafa5_raw_data/ Text format raw training set
Data/network_training_data/ InterLabelGO training dataset in numpy pickle format

The above commands create a new conda environment called “InterLabelGO”, activate the environment, and install all dependencies within that environment, making use of the explicit dependency file (requirements.txt) provided for InterLabelGO+. To run InterLabelGO+ in other shell sessions, make sure to run “conda activate InterLabelGO” beforehand.

3.2.2 Testing the InterLabelGO+ Standalone Package

  1. To perform function inference, we will create the input FASTA format sequence file “test/seq.fasta”, which corresponds to the Cyclin-H protein used in the previous example:
$ mkdir test/
$ echo ">076513" > test/seq.fasta
$ echo
"MYPVSSQKRSWTFANEGQLMEFRVEQNSKYIESHEEAAQGRDLNEHFLTSAEERLLLKQYEIYLFDCRRFEPTMPKCVCVGTAHYFKRFYLNNSPMDYHPKEILATCVFVACKVEEFNVSinQFNVIKKGDRNKATDIVLSNELLLLIGQLNYLTIHNPFRPIEGFLIDIKTRSNMQPDRLRPHIDSFIDSTYYSDACLLHTPSQIALAAVLHAASREQENLDSYVTDLLFVSAREKLPLGLIDAVRKIRIMVKQYQQPDREKVKAIEKKLDKCRNQANNPDSELYKERLRLRYTDEDDMPAEDASFHIADVSSDTSAMNISQ" >>test/seq.fasta

While in the above example, the input file “test/seq.fasta” has only one sequence, the input FASTA file may contain two or more sequences, in which case predictions will be performed separately for each protein in the input FASTA sequence.

  1. Run the inference script to predict the GO terms for the target sequence:
$ python predict.py -w test -f test/seq.fasta --use_gpu

The full process takes around 7 min on a modern Linux workstation without GPU and less than 1 min with GPU. In the above command, “-w test” specifies the folder to which output files are written. “-f test/seq.fasta” specifies the input FASTA file path. The flag “—use_gpu” requests the usage of GPU to accelerate ESM2 sequence embedding when available. The main output file in the test/ folder is InterLabelGO+.tsv, whose format is explained in Fig. 5. Other output files are explained in Table 3. The procedure for running InterLabelGO+ for standard function predictions is exactly equivalent to what is shown above, just using a different query FASTA file.

If attempting to run the standalone package results in error messages complaining that a module is unavailable, such as “ModuleNotFoundError: No module named torch”, even if the Python dependencies are correctly installed, make sure that the conda environment for InterLabelGO is activated by:

$ conda activate InterLabelGO

Please see the conda documentation (https://docs.conda.io/) for additional information.

A B C D E F
1 EntryID term score max_seqid aspect go_term_name
111 O76513 GO:0003674 1 0.382 MFO molecular_function
112 O76513 GO:0005488 0.814 0.382 MFO binding
113 O76513 GO:0005515 0.78 0.382 MFO protein binding
114 O76513 GO:0016538 0.673 0.382 MFO cyclin-dependent protein serine/threonine kinase regulator activity
115 O76513 GO:0003824 0.535 0.382 MFO catalytic activity
116 O76513 GO:0016740 0.535 0.382 MFO transferase activity
117 O76513 GO:0016772 0.535 0.382 MFO transferase activity, transferring phosphorus-containing groups

Fig. 5 \(\mathsf { I G O + }\) standalone package predictions for cyclin-H. The six columns in this Excerpt from the InterLabelGO+ standalone package predictions for cyclin-H. The six columns in this Tab Separated Values (TSV) format table are the query protein name, GO term, prediction score (i.e., C-score), the average sequence ident

Table 3 Output files for testing InterLabelGO+. Files are ranked in chronological order of creation

Filename Descriptionm
InterLabelGO_pred/ embed_feature/*npy ESM2 embedding for the query sequence
InterLabelGO_pred/names.npy List of names of the query proteins
InterLabelGO_pred/ InterLabelGO.tsv Deep learning prediction
AlignmentKNN_pred/*_blast.tsv DIAMOND search results
AlignmentKNN_pred/seqid.tsv Sequence identities for top ranking templates for MF, BP and CC
AlignmentKNN_pred/ AlignmentKNN.tsv AlignmentKNN prediction
InterLabelGO+.tsv Final GO term predictions from InterLabelGO+

3.2.3 Retraining the InterLabelGO+ Standalone Package

In this section, we detail how the InterLabelGO+ network can be retrained using the latest versions of the UniProt-GOA annotation database and Swiss-Prot protein sequence database.

  1. Download the latest UniProt Gene Ontology Annotation (UniProt-GOA) database and the Swiss-Prot protein sequence database, which are approximately 20GB in total file size:
$ python update_data.py
  1. Convert the format of the downloaded data files:
$ python prepare_data.py --make_db --ia

The above command will perform the following operations:

• Convert text format training set sequences and GO terms labels at “Data/cafa5_raw_data/train_” to binary format training data at “Data/network_training_data/”.
• Create DIAMOND format sequence databases at “Data/ alignment_database/” for the training data, as specified by the —make_db flag.
• Calculate the information content for each GO term for the training data and save the information contents to “Data/network_training_data/IA.txt”, as specified by the —ia flag.
• Calculate the ESM2 sequence embeddings for the training sequences at “Data/embeddings/*.npy”.

  1. Retrain the model using the new training data:

\(\$ 5\) python train_InterLabelGO.py

The newly trained model will overwrite the old InterLabelGO models at “models/ZLPR_PTF1_GOF1//model_.pt”. The full training procedure takes approximately 90 min on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.

Functional Annotation of Proteomes Using Protein Language Models: A High-Throughput Implementation of the ProtTrans Model

Ildefonso Cases, Gemma Martı´nez-Redondo, Rosa Ferna´ ndez, and Ana M. Rojas

Abstract

Protein function prediction is critical for a wide range of applications in biology, spanning from functional genomics to protein design and genome evolution, among others. However, accurately predicting protein function remains a longstanding challenge in computational biology, especially for non-model organisms. Traditional methods based on sequence similarity often fail to annotate a significant proportion of proteins. The emergence of protein language models has significantly improved this process, enabling more accurate and comprehensive functional annotation. In this work, we highlight how the ProtTrans language model outperforms other tools in per-protein annotation, offering a more precise approach to predicting protein function. We also introduce functional annotation based on embedding space similarity (FANTASIA; available at https://github.com/MetazoaPhylogenomicsLab/FANTASIA), a tool developed to harness these advances for large-scale annotation of uncharacterized proteomes. We provide a detailed overview of how to use FANTASIA, interpret its outputs, and demonstrate its utility in three case studies: (a) enrichment analyses from transcriptomics data, (b) assigning novel functions to unannotated genes in model organisms, and (c) identifying genes involved in important functions in non-model organisms. These results demonstrate the potential of protein language models to advance functional annotation in diverse biological contexts.

Key words Protein function prediction, Protein language models, Proteome annotation, FANTASIA, Computational biology

1 Introduction

UniProtKB is the main repository for protein sequences, currently containing over 250 million sequences (as of the January release of UniProtKB). However, less than \(0 . 5 \%\) of known proteins have experimental annotations [1].

Functional annotation on unknown sequences is typically predicted using sequence similarity-based methods, which rely on

evolutionary information from a limited set of model organisms. The rationale is that similar genes are likely to perform similar functions [2], allowing the transfer of function when sequence similarity exceeds a certain threshold. In this approach, functional information—often formalized as gene ontology (GO) terms [3] is transferred from known to unknown sequences based on these similarity criteria. This is the standard method for investigating the coding functional landscape of genomes.

However, there are several issues with this approach. It is known that unrelated genes can perform the same function [4], while highly similar genes can have orthogonal functions [5]. Additionally, many genes do not exhibit detectable similarity. Furthermore, the dynamic structural conformation of proteins complicates the identification of evolutionary relationships, contributing to the “darkness” of certain protein functions [6]. These challenges highlight the need to decouple function prediction from evolutionary assumptions in order to annotate novel genomes more effectively.

The direct consequence of these caveats of similarity-based annotation is the limited coverage of the protein space. Even for comprehensively studied model organisms like Caenorhabditis elegans and Drosophila melanogaster, approximately \(2 8 \%\) and \(1 8 \%\) of their genes, respectively, remain unannotated in the reference database Uniprot [7]. The situation is even worse for non-model organisms, where roughly \(5 0 \%\) of genes remain unannotated [8, 9]. These unannotated genes likely hold novel clues to understanding biology but remain “hidden” due to current limitations in annotation methods.

Recently, the use of large language models has emerged as a useful alternative that decouples function prediction from evolutionary information. There are many language models available that could be used to annotate proteins available here (https:// huggingface.co/). Among them, the ProtTrans autoencoder language model [10], trained on 393 billion amino acids (https:// github.com/agemagician/ProtTrans), is one of the most successful, as benchmarked against the CAFA3 datasets [1], and offers a promising alternative. Recent work validating the performance of ProtTrans at the level of model organisms [7] demonstrates its ability to recover functional information from transcriptomics data, making it a strong alternative to standard homology-based methods for downstream analyses.

ProtTrans is the core of the goPredSim [1] method, which is publicly available as a web server for exploring the functional annotation of individual sequences(https://embed.protein.properties/). However, it currently lacks automatic implementation for annotating whole proteomes and does not include essential steps for data preprocessing or output configuration. To address these limitations, we have developed functional annotation based on embedding space similarity (FANTASIA), an automated implementation of the goPredSim ProtTrans model with several enhancements.

The protocol we present here is twofold. First, it provides guidelines for evaluating the performance of different models in the context of function annotation at the organism-level. Second, it involves the use of a computational pipeline (FANTASIA) for largescale annotation. The steps include (1) data collection, (2) data preprocessing, (3) computation of embeddings and distances, and (4) GO term transfer. Here, we highlight the importance of using alternative methods to homology-based function assignment.

2 Materials

  1. This approach requires in-house computational tools, access to a high-performance computing (HPC) facility for intensive computations, ample storage capacity, and programming skills for data processing.
  2. Singularity container (available at https://docs.sylabs.io/ guides/3.0/user-guide/installation.html).

3 Methods

3.1 Comparison of Different ML-Based Annotation Methods: Selecting the best Suited Tool
3.2 Functional Annotation Based on Embedding Space Similarity (FANTASIA)

Performance of the different top performing machine learning methods (in CAFA datasets, see Note 1) was estimated in model organisms using different metrics (see Notes 2 and 3). The Prot-Trans (ProtT5) model was the best performer and selected to further scale-up the annotation of proteomes (see subheading 3.2).

FANTASIA is a large-scale implementation of the goPredSim [1] method and requires access to a workstation or a high-performance computing (HPC) facility due to the tool’s high RAM requirements (see Note 4). FANTASIA (Fig. 1) (1) takes a FASTA peptide file as input (Fig. 1a), (2) preprocesses (see Note 5) the data (Fig. 1d), (3) runs GOPredSIM (with the ProtT5 model) and performs GO annotation (Fig. 1b,e and see Note 6), and (4) generates output files ready to be used as inputs for topGO enrichment methods [11] (Fig. 1c,f). If all isoforms are used as input files (Fig. 1e), then GO terms from each gene isoform will be collapsed (Fig. 1f) to produce a repertoire of GO terms per gene. Specific instructions for downloading are available here (https://github. com/MetazoaPhylogenomicsLab/FANTASIA).

3.2.1 Installation from a Singularity Image, Only CPU Enabled

The current implementation of FANTASIA as a singularity image requires basic command-line skills for execution.

  1. Download the singularity image (available here https://cloud. sylabs.io/librar y/gemma.martinezredondo/fantasia/ fantasia).


Fig. 1 The FANTASIA pipeline. Top row: Conceptual steps. Bottom row: Implementation steps. (a) The FANTASIA pipeline. Top row: Conceptual steps. Bottom row: Implementation steps. ( )Processing step: This step takes an input file of reference sequences (from the GOA2022 dataset) with b GOPredSim execution: known functions (black background) and target sequences (gray backgrounds). ( ) Using the ProtTrans (T5) model, distances among embeddings from reference (black background) and target sequences are calculated. Distances are indicated as red arcs connecting the embeddings, with thicker arcs representing smaller distances (and therefore greater similarity). A dashed line indicates that the distance between the reference and sequence 3 embeddings is very large and will not be considered for the next step. A semi-supervised method then transfers GO annotations from reference sequences to the unknown ones see Note 6 c Output file generation: (blue lines). For the embedding similarity(*) calculation, . ( ) The GO terms — — corresponding to each gene ontology (biological process BP, molecular function MF, and cellular — component CC) previously transferred to the target sequences are transformed into topGO [11] compliant d Pipeline execution (input file generation): format for downstream enrichment analyses. ( ) \(( > 5 0 0 0\) The script “generate_gopredsim_input_files.sh” first removes identical and large sequences ( 5000 aa), generating the see Note 5 input file. Importantly, the removal of “sister species” ( ) effects should be done before running this e Pipeline execution (method running): step. ( ) The script “launch_gopredsim_pipeline.sh” runs the GOPredSim method. This step also provides the option to use the entire repertoire of isoforms, rather than f Pipeline just the longest isoform, to extract potential information contributed by different isoforms. ( ) execution (output production): The output files are formatted to topGO format via “convert_topgo_format. py.” If isoforms are used, an additional step collapses the annotations from all isoforms using “collapse_- go_isoforms.py,” which takes information from an isoform-to-gene mapping table. The final output is

  1. Once downloaded, you can execute it as follows (make sure that you have singularity installed):

. Syntax: ./fantasia –infile protein.fasta [–outpath output_- path] [–allisoforms gene_isoform_conversion.txt] [–keepintermediate]. For the options explanations, see Note 7.

3.2.2 Local Installation of FANTASIA, Also GPU Enabled

In some architectures where singularity cannot be used, the source code is provided for installation by users with more advanced programming skills.

  1. Download the scripts and files from http://gofile.me/5wDJy/ CI8zCiijR.
  2. Open the “installation_guide_FANTASIA.sh” shell script (also available at https://github.com/MetazoaPhylogenomicsLab/ FANTASIA) and follow the instructions.
  3. Execute FANTASIA (you can check the files and options required for each script by adding -h).

4 Case Studies

4.1 Downstream GO Enrichments from RNAseq Data

Understanding the functions that are either exerted or inhibited (i.e., upregulated or downregulated) under different conditions or treatments is one of the main objectives of functional genomics. These analyses typically utilize gene expression data that undergo downstream functional enrichments of genes or pathways. For these approaches to be effective, it is necessary to have most genes functionally annotated. In the case of model organisms, most genes are annotated; however, for non-model organisms, only about \(5 0 \%\) of their genes are annotated [8], making the discovery of novel functions or genes challenging. As these species exhibit functions that could be interesting to exploit—such as resistance to radiation and regeneration—it is important to increase the annotation coverage of these genomes. We demonstrated that ProtT5 can recover the same information as homology-based methods in differential gene expression experiments in model organisms [7] (see Note 3). In Fig. 2, we present the recovery of similar enriched terms using the reference annotations or the ProtT5-derived annotation (measured as semantic similarity) for each gene ontology for published transcriptomics experiments from C. elegans [12]. ProtT5- derived annotations are able to recover almost the same set of enriched terms than the reference annotation, even if we keep only the \(2 5 \%\) top-scored annotations (Fig. 2, \(S S > 0 . 8\) in downregulated genes). This case illustrates how language models recover and recapitulate functional information, making them reliable choices for transcriptomic-based downstream experiments to address the functional landscapes in non-model organisms.

4.2 Identifying Novel Functions in Unannotated Genes from Model Organisms

Model organisms are often considered the standard for comparative genomics studies, with the assumption that most of their genes are well characterized. However, this assumption is not entirely accurate. We observed that even in extensively studied model organisms, a significant number of genes remain unannotated. For


Fig. 2 Caenorhabditis elegans Daf-2 Semantic similarity from transcriptomics data of the gene in a long-lived C. elegans mutant. Differentially expressed genes from a long-lived mutant were retrieved from the literature [12]. To identify enriched/depleted functions, four independent topGO enrichment analyses [11] were conducted using different approaches: (i) UniProt reference annotations, (ii) ProtT5 predictions, (iii) HMMER see Note 3 predictions ( ), and (iv) DeepGOPlus predictions. Semantic similarity (SS) [20] was then calculated for the enriched gene ontology (GO) terms, comparing each method against the reference annotations. The semantic similarity between reference/ProtT5 (blue line), HMMER/reference (black line), and DeepGOPlus/ reference (red line) is depicted. The upper panel represents downregulated genes, while the lower panel represents upregulated genes. The Y-axis shows the semantic similarity values (calculated using the Wang method [25] and the Best Match Average (BMA) to handle multiple GO terms) per gene. The X-axis represents the fraction of annotations removed based on each method’s score. Since the scores are not directly comparable [7], they were divided into quantiles using the R quantile functio \(2 5 \%\) e first quantile includes all annotations regardless of score, while \(2 8 \%\) ast quantile includes only the top 25% of annotations with the of C. elegans highest scores. Given that approximately 28% genes are unannotated, GOPredSim-ProtT5 not only annotated most proteins from the genome but also achieved th

example, approximately \(1 7 \%\) of genes in \(D\) . melanogaster are still unannotated [ ]. For instance, the CG11373 protein-coding gene 7 lacks assigned gene ontology (GO) functions despite evidence that the protein is expressed in male germline cells, somatic cells of the testis, and adult males, as reported in the FlyBase repository ( ). This lack of https://flybase.org/reports/FBgn0040679.htm annotation may be attributed to the fact that the protein is predicted to be disordered, as determined by AIUPred [ ]. FANTA-13 SIA, however, inferred the GO terms “meiotic cell cycle” and “signal transduction involved in cell cycle switching, mitotic to

meiotic cell cycle,” which are consistent with the expression data [8]. This example demonstrates how language models can retrieve valuable information to further investigate experimental findings.

4.3 Identifying Novel Functions in Difficult to Address Non-model Organisms

An annelid lineage underwent massive genome rearrangements during the transition from marine to non-marine ecosystems. Using transcriptomic data from several stress experiments in the earthworm Eisenia andrei, we were able to identify 12 stress-related genes originating during the massive genome rearrangements that may have been involved in the adaptation to life out of the sea. One of those genes could not be annotated based on homology, and was predicted to be involved in sodium–potassium cell trafficking by FANTASIA (GO:0006814, GO:0010248, GO:0071805, GO:0005391, G-O:0005524, GO:0008900, GO:0016887, GO:0046872, G-O:0005886, and GO:0016324) [14].

5 Notes

  1. The Critical Assessment of protein Function Annotation algorithms (CAFA) is a competition focused on function prediction (https://biofunctionprediction.org/people/). CAFA evaluates and monitors the performance of various function prediction methods serving as the authoritative source for assessing the usability of computational tools in this context. It provides datasets of proteins, while the corresponding functions are hidden from the predictors during the competition. Participating teams then develop methods to predict these functions. The most recent analyses from CAFA3 evaluation highlighted that there is still room for improvement [15] regardless of the methods used. Remarkably, the latest competition held in 2024, hosted on Kaggle, demonstrated that all but one of the 10 top-performing teams used protein language models (https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/cafa-5-protein-func tion-prediction). Analyses of the competition results are still underway.
  2. The objective is to evaluate performance at the model organism level using methods that have been benchmarked with the CAFA datasets. The methods selected in this benchmark [7] are deep machine–learning based (DeepGO [16] and Deep-GOPlus [17] versus language models, namely ProtT5 and SeqVec [1]), while profile-based methods [18] serve as control low-coverage, high-precision methods.
  3. We used the UniProtKB [19] annotations from model organisms as reference annotations. While this approach can be problematic due to the prevalence of automatic annotations, relying solely on annotations with “experimental evidence” would significantly reduce coverage. Therefore, we recommend following the CAFA guidelines regarding evidence

codes from the Gene Annotation Files (https://geneontology. org/docs/go-annotation-file-gaf-format-2.2/): EXP, IDA, IPI, IGI, IEP, TAS, or IC, which are considered “experimental” by the CAFA consortium. Performance was measured by estimating semantic similarity [20], information content [21], and precision in predicting a GO term, as developed in our previous work [7]. Additionally, we evaluated the ability to recover functional information using topGO [11] enrichment analysis on transcriptomics data to identify the most suitable tool for large-scale annotation. The code used for evaluating methods in model organisms [7], which led to the selection of the ProtT5 model, is available: https://github.com/CBBIO/ func-lm and can be modified to test the performance of novel models in a transfer learning context. To ensure that GO annotations assigned to protein domains from the PFAM (Protein Families Database) correspond to the relevant regions, GO terms were extracted from http://current.geneontology.org/ ontology/external2go/pfam2go.

  1. RAM usage is linearly dependent on proteome size. For instance, a human-sized proteome of ~20,000 proteins will require around 50 GB of RAM.

  2. The preprocessing step involves removing identical sequences using CD-HIT [22] and filtering out sequences longer than 5000 amino acids, due to a length constraint in the original ProtT5 model [10]. This process is fast, taking only a few seconds to complete. Since the pipeline was designed to annotate organisms with little presence in UniProtKB, overfitting was unlikely. However, as the databases continue to grow, the likelihood of finding sequences from these organisms, or very similar ones, will increase over time. Therefore, to minimize the risk of overfitting, it is recommended to remove sequences and embeddings from the look-up table that correspond to the target organism, if they are present. Additionally, sequences or embeddings from closely related species (e.g., mouse and human) should be excluded to minimize the “sister species” effect [7] in the predictions.

  3. The annotations for transfer come from proteins with annotated GO terms in the GOA database [23] (https:// geneontology.org/). The GOA2022 dataset, available in FAN-TASIA, contains 295,558 proteins. Embeddings (n and m) are generated from both the reference dataset and the query proteins, and the distances between them are calculated as follows:

\[ d (n, m) = \sqrt {\sum_ {i = 1} ^ {1 0 2 4} \left(n _ {i} - m _ {i}\right) ^ {2}} \tag {1} \]

Based on these distances, given a query protein, the \(k\) - nearest neighbor algorithm (k-NN) [24] is used to select the k closest hits from the GOA2022 reference dataset for GO term transfer (k is a tunable parameter). All annotations from the selected hits are transferred, rather than only the most frequent ones. In other words, the smaller the distance, the more similar the sequence embeddings (Fig. 1b, red arcs), making the transfer more reliable (Fig. 1b, blue line); for more details, see [1].

The computing time for GO annotation depends on the number of sequences in the proteome. Larger datasets require more time, but using GPUs with a local FANTASIA installation significantly speeds up the process. Even on CPUs, the computation takes only hours, with the longest test (~250,000 sequences) taking about \(^ { 2 4 \mathrm { ~ h ~ } }\) . When installing the pipeline manually (outside the provided singularity container), users can choose between CPUs (default) or GPUs, depending on their system. GPU usage accelerates execution without affecting memory requirements. Memory usage for GOPredSim-ProtT5 is proportional to the number of sequences and can be estimated with the formula:

\[ y = 4. 4 + 0. 0 2 1 x \tag {2} \]

where

\(y\) is the memory in gigabytes.

\(_ x\) is the number of sequences.

  1. Options for running the pipeline

Syntax: ./fantasia –infile protein.fasta [–outpath output_- path] [–allisoforms gene_isoform_conversion.txt] [– keepintermediate].

At a given point, FANTASIA may raise a warning if your system only has CPUs or if the GPU CUDA library version is different from 11.0. You can safely ignore this message, as the singularity container is prepared to run on CPUs.

Mandatory inputs are

  1. -i/–infile is the input protein file. For a comprehensive guide to sequence formats, refer https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ jdispatcher/docs/formats/.
  2. -h/–help: prints this Help.

The following are optional tasks:

  1. -o/–outpath: (Optional) Output directory. If not provided, the input file directory will be used.

  2. -a/–allisoforms: (Optional) Tab-separated conversion file specifying the correspondence between gene and isoform IDs for obtaining per-gene annotations using all isoforms.

-p/–prefix: (Optional) Prefix to add to output folders and files (e.g., the species code). If not provided, the input file name will be used.
