  24. Cover T, Hart P (1967) Nearest neighbor pattern classification 13:21–27

  25. Wang JZ, Du Z, Payattakool R et al (2007) A new method to measure the semantic similarity of GO terms. Bioinformatics 23:1274–1281

Advances in Language-Model-Informed Protein–Nucleic Acid Binding Site Prediction

Sumit Tarafder, Xinyu Wang, Rahmatullah Roche, and Debswapna Bhattacharya

Abstract

Interactions between proteins and nucleic acids are essential for understanding a wide range of cellular and evolutionary processes. Recent advancements in protein language models (pLMs), trained on vast protein sequence data, have revolutionized various predictive modeling tasks, offering unprecedented scalability and generalizability. Consequently, a number of computational methods have been developed in the recent past for protein–nucleic acid binding site prediction powered by pLMs. To this end, we recently developed the EquiPNAS method that integrates pLM embeddings with E(3) equivariant deep graph neural networks for enhancing accuracy and robustness in predicting protein–DNA and protein–RNA binding sites, thereby reducing the dependency on evolutionary information. Here we present an overview of the recent protein– nucleic acid binding site prediction methods, emphasizing the recent advances in harnessing the potential of pLMs, and provide a detailed description of the EquiPNAS methodology as well as the necessary materials and procedures for the computational prediction of protein–DNA and protein–RNA binding sites.

Key words Protein–DNA binding site prediction, Protein–RNA binding site prediction, Language models, Graph neural networks

1 Introduction

Proteins interact with nucleic acids, including DNA and RNA, to perform critical cellular functions such as regulating gene expression, facilitating DNA replication, and mediating cellular signaling pathways. The precise identification of these interaction or binding sites where proteins interact with nucleic acids is fundamental to understanding these biological processes [1–5]. This understanding has significant implications not only for basic biological research but also for practical applications in drug design and therapeutic interventions. While traditional laboratory methods for identifying these binding sites are effective [6, 7], they are often timeconsuming, expensive, and technically challenging, prompting

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_9, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

researchers to explore computational approaches for predicting these sites based on available data. Computational methods for predicting protein–nucleic acid binding sites can be categorized into two main types: sequence-based methods [8–15] and structure-aware methods [8, 16–20]. Sequence-based methods rely solely on the amino acid sequences of proteins to make predictions about binding affinities and interaction sites. On the other hand, structure-aware methods utilize 3D structural information derived from experiments or computational predictions, resulting in more precise binding site predictions. However, these approaches frequently depend on limited experimental data from structural databases such as the Protein Data Bank (PDB) [21], which may not provide comprehensive coverage for all protein– nucleic acid interactions. The introduction of AlphaFold2 [22] and AlphaFold3 [23] significantly expands this landscape by providing highly accurate structural models for a wide range of proteins and their interactions, including with nucleic acids. Additionally, the AlphaFold Database [24] offers a comprehensive repository of these predicted structures, allowing researchers to access valuable data that was previously inaccessible. These advancements alleviate the limitations associated with reliance on PDB and enhance the predictive capabilities of protein structure modeling [25] and binding site identification.

Currently, large language models (LLMs) have emerged as transformative tools in the field of artificial intelligence [26–30], possessing the capability to comprehend and generate human language through the analysis of extensive textual datasets. Their applications extend across numerous domains, encompassing natural language processing tasks such as sentiment analysis, language translation, and content generation [31–33], thereby reshaping industries ranging from healthcare [34–39] to finance [40– 44]. Notably, in the area of protein–nucleic acid binding site predictions, LLMs demonstrate significant advancements by leveraging their understanding of biological sequences and molecular interactions. The advent of protein language models (pLMs) [45– 50], which are analogous to natural language processing models but specifically trained on extensive datasets of protein sequences, has opened new avenues for predicting protein characteristics and functions. While the application of pLMs in predicting protein– nucleic acid binding sites is still in its formative stages, their potential for enhancing prediction accuracy is considerable [48, 50]. Research initiatives such as EquiPPIS [51] lay critical groundwork for advancements in predicting protein–RNA binding positions.

Although numerous methods have been developed for protein–nucleic acid binding site prediction, EquiPNAS [52] i s one of the first methods to leverage pLM embeddings for the effective prediction of both DNA and RNA binding protein

residues, consistently surpassing state-of-the-art techniques across an array of widely utilized benchmarking datasets for protein–DNA and protein–RNA binding site prediction tasks. This innovative approach employs a specific type of graph neural network known as E(3) Equivariant Graph Neural Network (EGNN) [53], which excels at processing the intricate three-dimensional (3D) nature of protein structures. By being sensitive to positional, rotational, and orientational transformations, this model maintains robustness and accuracy, even when utilizing protein structures predicted by AlphaFold2 [22] as opposed to those resolved through experimental methods (see Notes 1 and 2).

2 Method

2.1 Overview of Existing Protein– Nucleic Acid Binding Site Prediction Methods

Protein–nucleic acid binding site prediction is a well-studied problem with numerous methods available in the literature utilizing a combination of network architectures and features as listed in Table 1. Currently, the available protein–nucleic acid binding site prediction methods can be broadly divided into sequence-based and structure-based approaches. Sequence-based methods, such as NCBRPred [9], DNAPred [10], and RNABindRPlus [12], leverage the abundance of protein sequence data to predict nucleic

Table 1 A selection of state-of-the-art, deep learning–based frameworks for protein–nucleic acid binding site prediction, arranged in descending order by year of publication

Name Year Architecture pLM_Method Protein-DNA/RNA
EquiPNAS (Roche et al.) 2024 EGNN ESM2 Both
CLAPE (Liu et al.) 2024 CNN ProtBert Protein-DNA
ULDNA (Zhu et al.) 2024 LSTM-attention ESM2, ProtTrans Protein-DNA
EGPDI (Zheng et al.) 2024 EGNN+GCN-II ESM2, ProtTrans Protein-DNA
ESM-NBR (Zeng et al.) 2023 BiLSTM + MLP ESM2 Both
GLMsite (Song et al.) 2023 GVP-GNN ProtTrans Both
GraphSite (Yuan et al.) 2022 Graph transformer - Protein-DNA
bindEmbed21DL (Littmann et al.) 2021 CNN ProtT5 Both
GraphBind (Xia et al.) 2021 GNN - Both
NCBRPred (Zhang et al.) 2021 BiGRU - Both
NucleicNet (Lam et al.) 2019 ResNet - Protein-RNA
aaRNA (Li et al.) 2014 DenseNet - Protein-RNA
RNABindRPlus (Walia et al.) 2014 SVM - Protein-RNA

acid binding sites. These methods often utilize machine learning techniques, such as hidden Markov models and bidirectional Gated Recurrent Units (BiGRUs), to capture patterns from sequence data. Although these methods are widely applicable, their reliance solely on sequence information limits their predictive accuracy, as they miss critical spatial details of protein–nucleic acid interactions. Structure-based methods, including COACH-D [16], NucBind [8], and GraphBind [19], incorporate three-dimensional structural information, which enhances prediction accuracy. By utilizing structural templates or advanced computational models like graph neural networks, structure-based methods can better capture the intricate spatial patterns essential for identifying binding sites.

In recent years, hybrid methods have emerged, combining both sequence-based and structure-based approaches to improve prediction outcomes. For instance, DNABind [54] and NABind [55] integrate machine learning with template-based methods, while NucBind [8] combines predictions from both COACH-D [16] and SVMnuc [8]. Graph-based approaches, such as GraphSite [20] and GLMSite [56], have introduced new levels of sophistication by encoding secondary structure and spatial positions of atoms. While these hybrid methods outperform some of the stateof-the-art deep learning-only based frameworks, their hybrid approaches and high dependence on the quality of the templates make them susceptible to orphan proteins with low homology depths. However, with the recent surge in the availability of various families of protein language models such as ESM and ProtTrans, an array of deep learning–based binding site prediction methods have emerged as shown in Table 1. Methods such as EquiPNAS [52], CLAPE [57], ULDNA [58], EGPDI [4], ESM-NBR [59], and bindEmbed21DL [60], utilizing these language model embeddings, have surpassed those without them in terms of prediction accuracy, reducing the overall dependency on evolutionary information for binding site prediction task [61].

2.2 Significance of pLMs for Protein– Nucleic Acid Binding Site Prediction

While computational methods have advanced in predicting protein and nucleic acid binding sites, significant challenges persist, particularly in effectively utilizing protein-related data. Sequence-based approaches, which depend heavily on evolutionary information, often struggle with orphan proteins and encounter difficulties with intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs) and regions (IDRs) due to their unstable and dynamic structures [62]. Additionally, many existing techniques rely on manually curated features that demand domain-specific expertise and may miss critical biological insights [17].

In contrast, protein language models (pLMs) present notable advantages in protein–nucleic acid binding predictions. Using selfsupervised learning, pLMs can capture long-range dependencies and structural information from sequences without requiring

manual feature engineering [63]. These large language models, originally designed for natural language tasks like translation and question-answering, have evolved into biological language models due to the linguistic parallels between human and biological languages. Through transfer learning, these models excel at capturing the structure and function of biological molecules such as proteins, creating rich feature representation from large datasets of protein sequences, which proves to be significant for binding site prediction tasks (see Notes 3 and 4). This approach minimizes the reliance on evolutionary data and enables more accurate and generalizable embeddings [64].

However, in the context of binding site prediction, especially with nucleic acid ligands, the information derived from pLMs should account for the interactions between proteins and nucleic acids. A promising approach can be the potential incorporation of multimodal learning, where pLMs designed for DNA and RNA sequences [65, 66], or a platform like BioSeq-BLM, capable of analyzing DNA, RNA, and protein sequences using a range of biological language models [67] can be leveraged. This is especially valuable for improving prediction accuracy and efficiency when dealing with highly flexible molecules like RNA [61].

2.3 Overview of EquiPNAS Framework

The primary objective of the EquiPNAS method is to accurately predict nucleic acid (both DNA and RNA)-binding residues for a given input protein 3D structure as shown in Fig. 1. The prediction framework follows a structured methodology that consists of three key components. The first component is the graph representation of the protein, which involves constructing a graph in which nodes correspond to individual residues and edges denote interactions among them. This graph structure effectively captures the spatial and relational information inherent to the protein. The second component involves feature generation for both nodes (residues) and edges (interactions) within the graph. This includes the extraction of sequence-based and structure-based information, alongside the utilization of protein language model (pLM) embeddings derived from the ESM-2 model [47], thereby enriching the data set available for predictive purposes. The final component employs an E(3) equivariant graph neural network (EGNN) to process the graph representation and make binding site predictions. By leveraging the coordinate information from the input monomer in conjunction with the generated features, the EGNN performs graph node classification, estimating the probability of whether each residue acts as a binding site for the corresponding nucleic acid as annotated in Fig. 1. In the subsequent sections, each of these components will be explored in detail, illuminating their significance within the overarching EquiPNAS architecture and the enhanced predictive capabilities they provide.


.1 Experimental structure f (a) proteinRNA complex (PDB ID5HR7B)and (b) proteinDNA complex Experimental structure of ( ) protein RNA complex (PDB ID: 5HR7_B) and ( ) protein DNA complex (PDB ID: 4ZM2_B) with proteins shown in surface repr \(( p )\) ntation. Each residue of the protein structure is color coded according to its predicted probability score ( ) by Eq

2.3.1 Protein Graph Representation

Protein as a Graph

Interacting Residue Pairs

In the EquiPNAS framework, proteins are represented as graphs wherein each residue is depicted as a node. An edge is established between two nodes if the \(\mathrm { C } \alpha\) atoms of the corresponding residues are within a defined distance threshold, thus transforming the protein structure into an interactive network of components.

Residues are classified as interacting based on specific criteria. Notably, the distance between their \(\mathrm { C } \alpha\) atoms must be less than or equal to 14 Å for DNA-binding interactions and \(1 5 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }\) for RNA-binding interactions. Furthermore, the residues must be at least six positions apart within the protein sequence. This requirement ensures that only relevant interactions are captured, offering a focused representation of the protein’s binding potential. Through this graph-based approach, EquiPNAS adeptly captures both spatial relationships and interaction dynamics, facilitating accurate predictions of P-NA binding sites.

2.3.2 Feature Generation

Sequence-Based Features

The generation of sequence-based features is essential for the effective representation of proteins. Each amino acid is encoded using a simple binary (one-hot) encoding scheme for the 20 standard amino acids. Additionally, a Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM) is obtained through PSI-BLAST [68], with subsequent normalization of values to maintain consistent scaling. To augment

the feature set, a Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA) is generated using MMseqs2 [69] and subsequently refined by ColabFold [70] pipeline to provide distilled evolutionary information to the model. Finally, the language model embeddings have been extracted from ESM-2, a pre-trained protein language model (pLM) containing 15 billion parameters represented by a feature dimension of 5120 for each residue in the protein sequence.

Structure-Based Features

Structure-based features capture critical aspects of the protein’s topology and spatial orientation [71]. One-hot encoding is utilized for secondary structure and solvent accessibility, indicating the exposure of each residue to the surrounding environment. Furthermore, geometric relationships—such as bond angles and the positioning of residues in three-dimensional space—are meticulously recorded. The framework also tracks the number of residues in contact and computes their spatial positions relative to the protein’s center, thus refining the understanding of interaction dynamics.

Edge Features

For edge features, the framework calculates the ratio of sequence distance to three-dimensional (3D) distance for each pair of interacting residues. This ratio provides valuable insights into the spatial relationships between residues, significantly contributing to the overall predictive capability of the framework.

Coordinate Features

Finally, coordinate features leverage the 3D coordinates (x, y, z) of each residue’s Cα atom, effectively capturing the spatial arrangement within the protein structure. By integrating these diverse feature sets, EquiPNAS substantially enhances its ability to accurately predict protein–nucleic acid binding sites.

2.4 Architecture

The core architecture of EquiPNAS is constructed utilizing deep E (3)-equivariant graph neural networks (EGNNs), specifically tailored to predict protein–DNA and protein–RNA binding sites. These networks operate by leveraging features pertinent to the protein’s structure and its spatial coordinates, such as the positions of Cα atoms in three-dimensional space. The architecture comprises multiple layers of equivariant graph convolution layers (EGCL), which simultaneously update both the node features, and the coordinates of the protein based on the interactions (edges) among residues.

The process can be outlined as follows: (1) Node representation: Each node, representing a residue in the graph, encompasses features (such as the amino acid type) and coordinates (indicating its spatial position). These attributes undergo transformation across several layers of the network (2) Feature and coordinate updates: The layers update the node features and coordinates by utilizing edge information, including the distances between residues.

  1. Interaction learning: Within each layer, the model learns how residues interact based on the provided features, continuously refining its predictions to enhance accuracy.

The EGNN employs 12 layers of transformations, each characterized by 768 hidden dimensions. To mitigate the risk of overfitting, dropout regularization is applied at every layer, ensuring the model generalizes effectively beyond the training data. Ultimately, the model predicts the likelihood of each residue acting as a binding site for nucleic acids (DNA or RNA), condensing all acquired information into a singular predictive score. The training procedure of this model is conducted using ADAM optimizer [72] and cosine annealing [73] for dynamic adjustment of learning rates. The training process extends for up to 40 epochs on a high-performance NVIDIA Graphics Processing Unit (GPU), ensuring robust learning from the datasets. To assess the efficacy of the EGNN structure in enhancing prediction accuracy, baseline models devoid of equivariant updates were also trained for comparative purposes. This allows for a thorough evaluation of the model’s performance enhancements attributable to the incorporation of equivariant features.

3 Materials

To train and evaluate a machine learning–based model for protein– DNA binding site prediction, the GraphBind [19] study’s Train_573 (573 protein chains) and Test_129 (129 chains) datasets are used, along with GraphSite’s [20] Test_181 (181 chains). All datasets, curated from BioLiP [74] and filtered with CD-Hit [75] for non-redundancy, span different timeframes: Train_573 (pre-2016), Test_129 (2016–2018), and Test_181 (2018–2021), with varying counts of binding and non-binding residues. For protein–RNA binding site prediction, Train_495 (495 chains) and Test_117 (117 chains) from GraphBind are utilized, also processed from BioLiP and filtered with CD-Hit. These datasets contain a higher proportion of non-binding residues compared to binding residues, necessitating the use of specialized metrics for evaluation, as detailed below.

The predictive performance of EquiPNAS is compared with an array of existing methods for protein–nucleic acid binding site prediction. The structure-aware methods such as NucBind [8], DNABind [54], GraphBind [19], and GraphSite [20] represent the state-of-the-art methodologies in the literature, with Graph-Bind currently being the top-performing method for protein–RNA binding site prediction. Two key metrics Receiver Operating Characteristic–Area Under the Curve (ROC-AUC) and Precision-Recall–Area Under the Curve (PR-AUC) are used for this comparison, which provides a robust, threshold-independent assessment of

classification models, especially when dealing with imbalanced datasets. The ROC curve plots the true positive rate (TPR) against the false positive rate (FPR), whereas the PR curve plots precision (positive predictive value) against recall (TPR) at different thresholds. Area Under the Curve (AUC) represents the overall ability of the model to distinguish between positive and negative classes, where a ROC-AUC of 1.0 indicates perfect classification. PR-AUC, which is the area under the Precision-Recall curve, is particularly useful for evaluating models on imbalanced datasets where the positive class is rarer than the negative class (see Note 5).

4 Notes

  1. All the structure-aware methods, such as GraphSite, Graph-Bind, and EquiPNAS rely on 3D structures of proteins for binding site prediction. While the use of experimental structures shows better accuracy for all the competing methods, EquiPNAS experiences a minimal performance drop when using AlphaFold2 predicted structures highlighting its robustness and generalizability with predicted input structures.
  2. The accuracy of EquiPNAS predictions correlates with Alpha-Fold2’s self-estimated accuracy, measured by pLDDT with high confidence predictions leading to better ROC-AUC and PR-AUC scores. This suggests that the self-estimated accuracy of AlphaFold2 models can reliably predict the accuracy of EquiPNAS binding site predictions, especially for highly confident AlphaFold2 structures.
  3. EquiPNAS incorporates pretrained pLM embeddings from the ESM-2 model as essential sequence-based features, which play a more critical role in performance compared to evolutionary features like PSSM and MSA. Excluding pLM features results in a significant performance drop; in contrast, removing evolutionary features leads to only minor reductions in accuracy. This highlights that pLM embeddings play a major role in EquiPNAS’s improved performance, whereas evolutionary features contribute only modestly.
  4. The ESM-2 provides a range of pre-trained protein language models (pLMs) with sizes from 8 million to 15 billion parameters. EquiPNAS uses the largest model, esm2_t48_15- B_UR50D, with 15 billion parameters by default. Interestingly, EquiPNAS with the smallest model, esm2_t6_8- M_UR50D, performed the worst and the performance consistently improved as the number of parameters increased, confirming that the largest model, esm2_t48_15B_UR50D, yields the best results for both protein–DNA and protein–

RNA binding site predictions, showing the significance of large LLMs in protein bioinformatics.

  1. While the ROC-AUC achieved by EquiPNAS demonstrates high accuracy, the PR-AUC results indicate poorer performance overall in terms of binding site prediction. A model with high ROC-AUC but low PR-AUC suggests that although it is effective at distinguishing between positive and negative classes, it struggles with precision, leading to a higher number of false positives, in this case, binding residue prediction. This discrepancy highlights the need for further optimization in refining the precision of EquiPNAS, especially in handling imbalanced datasets, to ensure more reliable predictions.

Acknowledgments

This work was partially supported by the National Institute of General Medical Sciences (R35GM138146 to D.B.) and the National Science Foundation (DBI2208679 to D.B.). The authors gratefully acknowledge high-performance computing resources and technical support from the National AI Research Resource Pilot award (NAIRR240093 to D.B.).

Practical Applications of Language Models in Protein Sorting Prediction: SignalP 6.0, DeepLoc 2.1, and DeepLocPro 1.0

Henrik Nielsen

Abstract

Protein sorting prediction, or protein subcellular location prediction, is an important task in bioinformatics. It is also a task where protein language models have proven their value in practice. SignalP 6.0 (for prediction of signal peptides), DeepLoc 2.1 (for prediction of subcellular location and membrane association in eukaryotes), and DeepLocPro 1.0 (for prediction of subcellular location in prokaryotes) are all based on large pretrained protein language models. This has led to substantial improvements over previous methods, especially for rare classes where available labeled data are limited.

Key words Protein sorting, Protein subcellular locations, Protein language models, Signal peptides

1 Introduction

Cells have a number of distinct compartments, each with their characteristic set of proteins. Eukaryotic cells have several membrane-bound organelles such as the nucleus, the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), and the mitochondria [1]. Prokaryotes generally do not have organelles (although there are exceptions, such as the thylakoids in cyanobacteria), but still, proteins can belong to the cytoplasm, the plasma membrane, the cell wall, or the extracellular environment. For diderm (i.e., most Gram-negative) bacteria, there are two more possible locations, namely periplasm and outer membrane. So how does the cell know where to put which proteins? The Nobel Prize in physiology or medicine was in 1999 awarded to Gu¨nter Blobel “for the discovery that proteins have intrinsic signals that govern their transport and localization in the cell” [2].

But how do we know which proteins belong to which compartments? Since the experimental determination of subcellular locations of proteins is slow and costly compared to DNA sequencing, there is a great interest in protein sorting prediction, i.e., the

inference of subcellular locations based on sequences. This has been a target in bioinformatics since the beginning of the field, see Ref. [3] for a historical review. Many machine learning techniques have been employed in this task, and pretrained protein language models are among them. The advantage of this technique is that the pretrained model has “seen” many examples of proteins and their intrinsic sorting signals and therefore is able to generalize from a relatively small set of experimentally labeled examples in the downstream task.

In 2019, one of the early published applications of protein language models used subcellular location prediction in eukaryotic proteins (ten categories) as one of its two downstream tasks [4]. This was done with the ELMo architecture [5] based on long short-term memory recurrent neural networks. The more computationally efficient transformer architecture [6] was used by the same group 2 years later on the same task [7], comparing several different pretrained protein language models and reporting the best performance for the 3B-parameters ProtT5 [8].

1.1 Signal Peptides and SignalP

A signal peptide (SP) is the N-terminal part of a protein that is targeted to the secretory pathway in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes (see, however, Note 1). In eukaryotes, a protein with an SP will be targeted to the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane and be cotranslationally translocated across the membrane. In prokaryotes, translocation takes place across the cytoplasmic membrane (inner membrane in diderm bacteria), and the process can happen during or after translation.

SPs are generally described as having three regions: an N-terminal n-region of variable length characterized by positive charge, a central h-region of typically 7–15 hydrophobic residues, and a C-terminal c-region of typically 3–7 polar residues. Positions \(- 1\) and \(^ { - 3 }\) relative to the cleavage site are occupied by small uncharged residues, most often Alanine. SPs of Gram-positive bacteria tend to be longer than those of Gram-negative bacteria, which in turn tend to be longer than eukaryotic SPs [9, 10].

SignalP was in 1995 the first machine learning-based SP prediction method to be made available as a web server [11]. It has gone through six major versions—see Ref. [12] for a historical account—and has gained a very wide usage. The papers about SignalP have been cited more than 30,000 times in total according to Google Scholar. While SignalP 5.0 from 2019 [13] was the first version to be based on deep learning, version 6.0 from 2022 [14] is the first version to be based on a pretrained protein language model, ProtBERT [8].

Conventionally, we think of prokaryotes as being simpler than eukaryotes, but regarding SPs, the opposite is the case. Prokaryotes (both bacteria and archaea) have two types of translocon (Sec and Tat) and three types of signal peptidase (I, II, and III). This gives

rise to five types of SPs (not six, since signal peptidase III substrates are only transported through the Sec translocon).

Prokaryotic lipoproteins have SPs that are cleaved by signal peptidase II [15]. They do not have a standard c-region but instead a motif termed a lipobox and a cysteine right after the cleavage site as the first residue of the mature protein. During cleavage, a lipid group is covalently attached to this cysteine, anchoring the protein to the membrane.

Those SPs that direct their proteins toward the Tat translocon have a special motif at the border between the n- and h-regions containing two consecutive arginines [16]. This is how the Tat translocon got its name—it is short for twin arginine translocation. It differs from the Sec translocon by being able to transport proteins that are already folded into their native 3D structure. Tat SPs differ from Sec SPs not only by the presence of the motif, they also tend to be longer and less hydrophobic [17].

Finally, signal peptidase III cleaves some very short SPs that do not contain an h-region; instead there is a hydrophobic region after the cleavage site [18]. Proteins cleaved by signal peptidase III typically belong to type IV pili and are always transported by the Sec translocon.

Previous versions of SignalP were only able to predict Sec/SPI SPs (the only type found at the eukaryotic ER membrane), and in order to predict SPII and Tat substrates, users had to consult other predictors such as LipoP [19] and TatP [20]. SignalP 5.0 changed this by including prediction of Sec/SPII and Tat/SPI SPs, but it could not predict Tat/SPII or Sec/SPIII SPs. Experimentally known examples of these two are extremely rare in UniProt [21] or PROSITE [22]—as a matter of fact, we failed to find even one example of Tat/SPII by our usual data retrieval protocol. When making the dataset for SignalP 6.0, we discovered that the PRO-SITE pattern for the Tat motif (PS51318) had a postprocessing rule that prevented simultaneous matching to the lipoprotein motif (PS51257), which we regarded as an error, since there is evidence for the existence of such proteins [23, 24]. By combining the two motifs and relabeling some proteins, we built a set of 36 Tat/SPII sequences.

This is an extremely small set for training one of the classes in a deep learning method, but we found that with a pretrained language model, it worked. We also tried to retrain the SignalP 5.0 architecture on the SignalP 6.0 dataset, and while it had comparable performances on the large classes of SPs, it failed to predict the rare classes Tat/SPII (33 examples after homology partitioning) and Sec/SPIII (70 examples after homology partitioning), see Fig. 2a of the SignalP 6.0 paper [14]. The ability to predict rare classes was the main advantage of the language model technology in SignalP 6.0, but there were two other substantial improvements.

The first additional improvement was better performance for sequences with low similarity to the training set. The partitioning of the SignalP 6.0 data to avoid leakage between training, validation, and test sets was done with the algorithm that later became known as GraphPart [25]. In contrast to the homology reduction algorithms of Hobohm et al. [26] that removes sequences until no pair with a similarity above the threshold remains in the data set, GraphPart strives to keep as many data points as possible while ensuring that no pair of sequences between partitions have similarity above the threshold. In many cases (including SignalP 6.0), this cannot be done without removing some data points. We used the removed sequences to estimate the how the performance of the finished SignalP 6.0 depended on the degree of similarity to the training set, and found that while the retrained SignalP 5.0 performed on par with SignalP 6.0 at high similarity values, it was substantially worse at similarity values below \(6 0 \%\) (see Fig. 2c of the SignalP 6.0 paper), showing that the pretrained language model improves generalization to distantly related sequences.

The other additional improvement was independence of the organism group label: Ever since SignalP 1.0, the user has been asked to specify which group of organisms the sequences came from; eukaryotes, Gram-positive bacteria, Gram-negative bacteria, or (from version 5.0) archaea. In SignalP 1–4, this was implemented by keeping the data sets for each group separate, while SignalP 5.0 was trained on the combined data set with an extra input informing the network about the organism group. In SignalP 6.0, we also initially had such an extra input, but when we made an experiment where the group labels were randomly shuffled, we found that SignalP 6.0 performance values were unaffected, while performance of the retrained SignalP 5.0 suffered (see Supplementary Fig. 4 of the SignalP 6.0 paper). Apparently, the pretrained language model is able to infer the peculiarities of each organism group directly from the sequence. This has two advantages: SignalP 6.0 can now be applied to sequences of unknown origin, and it can be applied to groups of bacteria that are not easily classified into the Gram-positive/Gram-negative divide (such as genus Deinococcus that stain Gram-positive even though they are diderm, class Mollicutes that stain Gram-negative even though they are monoderm, and class Negativicutes which are diderm and Gram-negative even though they belong to phylum Bacillota, f.k.a. Firmicutes).

One new feature in SignalP 6.0 that is not attributable to the language model is the region assignment. SignalP 6.0 assigns n-, h-, and c-regions in predicted SPs by training the conditional random field (CRF) layer to maximize the differences in amino acid composition between the regions (the architecture of the CRF layer is shown in Supplementary Fig. 8 of the SignalP 6.0 paper). Region predictions can be useful for investigating properties of groups of SPs. As an example, we used SignalP 6.0 on a library of synthetic

1.2 Eukaryotic Subcellular Locations and DeepLoc

SPs that were found to be either functional or non-functional in Bacillus subtilis [27]. In the original work, the authors did not find any discriminating properties between the two groups using traditional sequence analysis, but region predictions show a significant difference in n-region net charge and hydrophobicity between the groups (see Supplementary Fig. 5 of the SignalP 6.0 paper), revealing possible factors that contribute to in vivo functionality.

The presence or absence of a SP is not the whole story about the localization of a protein. It is important to stress that the presence of a SP does not necessarily mean that the protein is secreted to the extracellular environment, it only means that it enters the secretory pathway. In all kinds of organisms, the protein could have one or more transmembrane helices downstream of the SP and therefore be retained in the membrane [28]. In eukaryotes, the protein could also be retained in one of the compartments that belong to the secretory pathway: the ER, the Golgi apparatus, or the lysosome/ vacuole [29]; or it could be anchored to the outer face of the cytoplasmic membrane by a glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) group [30].

A computational method that can predict the location of a protein based on its amino acid sequence is therefore highly desirable. Many such methods have been published, starting with PSORT in 1992 [31]. Some of the methods use homology information, i.e., they search a database of annotated proteins using BLAST or another sequence similarity search software and use the annotations of the found hits to infer the location of the query sequence. This inference can be direct (using the location of the best hit as the predicted location) or indirect (using some annotation of the homologues found as input to a machine learning method). The annotations used in indirect homology inference can be, e.g., keywords or functional descriptions [32], titles and/or abstracts of literature references [33], or Gene Ontology terms [34, 35].

Homology-based methods, or hybrid methods containing homology-based components, often present the best measured performances, but the performance depends critically on the source of the query protein. Organisms that have been subject to intense research will naturally tend to have more high-quality annotations, so proteins from those and their close relatives will find more close homologues with richer annotations from which to make predictions, while predictions for less well-studied organisms will suffer from lack of annotations of close homologues. This is typically not taken into consideration when reporting the predictive performances of such methods. Another drawback is that such methods will not be able to distinguish between very closely related proteins that differ in the presence or absence of a sorting signal, and they

will not be able to predict the effects of small mutations that destroy or create a sorting signal.

DeepLoc 1.0, launched in 2017 [36], was a ten-category subcellular location predictor based on deep learning (convolutional and recurrent neural networks), but not language models. The input sequences were encoded either by the BLOSUM62 substitution matrix or by profiles. Profile encoding, where each position in an input sequence is represented by a vector of the frequencies of the 20 amino acids at that position in a multiple alignment, has been used extensively in protein sequence-based prediction methods for many years, beginning with secondary structure prediction in 1993 [37]. It should be stressed that the use of profiles does not make a method homology-based, since the annotations of the matching sequences are not used. We found that the BLOSUM62 encoding gave an accuracy four percentage points lower than profile encoding, but still better than the benchmark methods (which included homology-based methods). Profile encoding is, however, computationally heavy; in DeepLoc 1.0 we found that it was taking around ten times as long as the rest of the prediction, even though we used precomputed Pfam [38] profiles to speed up the calculation. Consequently, the web server of DeepLoc 1.0 allowed only 50 sequences per submission for profile encoding, where 500 sequences were allowed for BLOSUM62.

DeepLoc 2.0 from 2022 [39] was based on transformer language models (ESM1b [40] and ProtT5 [8]), which improved the performance, especially for the small categories where prediction is particularly hard (such as ER, lysosome/vacuole, Golgi, and peroxisome). Additionally, the language model encoding made profiles obsolete, allowing for a faster prediction than the profile mode. Two added features in DeepLoc 2.0 were multiple locations and sorting signal prediction.

Several proteins function in more than one subcellular location. When training DeepLoc 1.0, we experimented with adding an 11th category for “Cytoplasm+Nucleus” (the by far most abundant category of multi-localized proteins) but it turned out to be nearly impossible to predict, so in the end we discarded all multi-localized proteins from the data set. To remedy this situation, DeepLoc 2.0 is trained as a multi-label method. The average number of labels per protein in the DeepLoc 2.0 training data is 1.27, implying that a substantial minority of proteins are muti-localized.

The other added feature was multi-label prediction of sorting signals. We included nine sorting signals: SPs; transmembrane helices; transit peptides for mitochondria, chloroplasts, and chloroplast thylakoids; nuclear localization and export signals; peroxisomal targeting signals; and GPI anchor propeptides. It should be stressed that there is not necessarily a 1:1 correspondence between localizations and sorting signals. For example, some proteins are imported into mitochondria without a transit peptide [41], and for

peroxisomal proteins we only predict the C-terminal PTS1 signal, not the N-terminal PTS2 [42].

For DeepLoc 2.0, we did not do nested cross-validation (as for SignalP 6.0) but just plain five-fold cross-validation on the UniProt-derived data set. In addition, we had an external test set consisting of sequences from the Human Protein Atlas (HPA) [43]. Note that the HPA test set had fewer classes than the UniProt set it did not include extracellular (due to the experimental procedure), plastid (because it is a human dataset), or peroxisome and lysosome/vacuole (because there were too few examples with good confidence and low similarity to the UniProt dataset).

Most of the locations predicted by DeepLoc should actually be regarded as two locations, because proteins can be inside an organelle or associated with its membrane. For example, the ER category comprises both ER lumen and ER membrane proteins, which are rather different with respect to the sorting process and the involved sorting (retention) signals. In DeepLoc 1.0, this was addressed by adding a binary prediction of whether each query protein was membrane-bound or soluble. In DeepLoc 2.0, this prediction was missing.

This was the reason for updating DeepLoc to version 2.1 in 2024 [44]. Not only did we want to reinstate the “membrane or soluble” prediction, we also wanted to make it more fine-grained by distinguishing between transmembrane proteins, peripheral membrane proteins, and lipid-anchored membrane proteins. Since there were a substantial number of proteins in the data set belonging to two of these categories (e.g., soluble and peripheral), we decided to make this classification multilabel as well. The membrane type prediction was made with the same architecture as DeepLoc 2.0, using ESM1b and ProtT5. At the time, ESM2 had been published [45], so we also tried two versions of ESM2, one with \(6 5 0 \mathrm { ~ M ~ }\) parameters (like ESM1b) and the other with 15B parameters. The improvements over ESM1b and ProtT5 were at best marginal for ESM2-650 M (and ESM2-15B would have been too computationally heavy to deploy in the web server), so we decided to stick with ESM1b and ProtT5. In this way, only a single per-position representation for a query protein needs to be computed for the subcellular location and membrane protein type prediction tasks.

The data for DeepLoc 2.1 were derived from UniProt. We split the data set into five partitions using GraphPart [25] and set aside one partition as a fixed test set, using four-fold cross-validation on the rest.

1.3 Prokaryotic Subcellular Locations and DeepLocPro

Also in prokaryotes, the presence of a SP does not necessarily mean that the protein is secreted. In diderm bacteria, the protein could be retained in the periplasm, or be inserted into the outer membrane as a \(\beta\) -barrel transmembrane protein [46]. In cyanobacteria, there is also the possibility that the protein could be imported into the

thylakoids [47]. In Gram-positive bacteria and archaea, the protein could be attached to the cell wall [48]. In addition, there is the aforementioned possibility of transmembrane helices downstream in the sequence. It was reported in 1994 that SPs were rarely found in bacterial cytoplasmic membrane proteins [49], but it is uncertain whether that result holds today. On the other hand, the absence of a SP does not necessarily mean that the protein is cytoplasmic. There are several pathways for SP-independent secretion, especially in Gram-negative bacteria where types I, III, IV, and VI secretion happen without a SP [50].

For these reasons, multi-category protein sorting prediction is also of great interest in prokaryotes (see Ref. [51] for an overview). The very first published version of PSORT in 1991 was intended for Gram-negative bacteria [52]. Later, the development of PSORT forked, with WoLF PSORT from 2007 for eukaryotic sequences [53] and PSORTb from 2003 for prokaryotic sequences [54]. PSORTb version 3.0 from 2010 [55] is a homology-based method which is still regarded as state-of-the-art. Although some newer methods have been published, implementations are typically not functional or no longer available to the public [56–59]. From the PSORTb group came in 2020 PSORTm [60], a version of PSORTb 3.0 that is optimized for metagenomic data. However, it does not represent a retraining of the underlying method, so it is not expected to perform better on full-length proteins. Recently, two language-model-based methods have been described in the literature [61, 62], but they have not been made available to the scientific community.

Accordingly, we perceived a need for a new non-homologybased method using pretrained protein language models. That method is DeepLocPro 1.0 [63]. Unlike DeepLoc 2.1, DeepLoc-Pro 1.0 is a single-label multiclass method. We considered making a multi-label method but found that the amount of multi-localized proteins is much lower than in eukaryotes (only \(3 \%\) of the labeled data set had more than one label), so in the end, we decided to ignore this minority of proteins.

Since the number of membranes is so low in prokaryotes (one in archaea and monoderm bacteria, two in diderm bacteria, and three in cyanobacteria), there is not the same need for a separate membrane association prediction as in eukaryotes. Still, a user might want to know whether a prokaryotic membrane protein is integral, peripheral, or lipid anchored, but that is a feature to be added in future versions of DeepLocPro. The same is true for thylakoid membrane and lumen proteins, which are not included in this version.

Benchmarking DeepLocPro against PSORTb 3.0 was complicated, because PSORTb 3.0 includes a BLAST-based homology module with direct transfer of annotation. Since the annotation database used in PSORTb 3.0 is derived from UniProt exactly like

our training and test sets, this method could reach an artificially high performance value simply by retrieving the annotations of exactly the same sequences that we used for testing. The solution was to empty the BLAST database used in our installation of PSORTb 3.0 in order to disable the homology module, see details in the supplementary materials of the DeepLocPro paper.

2 Materials

2.1 Amino Acid Sequences

SignalP, DeepLoc, and DeepLocPro require amino acid sequences in FASTA format. They will not produce useful predictions from nucleotide sequences (see Note 2). For DeepLoc and DeepLocPro, sequences should be complete, i.e., not fragments, since many sorting signals are located in the N- or C-terminal regions. SignalP only considers the first 70 residues of each input sequence, so it is important that this part of the sequence is complete and has the correct start codon (see Note 3).

2.2 Web Servers

The web servers are found here:

• https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/SignalP-6.0/
• https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/DeepLoc-2.1/
• https://services.healthtech.dtu.dk/services/DeepLocPro-1.0/

In addition, SignalP 6.0 and DeepLocPro 1.0 have mirror sites at the company BioLib:

• https://dtu.biolib.com/SignalP-6
• https://ku.biolib.com/DeepLocPro

which can be used if the server load is high at the dtu.dk site. DeepLoc 2.1 does not have a mirror site. The web servers grant free access to all users, including commercial users.

2.3 Downloadable Packages

If you want to predict on large datasets, we recommend downloading the software packages for command line use on your own hardware. The packages are free for academic institutions, while there is a license fee for commercial users. For SignalP 6.0 and DeepLoc 2.1, academic users should click “Downloads” at the web server pages, choose the latest version, and fill out the form to accept the license. For DeepLocPro, academic users should go to https://github.com/Jaimomar99/deeplocpro. Commercial users are asked to contact health-software@dtu.dk.

In the case of SignalP 6.0, there is a choice between “Fast” and “Slow” versions, see “model mode” in Subheading 3.1.1. The “Slow” version takes six times as long as to run, and the download is also six times as large as the “Fast” version. If you want the

opportunity to choose between the two versions via a command line option, you need to download both packages.

Note that the programs, being based on large pretrained language models, run much faster on a system with a CUDAcompatible GPU. For SignalP 6.0, there is a script included in the package to convert the model files into GPU versions. For DeepLoc 2.1 and DeepLocPro 1.0, you can make the predictions run on GPU with the “-d cuda” command line option.

3 Methods

The interface to the three web servers is quite similar—in the “Submission” tab, you can paste your sequence(s) in the window or upload a file with the sequences, see an example in Fig. 1. Then, there are a few options and a “Submit” button. After you click the “Submit” button, you will be informed about whether your job is queued or actively being processed. On that page, there is an option to enter your email address to be informed when the job finishes. In the following, the options and the output will be explained in detail.

Submission Instructions Data Article abstract FAQ Version history Portable Downloads

Submit data

Sequence submission: paste the sequence(s) and/or upload a local file

Protein sequences should be not less than 10 amino acids. The maximum number of proteins is 1000.

The long output format might timeout for more than 100 entries.

Mirror Use SignalP 6.0 on BioLib if this server is heavily loaded.

For example proteins Click here

Slow

The slow mode takes 6x longer to compute. Use when accurate region borders are needed.

Fg. 1 The submission tab f he SignalP .server pe

3.1 Using SignalP 6.0

On the web server, paste or upload your sequences and click “Submit” (see Fig. 1). On the command line, run “signalp6 -ff input.fasta -od output-directory.”

3.1.1 Options

Organism As described in Subheading 1.1, SignalP 6.0 is independent of the organism group label, in the sense that we did not see any deterioration in performance when we randomized the group labels. When SignalP 6.0 still offers the user a choice between “Eukarya” (eukaryotes) and “Other,” it is solely to suppress false predictions of SPs that are not of the Sec/SPI type in eukaryotes (see, however, Note 4). If the origin of the sequences is unknown, choose “Other.” In the command line interface, the group is indicated by “-org eukarya” or “-org other.”

Output Format The “Long” output format supplies, for each sequence, a plot showing the probabilities of each region for each position in the sequence (see below). If you submit a large number of sequences, use the “Short” format without figures instead. In the command line interface, the output format is given by the “-fmt” option which can take the values “txt,” “png,” “eps,” “all,” or “none.” The last two values correspond to “Long” and “Short” format, while the three first are in-between possibilities (making all textual output, or additionally making one of the plot types).

Model Mode This can be “Fast” and “Slow.” SignalP 6.0 is trained with three-fold nested cross-validation, i.e., the dataset has been divided into three partitions (without any sequence being \({ > } 3 0 \%\) identical to any sequence in the other two partitions), and each partition has been used as a test set while the other two partitions have been used as training and validation in both combinations. In total, that gives six trained networks. Since we used finetuning, the ProtBERT models are not identical between these six. The “Slow” mode runs all six models sequentially and averages the output probabilities. The “Fast” mode is based on so-called model distillation, where one network is trained to emulate the output of the “Slow” mode (see Supplementary Note 1 in the SignalP 6.0 publication). Thus, the “Fast” mode takes only one sixth of the time taken by the “Slow” mode. The discrimination performance between non-SPs and the five SP types is very close to the original (see Supplementary Table 12 in the SignalP 6.0 publication), but the cleavage site performance will suffer. If precise cleavage sites and region borders are important, use the “Slow” mode.

3.1.2 Output

When using the “Long” output format, there will be a table and a plot for each sequence in the input. The table shows the per-sequence probabilities for the five SP types and “Other” (non-SP). For predicted SPs, the cleavage site is also indicated. The plot (see Figs. 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) shows the region probabilities


SignalP 6.0 prediction: Sp_P94691_CYC3B_DESAF Basic cytochrome c3 Desulfocurvibacter africanus
Fi.2 An example a plot from SignalP .0, showing a predicted Sec/SPl signal peptide with h- and An


SignalP 6.0 prediction: sp_POA912_PAL_ECOLI Peptidoglycan-associated lipoprotein Escherichia coli
Fig. 3 An example of a plot from SignalP 6.0, showing a predicted Sec/SPll signal peptide with n- and An example of a plot from Sig


SignalP 6.0 prediction: sp_P46923_TORZ_ECOLI Trimethylamine-N-oxide reductase 2 Escherichia coli
Fi.4 An example a plot from SignalP .0, showing a predictedTat/SPl signal peptide with - h- and An example of a plot from SignalP 6.


Fi. 5An example f a plot from SignalP 6.0 showing a predicted Tat/SP Tat-lipo signal peptide, avi An example of a plot from SignalP 6.0, showing a


Fig. 6 An example of a plot from SignalP 6.0, showing a predicted Sec/SPll signal peptide with consensus An example of a plot from SignalP 6.0, showinafter

for each position in the sequence (truncated to 70 residues). In the bottom of the plot, the sequence is indicated, and above the sequence is the assignment of the regions. For SPI-cleaved SPs (Fig. 2), there are n-, h-, and c-regions assigned (see Note 5). The cleavage site is per definition the last position of the c-region and is marked by a vertical dashed line. Note that the cleavage site does not have its own state in the CRF layer, so the cleavage site probability is defined as the marginal probability of “c-region” in the cleavage position (see Note 6).

SPII-cleaved lipoprotein SPs have no c-region, but their n- and h-regions are indicated (Fig. 3). In addition, the lipid-modified cysteine is marked by a peak right after the cleavage site (see Note 7). Tat/SPI SPs have an annotated twin-arginine motif (Fig. 4) within their n-region. Note the long h-region, which is characteristic of Tat SPs. An example of the rare Tat/SPII type is seen in Fig. 5, showing both the twin-arginine motif and the lipidmodified cysteine. Finally, the short Sec/SPIII prepilin SPs are

not divided into regions, but after their cleavage site, there is a consensus region and a hydrophobic region (see Fig. 6).

The full output from SignalP 6, attained via the “Long” output format or the “-fmt all” option, comprises five files pertaining to the whole prediction plus three files per sequence. The five files available per prediction are:

• JSON Summary: used for defining the output that the user sees on the webpage.
• Prediction summary: a tab-delimited file with one line per sequence, containing the probabilities for the five SP types and “Other” (non-SP). For predicted SPs, the cleavage site is also indicated.
• Processed entries fasta: a FASTA sequence file containing the sequences of those proteins that had predicted SPs, with the SP removed. This can be useful for certain types of downstream analysis.
• Processed entries gff3: a GFF3 (generic feature format version 3 [64]) file showing the length of each predicted SP.
• Region predictions gff3: a GFF3 file showing the start and end positions of each region in each predicted SP.

And the three files available for each sequence are:

• PNG: the plot shown on the webpage in Portable Network Graphics format.
• EPS: the plot in Encapsulated PostScript, a text-based vector graphics format.
• Tabular: the data for the plot in a tab-delimited format with one line per position. It contains the probabilities for each region of each SP type.

Additionally, the web server gives an option for downloading all of these files as a ZIP archive.

3.2 Using DeepLoc

2.1

On the web server, paste or upload your sequences and click “Submit.” On the command line, run “deeploc2 -f input.fasta -o output-directory.”

3.2.1 Options

Model This can be “High-quality (Slow)” or “High-throughput (Fast).” These options select the ProtT5 (3B parameters) or ESM1b (650M parameters) pretrained models, respectively. The “Slow” model takes approximately two to four times as long as the “Fast” model (see Supplementary Table 1 of the DeepLoc 2.0 and 2.1 papers [39, 44]). In the command line interface, the model is selected by “-m Accurate” or “-m Fast.”

Output Format The “Long” output format supplies, for each sequence, a logo-like attention plot (see below). If you submit a large number of sequences, use the “Short” format without figures instead. In the command line interface, plots are requested by the “-p” option.

3.2.2 Output

If the “Long” output is chosen, there will, for each sequence, be a header, two tables, and a figure. The header shows predicted localizations, membrane association, and sorting signals. Since DeepLoc 2.1 is a multi-label method, there can be more than one label predicted in each of these categories. “Localizations” and “membrane association” will always have at least one label, while “signals” can be empty.

Figure 7 shows the header and the tables for the Thy-1 antigen from rat, which is correctly predicted to localize to the cell membrane as a lipid-anchored protein. However, the prediction is not perfect, since there are no predicted signals, while there should have been a SP and a GPI anchor. The chosen labels in the “Localization” and “Membrane association” categories are marked by a green background. The intensity of the green color reflects the difference between the probability score and its associated threshold. The higher the probability difference, the darker the green color. The threshold values can be displayed by clicking the button labeled “Probability thresholds” at the top of the output page.

If none of the labels in “Localization” or “Membrane association” scores above its threshold, the label that is closest to its threshold is picked as the prediction. In that case, there will be no green background, and the confidence in the prediction is low.

Below the tables, there is a logo-like attention plot showing, using the letters of the input sequence, which parts of the sequence were most important for the localization prediction (see Fig. 8). The output probabilities are computed as weighted sums of scores pertaining to every position in the sequence, and these weights are

sp_P01830_THY1_RAT

Predicted localizations: Cell membrane

Predicted membrane association: Lipid anchor

Predicted signals:

Localization Cytoplasm Nucleus Extracellular Cell membrane Mitochondrion Plastid Endoplasmic reticulum Lysosome/ Vacuole Golgi apparatus Peroxisome
Probability 0.1207 0.0555 0.4058 0.8029 0.1195 0.0031 0.3417 0.1773 0.2141 0.0803
Membrane association Peripheral Transmembrane Lipid anchor Soluble
Probability 0.1480 0.4440 0.9490 0.3160

Fig. 7 A header and two tables showing DeepLoc 2.1 prediction results for the UniProt sequence P01830/ A he


Fig. 8 Example of the logo-like attention plot from DeepLoc .1, showing results or the UniProt sequence Example of

called alpha values or attention scores. In the model, the alpha values per definition sum to 1 over the entire sequence, so they are rather small numbers. In the plot, they are rescaled so that the highest scoring position in the sequence is set to 1, making it easier to compare attention plots from sequences of varying lengths.

In the attention plot for Thy-1 from rat (Fig. 8), it is evident that two regions are important; from the N-terminus (1) to approximately 20, and from approximately 125 to the C-terminus (161). These correspond well to the experimentally known SP (1–19) and propeptide (131–161), with the ω-site of the lipid anchor being the cysteine in position 130. So, even though no signals were predicted, it is clear that DeepLoc 2.1 has in some sense captured these two signals.

Even if there are predicted signals, DeepLoc 2.1 will only predict their presence, not their exact positions, but the attention plot can help point out where the signals are. Note, however, that there is a general tendency to have higher attention in the N- and C-termini, so a high attention value in the first or last handful of residues is not necessarily a sorting signal.

For cleaved signals, there are alternative specialized predictors which will predict the exact positions of their cleavage sites: SignalP 6.0 for SPs, TargetP 2.0 for N-terminal mitochondrial, plastid and thylakoid transit peptides [65], and NetGPI 1.1 for C-terminal GPI anchor propeptides [66]. (SignalP 6.0 and NetGPI 1.1 predict the SP and GPI anchor of rat Thy-1 correctly.) For transmembrane helices, there are two language model-based methods available, DeepTMHMM [67] and TMbed [68]. Regarding DeepLoc 2.1’s prediction of transmembrane helices, see Note 8.

The remaining three signals—nuclear localization signals (NLS), nuclear export signals (NES), and C-terminal peroxisomal targeting signals (PTS1)—are not cleaved, and not structurally defined like transmembrane helices, so it is hard to define their

limits in the sequences. We used NLSdb [69] for NLS and NES definitions, and “motif” features of UniProt for defining PTS1 sequences. In a quantitative comparison of the correlation between attention values and annotated sorting signals (Table 4 of the DeepLoc 2.0 paper), the PTS1 signal was found as efficiently as SPs, while NLS and NES were much harder to find.

Note that the three predictions of DeepLoc 2.1 (localization, membrane association, and sorting signal) are independently trained, even though they are made by the same language models. Since they do not depend on each other, they can sometimes disagree. This can be the case if one of the two soluble-only localizations (“cytoplasm” or “extracellular”) is predicted alongside a membrane association other than “soluble,” or if the “cell membrane” localization is predicted together with “soluble.” In such cases, the results should be taken with caution. The sorting signal prediction can also disagree with the other two predictions. For example, we have seen cases of “peroxisomal targeting signal” together with “cytoplasm.” Here, it is advisable to check the performances of the two categories in the DeepLoc 2.0 paper; “peroxisomal targeting signal” has a markedly higher performance (MCC 0.90) than the localization “peroxisome” (MCC 0.56), so in this case, it is probably best to believe the sorting signal prediction and regard the protein as peroxisomal (in addition to cytoplasmic).

3.3 Using DeepLocPro 1.0

On the web server, paste or upload your sequences and click “Submit.” On the command line, run “deeplocpro -f input.fasta -o output-directory.”

3.3.1 Options

Organism Group The options here are “Any” (the default), “Archaea,” “Gram negative,” and “Gram positive” (see Note 9). The model is in itself agnostic to the organism group; the effect of this option is simply to suppress the biologically implausible predictions of “outer membrane” and “periplasmic” if the data are from archaea and Gram-positive bacteria. Such predictions will be remapped to “extracellular.” In the command line interface, the group is indicated by the “-g” option with the arguments “any,” “archaea,” “positive,” or “negative.”

Output Format The “Long” output format supplies, for each sequence, a logo-like attention plot (see below). If you submit a large number of sequences, use the “Short” format without figures instead. In the command line interface, plots are requested by the “-p” option.

sp_P0ADC1_LPTE_ECOLI Predicted localization: Outer Membrane

Localization Cell wall & surface Extracellular Cytoplasmic Cytoplasmic Membrane Outer Membrane Periplasmic
Probability 0 0 0.0001 0.0213 0.9783 0.0002


Fig. 9 DeepLocPro 1.0 prediction results for the UniProt sequence POADC1/LPTE_ECOLI
Fig 10 Examplef the logo-like atention plot from DeepLocPr1.0, showing results r the UniProt sequence Example of th

3.3.2 Output

The DeepLocPro 1.0 output is very similar to the DeepLoc 2.1 output, but it is in several respects simpler. There are no sorting signal or membrane type predictions, Additionally, there is no table of prediction thresholds, since DeepLocPro 1.0, being a singlelabel method, simply takes the highest scoring category to be the prediction.

There is a table and an attention plot for each sequence. In the example shown, the LPS-assembly lipoprotein LptE from Escherichia coli is correctly predicted to be localized to the outer membrane. In the table (Fig. 9), the background color is green with an intensity proportional to the probability of each location. If there are two locations with a visibly green background, it is not a prediction of multiple locations, but a low-confidence prediction where DeepLocPro 1.0 is in doubt about the correct location.

In the attention plot (Fig. 10), it seems that DeepLocPro 1.0 puts emphasis on the lipobox of the signal peptide (which is of the Sec/SPII type and covers positions 1–18).

4 Notes

  1. Sometimes, especially in introductory textbooks [1], the term “signal peptide” is used in a broader sense, meaning any sorting signal embedded in the amino acid sequence of a protein. However, the definition of SP used in this chapter corresponds

to the usage in most of the scientific literature [9, 10], as well as in UniProt [70] and the Sequence feature ontology [71].

  1. The servers have no detection feature for nucleotide sequences; they will treat such sequences as proteins exclusively consisting of Ala, Cys, Gly, and Thr, leading to nonsensical output without a warning.

  2. Since SignalP predicts an N-terminal signal, it is dependent on a correct start codon assignment. A start codon assigned too far downstream will cut part of the true amino acid sequence, while a start codon assigned too far upstream will add arbitrary sequence to the N-terminus, in both cases making it difficult to recognize a possible SP.

  3. When we say that the only SP type in eukaryotes is Sec/SPI, that statement pertains to the translocation system at the ER membrane. In mitochondria and (especially) plastids, Tat SPs can occur [16].

  4. Occasionally, it can happen that some positions within a long SP are predicted as “Other.” In other words, the SP does not follow the grammar defined by the CRF layer in SignalP 6.0. In those cases, the region assignments in the GFF3 file will not make sense, since the regions will be annotated as overlapping. If this happens, we recommend taking the predicted SP with caution.

  5. The “cleavage site probability” is not the probability that the SP is cleaved, but a measure of confidence in the predicted placement of the cleavage site. A low probability means that cleavage might occur in another position.

  6. Occasionally, there are predicted Sec/SPII or Tat/SPII SPs that do not have a cysteine immediately after the cleavage site. These predictions are false; a prokaryotic lipoprotein without an N-terminal cysteine has never been observed in nature. For a simpler computational model, it would be easy to avoid these predictions that do not correspond to any example in the dataset, but for a language model, there is apparently a tendency to extrapolate beyond the training data, sometimes in unwanted directions (analogous to the “hallucinations” observed in ChatGPT and related models). Such sequences are most probably actually Sec/SPI or Tat/SPI SPs.

  7. In multispanning transmembrane proteins, it was only the first (most N-terminal) transmembrane segment that was labeled as a sorting signal for DeepLoc 2.0 training, since this has been shown to be important for protein sorting in the secretory pathway. Therefore, DeepLoc 2.1 should not be used as a transmembrane topology predictor.

  8. “Gram negative” and “Gram positive” should in this context be interpreted as diderm and monoderm, respectively. Class Mollicutes should thus be regarded as “Gram positive” even though they stain Gram-negative.

Acknowledgments

Thanks go to all coauthors of the SignalP 6.0, DeepLoc 2.1, and DeepLocPro 1.0 publications: Felix Teufel, Jose´ Juan Almagro Armenteros, Alexander Rosenberg Johansen, Magnu´s Halldo´r Gı´slason, Silas Irby Pihl, Konstantinos D. Tsirigos, Ole Winther, Søren Brunak, Gunnar von Heijne, Marius Thrane Ødum, Vineet Thumuluri, and Jaime Moreno. In addition, I want to thank Peter Wad Sackett, who is responsible for keeping the services.healthtech. dtu.dk website up and running.
