CNN-Meth: A Tool to Accurately Predict Lysine Methylation Sites Using Evolutionary Information-Based Protein Modeling

Austin Spadaro, Alok Sharma, and Iman Dehzangi

Abstract

Lysine methylation is a crucial posttranslational modification influencing both histone and nonhistone protein functions. Disruptions in lysine methyltransferase activity are linked to numerous diseases, including various cancers and developmental disorders. Accurate identification of lysine methylation sites is essential for early diagnosis and therapeutic development. Here, we present CNN-Meth, a newly developed Web-based utility that employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) to predict lysine methylation sites. CNN-Meth leverages evolutionary, structural, and physicochemical data alongside binary encoding for its training process. Evolutionary and structural features used to build CNN-Meth are extracted using protein modeling, which works similarly to using Protein Language Models (PLM). Unlike traditional approaches that rely on manually extracted features, CNN-Meth uses CNNs for automated feature extraction, ensuring minimal information loss. This novel methodology enhances prediction accuracy, achieving \(9 6 . 0 \%\) Accuracy, \(8 5 . 1 \%\) Sensitivity, \(9 6 . 4 \%\) Specificity, and a Matthew’s Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.65. This demonstrates the possible effectiveness of using PLM to predict Methylation sites as a future direction. The CNN-Meth tool and its source code are readily accessible at https://github.com/MLBC-lab/CNN-Meth, providing a robust resource for researchers and clinicians.

Key words Posttranslational modification, Methylation, Automated feature extraction, Convolutional neural network, Evolutionary features, Structural features, Physicochemical features

1 Introduction

Posttranslational modifications (PTMs) are enzymatic changes in protein after translation. These modifications can alter protein structure, impacting their roles within various biological processes [1, 2]. PTMs are implicated in a wide range of biological functions, including protein folding, signal transduction, and the regulation of gene expression. Conversely, aberrant PTMs are associated with various diseases, such as cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, and cardiovascular diseases, underscoring the importance of accurately identifying PTM sites [3, 4].

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_11, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

With its reversible nature, lysine methylation emerges as a promising candidate from a drug design perspective [5]. The dynamic regulation of lysine methylation by lysine methyltransferases (KMTs) and demethylases (KDMs) underscores its potential impact on drug development. Identifying lysine methylation sites is essential for understanding disease mechanisms and developing targeted therapies.

Traditionally, lysine methylation sites have been identified using experimental techniques such as mass spectrometry [6]. While these methods provide high accuracy, they are laborintensive, time-consuming, and costly, making them impractical for large-scale studies. Consequently, there is a growing need for computational methods that can predict lysine methylation sites efficiently and accurately.

Over the past few years, several machine learning-based tools have been developed to predict lysine methylation sites. Notable among them are PLMLA, iLM-2 L, MethK, mLysPTMpred, predML-Site, and ilRMethyl [7–12]. These tools typically rely on handcrafted features derived from protein sequences, such as amino acid composition, secondary structure, and evolutionary information. For instance, PLMLA, developed by Shi et al., leverages a combination of group weight, amino acid composition, and secondary structure features extracted from the tool PSIPRED 2.0 to predict lysine methylation. It employs a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to identify methylation sites based on these features. PLMLA demonstrated moderate success in methylation site prediction, but like many early tools, it relies on predefined features, limiting the complexity of the patterns it can capture from protein sequences.

Similarly, iLM-2 L, proposed by Ju et al., also utilizes an SVM classifier but introduces a more sophisticated feature representation by incorporating k-gap amino acid pairs and pseudo-amino acid composition into its feature set. This model employs a two-level classification approach, allowing it to predict whether a lysine residue is methylated and infer the degree of methylation. By incorporating sequence-based features that capture more nuanced relationships within the protein sequence, iLM-2 L improved upon earlier methods, demonstrating better performance in predicting methylation degrees. However, like PLMLA, iLM-2 L is constrained by its reliance on handcrafted features, which may not fully encapsulate the complexities of lysine methylation, potentially leading to information loss during the feature extraction process.

These approaches highlight a common trend in early machine learning-based PTM prediction tools—while they achieved promising results, they often relied on predefined, manually extracted features, which may overlook subtle but important aspects of the underlying biological processes. For example, MethK, a tool designed by Lee et al., predicts lysine methylation

for both histone and nonhistone proteins. It combines sequencebased features and evolutionary data derived from PSSM matrices, further demonstrating the importance of incorporating multiple layers of biological information in PTM prediction. Despite these innovations, limitations persist in these tools due to their manual feature extraction processes, which can inadvertently miss relevant data patterns. The need for more comprehensive and automated methods became apparent as methylation site prediction models sought higher accuracy and deeper insights into the biological mechanisms driving these modifications.

CNN-Meth is a novel tool designed to predict lysine methylation sites using a convolutional neural network (CNN) [13]. This tool uses deep learning to automatically extract features from protein sequences, incorporating evolutionary, structural, and physicochemical data. Using CNNs, CNN-Meth avoids the limitations associated with handcrafted feature extraction, ensuring minimal information loss and capturing complex patterns in the data. Our approach combines multiple types of information, including Position-Specific Scoring Matrices (PSSM), secondary structure predictions, physicochemical properties, and binary encoding of amino acids, to create a comprehensive feature set.

Our evolutionary and structural protein modeling is similar to using large language models (LLMs), which have emerged as promising and powerful paradigms for sequence modeling. In this approach, a very large transformer model is trained on a large natural language dataset. The transformer model comprises neural networks, including an encoder and a decoder, both of which utilize self-attention mechanisms. The resulting transformer is able to encode a given sequence using all the information that it learned during the training process. LLMs are also being used to encode proteins through Protein Language Models (PLMs). In this case, a large transformer is trained using large unlabeled protein datasets such as UniProt. PLMs have been successfully used to predict different PTMs, such as Succinylation [14, 15], Sumoylation [16], Glycation [17], Phosphoglycerylation [18], and many more [19–21].

Although we do not directly use PLMs, we use the PSSM as the output of PSI-BLAST for protein encoding. PSI-BLAST conducts a sequence alignment on a very large protein dataset and encodes the level of similarity as the PSSM. In other words, PSSM is also an encoding of a given protein sequence with respect to a large protein dataset. Using PSSM in this study can be considered as a proof of concept in using PLMs for methylation site prediction. CNN-Meth has been trained and validated on a large benchmark dataset, achieving superior performance compared to existing methods. The tool demonstrates high accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, making it a robust resource for researchers and clinicians.

The promising performance of CNN-Meth also demonstrates the effectiveness of modeling using large protein datasets. Therefore, this study can be considered as a proof of concept that shows the possible promising performance of PLMs to accurately predict methylation sites as the next step. CNN-Meth is publicly available as a Web-based utility (at: https://mlbclab.camden.rutgers.edu/ research/), providing easy access for the scientific community. This paper presents the details of CNN-Meth, including the datasets, feature extraction methods, model architecture, implementation, and evaluation results.

2 Materials and Methods

This section details the methods and data used to design and implement CNN-Meth.

2.1 Dataset

For this analysis, we used data extracted from the Protein Lysine Modification Database (PLMD), which has since been transitioned to CPLM 4.0 [22–24]. This data is publicly available at https:// cplm.biocuckoo.cn/. This dataset contains various types of lysine PTMs that have been experimentally verified and is the most extensive publicly available collection of confirmed lysine methylation sites. The lysine methylation dataset consists of 6322 methylation sites and 133,293 non-methylation sites. To reduce bias within our model, CD-HIT was used to remove sequences with greater than \(4 0 \%\) sequential similarity, resulting in a dataset with 4913 methylation sites and 108,958 non-methylation sites [25].

Given the significant imbalance between positive and negative samples, a balancing strategy was employed to improve model performance [26]. The training data was balanced to a ratio of 3: 1 by down-sampling the negative samples using K-nearest neighbor (KNN) [27]. This down-sampling strategy maximizes the distance between selected training data points, preserving as much nonredundant information as possible. As a result, the final balanced dataset contains 4913 positive and 14,739 negative samples. For the reader’s convenience, the number of samples in our dataset at the beginning, after removing those with over \(4 0 \%\) sequential similarities, and after the down-sampling is presented in Table 1.

Table 1 Positive and negative samples

All CD-HIT Balanced Test
Positive 6322 4913 4913 983
Negative 133,293 108,958 14,739 21,617

2.2 Feature Representation

The feature representation for CNN-Meth was extracted from sequences of 15 upstream and 15 downstream amino acids from the target lysine [26]. This window size was chosen based on its demonstrated effectiveness in previous studies. A mirroring technique was used to ensure a consistent window size for sequences where the lysine is near the termini depicted in Fig. 1 [28].

Evolutionary Features Evolutionary information was extracted using Position-Specific Scoring Matrices (PSSM), calculated with PSI-BLAST [29]. PSI-BLAST aligned a given sequence against a very large protein dataset (in this case, nonredundant protein dataset (NR) containing over 80 million proteins). This modeling is similar to using PLMs for protein modeling. PSSMs provide a matrix of substitution probabilities, capturing evolutionary conservation at each position in the sequence. Instead of using handcrafted features from PSSMs, CNN-Meth inputs the entire PSSM matrix directly into the convolutional neural network, allowing for automated feature extraction.


Fig. 1

Structural Properties Structural features were calculated using SPIDER2 and include accessible surface area (ASA), torsion angles \(( \theta , \tau , \psi , \varphi )\) , and secondary structure states (helix, sheet, coil) [30– 36]. These properties provide critical information about the protein’s three-dimensional structure and local folding patterns. The input to SPIDER2 is the PSSM of a given protein. Therefore, it also uses PSSM modeling for feature encoding. Therefore, it can also be considered a modeling similar to PLMs. It is important to note that despite similarities, we are fully aware of the differences between PSSM and PLM protein modeling. Therefore, using PSSM here can solely be considered as a proof of concept for using PLMs in the future and not showing their absolute effectiveness in methylation site prediction.

Physicochemical Properties Each amino acid’s physicochemical attributes were sourced from the AAIndex database, with 55 selected properties shown to be effective in previous studies [37–39]. This results in a 31x55 feature matrix for each sample, which captures a comprehensive set of physicochemical characteristics.

Binary Encoding Amino acids were also encoded as 20-element binary vectors, where each element represents the presence of a specific amino acid. This encoding creates a \(3 1 \mathrm { x } 2 0\) feature matrix for each sequence window, facilitating the input of sequence information into the CNN.

2.3 Web Server

CNN-Meth is accessible as an online tool and is publicly available at https://mlbclab.camden.rutgers.edu/research/ (see Note 1). A screenshot of the CNN-Meth Web utility is shown in Fig. 2. The Web utility takes a PSSM matrix file as an input (see Note 2). A PSSM file represents a protein sequence’s evolutionary profile, capturing each amino acid’s substitution probabilities at every position. Users can use the PSI-BLAST tool to generate a PSSM file from a FASTA file, which aligns the protein sequence against a database to compute the PSSM [29] (see Note 3). The required PSSM file format is shown in Fig. 3.

The uploaded file should contain the entire protein sequence that includes the target lysine residue (see Note 4). The file name of the uploaded PSSM file must be the row number of the target lysine followed by “.pssm” (e.g., 124.pssm) (see Note 5). It is important to ensure that a valid PSSM file is uploaded using this naming convention, as an invalid file will result in an incorrect prediction from the Web utility. Once a valid PSSM file is provided, CNN-Meth will automatically handle feature extraction, including generating the corresponding SPD3 file, based on the provided PSSM file (see Notes 6–8). This streamlined process ensures that users can easily obtain accurate predictions for lysine methylation sites.

Please cite CNN-Meth using the following reference:

n Methods (Vol. 226, pp. 127-132). Elsevier BV. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ymeth.2024.04.007


Fig. 2
Fig. 3

2.4 Algorithm

To build CNN-Meth, we utilized a CNN because it can automatically extract relevant features from raw data, eliminating the need for manual feature engineering. CNNs are well-suited for tasks where spatial hierarchies in data are essential, such as image recognition or sequence-based [18, 40, 41]. The key advantage of CNNs lies in their convolutional layers, which apply filters to input data, enabling the model to capture intricate patterns and relationships between neighboring features [42, 43]. By progressively learning abstract representations, CNNs can automatically identify relevant biological signals, such as evolutionary, structural, and

physicochemical properties, without the limitations associated with predefined feature sets [44–46]. This makes them ideal for the lysine methylation prediction problem, where multiple types of complex data interact, and manual feature extraction can be prone to information loss.

The specific architecture of CNN-Meth consists of two convolutional layers, each with 64 filters and a [3] window size, followed by max-pooling layers with a [2] window size. These convolutional and pooling layers extract and condense the relevant features from the input data. Afterward, the model passes through five fully connected layers containing 50 neurons, enabling representation learning. To prevent overfitting, two dropout layers with a dropout rate of 0.2 are included [47–49]. Finally, the output layer consists of a single neuron with a sigmoid activation function, which performs binary classification by predicting whether a lysine site may become methylated.

The model was trained using the Adam optimization algorithm and binary cross-entropy loss function. The evaluation involved five-fold and ten-fold cross-validation and an independent test set to ensure robust performance evaluation. The results of crossvalidation are shown in Table 2. Table 3 presents a comparison of CNN-Meth being evaluated on the independent test against methods proposed in previous studies.

Table 2 The results achieved for fivefold and tenfold cross-validation

Cross validation Accuracy Specificity Sensitivity MCC
5-fold 88.4% 92.6% 78.7% 0.69
10-fold 90.1% 93.0% 81.7% 0.74

Table 3 Comparing CNN-Meth with previous studies found in the literature on the independent test set

Model Accuracy Specificity Sensitivity MCC
PLMLA 82.6% 83.5% 83.0% 0.66
iLM-2 L 85.3% 91.9% 76.5% 0.69
MethK (histone) 81.4% 80.3% 85.6% 0.56
MethK (nonhistone) 87.2% 88.7% 69.1% 0.44
mLysPTMpred 83.7% - - -
predML-Site 84.2% - - -
CNN-Meth 96.0% 96.4% 85.1% 0.65

3 Notes

  1. CNN-Meth is accessible as an online tool and is publicly available at https://mlbclab.camden.rutgers.edu/research/,
  2. The Web utility takes a PSSM matrix file as an input. An invalid PSSM will result in an inaccurate prediction.
  3. Using PSSM for protein modeling can be considered similar to protein modeling using PLMs.
  4. Promising CNN-Meth prediction performance demonstrates the possible effectiveness of PLMs in enhancing methylation site prediction as a future direction.
  5. Users can use the publicly available tool PSI-BLAST to generate a PSSM file from a FASTA file.
  6. The entire PSSM file is required to generate secondary structure features. The provided PSSM file must be generated against the entire protein sequence that contains the target lysine.
  7. The target lysine in question must be specified by the naming convention of the passed in PSSM file. The file name of the uploaded file must be the row number of the target lysine followed by “.pssm”. For example, if the target lysine is the 123rd amino acid in the protein sequence, the file name must be 123.pssm.
  8. Only the PSSM file is required by CNN-Meth. Other features required will automatically be generated by referencing the provided PSSM file.

Predicting the Pathogenicity of Human Protein Variants: Not Only a Matter of Residue Labeling

Matteo Manfredi, Gabriele Vazzana, Giulia Babbi, Elisa Bertolini, Castrense Savojardo, Pier Luigi Martelli, and Rita Casadio

Abstract

The pathogenicity of human variants is an important annotation feature that may help in understanding, at a molecular level, the propensity for a human being to develop a certain disease or pathology. Recently, protein sequence embedding associated with machine and/or deep learning has been proven useful in improving results in this area. Different aspects of pathogenic variants can help in understanding the molecular mechanisms of the disease at a molecular level. These include solvent accessibility in the folded gene, the effect on the protein stability, and eventually the perturbation on interaction networks important for biological processes. Here, we describe how, once a variant is predicted “pathogenic”, other important structural and functional properties can be derived computationally at the same website (https:// bioinformaticsweeties.biocomp.unibo.it/), including the protein structure, if not available. All the properties can help to understand variant effects within the complex context of the cell environment.

Key words Single residue variations, Pathogenicity, Machine learning, Deep learning, Protein embedding, Suite of tools, Solvent accessibility, Protein stability, Structure prediction, Human proteome

1 Introduction

The introduction of protein Language Models (pLMs), somehow inspired by natural Language Models [1, 2], has been adopted in the development of several tools as a unique and powerful representation of protein sequences along with machine learning and more recently deep learning [3] methods. In the ‘90, it was noticed that inference of protein structural properties, such as secondary structure from sequence, improved if the protein was represented by a sequence profile, computed after sequence alignment with similar proteins in the family, sharing structural templates [4– 6]. Nowadays, the introduction of embeddings to represent a protein sequence stands upon the same notion, with the difference that the background knowledge may include all the known protein

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_12, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

sequences that have been derived from next-generation sequencing experiments (several billion for the last ESMFold language) [7– 11]. Each residue in the protein sequence can carry along the information derived from the background database and this has proven useful to improve the performance of different predictors, including the computation of protein functional and structural features and of protein–protein interactions along with the development of deep learning schemes for training the algorithms on given selected properties [9, 12–16]. More recently, embedding procedures have been adopted by ESMFold for structure prediction from protein sequences, and for distantly remote homology search [10, 17, 18]. In the following, we will guide the end user to solve a specific problem where, after detecting the presence of pathogenic variants in the protein sequence, he/she may follow a given workflow to endow its protein with other relevant physicochemical features. We will use as a test case a gene, AGPS (https:// www.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/O00116/entry), a fairly wellcharacterized human enzyme, whose variants are associated with a form of rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata 3 (RCDP3)). Our analysis with state-of-theart predictors characterizes the physico-chemical properties of the pathogenic variants, and frames the protein within Reactome Pathways (https://curator.reactome.org/), from where all the possible protein–ligand and protein–protein interactions can be derived. This can help to verify putative hypotheses on the molecular mechanisms of the genotype–phenotype relationships.

2 Predictors Relevant to Pathogenicity Aspects at Bioinformatic Sweeties

Bioinformatic Sweeties (Fig. 1a) is a suite of in-house developed tools based on machine and/or deep learning and derived databases for characterizing proteins and their variants. Here, the end user can start an analysis without any installation, login, or code skills [19]. Among the tools and databases, we will focus on (1) E-SNPs&GO for predicting the pathogenicity of protein variants [20]; (2) E-pRSA [16], for predicting residue Relative Solvent Accessibility, including also (3) ISPRED-SEQ [14] for predicting protein–protein interactions; (4) INPS for predicting protein stability upon variation starting from protein sequence [21]; (5) Alpha&ESMhFolds, a database for the comparison of structural models predicted with ESMFold and AlphaFold2 for 42,942 human reference proteins, which allows to retrieve the precomputed structure of the protein and map the protein variants for further studies [22]; and (6) DAR, a database mapping diseaseassociated enzyme into Reactome pathways to derive possible relevant interactions among biological processes [23] (Fig. 1b). E-SNPs&GO, E-pRSA, ISPRED-SEQ, and ESMFold adopt

Fig. 1 Bioinformatic Sweeties.The suite hosts the tools and databases developed by the Biocomputing Group Bioinformatic Sweeties. The suite hosts the tools and databases developed by the Biocomputing Group for different purposes (Fig. 1a). Red ovals encircle tags to press in

DAR

Protein stability upon variation

Al modeling comparison

Disease-associated enzyme pathway

embedding procedures for protein sequence representations. All the details relevant to understanding tools and database developments can be easily retrieved directly on the suite, along with the reference papers, and help pages for their usage (https:// bioinformaticsweeties.biocomp.unibo.it/).

3 Methods

Here, as an example, we focus on the AGPS gene, whose product Alkyldihydroxyacetonephosphate synthase peroxisomal, is an enzyme which is known to catalyze “the exchange of the acyl chain in acyl-dihydroxyacetonephosphate (acyl-DHAP) for a long chain fatty alcohol, yielding the first ether-linked intermediate, i.e. alkyl-dihydroxyacetonephosphate (alkyl-DHAP), in the pathway of ether lipid biosynthesis” (EC:2.5.1.26, short name Alkyl-DHAP synthase, see for details and supporting literature the protein Swiss-Prot/UniProt file O00116, https://www.uniprot.org/

uniprotkb/O00116/entry). According to OMIM, the gene is located in chromosome 2 (2q31.2) and its pathogenic mutations, both homozygous and compound heterozygous, promote an alkyl-DHAP synthase deficiency in the protein, and are associated with a form of rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata 3 (RCDP3)), a peroxisomal disorder characterized by disproportionately short stature primarily affecting the proximal parts of the extremities (for details and references see the OMIM site at https://www.omim.org/entry/600121)). Protein pathogenic variations are derived from the UniProt file, filtering out those of uncertain annotation. Similarly, benign variations were retrieved for the sake of comparing predictions. The genetics, biochemistry, and physiology of the protein have been extensively characterized. Still, the protein structure is lacking. A reason to select this protein is that while applying our predictors to understand the pathogenicity of the protein at a molecular level, we will be able to score the efficiency of our computations.

3.1 Predicting the Pathogenicity of SRVs

Following our flowchart (Fig. 1), we will start by asking whether all the pathogenic variations known to be associated with the pathology are correctly predicted. In Bioinformatic Sweeties [19] w e select under the tag “variant” E-SNPs&GO to predict whether the variants are or are not pathogenic. To this aim, we also add some neutral natural variations not disease-related. All the variants are downloaded from the O00116 UniProt entry, where the variant origin is also detailed. E-SNPs&GO [20] is a fast and accurate method that, given an input protein sequence and a single residue variation or more, can predict whether the variation/s is/are related to diseases or not. Protein representation relies on an encoding scheme based on protein language models (ProtT5 [9], ESM-1v [7]), and Anc2Vec [24] for Gene Ontology (GO) terms. The predictor implements a Support Vector Machine [25] for classifying the variant as pathogenic. A reliability index is associated with each prediction, scoring the prediction confidence [20]. In Fig. 2, the results of the predictions match what we expected, supporting the confidence of the predictor. One should consider however that when experimental validation is lacking, only the reliability score can rate the quality of the prediction, which could still be wrong.

3.2 Predicting the Solvent Exposure of Residues

An interesting feature of the variations can be their relative solvent exposure [26]. This may help in understanding whether the mutated residues are or are not exposed to the polar solvent and eventually are or are not part of possible interaction sites. In Bioinformatic Sweeties [19], we can now select the tag “Accessibility” and adopt E-pRSA [16], a predictor based on embedding and deep learning to predict whether a residue is or is not at the protein surface. The same website implements ISPRED-SEQ [14], based

E-SNPs&GO submission form

INPUT

OUTPUT

Protein Q00116
Variant Pathogenicity class Pathogenicity probability (Pathogenic: ≥ 0.5) Reliability index
A7V Benign 0.07 9
G12V Benign 0.06 9
A22G Benign 0.05 9
R182Q Pathogenic 0.93 9
T309I Pathogenic 0.93 9

E471K Pathogenic 0.95 9
T568M Pathogenic 0.93 9

F 2E-NPs&GO:Prediction o protein variant pathogenicity.This is a predictor basedon theembeddings o E-SNPs&GO: Prediction of protein variant pathogenicity. This is a predictor based on the embeddings of protein sequences and Gene Ontology terms (GO, https:/www.geneontology.org/) to compute with a machine learning method (support vector machine) the pathogenicity or not of protein variants [20]. The website allows the simultaneous prediction of different variants, and alongside the predicted pathogenicity or not, it gives the associated confidence (measured as the Reliability index of the prediction). Details on the method and its implementation can be found a

again on protein embedding and deep learning to predict the likelihood that a residue is or is not an interaction site. Figure 3 shows the sequence feature view of the prediction. According to the predictor, \(5 8 \%\) of the protein residues (the protein sequence comprises 658 residues) are exposed with 77 interaction sites, confirming the high number of interacting proteins (https://www.uniprot. org/uniprotkb/O00116/entry#interaction). The region including R419H is highlighted indicating that the residue is buried and it is not a protein–protein interaction site. None of the pathogenic variations are in exposed regions and in interaction sites (Table 1).

3.3 Predicting Effects on the Protein Stability

A related property to a pathogenic variation in a protein region is whether the substitution may or not affect the protein stability. In Bioinformatic Sweeties we have available INPS [21], which is a classical predictor based on machine learning and sequence profile as input to the Support Vector Machine (SVM). It will be soon complemented with another predictor, not yet released, based on embedding techniques and deep learning [28]. INPS computes the change in free energy (DDG) associated with variants in the protein (Fig. 4). According to INPS, only three out of the six pathogenic variants affect the protein stability.

Sequence feature view


Fig. 3 Output of E-pRSA and ISPRED-SEQ. Embedding schemes of protein sequences in E-pRSA [16] improve Output of E-pRSA and ISPRED-SEQ. Embedding schemes of protein sequences in E-pRSA [16] improve the prediction of relative solvent accessibility. Furthermore, the same procedure improves the prediction of interaction sites [14]. Both predictors are running on the same website. The output allows the association of exposed residues with interaction sites. Orange and blue colors identify exposed and buried regions along the protein sequence. Red color marks interaction sites. In the figure, the region including the pathogenic variant – R419H is highlighted indicating that the residue is buried, and it is not a protein prote

3.4 Comparing Results of Different Tools

In Table 1, we sum up the results of our prediction. Considering the six known pathogenic variations associated with RCDP3, we found that they are correctly predicted as pathogenic, they are buried, and not involved in interaction sites. We previously defined the threshold value between neutral, destabilizing, and stabilizing variations after comparing it with other state-of-the-art predictors for the same task [27].

For all variants, the associated disease is Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata 3 (RCDP3). Specifically, R182Q and E471K are associated with a reduction of the protein level and R419H to a loss of enzyme activity (see the UniProt file O00116, https://www. uniprot.org/uniprotkb/O00116/entry).

3.5 Mapping SRVs on Predicted 3D Structures

We used our database of models, Alpha&ESMhFolds [22], (in Bioinformatic Sweeties under the tag “New”) to derive the pairwise models of our protein. Both methods agree on the putative structure (TM-score is 0.88 [29]). With the model/s which can be

downloaded from the website, we can then map the pathogenic variants and realize to what extent their location in the protein structure is consistent with the predicted features of Table 1 (Fig. 5, [22]).


Fig. 5 Visualization of the three-dimensional structural models of ADAS_HUMAN Visualization of the three-dimensional structural models of ADAS_HUMAN (UniProt ID: O00116) predicted with ESMFold (in green) and AlphaFold2 (in purple). The pairwise models are downloaded from Alpha&ESMhFolds (https:/alpha-esmhfolds.biocomp.unibo.it/viewer/O00116). The two models have a good overlap (TM-score 0.88). The light blue region indicates the FAD – binding domain of the protein (Pfam PF01565, covering residues 206 345) and the dark blue region indicates the FAD-linked oxidase, C-terminal domain (Pfam – PF02913, covering residues 385 654). The Pfam domains overlap in the two models, which have however no overlapping regions at the N-terminus. The six pathogenic variations characterized in this case study are marked in red: one is outside the two domains (R182Q), one (annotated as FAD binding domain) is in the light blue domain (T309I) and 4 are located in the same beta-sheet of the dark blue domain (R419H, L469P, E471K, and T568M). The three benign variations, to be found at N terminal of the protein, are located in the diverging portion of the two models, corresponding to the Transit peptide – (1 58). They are marked in yellow in the figure and a dashed line links the corresponding positions in the two models. E-pRSA predicts the six pathogenic variations as buried; by adopting either model, this can be confirmed by running the DSSP program [31, 32] a t ht \(1 \%\) /pd \(5 \%\) do.eu/dssp. The pathogenic variants have RSA values ranging from 1% to 5%. The benign variants \(8 6 \%\) dic \(9 9 \%\) y E-pRSA Exposed) are solvent-accessible (RSA values ranging from 86% to 99%). For molecular visualizations, we adopt the PDBe Mol* v

3.6 Analyzing Biological Processes

Finally, we can try to frame the protein into the Reactome, which includes a hierarchical graphical organization of known biological processes and pathways containing detailed information on components and their relations (https://curator.reactome.org/).


Fig. 6 DAR output. Our AGPS gene is involved in three Reactome pathways: TYSND1 cleaves peroxisomal DAR output. Our gene is involved in three Reactome pathways: TYSND1 cleaves peroxisomal (R-HSA75896).This last one is highlighted in the insert. HereAGSP is in complex with GNPAT, Dihydroxyace-(R-HSA-75896). This last one is highlighted in the insert. Here AGSP is in complex with GNPAT, Dihydroxyaceand whose variants are associated with Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 2

Reactome describes human biological processes, and by mapping the disease-associated enzyme, a Reactome-disease association can be established [30]. This procedure allows the integration of the protein in a more complex scenario of interactions with other proteins whose functioning can also affect the overall network of biological processes. By tagging “database” in Bioinformatic Sweeties, we find DAR, which provides the mapping of human disease-associated enzymes into Reactome and allows the characterization of disease groups and their interactions [23] (Fig. 6).

4 Conclusions

We implemented a suite of different state-of-the-art predictors based on protein embedding and shallow and/or deep learning methods. Taking advantage of the suite we derived different physicochemical properties related to pathogenic variants of a selected protein, and how the protein can be characterized in the context of

the Reactome pathways for better exploitation of the genotype-tophenotype relationship. The predictors, starting from E-SNPs&GO for predicting whether a variant is pathogenic or not, are state-of-the-art. This, however, does not guarantee their success in every case, as long as an experimental validation is not available.

A Survey of Biological Function Prediction Methods with Focus on Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Models (LLM)

Dana Mary Varghese, T. Athulya, Vikash K. Mohani, and Shandar Ahmad

Abstract

Protein function prediction from sequence, structure, gene expression profiles, and published literature are needed to understand all biological processes. Natural language processing of biological text and large language model (LLM)-based encoding of sequence and structure opens powerful paths to rapid function annotation and novel training models. In this survey, we take a look at the available models for function prediction, especially the NLP- and LLM-based models. The survey highlights the major advances made and the ground that still needs to be covered to automate the process of function prediction from two major sources namely protein sequences and published research documents.

Key words Function prediction, Natural language processing, Large language models, Protein function, Gene ontologies

1 Introduction

Protein function is most confidently determined through experimental methods like inhibition of gene expression [1], gene knockout [2], expression of fusion proteins [3], chromatography [4], protein-protein interaction [3, 5], biochemical and enzymatic assay [6], spectroscopy [7], and crystallography [5]. However, direct determination or even a systematic curation of function from literature for each protein is costly and perhaps not needed in view of the ability of computational methods to transfer annotations from one protein to another with various degrees of accuracy. Many successful methods of protein function prediction have been developed, including those based on homology relationship [8, 9], gene expression or co-expression-based guilt-by-association law [10, 11], protein-protein interactions or structural, computed or predicted features [8, 12–18].

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_13, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

Problem of function prediction can be defined in a focused manner of predicting the ontology terms, i.e., predicting a specific molecular function, cellular composition or biological process. The least accurate models among them are the ones trying to predict the cellular component or the localization of the proteins [19–21].

On the other hand, the prediction of protein-protein and other biomolecular interactions itself, may be regarded as a kind of function prediction. Protein function prediction in both broad and narrow contexts has been carried out using feature sets individually or through an integration of different levels of information with various degrees of success [22]. For example, amino acid sequence alignment [23], structural information [24], gene expression data in the form of co-expressed genes at proteomic or RNA expression level, protein-protein interaction [25] networks, domains or motif contents [26, 27], subcellular localization [28] and biophysical properties of amino acids have resulted in improved prediction of protein function as seen in critical assessment of functional annotation (CAFA2 and CAFA3) community challenges [14, 29, 30].

Apart from the predictive features of proteins described above, a path to function annotation is by direct or indirect evidence already discovered but not systematically documented, i.e., literature search. Faster mining of the existing knowledge of protein functions has become possible with dedicated databases and efficient text mining methods [31, 32] and genomic context-based predictions [33]. However, when functional annotation cannot be directly inferred from literature, function transfer relying on amino acid sequence, gene expression environment, three-dimensional structure and protein-protein interaction networks, as outlined above is indispensable. The most obvious among them, are the methods of function prediction based on homology relationships. Sequence similarity frequently indicates evolutionary relationship and functional similarity. However, this association is not absolute. Proteins have linear and structural motifs [34], that indicate their implicit functional capabilities. The existence of these motifs has been used to predict protein function, e.g., by a Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST) search of a database of already annotated proteins [35] or CATHEDRAL [36] and pair-wise annotation transfer. Unfortunately, as many as \(3 0 \%\) of proteins cannot be computationally annotated using the above features as they are not closely related to well-annotated proteins [37, 38]. This highlights the need for alternative methods of sequence-based function prediction beyond homology relationships. For this purpose, protein sequences have been represented as k-mer frequencies and evolutionary profiles or characterized by smaller patterns contained in them. Single amino acid composition or K-mer counts of peptide subsequences are some of the basic so-called alignment-free methods of sequence comparison, applied to function prediction [39–41]. K-mer counts have been widely

used but the success of this method is limited as protein function is encoded in a more complex manner than a simple 20 letter amino acid language or even longer k-mers at the sequence level.

Despite considerable success with prediction methods based on k-mers or single amino acid compositions, the real amino acid language of protein function has remained unclear. A major breakthrough to represent this latent language has recently been provided by the advent of large language-based models [42, 43]. Proteins are made up of sequences of letters from a predefined set of alphabets, the natural amino acids. These sequences store a major part of all the information about the protein’s structure and function [42, 44]. They come together to form secondary structural elements and domains which are equivalent to words and sentences in the natural language [45–47]. If we could decode the amino acid language of proteins that describes their function, it would be possible to directly annotate proteins from their sequence.

In Table 1, we have collected a representative set of function prediction methods using different feature sets and computational models. We summarize here that the protein function annotation methods can be grouped into four categories as follows:

  1. Classical models for direct prediction by sequence using homology, evolutionary profiles, or its derived features such as k-mer compositions [48–50].
  2. Indirect predictions from interaction networks, gene expression patterns, and related properties [11, 51–56].
  3. Mining of published literature using NLP techniques [57, 58] and methods based on language using large language model (LLM) techniques [59].

Being among the early groups of protein function prediction, a number of reviews and books are available for covering variants of methods that fall in (1) and (2). However, review literature is scarce on NLP- and LLM-based methods mentioned in (3). We, therefore, review the methods in all three groups but specifically focus on the last group of two methods in this chapter. Protein function annotation methods based on NLP and LLM share the language properties of literature or amino acid sequences, but are indeed quite different from one another and need independent treatment, as discussed in the later sections. Indeed several computational models, specifically dedicated to interactions have relied on both LLMs and NLP techniques, some of which will be covered in this survey.

2 Classical Methods of Function Annotation

2.1 Prediction from Sequence, Structure and Evolutionary Profile

The fact that sequence determines the structure and structure determines the function of a protein has led to various methodologies in which computer-encoded protein sequences are the primary features for predictive models [60, 61]. A classical method of annotating proteins is the transfer of annotation of functions from proteins with high homology in their sequence or structure [62]. High sequence similarity between proteins or genes generally can suggest an evolutionary relationship, which often implies functional similarity. Function prediction based on sequence similarity can be performed using different tools like FASTA, BLAST, PSI-BLAST, HMMER, SSEARCH which try to capture global and local regions of similarity between sequences. Functional annotation from homology searches is typically derived from sequences that meet threshold scores, such as an \(E\) -value of 0.01 or 0.001 and a Smith-Waterman alignment score of 200, on a general scale [63, 64].

Apart from the whole protein sequence comparisons, other properties can be utilized. For example, many proteins possess basic functional units called sequential, and structural motifs representing the functional capability of that protein. Function annotation of domains, motifs, and protein families is available comprehensively in several databases like SCOP [65], CATH [66], Gene3D [27], InterPro [67], SMART [68], Pfam [69, 70], PRINTS [71], and PROSITE [72] which have been used to transfer function by local sequence or structure comparison through a database. Some proteins possess structural motifs which are fingerprints of globular domains while some groups have linear motifs that are locally unstructured regions with a few specificitydetermining residues and a tendency to transition from disordered to ordered states upon binding [34]. These sequence regions are usually evolutionarily conserved because of their functional importance and hence the presence of these motifs has been used to predict protein function.

However, an approach based on motif search or any sequence comparison method has limitations. First of all, such an approach does not account for the varying levels of sequence conservation across different protein families. Secondly, many homologs exist that perform different functions facilitated by very small changes in the sequence or structure. Inadequate or erroneous detection of multiple sequence alignment also poses a problem in function transfer via homology search [73]. For example, it has been stated that a lack of enough representative members of some protein families makes it difficult to extract HMM profiles leading to a lot of unannotated sequences in Pfam [74]. Consequently, many genes in most genomes remain unannotated and cannot be used for

homology-based function search because none of them is annotated in the first place [29]. The use of structural features in function annotation has been shown to play an important role in determining function, which suffered from the lack of structures for a large number of proteins until recently. Arguably, with the advances in structure prediction using AlphaFold2 [75], ESMfold [76], and RoseTTAFold [77] the availability of high-quality structures is poised to solve this problem to an extent.

2.2 Moonlighting and Multifunctionality

The eukaryotic cell has membrane-bound and membrane-less organelles. Different biological processes can occur simultaneously because proteins relocate to these locations to function. The compartmentalization of cells allows the maintenance of different microenvironments and different concentrations of interacting partners that lead to proteins’ capability to perform different functions. This enhances the capability of a protein to perform different functions that increase the complexity of the protein function prediction problem.

A protein domain is a conserved region of a protein sequence or structure that can operate and evolve separately from the other parts of the chain. Many proteins are multifunctional having multiple domains which during evolution undergo domain shuffling. Each domain might perform its function independently or in cooperation with the other domains. Thus, remote homologs might share the same function requiring a domain-level comparison than at the global sequence level. Most of the domain-related functional annotation was in free text and the mapping to gene ontology came Pfam2GO and Inter-Pro2GO. Thus protein function prediction is a “Mult-Instance Multi-Label”(MIML) task [78]. The presence of multifunctional proteins especially moonlighting proteins which perform multiple functions without processing different domains to perform these different functions by partnering with different ligands, under different cellular conditions, localizations, or using different binding sites makes protein function prediction more of a micro-environment based problem. They follow varied mechanisms and the characteristics that enable a protein to moonlight have not been fully understood which adds chaos to the automation of function prediction. Homologs of moonlighting proteins do not necessarily perform the same moonlighting function, making the comprehensive prediction of function even more challenging.

3 Indirect Function Prediction Using Protein-Protein Interactions and Gene Expression Patterns

Several methods are available that can predict the interactions of a protein from its sequence or structure. Protein interactions can also be predicted via homology through inheritance from similar

proteins. STRING [79] transfers association from orthologous proteins in the COG database [80]. Other tools that attempt to predict protein-protein interaction include DeepBank [81]. The protein interaction-based function prediction is affected by the noise and lack of complete interaction data [82]. The lack of conserved features or specific properties in relation to some types of functions makes it difficult to automate their functional identification. This kind of ambiguity is seen in the case of protein-protein interactions, intrinsically disordered proteins, etc.

4 Function Prediction Using NLP and LLM Techniques

4.1 General Principles of NLP and LLM

Natural language is a field of computer science that tries to formalize the natural human spoken language to enable computers to understand the meaning, analyze the structure, and generate human-like language [83, 84]. Modeling natural language with computational techniques has been approached in several ways, which can be enumerated as (1) rule-based, (2) statistical methods, and (3) neural network–based methods, which form the foundation of modern techniques such as transformers and large language models (LLMs) [83, 85].

Historically, NLP research dates back to the 1940s after World War II in an effort to create machines that can translate one language to another. Early approaches in NLP focused on automating the analysis of linguistic structures and developing foundational technologies such as document summarization, sentiment analysis, content categorization, machine translation, and transliteration [86–88]. The concept of machine translation (MT) was proposed by Warner Weaver in 1947 [89, 90]. A rule-based system was the initial approach. The grammar of language has been divided into multiple levels like phonetics (speech sounds), phonology (sound patterns), morphology (words), syntax (phrases and sentences), semantics (literal meaning of phrases and sentences), pragmatics (meaning in context), and discourse (meaning in extra-linguistic context) [91]. It was proposed that the main difference between different spoken languages was the vocabulary and the order of words so the tools looked up in a dictionary and arranged the words based on the syntax of the target language. These techniques use predefined patterns and language-specific rules which were applied to tasks like pattern recognition, tokenization, part-of-speech (POS) tagging, named entity recognition (NER), morphological analysis, and rule-based sentiment analysis [85]. This compounded the lexical ambiguity in language and scalability with the difficulty of maintaining and transferring manmade rules to newer domains.

A key turning point in the field of NLP was achieved through the works of Naom Chomsky who introduced the concept that grammar was a mathematical system with precise and finite rules

using which sentences are generated [92]. The focus was shifted from a rationalist approach to empirical or corpus-based method which was automated by statistical methods and later by machine learning methods. One of the key applications of statistical NLP models was language modeling which involves predicting the probability of a word given the context. Statistical language models (SLMs) are word prediction models based on statistical learning [93]. In this model, probabilities are assigned to the word sequence to predict the next word based on the previous data sequence using methods such as Markov assumption. These techniques help to capture the probabilistic nature of language, leading to N-gram models, hidden Markov models for part of speech tagging, bag of words models, Topic Modeling using methods like Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) [94], word embedding methods like Word2Vec, Global vectors (GloVe), Term Frequency Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF). The main challenge of such methods lies in dimensionality reduction.

The next and perhaps even bigger revolution in NLP came with the application of neural networks namely, the convolutional neural networks (CNNs) [95], recurrent neural networks (RNNs) [96], and lengthy short-time memory (LSTM) networks, allowing machines to capture sequential dependencies in language. Bengio et al., introduced neural network–based language models [97]. The use of multilayer perceptron (MLP) and RNN in neural language models became popular to understand the relationships in the language.

LLMs have advanced significantly from statistical language models (SLMs). The developmental phase included statistical language models, neural language models, and large language models. Neural language models emerged to improve upon the limitations of traditional SLMs. This shift marked the beginning of deep learning methods in language modeling, ultimately leading to the development of large language models [98]. The advancement of transformers and pretraining models enhanced the performance of language modeling tasks extending to a generative nature. Vithanage et al., 2018 have reviewed the contextualized word arepresentation models [99]. In that direction, BERT [100] is language representation model that uses a transformer-based neural network. The multilayer bidirectional transformer encoder is the state-of-the-art feature of the BERT. In the implementation of BERT, there are two phases namely pretraining and fine-tuning. During the pretraining phase, the BERT model is trained on unannotated data by means of different pretraining tasks. In the finetuning phase, after initializing the pretrained parameters on the BERT model, all of these parameters are finetuned using the annotated data for downstream tasks. Later on various LLMs were introduced by extending the pretrained model mainly scaling up in terms of the size of the model and parameters of the model

[98]. The state-of-the-art performance of LLMs relies on in-context learning, instruction following, and reasoning. LLMs significantly employs the role of understanding the natural language and generating the text data although it is based on transformers and a large number of parameters, trained on huge amounts of data. During the embedding phase, LLMs extracts the syntax and meaning from the language followed by a feedforward layer which has fully connected layers. Recurrent layer understands the relationships from the input text or between the words. The scope of LLMs is eminent because of its attention mechanism. In the self-attention phase, the model can focus on some relevant texts and it is helpful to detect the dependencies between the words.

The foundational technology for these LLMs lies in pretrained models (PTMs). In 2018, PTMs utilizing self-supervised learning over large-scale text corpora gained significant attention [101], fostering a two-stage learning paradigm of pretraining followed by fine-tuning. Notable examples of such models include ELMo [102], BERT [100], and GPT-3 [103]. These pretrained models have entered an era of large-scale parameters, commonly known as foundation models. Such models have recently achieved unprecedented success across various fields; for example, DALL.E 2 [104] and GPT-4 have made strides in computer vision and natural language generation (NLG). In the biological domain, models like Enformer [105], which predicts gene expression from DNA sequences, scGPT [106], a foundational model for single-cell biology, and scVI [107], a tool for single-cell data integration, cell annotation, and function prediction, have demonstrated the versatile applications of LLMs in single-cell biology and other biological fields. Large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, Gemini [108], and LLaMA [109], developed by OpenAI, Google, and Meta AI, have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in language understanding and generation, especially in conversational contexts.

4.2 NLP Application to Function Annotation

Initially, functional annotation from literature was carried out in an ad hoc fashion using free text with a whole spectrum of terminologies and synonyms. Automated prediction of protein function for large-scale datasets will be possible if there is a universal, controlled, and well-established vocabulary across the species. At the protein level itself, there is conflict in annotation with a lot of protein name variations, synonyms of protein names, non-specific descriptions of function with the textual description of function often varying with different investigators. To reduce the inconsistencies in terminology, Gene ontology consortium (GO)(Gene ontology consortium, 2004) and Enzyme Commission (EC) number have tried to organize the functional annotation data in a structured manner with minimum ambiguity but with a lot of manual reviewing. These

processes are time-consuming and there is a huge gap between the annotations and published literature. Experimental annotation is only available for a restricted number of proteins. The function annotation of a vast number of proteins is achieved through ongoing extrapolation, which means that errors are anticipated. Even in manual curation, there can be a conflict in the assignment by different curators. Tools like Unifunc [110] and DeepText2GO [111] which use semantic text representation along with other protein information like homology, families, domain, and motif, that try to unify functional information from multiple sources to increase confidence in functional annotation, do automated sequence analysis with automated annotation using text mining either from gene ontology annotation or literature.

One of the main complexities in using automated processes or NLP is that the rules are very complex to define the manual annotation process, that has been followed for decades. Yet another complexity lies in the fact that it is difficult to assign the correct ontology, especially in the case of new functions where new terms need to be assigned.

NLP-based models for literature mining can be applied for the identification of a protein’s capability to perform a certain function in a binary form. Literature mining in these cases may be able to capture an already-reported function from published literature repositories like PubMed, which was not added to the functional annotation of the protein in standard databases.

4.3 Large Language Model (LLM) Applications to Function Prediction

4.3.1 General Principles

An alternative to utilizing evolutionary information or sequence and structural attributes for function identification is the application of deep learning to derive insights from the ever-expanding protein sequence data, which can be perceived as the language of proteins, similar to the natural language processing of textual data. These protein language models can learn the evolutionary, structural, and functional aspects of the proteins via self-supervised learning. These methods can be trained on the billions of protein sequences found in the databases and then used for answering different questions with fine-tuning on the specific dataset. The main advantage of these methods is the fact that because of the capability of transfer learning the computationally costly training needs to be performed only once during training after which the embeddings can be used for further tasks [112, 113].

Earlier, a supervised NLP technique that trains input sequences alongside their labels has been utilized for detecting structural similarity and predicting stability [42, 114]. The introduction of transformers in language modeling has facilitated the emergence of unsupervised training across various tasks. Many of the current models utilize a BERT-like architecture with denoising autoencoding, wherein the training process includes masking tokens and attempting to reconstruct the original text. The primary function

of these is encoding, although this can be expanded to develop generative models. The representation learning for proteins has been reviewed in detail by Ofer et al. [47].

Naturally developed proteins are usually made up of repeated modular parts with small differences that can be rearranged and put together in a hierarchical way, just like natural language. Most of these language models have been developed for natural language processing and cannot be directly applied to protein sequences. Proteins are not completely made of pre-defined blocks like words or sentences, have highly variable lengths, and involve very distant interactions due to folding, making LLM-based function prediction more complex [115].

4.3.2 LLMs for Proteins

Several large language models (LLMs) trained on protein data have been developed and UniProtKB also provides per-protein and per-residue embeddings generated from ProtT5 model (a sample use case is shown in Fig. 1 and detailed tutorial is being available at https://github.com/Sciwhylab/protein-embedding-andclassification.git) for several model organism (https://ftp.uniprot. org/pub/databases/uniprot/current_release/knowledgebase/ embeddings/). A detailed survey of various other embeddings available for protein function prediction methods is presented in Table 2, with the key characteristics of each of them. In most cases, LLM-based encodings and in some examples combinations of multiple embeddings have been shown to provide high predictive performance [116–121]. These embeddings are particularly helpful in high throughput benchmarks [122]. A recent review compared the performance of several protein language models in several tasks and found evolutionary scale modeling (ESM) and ProtTans both bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) for models that outperform other protein representation methods [123]. The embedding-based annotation transfer (EAT) has been shown to be very efficient in attempts to predict the molecular function, binding sites, secondary structure, disorder, and transmembrane regions. Structure prediction models (RoseTTAFold [77], ALPHAFold 2 [75]) can be used to add structural


Fig. 1 General workflow of utilizing LLM-based embeddings in predicting DNA-binding proteins

able 2

ProtLLM PubMed,Uniprot,STRING Protein-encoder,Cross-modal connectors,LLM,Dynamic protein mounting mechanism Protein-as-word language modeling,interleaved protein-text dataset generation-InterPT [155]

ontinued able2

Protein LLMs Data source Implementation specification Prediction task Reference
ESM2 UniRef50 Used attention mechanism, 15 billion parameters trained, Executed completely on GPU End-to-end atomic level structure prediction, Efficient prediction of protein structure [76]

information to these prediction models to increase the accuracy of function prediction. Several studies have used pretrained protein models, which include ESMFold [76], ProtTrans [124], UniRep [114], ProteinBert [115], and ProtNLM [125].

4.3.3 Tokenization

Since proteins do not have well-defined vocabulary, word-level tokenization is not an option. The simplest and most common method of tokenization used for proteins is an individual amino acid or character-level tokenization. However, in character-level tokenization though the number of tokens will be much less, more tokens must be used to form a sentence which again increases the memory requirement. Bag of words (BoW) approach where the amino acid count is utilized has been used in many bioinformatics approaches. Other sequence representations like the content or occurrence of 2D structure or representation of amino acid properties like hydrophobicity/polarity can be used with the BoW. Amino acid level N-gram/k-mer representations can also be used but could lead to a sparsity in the feature space. K-mer symmetry can be used meaning “ALG” and “GLA” can be treated as the same feature to reduce the dimensionality [47].

Altogether LLMs for function prediction have provided a strong alternative to sequence-based annotation by homology detection or template-based search and continue to show an improving performance. It is hoped function prediction of natural and synthetic proteins will become possible from sequence in the future just like the structure determination has been revolutionized by AlphaFold-like programs.

5 Conclusion

Function prediction from sequence, structure, and other contextual information has remained a highly active area of bioinformatics research. Natural language and LLM-based methods have not only enabled faster mining of published literature but also paved the way for representing amino acid sequences in latent lower-dimensional space, which allows their use in machine learning models. Different types of embedding, tokenization, transfer learning, and training methods distinguish LLM models, and improvements are being made in each direction. It is expected that in the future most function prediction can be made directly from sequence with a comprehensive use of sequence and structure-based encoding, just like similar success stories of structure prediction.

neural networks. NAR Genomics Bioinformatics 4(1)
