PLMSearch and PLMAlign: Protein Language Model (PLM)-Based Homologous Protein Sequence Search and Alignment

Wei Liu, Ziye Wang, Ronghui You, Chenghan Xie, Hong Wei, Yi Xiong, Jianyi Yang, and Shanfeng Zhu

Abstract

Homologous protein search and sequence alignment are fundamental tasks in computational biology. While structure search methods can provide valuable insights, inferring distant evolutionary relationships with sequences alone remains challenging. Here, we introduce PLMSearch and PLMAlign, two protein language model (PLM)-based tools for searching and aligning remote homologous sequences. PLMSearch is a novel method for homologous protein search with only sequences as input. It leverages deep representations from a pretrained protein language model and trains the similarity prediction model with a large number of real structure similarities, enabling it to detect remote homologies embedded behind the sequences. PLMAlign is a protein sequence alignment tool designed to accurately align the identified homologous pairs. We offer two distinct Web servers for PLMSearch and PLMAlign, freely available at https://dmiip.sjtu.edu.cn/PLMSearch and https://dmiip.sjtu.edu.cn/PLMAlign, respectively. We demonstrate how to operate the Web servers and interpret the resulting outputs. Furthermore, we offer a large range of free and open resources, including open-source code, datasets, and containers, all aimed at advancing scientific research. We also demonstrate how these resources can be leveraged to run PLMSearch and PLMAlign either locally or in the cloud.

Key words Protein language model, Homologous protein search, Protein sequence alignment, Large-scale vector database search, Protein structure similarity prediction

1 Introduction

Homologous protein search is a critical and fundamental component in computational biology, serving as a cornerstone for nearly all sequence-related research, such as protein function prediction [1–11], protein–protein interaction prediction [12], and protein– phenotype association prediction [13]. The goal of homologous protein search is to identify homologous proteins for a given query from a target dataset (typically a large-scale standardized database

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_14, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

like UniProt [14]), ranking those with a higher likelihood of homology at the top. Homologous protein search methods can be broadly categorized into sequence search and structure search, depending on the type of input data.

Due to the low cost and extensive availability of sequence data, sequence similarity-based methods are the most commonly used for homologous protein search. Examples include MMseqs2 [15], BLASTp [16], and Diamond [17]. Despite their success in homology detection, identifying distant evolutionary relationships based solely on sequence similarity remains challenging [18]. When sequence identity falls below 0.3, profile HMM-based methods such as HMMER [19], HHsearch [20], and HHblits [21, 22] tend to be more effective for homologous protein search.

In cases involving highly distant evolutionary relationships, sequences may have diverged so significantly that detecting their relatedness becomes increasingly difficult. Given that protein structures tend to evolve more slowly than sequences, detecting similarities through 3D structural superposition offers higher sensitivity [23–27]. Nonetheless, sequence search methods remain faster and more convenient in the vast majority of cases.

Meanwhile, protein language models (PLMs), such as ESMs [28–31] and ProtTrans [32], which are trained on hundreds of millions of unlabeled protein sequences using self-supervised tasks like masked amino acid prediction, have shown impressive performance across various downstream tasks [33]. These include structure-related tasks like secondary structure prediction and contact prediction [34]. Recently, DEDAL [35], DeepBLAST [36], and the latest pLM-BLAST [37] have demonstrated that integrating deep representations of protein sequences with alignment algorithms such as Smith-Waterman (SW) [38] or Needleman-Wunsch (NW) [39] can lead to more accurate pairwise sequence alignment and homology detection. However, protein language models are not fully utilized for the large-scale protein sequence search.

To enhance sensitivity while preserving the universality and efficiency of sequence search, we introduce PLMSearch (Fig. 1a–c [40], reproduced from PLMSearch: Protein language model powers accurate and fast sequence search for remote homology 2024 with permission from Nature Communications). In addition, we present PLMAlign (Fig. 1d), a sequence alignment tool designed to precisely align the homologous pairs identified by PLMSearch, both leveraging protein language models. We outline the architecture of our methods and provide detailed guidance on how to use the Web server and interpret the results. Finally, we explain how to utilize the extensive open-source resources we offer to run PLMSearch and PLMAlign either locally or in the cloud, enabling the reproduction of our experimental findings.


Fig. 1 Overview of PLMSearch and PLMAlign

2 Materials and Method

2.1 Architecture

PLMSearch primarily consists of three key steps (Fig. 1a–c): (1) PfamClan filters out protein pairs that share the same Pfam clan domain [41]. (2) The SS-predictor (Structural Similarity predictor) estimates the similarity between all query-target pairs using embeddings generated by the protein language model. PLMSearch will not lose much sensitivity without structures as input, because it uses the protein language model to capture remote homology information from deep sequence embeddings. In addition, the SS-predictor used in this step uses the structural similarity (TM-score) as the ground truth for training. This allows PLMSearch to acquire reliable similarity even without structures as input. (3) PLMSearch then ranks the pre-filtered pairs by their predicted similarity and outputs the search results for each query protein.

It is important to note that PLMSearch can only predict the similarity between protein pairs but is unable to provide alignment suggestions. To address this, we introduce PLMAlign, a sequence alignment method based on protein language models (Fig. 1d). In the final integrated pipeline, PLMSearch \(^ +\) PLMAlign, PLMAlign

2.2 Predictive Quality and Runtime

generates sequence alignments and alignment scores for the protein pairs filtered by PLMSearch.

The original manuscript [40] presents our method’s evaluation results on the all-versus-all search test using the SCOPe40-test dataset. PLMSearch performs favorably across most metrics, particularly at the superfamily and fold levels, which have less significant similarity between protein sequences and are more challenging to detect. Specifically, PLMSearch outperforms MMseqs2 in AUROC by factors of 3, 16, and 219 at the family level (improving from 0.318 to 0.928), superfamily level (from 0.050 to 0.826), and fold level (from 0.002 to 0.438), respectively. The primary driver of this improvement is PLMSearch’s robustness, which reduces the rank of the first false positive (FP), significantly increasing the number of true positives (TPs) up to the first FP (outperforming MMseqs2 by 38 times, from 2.74 to 104.78). In the integrated workflow of PLMSearch \(^ +\) PLMAlign, PLMAlign aligns protein pairs that PLMSearch predicts to have a similarity score above 0.3. The alignment score is subsequently used for reranking, resulting in improved precision, particularly in cases involving novel proteins that lack identifiable Pfam domains.

The original manuscript [40] also demonstrates that by utilizing the SS-predictor to estimate similarity instead of computing structural similarity (TM-score) across all protein pairs, both SS-predictor (10 s) and PLMSearch (4 s) rank among the fastest methods available, achieving speeds over four orders of magnitude faster than TM-align (11,303 s).

Overall, PLMSearch consistently ranks among the top methods, offering an optimal balance between accuracy and speed. Specifically, like MMseqs2, PLMSearch can process millions of query-target protein pairs within seconds, yet it achieves a more than threefold increase in sensitivity, rivaling state-of-the-art structure search methods. Additionally, the integrated pipeline of PLMSearch \(^ +\) PLMAlign not only addresses the limitation of PLMSearch’s inability to provide alignments but also avoids the inefficiency of direct use of PLMAlign without pre-filtering, thereby ensuring the efficiency of the pipeline.

It is noteworthy that PLMSearch can achieve comparable efficiency on our publicly accessible Web server, which relies solely on CPU (64 * Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5–2682 v4 @ 2.50 GHz with 512 GB RAM). Searching a query protein against Swiss-Prot (568 K proteins) and UniRef50 (53.6 M proteins), followed by employing PLMAlign to align the query with the Top-10 targets [14], takes approximately 0.2 and \(2 . 3 \ \mathrm { m i n }\) , respectively (Fig. 2). This efficiency is achieved as PLMSearch pre-generates and preloads the embeddings for all target proteins. This strategy significantly reduces time by eliminating the need for repeated forward propagations of the protein language model, which is parameter-


Fig. 2

heavy, and minimizes the time spent loading embeddings from disk to RAM. Consequently, only a single forward pass through the SS-predictor network is required to predict the similarity for millions of query-target pairs.

3 Web Server

The Web server interfaces for PLMSearch and PLMAlign are highly user-friendly. PLMSearch is freely accessible at https://dmiip.sjtu. edu.cn/PLMSearch, and PLMAlign is available at https://dmiip. sjtu.edu.cn/PLMAlign. Users simply require a modern Web browser (such as Firefox, Internet Explorer, Microsoft Edge, Mac Safari, or Chrome) and an internet connection to access the Web servers.

3.1 Submission to PLMSearch

Users can submit queries and check results through the following five steps (Fig. 3):

  1. Input a protein sequence(s). Users can either type the sequences directly into the provided text area or upload them from a file. If both methods are used, the server will prioritize the sequence(s) in the uploaded file. Only the FASTA format is acceptable, and all sequences must be in the standard singleletter amino acid code ( ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTV WYVBZX*).
  2. Choose a target dataset. For the target dataset, users have the option to select from Swiss-Prot (568 K proteins), PDB (680 K proteins), UniRef50 (53.6 M proteins), or the query dataset itself (Self).


Fig. 3


Fig. 4 PLMSearch guidance page after submissions

  1. Choose a method. Users can choose between PLMSearch or the SS-predictor. Brief descriptions are as follows:

• PLMSearch. This method uses pairs pre-filtered by Pfam-Clan, rather than searching all protein pairs from scratch.
• SS-predictor. This calculates the similarity across all proteins, ranking pairs based on this similarity.

  1. Submission. After making their selections, users can press the “Submit” button to process the prediction. The server then provides a Job ID and a Web link to the results (Fig. 4). For example, using the submitted protein “P32352,” the Job ID is


Fig. 5


Fig. 6

“1723984300”users can also use the “Reset” button to clear all inputs.

  1. Checking Results. Users can access their search results at any time by entering the previously provided Job ID on the “Check” page (Fig. 5). Alternatively, users can directly enter the Web link provided by the server into their browser to access the results.

3.2 Results Generated by PLMSearch

Users can “Download” or “Preview” the search results on the results page (Fig. 6).

1. Download

• Query: All the query proteins in the Fasta format.
• Similarity: The query-target pairs and their predicted similarity.

• NW sequence identity: Sequence identity from Needleman-Wunsch algorithm.
• NW alignment: Sequence alignment from Needleman-Wunsch algorithm.
• PLMAlign score: Score from PLMAlign.
• PLMAlign alignment: Sequence alignment from PLMAlign.

2. Preview

Each table is presented for a query protein.

Each row contains a target protein, arranged in descending order of similarity.

Some relevant information is provided for each target protein. Column by column from left to right: Target (Protein ID), Similarity, UniProt Link, AlphaFold Link, Sequence & Structure & (Avg. pLDDT), Protein Name, Organism, Taxonomic Identifier.

Note that depending on the target data set you choose, the style of the output table will be different.

3. Reference similarity

Researchers often seek to understand the approximate similarity indicative of homologous protein pairs. In response, we have calculated the posterior probabilities for proteins at certain similarities to assess whether they share the same or different folds, as defined by SCOP.

Generally, a score lower than 0.3 suggests that the proteins do not share the same fold. For a more detailed discussion, refer to the “Reference Similarity” Supplement Section in the original manuscript [40].

3.3 Submission to PLMAlign

Users can submit query and target proteins and check results through the following three steps (Fig. 7):

1. Input query and target protein sequence(s)

The sequences can either be typed directly into the text area, or uploaded from a file. If both, the server will only consider the sequence(s) in the uploaded file.

Only the FASTA format is acceptable. All sequences have to be amino acids specified in a single-letter code ( ACDEF GHIKLMNPQRSTVWYVBZX*).

2. Submit

Press the “Submit” button to process the alignment. A Job ID and a Web link to the results will be provided.

Use the “Reset” button to clear all the inputs.


Fig. 7

3. Checking Results

Similar to the PLMSearch Web server, users can access their search results through either a Job ID or the provided Web link.

3.4 Results Generated by PLMAlign

Users can “Download” or “Preview” the alignment results on the results page (Fig. 8).

1. Download

• Query: All the query proteins in the Fasta format.
• Target: All the target proteins in the Fasta format.
• Score: The query-target pairs and their score.
• Alignment: Sequence alignment results of query-target pairs.
• Sorted score: Consistent with “Score,” but the protein pairs are sorted from high to low scores.


Fig. 8

2. Preview

Each table is presented for a query protein.

Each row contains a target protein, arranged in descending order of score.

3. Reference similarity

For a more detailed discussion, refer to the “Reference Similarity” Supplement Section in the original manuscript [40].

4 Open-Source Resources

4.1 Code and Visual Reproduction

Our structural visualizations employ PyMOL v.2.4.0, accessible at https://github.com/schrodinger/pymol-open-source. For data visualization related to PLMSearch, we utilize Python (version 3.8.16), Seaborn (version 0.12.2), and matplotlib-base (version 3.6.2).

1. PLMSearch

PLMSearch is freely available on GitHub at https://github. com/maovshao/PLMSearch. Specifically, researchers can set up

and utilize the PLMSearch pipeline locally with a Jupyter Notebook, available at https://github.com/maovshao/PLMSearch/ blob/main/pipeline.ipynb. Additionally, researchers can replicate PLMSearch experiments and regenerate figures in both the main text and supplementary materials using another Jupyter Notebook, found at https://github.com/maovshao/PLMSearch/blob/ main/main.ipynb.

2. PLMAlign

PLMAlign is also freely available to the public on GitHub at https://github.com/maovshao/PLMAlign. Researchers can deploy and use the PLMAlign pipeline locally with a Jupyter Notebook available at https://github.com/maovshao/PLMAlign/ blob/main/pipeline.ipynb. Additionally, experiments on malidup, malisam, and scope40 can be replicated using three separate Jupyter Notebooks at https://github.com/maovshao/PLMAlign/blob/ main/malidup.ipynb, h t tp s://gi thub.com/maov shao/ PLMAlign/blob/main/malisam.ipynb, and https://github.com/ maovshao/PLMAlign/blob/main/scope40.ipynb, respectively.

3. Dataset

Furthermore, we have published our experimental data for PLMSearch and PLMAlign on Zenodo at https://zenodo.org/ records/11480660 and the target datasets used on our Web server at https://zenodo.org/records/11393990.

4.2 Containers

The Code Ocean platform standardizes the creation, tracking, and sharing of computational experiments in scientific research. It offers open, exportable, and readily reproducible containers—container technologies specifically designed for scientific investigations. Each container represents an independent computational experiment, encompassing the computing environment, code, data, version history, and results, facilitating the reproducibility of experiments contained within for researchers at any later date.

The results of PLMSearch can be directly replicated using a container published on Code Ocean, accessible at https://doi.org/ 10.24433/CO.8325548.v1. This allows researchers to replicate findings without the need to download source code or set up a local environment.

4.3 Limitation

  1. Query: The maximum supported number of query proteins for each submission of PLMSearch and PLMAlign is 100. If the number of proteins in your job exceeds, just divide them into separate jobs and submit them.
  2. Similarity: Only protein pairs with a similarity higher than 0.3 will be retained (0.5 for UniRef50). The maximum value of the total number of query-target pairs is 10,000. In other words, the server will return the Top-K highest similarity for each

protein in n queries, and \(K = 1 0 , 0 0 0 / \mathrm { n \Omega }\) (If one query’s actual search results are less than K, it will not be affected).

  1. NW & PLMAlign: For each query, always return Top-10 alignment results.

Use our source code to implement more searches (github. com/maovshao/PLMSearch) and alignments (github.com/ maovshao/PLMAlign).

Large Context, Deeper Insights: Harnessing Large Language Models for Advancing Protein–Protein Interaction Analysis

Kaicheng U, Sophia Meixuan Zhang, Suresh Pokharel, Pawel Pratyush, Farah Qaderi, Dongfang Liu, Junhan Zhao, Dukka B. KC, and Siwei Chen

Abstract

Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) are involved in nearly all biological processes. Understanding and analysis of PPI is key to revealing biological networks and identifying new therapeutic targets. Various computational approaches have been proposed as an alternative to the experimental investigation of PPIs. More recently, with the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs), a plethora of approaches using LLMs have been developed, enabling efficient analysis of interaction networks and binding sites directly from protein sequences. These models capture intricate biological patterns, offering scalability and adaptability across diverse datasets. However, challenges remain, including computational costs, data imbalance, and the integration of multimodal information. Advancements in addressing these limitations are set to further enhance the potential of LLMs in protein–protein interaction analysis, driving deeper insights and broader applications in biological research.

Key words Protein language model, Protein–protein interaction (PPI), Large language models (LLMs), Sequence-based models, PPI prediction

1 Introduction

Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) are the functional orchestration of cellular mechanisms involved in nearly all biological processes. Understanding PPIs and their specific interaction interfaces is key to revealing biological networks and identifying new therapeutic targets, especially in diseases driven by dysregulated PPIs [1–5]. Experimental investigation of PPIs is often labor-intensive and costly with traditional methods like protein arrays, Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H) systems, or newer technologies such as mass spectrometry and protein-fragment complementation assays

(PCAs) [6–9]. In contrast, in silico bioinformatics approaches offer promising and efficient alternatives [10–13].

Deep learning has paved new avenues for exploring PPIs at an unprecedented scale and depth. Previous studies have utilized various deep neural networks, including convolutional, graph, and language models, to extract protein structural features for predicting PPIs [14–16]. The field has observed the application of large language models (LLMs), originally developed for natural language processing (NLP). Pretrained on large-scale protein sequence datasets, these models have shown an unmatched ability to capture intricate biological patterns and predict protein structures and functions [17–19]. This chapter outlines key biologically relevant applications in PPI research where LLMs hold transformative potential and highlights recent exploratory advancements in LLM-based approaches, from predicting PPI networks to protein interaction sites and beyond. In the beginning, we describe LLM-based approaches for predicting protein–protein interactions followed by LLM-based approaches for predicting protein–protein interaction sites. In both sections, we initially summarize some existing computational approaches followed by LLM-based approaches, summary and trends, and then limitations and future directions. Finally, we briefly describe other potential applications of LLMs for protein–protein interaction analysis.

2 Predicting Protein–Protein Interactions: Unveiling New Connections

Approximately \(8 0 \%\) of proteins interact with others to perform essential cellular functions, and disruption of such interactions can lead to disease states such as cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, and infectious diseases [20–22]. Understanding PPIs is fundamental for interpreting cellular mechanisms, identifying therapeutic targets, and characterizing the roles of proteins with limited functional annotation [23]. Experimental techniques for determining PPIs, meanwhile, are time-consuming, resourceintensive, and are challenged by high rates of false positive and false negative predictions [24, 25]. These limitations highlight the necessity for computational approaches that can predict PPIs efficiently and accurately, leveraging the wealth of genomic, and proteomic data now available [22, 26].

2.1 Computational Approaches for Protein–Protein Interaction Prediction

Early computational methods for PPI prediction relied on machine learning (ML) algorithms such as support vector machines (SVMs), random forests, and decision trees [22, 25, 26]. These approaches utilized manually engineered features derived from protein sequences, such as amino acid composition, physicochemical properties, and evolutionary information like position-specific scoring matrices (PSSMs) [25, 27]. While these methods expanded the

field’s understanding of PPI prediction, they were constrained by their dependence on feature engineering and often lacked scalability and generalizability across different datasets [25, 26]. The advent of deep learning introduced models capable of automatic feature extraction, significantly improving prediction performance [22]. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) were employed to capture local and global sequence patterns, respectively. For example, models like DeepPPI incorporated statistical descriptors alongside deep neural networks to refine feature representation [28]. Additionally, graph-based models, such as graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and graph neural networks (GNNs), modeled proteins as nodes and their interactions as edges, effectively capturing the structural and topological characteristics of interaction networks [27]. Despite these advancements, traditional deep learning models faced methodological limitations, including overfitting in scenarios with constrained training data and a lack of generalizability across species, often due to biases introduced by sequence similarity [29].

2.2 LLM-Based Approaches for Protein–Protein Interaction Prediction

In this section, we briefly review recent advances in PPI prediction using LLMs. LLMs pretrained on extensive protein sequence databases have transformed PPI prediction. These models, including ESM-2 [20], ProtBERT [21, 30], ProtT5 [30], and SeqVec [31], treat protein sequences analogously to sentences in natural language, capturing contextual and evolutionary information without relying on multiple sequence alignments [23–27, 32, 33]. Transformer architectures enable these models to identify long-range dependencies within protein sequences, which are critical for understanding structural and functional properties [23, 24, 32, 33]. One example is the work by Jha et al. [24], who developed a framework integrating SeqVec [31] embeddings based on ELMo language models [34] with Graph-BERT [24, 35], a graph-based transformer model. By representing protein pairs as nodes within a graph and using linkless subgraphs, the model addresses limitations like suspended animation and over-smoothing encountered in traditional GCNs [24]. It achieved \(9 9 . 1 0 \%\) accuracy on the human PPI dataset, demonstrated strong cross-species generalization, and effectively managed imbalanced datasets. Performance metrics, including sensitivity and specificity, consistently outperformed earlier approaches across multiple benchmarks.

Similarly, xCAPT5 [23] uses a hybrid classifier that integrates ProtT5-XL-UniRef50 [30] embeddings with multi-kernel CNNs within a Siamese architecture. ProtT5 provides rich amino acid embeddings capturing contextual, evolutionary, and functional information. The multi-kernel CNNs capture both local and global sequence patterns, refining these features through pooling and concatenation. By integrating XGBoost [36] for enhanced classification, xCAPT5 achieved state-of-the-art performance across

various datasets, with accuracy rates of \(9 7 . 2 7 \%\) on the Martin dataset [37] and over \(9 9 \%\) on the Guo and Pan datasets [38, 39]. The model outperformed existing models, demonstrating robustness across species and maintaining high recall and F1 scores even under stringent sequence similarity thresholds. However, the model’s recall-oriented design may compromise precision in certain applications, and it requires extensive hyperparameter tuning.

The sequence-structure-function (S2F) multimodal pretraining model broadens the scope of LLM applications by integrating sequence, structural topology, and functional annotations to produce embeddings that outperform single-modality methods [26]. S2F employs a single-stream transformer for encoding and pretraining tasks like masked sequence modeling and sequencestructure-function alignment. This multimodal framework enhances PPI predictions, particularly in cases involving structural flexibility and disordered regions. The study reported that S2F embeddings outperformed existing methods such as ELMo [34], TAPE [40], and ProSE [41] across tasks, including cross-species PPIs, antibody–antigen interactions, and mutation-driven affinity predictions.

Moreover, Wang et al. introduced a biological language model for PPI prediction that only relies on protein sequence data [25]. Their method employs Bio2Vec, a feature representation technique based on “bio-word” segmentation and the Skip-Gram word model, to generate embeddings capturing local and global semantic features of protein sequences [42]. These embeddings are processed through CNN for prediction, achieving superior performance across datasets, including human, yeast, and Helicobacter pylori. This approach reinforces the value of analyzing protein sequences with language models.

In another application, TT3D combines sequence and structural information to predict PPIs genome-wide [43]. By combining one-hot-encoded 3Di sequences from Foldseek with embeddings from a protein language model [44], TT3D demonstrated higher precision and recall in cross-species PPI prediction compared to baseline methods. The model excels in generalizing predictions to nonhuman species, achieving significantly higher precision than sequence/structure homology transfer approaches. However, it relies on precomputed structural models; predictions for proteins without such data default to uninformative 3Di sequences.

Furthermore, ProLLM represents a novel approach by converting PPI prediction into a natural language processing task using Protein Chain of Thought (ProCoT) [33]. ProCoT simulates stepwise signal transduction in proteins, enhancing the model’s understanding of signaling pathways. By replacing standard embeddings with those derived from ProtTrans [30] and fine-tuning the model on the Mol dataset, ProLLM outperformed GNN- and LLM-based baselines with high micro-F1 scores across multiple benchmark

2.3 Summary and Trends for LLM-Based Approaches for PPI Prediction

datasets. The model excels in reasoning indirect interactions and predicting complex multi-step relationships in signaling pathways. However, dependency on curated datasets means performance is tied to the quality of signaling pathway annotations, and larger LLMs exhibited instability compared to smaller, optimized backbones. LLM-based approaches described for PPI prediction described in this chapter are summarized in Table 1.

The integration of LLMs into PPI prediction has enabled more nuanced and comprehensive analyses, facilitating advancements across various biological and biomedical applications. For instance, the Graph-BERT framework has been instrumental in mapping large-scale PPI networks [24], providing insights into complex interaction patterns that were previously difficult to discern. Its ability to generalize across species makes it a valuable tool for comparative genomics and evolutionary studies, allowing researchers to infer PPIs in less-studied organisms based on human data. xCAPT5 has proven effective in diverse PPI prediction scenarios, including intra-species and cross-species interactions [23]. Its robust performance under stringent sequence similarity thresholds suggests its utility in studying novel or highly divergent proteins, which is particularly relevant in the context of emerging pathogens or rare genetic variants. The integration of XGBoost further enhances its classification capabilities [36], making it suitable for applications requiring high precision and recall, such as identifying potential drug targets or understanding disease mechanisms. The S2F model has significant implications for drug discovery and therapeutic development [26]. By effectively predicting antibody– antigen interactions and mutation-driven affinity changes, it aids in the design of more effective vaccines and antibody-based therapies. Additionally, its ability to handle structurally flexible and intrinsically disordered regions expands its applicability to a wider range of proteins. Bio2Vec [25] offers a scalable and linguistically inspired approach to PPI prediction, making it suitable for high-throughput analyses and large-scale proteomic studies. TT3D enhances genome-wide PPI predictions by incorporating structural information [43], which is crucial for understanding the mechanistic basis of protein interactions. This method is particularly useful in structural biology and computational docking studies, where accurate PPI predictions can streamline the identification of viable protein complexes for experimental validation. ProLLM introduces a novel reasoning-based approach to PPI prediction, leveraging the concept of Protein Chain of Thought to model signaling pathways [33]. This capability is particularly valuable in systems biology and network medicine, where understanding the flow of signals through protein networks is essential for deciphering cellular processes and identifying intervention points for therapeutic strategies. Overall, the application of LLMs in PPI prediction has not only

able 1

Method Method type Key features Applications/results
Graph-BERT with SeqVec embeddings [24] GNN, LLM Integrates SeqVec embeddings (based on ELMo language models) with graph-BERT Achieved 99.10% accuracy on human PPI dataset Demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity across multiple PPI datasets Effectively handled imbalanced datasets
Represents protein pairs as nodes in a graph
Uses linkless subgraphs to improve representation
xCAPT5 [23] DL, CNN, LLM Uses ProtT5-XL-UniRef50 embeddings for rich contextual information Achieved 97.27% accuracy on the Martin dataset Over 99% accuracy on Guo and Pan datasets
Employs multi-kernel CNNs within a Siamese architecture Maintained high recall under stringent sequence similarity thresholds
Integrates XGBoost for classification Effective for intra-species and cross-species PPI prediction
S2F Model [26] Transformer, LLM Multimodal pretraining integrating sequence, structural topology, and functional annotations Improved cross-species PPI prediction
Single-stream transformer encoding three modalities Pretrained on multiple tasks Enhanced antibody-antigen interaction prediction Effective in mutation-driven affinity change prediction
Outperformed single-modality approaches in capturing complex PPIs
Bio2Vec [25] DL, CNN Generates protein sequence embeddings using bio-word segmentation and skip-gram model Achieved 97.31% accuracy on human dataset
Captures local and global semantic information 93.30% accuracy on yeast dataset
Utilizes CNN for prediction 88.01% accuracy on H. pylori dataset
Demonstrated scalability and effectiveness across different species
ProtInteract [45] DL, CNN Uses Conv-TCN-AE for dimensionality reduction Employs deep CNN for PPI prediction Achieved 95.68% accuracy for Homo sapiens in two-class scenario
Addresses imbalanced datasets with downsampling 92.44% accuracy in three-class scenario
Faster training times compared to Conv-LSTM-AE baseline
Effective across different species datasets
TT3D [43] DL Combines one-hot-encoded 3Di sequences from Foldseek with protein language model embeddings Integrates sequence and structural representations Provided higher early precision in cross-species PPI prediction Improved performance in identifying PPIs for downstream experiments Effective in generalizing predictions to nonhuman species
ProLLM [33] LLM, Transformer Introduces protein chain of thought (ProCoT) for modeling signaling pathways Uses ProtTrans embeddings capturing structural attributes Fine-tuned with domain-specific data Achieved micro-F1 scores: 91.05% on human dataset 78.09% on SHS27k 87.66% on SHS148k 89.21% on STRING dataset Effective in reasoning over signaling pathways and indirect interactions
TUnA [32] Transformer, LLM Combines ESM-2 embeddings with dual transformer encoders Integrates uncertainty estimation using SNGP Quantifies prediction uncertainty Achieved Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) of 0.54 and precision of 0.57 on the Bernett dataset Higher area under precision-recall curve (AUPR) on cross-species datasets compared to previous models Effective in filtering high-confidence predictions
PEFT with LoRA [29] PEFT, LLM Uses LoRA to add low-rank matrices to attention layers Updates less than 1% of model parameters Reduces memory and computational costs Achieved comparable performance to full fine-tuning with significantly less memory usage Enabled fine-tuning large PLMs on standard GPUs Demonstrated scalability to larger datasets

This table summarizes various methods incorporating LLMs, detailing their methodological types, key features, and performance outcomes across different PPI datasets

2.4 Limitations and Future Directions for LLM-Based PPI Prediction

improved the accuracy and scalability of these models but also expanded their utility across a wide range of biological and biomedical research areas. These advancements facilitate deeper insights into cellular mechanisms, enhance drug discovery efforts, and contribute to the development of novel therapeutic interventions.

Despite significant progress, LLM-based models for PPI prediction face several limitations. One major challenge is the high computational cost associated with training and deploying large models [23, 29, 32, 43]. Models like ProtT5-XL-UniRef50 [30] and ESM-2 [20], while powerful, require substantial computational resources, which may not be accessible to all research groups [23, 29, 32, 43]. Efforts to address this include the use of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods such as low-rank adaptation (LoRA) [46], which significantly reduce memory and computational requirements by adding a small number of trainable parameters while achieving performance comparable to traditional fine-tuning [29, 43]. Sledzieski et al. showed that PEFT achieves state-of-the-art results on PPI prediction benchmarks, outperforming traditional fine-tuning and frozen embedding methods. Using embeddings from ESM2 with PEFT reduces GPU memory requirements, enabling deeper model adaptation within resource constraints [29, 43]. Another limitation is the reliance on highquality training data. Models often perform well on datasets with abundant and accurate annotations but may struggle with proteins lacking sufficient sequence similarity or structural information [23]. Class imbalance within datasets further complicates performance, as models tend to prioritize recall over precision in imbalanced scenarios [23]. Moreover, models like TUnA incorporate uncertainty awareness to address the limitations of existing models but still face challenges related to computational cost and sequence length constraints [29, 32, 43].

Future research directions include enhancing model interpretability and integrating additional protein data modalities [47]. Integrating structural predictions from tools like AlphaFold2 [15] could enrich the training data and improve model generalization. Developing methods to better handle intrinsically disordered regions and transient interactions remains a critical area of focus, as these aspects are often underrepresented in current datasets [47]. Expanding the applicability of LLM-based models to genome-wide PPI predictions and exploring their integration with other computational approaches could provide more comprehensive interaction networks [33]. Additionally, further development of parameter-efficient methods tailored for protein language, such as QLoRA, which incorporates model quantization for resource efficiency, may unlock additional performance gains [48]. Scaling protein language models with larger and more diverse datasets, including those from metagenomes, could also enhance

generalizability [33]. Efforts should also focus on optimizing transformer models by adapting specific layers to improve performance in memory-constrained environments and combining more informative embeddings with advanced architectures for PPI prediction [29].

LLM applications in PPI prediction have significantly improved accuracy, scalability, and the capacity to capture complex sequences and structural features. By leveraging large protein sequence datasets and sophisticated architectures, these models address many limitations of traditional computational approaches [45, 47]. While challenges remain, ongoing research and innovations continue to push the boundaries of what is possible in PPI prediction [23, 29, 43, 47]. The integration of LLMs holds immense potential for accelerating biological discoveries and advancing therapeutic development, contributing to a deeper understanding of cellular processes [47].

3 Mapping Interaction Interfaces: Predicting Protein–Protein Interaction Sites

PPIs form dynamic networks that govern a wide range of biological processes. Central to these interactions are the specific binding sites, known as PPI interfaces, which consist of distinct amino acid residues that determine the specificity, affinity, and kinetics of the interactions. These interfaces not only facilitate protein binding but also modulate the strength and stability of the resulting complexes, playing pivotal roles in the assembly and regulation of protein machinery within the cell.

Disruptions at PPI interfaces, whether due to genetic mutations, post-translational modifications, or environmental factors, can lead to aberrant protein interactions and dysregulated cellular activities. Such perturbations are implicated in a wide array of diseases, including cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, cardiovascular diseases, and developmental abnormalities. For instance, mutations that alter interface residues can impede critical signaling pathways or metabolic control mechanisms, leading to pathological conditions [49, 50]. Detailed molecular characterization of PPI interfaces is essential for understanding disease mechanisms and designing targeted therapeutic strategies.

3.1 Modern Computational Approaches for Protein–Protein Interaction Site Prediction

The computational prediction of PPI sites has evolved significantly, transitioning from traditional ML methods to sophisticated deep learning architectures. Early ML approaches relied heavily on engineered features derived from protein sequences and structures, such as amino acid composition, evolutionary conservation scores, physicochemical properties, and secondary structure elements. Statistical classifiers like support vector machines, random forests, and ensemble methods were employed to identify patterns indicative of

interaction sites [49, 50]. For example, the Interactome INSIDER tool [50] employed the ECLAIR algorithm, an ensemble machine learning classifier, to predict unresolved PPI interfaces by integrating features like co-evolutionary metrics and docking-based scores [51]. By leveraging both sequence-based and structural features, ECLAIR achieved high predictive performance, allowing for the large-scale prediction of PPI interfaces across the human proteome and several model organisms. This approach deepened our understanding of how genomic variants may disrupt protein interactions, as evidenced by the enrichment of disease-associated mutations at predicted interface sites. Traditional ML methods, while valuable, faced limitations due to their reliance on handcrafted features and their inability to capture the complex, nonlinear relationships inherent in protein interactions. To address these challenges, researchers turned to deep learning, which offers the capability to automatically learn hierarchical representations from raw data.

CNNs have been widely applied due to their strength in capturing local spatial patterns. DeepRank, a CNN-based framework, transforms three-dimensional protein structures into voxelized grids encoding physicochemical properties [27]. By applying convolutional filters over these grids, DeepRank learns spatial features associated with PPI interfaces. The model was effective in classifying biological versus crystallographic interfaces and ranking docking models. However, CNNs face challenges in handling rotational and translational invariance inherent in protein structures, often necessitating extensive data augmentation to capture different orientations and conformations. To overcome these limitations, GNNs emerged as a powerful alternative. GNNs represent proteins as graphs, with residues as nodes and interactions as edges, naturally accommodating the complex topology of protein structures. DeepRank-GNN extends the DeepRank framework by employing GNNs to process protein–protein interfaces [52]. By applying message-passing algorithms, the model captures both local and global structural relationships, providing rotation and translation invariance without requiring data augmentation. DeepRank-GNN demonstrated strong performance in docking model scoring and differentiating between biological and crystallographic protein dimers, highlighting the efficacy of GNNs in PPI site prediction [52].

Another notable GNN-based approach is PIQLE, which employs a multi-head graph attention network to assess the quality of PPI interfaces [53]. PIQLE represents interfacial residues as nodes and incorporates multimeric interaction geometries, such as interatomic distances and orientations, as edge features. The attention mechanism allows the model to focus on critical interactions within complex multimeric interfaces, enhancing the estimation of interface quality. PIQLE has consistently outperformed existing methods like DProQA [54] and TRScore [55] in benchmarking

on various datasets. Furthermore, methods that integrate evolutionary information have been shown to enhance prediction accuracy. PIQLE incorporates multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) to leverage evolutionary conservation, which allows the model to capture conserved patterns across homologous proteins, improving the detection of critical interface residues.

Ensemble neural networks have also been employed to combine the strengths of different architectures. PIPENN, an ensemble framework, integrates multiple neural network architectures including fully connected networks, CNNs, dilated convolutional networks, U-Nets, residual networks, and recurrent neural networks [56]. The ensemble approach leverages the strengths of each architecture to address the limitations of individual models, though it increases computational complexity and may require careful tuning to optimize the balance among the models. PIPENN utilized U-Net architectures, which are particularly effective in biomedical image segmentation and can capture both local and contextual information through their encoder-decoder structures. Residual networks (ResNets) [57] have been employed to facilitate the training of deeper networks by mitigating the vanishing gradient problem, allowing the models to learn more complex features. RNNs [58], including long short-term memory (LSTM) units, have been used to model sequential dependencies in protein sequences, which capture long-range interactions that may be critical for PPI site prediction.

Despite the advancements brought by deep learning, challenges remain in terms of computational demands and data requirements. Deep learning models often require large amounts of highquality labeled data to train effectively, which can be a limitation where certain annotated datasets are scarce. Additionally, processing three-dimensional structures or large protein complexes can be computationally intensive. These limitations highlight the need for future LLMs that can effectively integrate sequence and structural information while being computationally efficient and scalable. By leveraging the vast amount of protein sequence data and advanced architectures capable of capturing complex dependencies, LLMs offer a promising avenue for improving PPI site prediction.

3.2 LLM-Based Approaches for PPI Site Prediction

Building upon the advancements in traditional computational methods, the integration of LLMs into bioinformatics has transformed PPI site prediction. Inspired by advancements in natural language processing (NLP), transformer architectures have been repurposed to analyze protein sequences, enabling the prediction of PPIs at the sequence level without reliance on structural data. Central to this shift is the conceptualization of protein sequences as linguistic constructs, where amino acids serve as “words” and the entire sequence forms a “sentence.” This analogy allows for the utilization of transformer-based models to capture the intricate

dependencies and contextual relationships among amino acids. Pretrained protein language models such as ESM (Evolutionary Scale Modeling) [59], ProtBERT [21], and ProtT5 [30] have been trained on large protein sequence datasets, generate embeddings that encapsulate local and global sequence features, serving as robust inputs for downstream tasks.

One of the pioneering efforts in this domain is the introduction of an ensemble deep learning model (EDLMPPI), which leverages the transformer-based protein language model ProtT5 [60]. By encoding amino acid sequences into distributed vector representations, ProtT5 captures the global contextual associations inherent in protein sequences. EDLMPPI further enhances its predictive capabilities by integrating Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) networks and capsule networks within its architecture [61]. The BiLSTM networks are adept at capturing long-range dependencies in sequences, essential for modeling interactions that may occur between distant residues. Capsule networks, on the other hand, explore the intrinsic associations between features, allowing the model to understand hierarchical relationships within the data [62]. This ensemble approach not only improves the model’s ability to capture complex patterns relevant to PPI sites but also addresses the challenge of data imbalance commonly encountered in PPI datasets. Moreover, the model offers explainability, providing insights into the protein binding site identification mechanisms and the understanding of biological processes.

In parallel, EnsemPPIS, an ensemble framework, combines transformer-based models with a model employing gated convolutional neural networks (GatCNNPPIS) to predict PPI sites using only protein sequence information [63]. The transformer component, TransformerPPIS, leverages the self-attention mechanism to capture global sequence features and model long-range dependencies. By utilizing ProtBERT [21], a pretrained protein language model, EnsemPPIS generates rich contextual embeddings for amino acid residues, effectively capturing the nuances of protein sequences learned from large-scale protein data. The ensemble nature of EnsemPPIS allows it to integrate the strengths of both models. While TransformerPPIS excels at capturing global features and long-range interactions, GatCNNPPIS focuses on local sequential features through gated convolutional operations. Notably, EnsemPPIS achieves high accuracy without relying on structural data, making it broadly applicable across the proteome, including proteins without known structures.

The combination of protein language models with GNNs represents another promising direction for PPI site prediction. Pair-EGRET, an edge-aggregated graph attention network, utilizes features extracted from ProtBERT to predict PPI sites [64]. Pair-EGRET represents proteins as k-nearest neighbor graphs based on their three-dimensional structures, capturing spatial relationships

between residues. By combining ProtBERT embeddings with a graph neural network architecture [21], the model leverages both the sequential information captured by the language model and the structural information represented in the graph. A key innovation in Pair-EGRET is the use of a multi-headed cross-attention mechanism, which allows each protein to attend to relevant features of its interacting partner. This mechanism enhances the model’s ability to accurately identify interfacial residues by focusing on important interactions between residues of different proteins. The integration of sequence and structural information, along with the attention mechanism, enables Pair-EGRET to outperform several state-ofthe-art methods in predicting PPI sites. Another example is DeepRank-GNN-esm, which integrates ESM-2 embeddings into a graph neural network framework to predict interaction interfaces and score protein–protein docking models [27]. Unlike sequenceonly methods, this approach constructs residue-level graphs from the 3D structures of protein complexes, treating residues within interaction interfaces as nodes. ESM-2 embeddings replace computationally expensive PSSM features, enabling the model to combine evolutionary, contextual, and structural information for improved accuracy in identifying biologically relevant interfaces and discriminating docking conformations from decoys.

Another significant contribution is MP-BERT (MindSpore Protein BERT), a bidirectional encoder representation model designed to process protein pairs directly [65]. By training MP-BERT on protein pairs, the model inherently understands the interactions between proteins. The pretrained MP-BERT is finetuned for specific tasks: MPB-PPI for predicting PPIs and MPB-PPISP for predicting PPI sites. MP-BERT demonstrates high accuracy in predicting PPIs across various organisms, achieving an aggregated accuracy of \(9 2 . 6 5 \%\) . Importantly, MPB-PPISP predicts interaction site propensity using sequence data alone, achieving competitive performance compared to models that require additional structural features. This approach reduces the computational resources typically associated with methods that rely on evolutionary data such as multiple sequence alignments.

Advancements in inter-protein contact prediction using LLMs are exemplified by DRN-1D2D_Inter, a deep learning-based model that leverages dimensional hybrid residual networks and protein language model embeddings [66]. By employing embeddings from pretrained models like ESM-1b and ESM-MSA-1b, the model predicts residue–residue contacts between interacting proteins [59]. The dimensional hybrid residual networks combine one-dimensional and two-dimensional convolutional operations, effectively capturing both sequential and spatial features. Remarkably, DRN-1D2D_Inter outperforms state-of-the-art methods that rely on structural data, despite using only sequence information. Integrating the predicted contacts into protein–protein docking

significantly improves the accuracy of protein complex structure prediction, opening the possibility of predicting protein complex structures without experimental structural data.

Addressing data challenges and enhancing model interpretability are critical aspects of advancing PPI site prediction. Data imbalance, where non-interacting residues vastly outnumber interacting ones, remains a pervasive issue in PPI site prediction. Ensemble deep learning architectures employed in models like EDLMPPI [60] and EnsemPPIS [63] effectively mitigate this problem by improving generalization and reducing overfitting. Techniques such as attention mechanisms and interpretable deep learning frameworks provide insights into model decision-making, fostering trust in predictions and guiding biological investigations. Graphbased approaches complement LLMs by offering alternative representations for processing protein data. MEG-PPIS utilizes multiscale graph information and Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (EGNNs) for PPI site prediction [67]. MEG-PPIS focuses on leveraging structural information through graph representations and achieves improved speed and accuracy, outperforming other methods. Integrating the complementary strengths of graph-based and LLM methods could lead to hybrid models that combine rich contextual information from sequences and detailed structural relationships.

In conclusion, the integration of LLMs into PPI site prediction represents a significant leap forward in computational biology. Transformer-based architectures and pretrained embeddings allow these models to capture complex patterns and contextual relationships in protein sequences. Continued innovation in hybrid modeling, addressing data challenges, and improving interpretability will drive further progress in this domain, enabling deeper exploration of protein interactions and accelerating discoveries in biology and medicine. The tools described in this section are presented in Table 2.

3.3 Summary and Trends for LLM-Based Approaches for PPI Site Prediction

The development of LLM-based approaches in PPI site prediction represents a significant advancement in our understanding of cellular processes and the acceleration of drug discovery. One of the most impactful applications is the identification of novel therapeutic targets. Accurate prediction of PPI sites allows researchers to pinpoint critical residues involved in disease-related interactions, thereby facilitating the design of molecules that can modulate these interactions with high specificity. For instance, the EDLMPPI model has demonstrated high precision in predicting PPI binding sites, which has informed efforts to target protein interfaces implicated in cancer and neurodegenerative diseases [60]. The explainable nature of EDLMPPI’s predictions enables the identification of key binding residues, which is instrumental in developing targeted therapies with minimized off-target effects. In immunology,

This table provides an overview of diverse methods, including their methodological classifications, distinguishing features, and key application results in the context of PPI interface and site prediction

models such as Pair-EGRET have excelled in predicting antibody– antigen interactions, supporting the design of antibodies with enhanced specificity and affinity, thereby improving therapeutic efficacy against pathogens [64].

Moreover, the integration of LLM-based predictions with structural biology techniques significantly enhances the pipeline for protein complex characterization. Si and Yan illustrated that incorporating predicted inter-protein contacts from their DRN-1D2D_Inter model into docking algorithms like HAD-DOCK markedly improved the accuracy of protein complex structure predictions [66]. This synergy accelerates the structural determination process by providing constraints that guide experimental methods such as cryo-electron microscopy and X-ray crystallography, thereby prioritizing targets for structural studies and conserving valuable time and resources. In biotechnology, understanding PPIs is essential for engineering proteins with novel functions. LLM-based models enable the rational design of proteins by predicting how mutations may affect interaction sites, thereby informing strategies for protein engineering. For example, MEG-PPIS, with its fast runtime and high accuracy, facilitates designing enzymes with improved catalytic properties, developing biosensors, and creating synthetic biological systems [67]. The ability to predict PPIs proteome-wide supports high-throughput screening and accelerates the development of bioengineered products.

The generalization capabilities of models like MP-BERT further expand the utility of LLM-based approaches by enabling crossspecies analysis of PPIs [65]. This is particularly beneficial for studying zoonotic diseases and developing broad-spectrum therapeutics. By predicting PPIs in organisms with limited data, MP-BERT aids in exploring conserved interaction mechanisms and enhancing our understanding of biological diversity. Additionally, the interpretability of LLM-based models enhances their practical utility in biomedical research. EnsemPPIS, for instance, provides insights into the importance of specific residues and interactions, supporting hypothesis generation for experimental validation [63]. This transparency not only increases confidence in the predictions but also aids in uncovering novel biological mechanisms underlying protein interactions.

Overall, the integration of LLM-based methods into PPI site prediction workflows offers substantial improvements in both accuracy and applicability. By leveraging rich sequence information and advanced neural network architectures, these models advance biomedical research from elucidating fundamental biological processes to developing novel therapeutics. As these models continue to evolve, their applications are expected to expand further, driving significant breakthroughs in our understanding and manipulation of protein interactions for therapeutic and biotechnological advancements.

3.4 Limitations and Future Directions for LLM-Based PPI Site Prediction

Despite the significant advancements brought by LLM-based methods in PPI site prediction, several challenges and limitations remain that warrant further investigation. One major limitation is the computational demand associated with training and deploying large models, particularly those utilizing transformer architectures and processing long protein sequences. Models like EDLMPPI [60], EnsemPPIS [63], and DRN-1D2D_Inter [66] require substantial computational resources, which can hinder scalability and accessibility, especially for researchers with limited computational infrastructure.

Future work may focus on optimizing model architectures for efficiency, such as through model compression techniques, efficient transformer variants, or leveraging specialized hardware accelerators to reduce computational overhead. Recent studies have explored deep compression of pretrained transformer models through quantization and sparsity techniques, achieving significant reductions in model size while maintaining accuracy. For instance, Wang et al. demonstrated that transformer models could be quantized down to 4-bit precision and achieve \(5 0 \%\) structural sparsity, resulting in a 16-fold compression without significant loss of performance [69]. Similarly, modular transformers can modularize transformer layers to allow flexible model compression ratios, providing a trade-off between performance and efficiency.

Adapting these compression techniques to protein sequence models could alleviate computational demands and make LLM-based PPI site prediction more accessible.

Another challenge lies in the reliance on high-quality sequence data. While LLMs have shown remarkable ability to learn from vast datasets, the presence of noisy or incomplete data can adversely affect model performance. Sources of noise in biological sequence data include sequencing errors, misannotations, and the presence of low-complexity regions or disordered segments that can confuse the models. For example, improper tokenization of protein sequences can lead to the loss of important contextual information. Tokenization of biological sequences significantly impacts the performance of transformer models [70], and applying alternative tokenization strategies not only increases accuracy but also reduces input length, mitigating the effects of noise and improving interpretability.

Moreover, imbalanced class distributions in PPI datasets pose significant challenges for model training and evaluation. The ratio of positive to negative samples in natural PPI datasets can be as low as \(2 \%\) , leading to models that are biased toward the majority class and have poor performance on the minority class [71]. Addressing this issue requires advanced class balancing methods, such as undersampling, over-sampling, or the use of cost-sensitive learning techniques [71]. For instance, Bhat and Patil highlighted the need to explore or devise advanced class balancing methods to enhance prediction performance in PPI site prediction [72]. Developing models that can effectively handle imbalanced data is essential for improving prediction accuracy.

The presence of homologous sequences in training datasets can also affect the generalization ability of PPI prediction models. Highly similar or identical sequences can lead to overestimation of model performance due to overfitting [73]. To mitigate this issue, constructing non-redundant datasets by removing protein sequence pairs with high identity thresholds is a common practice69. Ensuring diversity in training data helps models learn interaction-specific features rather than relying on sequence similarity alone.

Furthermore, the prediction of PPI sites in oligomeric proteins of higher order remains challenging. Most current methods focus on dimers or small complexes, and extending prediction capabilities to larger oligomeric assemblies requires developing efficient prediction systems that can handle the increased complexity [72]. Incorporating structural information, such as three-dimensional atomic coordinates or predicted distance maps, can provide valuable insights into PPI site prediction for these larger complexes69. Integrating structural predictions from tools like AlphaFold2 [15] could bridge the gap between sequence-based and structure-based methods, leveraging the strengths of both approaches. For

instance, AF2Complex utilizes AlphaFold2 to predict both the presence of PPIs and the corresponding quaternary structures without the need for paired multiple sequence alignments [74]. Although methods exist that incorporate structural predictions into PPI site prediction, integrating these structural insights with LLM-based sequence models remains an open area of research.

Combining multimodal data, such as integrating sequence data with structural information and high-level semantic features, has shown promise in enhancing PPI site prediction models. For example, MIPPIS fuses multi-information from different perspectives by integrating a graph convolutional network (GCN) channel, a bidirectional long short-term memory (Bi-LSTM) channel [61], and a ProtT5 language model channel [75]. This approach effectively extracts and integrates features from amino acid spatial structures, primary sequences, and high-level semantic representations, leading to superior predictive performance. Future research could explore the integration of additional data types, such as protein localization and function information, to further boost prediction accuracy. Bhat and Patil emphasized the importance of incorporating the correlation information between different protein locations and investigating how the structural information of full-length proteins can add value to prediction models [72]. The integration of multimodal approaches, particularly with available imaging data, has been thoroughly explored, demonstrating promising potential for uncovering relationships between functional proteins and cell morphologies [76, 77]. Bhat and Patil emphasized the importance of incorporating the correlation information between different protein locations and investigating how the structural information of full-length proteins can add value to prediction models [72]. The integration of multimodal approaches, particularly with available imaging data, has been thoroughly explored, demonstrating promising potential for uncovering relationships between functional proteins and cell morphologies [76, 77].

In conclusion, while LLM-based methods have significantly advanced the field of PPI site prediction, ongoing efforts to address these limitations through methodological innovations and interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential. By continuing to refine these models, incorporating diverse data sources, and enhancing model robustness against data quality issues, the scientific community can deepen our understanding of protein interactions. These advancements will ultimately contribute to breakthroughs in biomedical research, therapeutic development, and our fundamental comprehension of cellular mechanisms.

4 Expanding Horizons: Applications of Large Language Models for Interactions

The versatility of LLMs in protein research extends beyond PPI site prediction, finding utility in diverse yet related domains. One such application is the extraction of PPI information from biomedical literature. With the exponential growth of published research, manually curating PPIs from text has become impractical. LLMs, particularly transformer-based models trained on biomedical corpora, have demonstrated effectiveness in automating the identification of PPIs within scientific texts [68]. By employing advanced natural language processing techniques, these models can parse complex sentences to extract relevant interaction data, thereby accelerating the compilation of PPI databases and facilitating downstream biological research.

Another promising application lies in the prediction of PPI hotspots—specific residues that are crucial for the stability and specificity of protein interactions. Traditional hotspot identification relies heavily on structural data and extensive mutagenesis experiments, which are time-consuming and resource-intensive. Recent approaches leverage protein language models to predict these critical residues directly from sequence information [78]. By capturing the evolutionary and contextual nuances encoded within protein sequences, LLMs can identify potential hotspots even in the absence of structural data, providing valuable insights for drug design and protein engineering efforts aimed at modulating PPIs.

LLMs have also been applied to predict protein–membrane interfaces, particularly in peripheral membrane proteins that transiently associate with lipid bilayers. Accurate identification of membrane-interacting residues is essential for understanding membrane-related functions and for the development of therapeutics targeting membrane-associated processes. Utilizing embeddings from protein language models, researchers have developed methods to predict membrane-binding sites with reduced computational cost compared to structure-based techniques [79]. These models can rapidly analyze protein sequences to identify potential membrane-interacting regions, aiding in the characterization of proteins with limited structural information.

Collectively, these applications underscore the expanding role of LLMs in addressing complex biological questions beyond traditional PPI site prediction. By harnessing the power of deep learning and vast protein sequence data, LLMs offer innovative solutions for extracting meaningful biological insights from both textual and sequence information. The continued exploration of these underinvestigated areas holds significant promise for advancing our understanding of protein function, interactions, and their implications in health and disease.
