Prediction of Protein–Peptide Binding Sites Using PepBCL

Ruheng Wang, Kenta Nakai, and Leyi Wei

Abstract

Identifying the protein–peptide binding residues is fundamentally important to understanding the mechanisms of protein functions and drug discovery. Although several computational methods have been developed, they highly rely on third-party tools or information for feature design, easily resulting in low computational efficacy and suffering from low predictive performance. We describe how to use an endto-end computational method PepBCL that is free with feature design for high-throughput prediction of protein–peptide binding sites. PepBCL outperforms the state-of-the-art methods under benchmarking comparison and achieves more robust performance based on protein sequences only. We can automatically extract and learn high-latent representations of protein sequences relevant to protein structure and functions by the introduction of a well pretrained protein large language model. We overview our method and discuss how to run the supported codes to reproduce our predictor.

Key words Protein, peptide binding residues, Protein large language model, Pretraining, Contrastive learning, PepBCL

1 Introduction

Protein–peptide interactions play a crucial role in understanding biological processes, including protein function and drug discovery [1, 2]. Identifying the binding sites where peptides interact with proteins can provide key insights into these interactions [3, 4]. Traditional computational approaches for predicting binding sites often rely on complex feature engineering and structural data. However, recent advancements in machine learning have paved the way for sequence-based methods that simplify this process. In this context, PepBCL [5] has emerged as a state-of-the-art tool designed to predict protein–peptide binding sites using deep learning techniques. This innovative approach leverages pretrained language models and contrastive learning to enhance prediction accuracy. By focusing on sequence embeddings, PepBCL minimizes the reliance on manually engineered features, offering a more automated and scalable solution.

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_16, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

Historically, methods for predicting protein–peptide binding sites fall into two categories: structure-based and sequence-based approaches. Structure-based methods, such as PepSite [6] and PepNN-Struct [7], rely on the 3D conformation of proteins and peptides to identify potential binding regions. These methods often require detailed structural data, which can be difficult to obtain and computationally intensive to process. On the other hand, sequencebased methods, like PepBind [8] and SPRINT-Seq [9], predict binding sites directly from the amino acid sequence. Sequencebased approaches are generally faster and easier to apply since they do not require structural data. However, they often face challenges with prediction accuracy due to the limited information contained in the sequence alone.

To address these challenges, PepBCL combines the strengths of pretrained language models, which capture rich sequence representations, and contrastive learning, which improves performance on imbalanced datasets by learning more distinguishable features for binding and nonbinding sites. We overview our model architecture and provide details of our benchmark datasets. Finally, we show users how to reproduce our model based on our supported codes.

2 Materials and Method

2.1 Datasets

This study utilizes two widely used benchmark datasets, referred to as Dataset 1 and Dataset 2, to evaluate and compare our proposed method with existing approaches. Both datasets underwent similar pre-processing to ensure robust model training, including reducing sequence identity to \(3 0 \%\) using “blastclust” in the BLAST package [10] to mitigate evaluation bias.

Dataset 1 originally proposed for the SPRINT-Str [11] method, contains 1279 protein–peptide complexes. It is divided into a training set (TR1154) with 1154 proteins and an independent testing set (TE125) with 125 proteins. Specifically, TR1154 comprises 15,030 binding residues and 261,792 nonbinding residues, while TE125 contains 1719 binding residues and 29,151 nonbinding residues.

Dataset 2 derived from Zhao et al.[8]’s work comprises the same 1279 protein–peptide complexes. It is split into a training set (TR640) with 640 complexes and an independent testing set (TE639) with the remaining complexes. TR640 includes 8259 binding residues and 149,103 nonbinding residues, while TE639 contains 8490 binding residues and 141,840 nonbinding residues.

Both datasets exhibit a significant class imbalance, with nonbinding residues substantially outnumbering binding residues. This imbalance is a common characteristic in protein–peptide binding site prediction tasks and presents a challenge for developing effective prediction models. To ensure a fair comparison with existing studies, we adhered to the same dataset-splitting procedure for model training and testing. This standardized approach enables consistent evaluation across different prediction methods in the field of protein–peptide interaction prediction.

2.2 Architecture

Recently, we developed the PepBCL method to predict protein– peptide binding residues in input protein sequences. Our method is based on an efficient four-module design; see Fig. 1. First, we represent the input protein using a sequence embedding module. Second, these embeddings are processed by a BERT-based encoder module. Third, a contrastive learning module optimizes the feature representations. Fourth, an output module generates the final predictions. Following we provide a more detailed explanation.

In the sequence embedding module, we translate the raw protein sequence into a digital sequence according to a defined vocabulary dictionary. Each amino acid is mapped to a digit value, with rare amino acids uniformly replaced by a specific digit. The encoded vector is then embedded by a pretrained embedding layer to


Fi. 1The architecture the PepBCL method The fourmodules are denoted by the corresponding lettr (), (b), (c), and (d). (a) The sequence embedding module translates raw protein sequences into numerical ( ), ( ), and ( ). ( ) The sequence embedding module translates raw protein sequences into numerical b embeddings using the ProtBert-BFD model. ( ) The BERT-based encoder module applies multi-head attention to capture contextual relationships between amino acids, generating high-dimensional feature representa-c d tions. ( ) The output module converts feature vectors to residue-level peptide-binding probabilities. ( ) The contrastive learning module opti

generate an improved initial embedding. In the BERT-based encoder module, we utilize a pretrained BERT model called ProtBert-BFD [12], which has been trained on 2.1 billion protein sequences [13]. This module applies multi-head attention to capture contextual relationships between amino acids, generating high-dimensional feature representations. These representations are then refined using fully connected layers to reduce dimensionality and enhance prediction accuracy. The contrastive learning module, a novel component of PepBCL, applies a contrastive loss function to improve feature distinction between binding and nonbinding sites. This module is designed to address the inherent imbalance in binding site datasets by learning from both positive and negative sample pairs. In the output module, the residue representation vector generated by the previous modules is fed into a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to convert the feature vector to a residue-level category output. This module generates a residue-level peptide-binding probability and determines whether each residue in the input sequence is peptide binding or not.

PepBCL’s architecture is designed to provide accurate predictions while processing raw protein sequences without relying on complex feature engineering or structural data. By leveraging pretrained language models and contrastive learning, PepBCL offers a more automated and scalable solution for predicting protein–peptide binding sites.

2.3 Model Performance Evaluation

2.3.1 Evaluation Metrics

To evaluate the performance of PepBCL, we employed five metrics commonly used in imbalanced classification tasks: Recall, Specificity, Precision, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC). Recall measures the proportion of correctly predicted binding residues, while Specificity assesses the accuracy of nonbinding residue predictions. Precision indicates the accuracy of residues predicted as binding. MCC provides a comprehensive assessment of prediction ability for both binding and nonbinding residues. Additionally, AUC quantifies the overall performance of the model.

2.3.2 Model Performance

To assess the predictive power of PepBCL, the model was compared to eight existing methods, including both structure-based and sequence-based approaches. PepBCL demonstrated superior predictive performance across two independent testing sets (TE125 and TE639). On TE125, PepBCL achieved a Precision of 0.540, an AUC of 0.815, and an MCC of 0.385, outperforming PepBind by \(7 . 1 \%\) , \(2 . 2 \%\) , and \(1 . 3 \%\) , respectively. Similarly, on TE639, PepBCL surpassed PepBind in AUC by \(3 . 7 \%\) and outperformed state-of-the-art methods like PepNN-Seq and PepNN-Struct in MCC by \(6 . 1 \%\) and \(1 . 1 \%\) , respectively. The results showed that PepBCL outperformed most sequence-based methods in terms of precision, AUC, and MCC. Furthermore, PepBCL

maintained high AUC values for over \(8 7 \%\) of protein sequences, indicating its robust predictive capabilities across varying contexts. In addition to these metrics, PepBCL demonstrated significant improvements in handling imbalanced data. The contrastive learning module contributed to more accurate predictions by enhancing the separation between binding and nonbinding site features. Ablation studies, where the contrastive learning module was removed, showed a clear drop in performance, reinforcing the module’s importance.

2.3.3 Run Time

Using a modern GPU (Nvidia RTX 3090), we evaluated the computational efficiency of PepBCL compared to PepBind. As shown in Fig. 2, the runtime of PepBCL for a single protein (PDBID: 1a81A) is approximately 28 seconds, while processing ten proteins takes about 44 seconds. In contrast, PepBind requires around 600 seconds to process a single protein. This significant difference in processing time demonstrates the superior computational efficiency of PepBCL. Our method was implemented in Python using the PyTorch framework, allowing for optimal utilization of GPU resources. Here we also give some guide about how to run PepBCL on CPU (see Note 1).


Fig. 2 The comparison of time-consuming between our method and the PepBind model

To provide a comprehensive comparison, Fig. 2 illustrates three specific runtime scenarios: PepBind-1 (time for PepBind to process one sequence), PepBCL-1 (time for PepBCL to process one sequence), and PepBCL-10 (time for PepBCL to process ten sequences). For the PepBCL-10 test, we repeated the single sequence (1a81A) ten times to simulate a multi-protein prediction scenario. These results indicate that PepBCL can efficiently handle large-scale protein datasets, making it particularly suitable for highthroughput analyses of entire proteomes or genomes. The minimal increase in processing time from one to ten sequences, as visualized in Fig. 2, further highlights PepBCL’s scalability and potential for rapid, large-scale predictions of protein–peptide binding sites. Discussions about some factors that influence running time are shown in Note 2.

3 Reproduction of PepBCL

3.1 Environment Setup

To reproduce the PepBCL model or utilize it with custom data, adhere to the following protocol:

  1. Clone the repository:
git clone https://github.com/Ruheng-W/PepBCL cd PepBCL
  1. Generate a new conda environment:
conda create -n PepBCL python=3.8  
conda activate PepBCL
  1. Install the required packages:
pip install -r requirements.txt

Should you encounter notifications of uninstalled packages post-execution of the above command, please manually install any missing dependencies.

3.2 Model Preparation

Establish a directory for the pretrained BERT model and acquire it:

mkdir -p prot_bert_bfd  
wget https://huggingface.co/Rostlab/prot_bert_bfd/resolve/main/pytorch_model.bin -0 prot_bert_bfd/pytorch_model.bin

Ensure the pytorch_model.bin file is correctly situated within the prot_bert_bfd directory of your cloned PepBCL repository. This protocol facilitates the proper setup and preparation of the PepBCL model for subsequent utilization or customization.

3.3 Custom Training and Testing

To train and evaluate the model on custom datasets:

  1. Locate the select_dataset() function within the train/prot-Bert_main.py file. Modify the function to specify the paths to your custom training and testing datasets:
def select_dataset():
    path_train_data = '/path/to/your/training/dataset.tsv'
    path_test_data = '/path/to/your/testing/dataset.tsv'
    return path_train_data, path_test_data

Replace the placeholder paths with the actual file paths to your training and testing datasets, respectively.

  1. If necessary, adjust other hyperparameters and configurations within the load_config() function to optimize the model for your specific use case. Refer to the inline comments for guidance on each parameter. Users could also improve Recall by customizing probability thresholds (see Note 3).
  2. Execute the main program to initiate the training and testing process:

python train/protBert_main.py

This procedure allows for the customization of the PepBCL model to accommodate various datasets and experimental requirements. Ensure that your custom datasets adhere to the same format as the original training and testing files to maintain compatibility with the model architecture.

3.4 Reproducing Results on the Paper

To reproduce the results from the paper:

  1. Download our saved model parameters of PepBCL from the following link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/ 1UTZxnR34UaUryKkXRaM0ts3ufjVQtCas
    The directory contains two parameter files corresponding to two datasets (Dataset1 and Dataset2).
  2. Create a directory for the model parameters and download the files:
mkdir -p model_parameters   
# Download Dataset1 parameters   
wget https://drive.google.com/uc?id=FILE_ID_FOR_DATABSET1 -0   
model_parameters/dataset1_parameters.pth   
# Download Dataset2 parameters   
wget https://drive.google.com/uc?id=FILE_ID_FOR_DATABSET2 -0   
model_parameters/dataset2_parameters.pth

Replace FILE_ID_FOR_DATASET1 and FILE_ID_FOR_- DATASET2 with the actual file IDs from the Google Drive link. Alternatively, you can manually download the files from the webpage and place them in the model_parameters directory.

  1. Create a directory for the model parameters and download the files:
import torch
import prot_bert
config = ... # Your model configuration
model = prot_bert.BERT(config) # model instantiation
model_dict = torch.load('model_parameters/dataset1_parameters.pth')[ 'model'] # load the state_dict from file path
model.load_state_dict(model_dict) # load the state_dict to model

You can replace ’model_parameters/dataset1_parameters.pth’ with ’model_parameters/dataset2_parameters.pth’ to use the parameters for Dataset2.

3.5 Directory Structure

After completing these steps, your directory structure should look like this:

PepBCL/ prot_bert_bfd/ pytorch_model.bin model_parameters/ dataset1_parameters.pth dataset2_parameters.pth train/ protBert_main.py (other files and folders)

3.6 Input file format

To facilitate the utilization of our model for custom training purposes, it is essential to adhere to a specific input file format. The input file should be structured as a tab-separated values (TSV) file, containing two columns: “seq” and “label.”

The “seq” column represents the protein sequence, encoded as a string of single-letter amino acid codes. Each row corresponds to a single protein sequence. The “label” column contains the binary classification for each residue in the corresponding protein sequence, where \(^ { \mathfrak { c } } 0 ^ { \mathfrak { s } }\) denotes a nonbinding residue and “1” denotes a binding residue.

An exemplary excerpt from the input file is as follows:

seq label
MGSSEQELKAIVKDLGCGPY 0000000000000000000000
ADVPAGVQLADKQTLVRNNG 000000000000000000001
IVEGQDAEVGLSPWQVMLFR 000000000000000001000

It is imperative that users preparing their own datasets for model training strictly adhere to this format to ensure compatibility with the PepBCL framework. The length of the “label” string must precisely match the length of the corresponding protein sequence in the “seq” column. This format enables the model to accurately associate each amino acid residue with its corresponding binding status during the training process.

4 Notes

1. Hardware Requirements and Running on CPU

Due to the use of large protein language models, running computations on a GPU may require significant memory resources—approximately 20 GB. If users intend only to replicate the model and perform testing, they can execute inference on a CPU. For model training, it is advisable to utilize a GPU. To run the model on a CPU, users should remove code segments that allocate data and the model to CUDA devices. For example, change:

label = torch.tensor.label, dtype=torch.long).CUDA()

to:

label = torch.tensor.label, dtype=torch.long)

2. Impact of Sequence Length on Inference Time

Owing to the transformer architecture, the model performs rapid inference on short sequences. However, increasing the sequence length can substantially extend the inference time. It is important to note that sequence length has a more pronounced effect on inference time than the number of sequences.

3. Customizing Probability Thresholds to Improve Recall

Existing methods are often trained on benchmark datasets that are extremely imbalanced, leading to low recall values and the omission of many true protein–peptide binding sites in realworld applications. To mitigate this issue, different probability thresholds can be selected. Our findings indicate that decreasing the threshold significantly enhances recall, although it also impacts other evaluation metrics. Users wishing to set a custom

threshold can modify the model_eval function in the train/ protBert_main.py file. For instance, to set the threshold to 0.3, replace the following code:

# Probability of predicting positive classes [batch_size, seq_len]
pred_prob_sort = torch.max(pred_prob_all, 2)
# The maximum probability of prediction in each sample [batch_size]
pred_class = pred_prob_sort[1]
with:
threshold = 0.3
pred_class = (pred_prob_positive > threshold).long()

References

  1. Pawson T, Nash P (2003) Assembly of cell regulatory systems through protein interaction domains. science 300(5618):445–452
  2. Rubinstein M, Niv MY (2009) Peptidic modulators of protein-protein interactions: progress and challenges in computational design. Biopolymers: Original Research on Biomolecules 91(7):505–513
  3. Lee H, Heo L, Lee MS, Seok C (2015) GalaxyPepDock: a protein–peptide docking tool based on interaction similarity and energy optimization. Nucleic acids research 43(W1): W431–W435
  4. Neduva V, Linding R, Su-Angrand I, Stark A, Masi F, Gibson TJ, Lewis J, Serrano L, Russell RB (2005) Systematic discovery of new recognition peptides mediating protein interaction networks. PLoS biology 3(12):e405
  5. Wang R, Jin J, Zou Q, Nakai K, Wei L (2022) Predicting protein–peptide binding residues via interpretable deep learning. Bioinformatics 38(13):3351–3360. https://doi.org/10. 1093/bioinformatics/btac352
  6. Petsalaki E, Stark A, Garcı´a-Urdiales E, Russell RB (2009) Accurate prediction of peptide binding sites on protein surfaces. PLoS computational biology 5(3):e1000335
  7. Abdin O, Wen H, Kim PM (2020) Sequence and structure based deep learning models for the identification of peptide binding sites. Adv Neural Info ProcessSyst:33
  8. Zhao Z, Peng Z, Yang J (2018) Improving sequence-based prediction of protein–peptide

binding residues by introducing intrinsic disorder and a consensus method. J Chem Information Modeling 58(7):1459–1468. https://doi. org/10.1021/acs.jcim.8b00019
9. Taherzadeh G, Yang Y, Zhang T, Liew AWC, Zhou Y (2016) Sequence-based prediction of protein–peptide binding sites using support vector machine. J Comput Chem 37(13): 1223–1229
10. Altschul SF, Madden TL, Sch€affer AA, Zhang J, Zhang Z, Miller W, Lipman DJ (1997) Gapped BLAST and PSI-BLAST: a new generation of protein database search programs. Nucleic Acids Res 25(17):3389–3402
11. Taherzadeh G, Zhou Y, Liew AW-C, Yang Y (2018) Structure-based prediction of protein– peptide binding regions using Random Forest. Bioinformatics 34(3):477–484
12. Elnaggar A, Heinzinger M, Dallago C, Rehawi G, Wang Y, Jones L, Gibbs T, Feher T, Angerer C, Steinegger M, Bhowmik D, Rost B (2021) ProtTrans: towards cracking the language of lifes code through self-supervised deep learning and high performance computing. In: IEEE Transactions on pattern analysis and machine intelligence, p 1. https://doi.org/10.1109/TPAMI. 2021.3095381
13. Steinegger M, Mirdita M, So¨ding J (2019) Protein-level assembly increases protein sequence recovery from metagenomic samples manyfold. Nature methods 16(7):603–606

Predicting Peptide Bioactivity Using the Unified Model Architecture UniDL4BioPep

Zhenjiao Du, Nandan Kumar, and Yonghui Li

Abstract

The unified prediction model architecture, UniDL4BioPep, offers a significant advancement in applying machine learning approaches to bioactive peptide discovery. By streamlining the model development process, this architecture reduces the effort required to create custom models, allowing wet-lab researchers to accelerate scientific discovery by easily tailoring models to their specific needs. UniDL4BioPep leverages protein language models, specifically evolutionary scale modeling (ESM), and simplifies model preparation to a single click, making it both accessible and efficient for users. This chapter provides the technical details and practical operation guide for utilizing this unified architecture and demonstrates its effectiveness in binary classification tasks.

Key words Bioactive peptide, Machine learning, Transfer learning, Large language model, Deep learning, Protein sequence annotation

1 Introduction

Identifying and characterizing a new bioactive peptide experimentally is resource-intensive in the field of bioactive peptide discovery [1, 2]. Therefore, as an alternative approach to accelerate the discovery and reduce the monetary and time costs associated with trial-and-error experiments, machine learning-based prediction approaches are gaining prominence in bioactive peptide discovery across the field of food, nutraceuticals, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals [1].

Building a prediction model generally involves three steps: (1) dataset construction, (2) peptide representation/embedding/ encoding (i.e., converting a peptide sequence into a numerical vector/matrix), and (3) mode development [1]. The most challenging aspect is the peptide representation. Traditional methods, such as

those based on physicochemical and biochemical properties of amino acids (e.g., AAIndex), amino acid descriptors, composition, molecular descriptors, peptide fingerprints, etc., have been widely explored over the past decades [1]. The key idea is to maximize the information contained in the numerical vector for the representation of a given peptide. Those traditional approaches are generally underrepresented when used alone but can become overrepresented when most of them are combined, resulting in significant efforts in feature selection and model development. This complicates the development of high-quality models. To overcome these challenges and make machine learning more accessible, a unified model architecture, without the need for intricate feature selection and tedious model selection and hyperparameter tuning, is critical to popularizing the machine learning models among biochemistry researchers who are interested in building their own customized models for specific applications.

Deep learning-based language models are a new class of machine learning approaches for learning representation in natural language processing (NLP), where the semantic properties and linguistic relationships between words are autonomously learnt from millions or billions of text data. This is done by conditioning on predicting the next word in a sentence given the previous context or by reconstructing masked text [3, 4]. Protein sequences, represented by single-letter amino acid codes, are highly similar to human natural language, where each single letter (amino acid) corresponds to a word in a sentence. Similarly, protein language models (PLMs) are trained on millions of protein sequences to internalize the information encrypted in protein sequences and generate numerical vectors/matrices for a given protein sequence [4]. Most recently, some PLMs, such as evolutionary scale modeling (ESM), ProtTrans, have been released and have achieved stateof-the-art performance in various downstream tasks with a simple classifier [5–7].

To make this approach more accessible and user-friendly, we built this unified architecture for researchers in biochemistry who are interested in employing this cutting-edge approach for their own bioactive peptide prediction model development. We provide an application example, along with a detailed explanation and annotation of each line and function in the code. By following the protocol, the interested users only need to prepare their dataset for model development and then the trained mode can be used for future prediction tasks. Additionally, we will provide guidance on how to extend the usage of the protocol, when modifications are needed.

2 Prerequisites Before Model Development

In this protocol, we use Google Colab for the demonstration. This allows users to run the protocol anywhere with internet access, a Google Account, and any browser. Colab is a hosted Jupyter Notebook service that requires no setup to use and provides free access to computing resources (e.g., GPU). We highly recommend using Colab for the running if not familiar with Python and the environment setup. For local execution of this protocol, the necessary Python packages are listed in “requirements.txt” and can be found at https://github.com/dzjxzyd/LLM_book_chapter.

3 Protocol for Application in Binary Classification Tasks

The protocol is based on the UniDL4BioPep paper [5], with a few modifications. Users can find it at https://github.com/dzjxzyd/ UniDL4BioPep, where additional benchmark datasets for practice, as well as the original references are also available.

3.1 Dataset Construction

To begin, we create a bioactive peptide dataset for embedding and model development. Generally, the peptide dataset size is relatively small \(_ { < 1 0 , 0 0 0 }\) sequences). To simplify the dataset construction process, we use Microsoft Excel (xls or xlsx format). Example files are provided at https://github.com/dzjxzyd/LLM_book_chapter.

• Step 1: Create a Microsoft Excel file with the first column named “sequence” and the second column named “label,” for example, “bitter_single_dataset_example.xlsx.”
• Step 2: Fill out the two columns with available bioactive peptide data, labeling positive samples as 1 and negative samples as 0 (see Note 1).
• Step 3 (optional): Dataset splitting. If you are building a model from scratch, it is recommended to save all your data into a single dataset file and randomly split it for evaluation. If you are comparing model performance with existing models whose datasets have already been divided, it is suggested to use their training and test datasets directly to ensure a fair comparison.

Common Issues

  1. Peptide sequences must be fully represented using single-letter amino acid code in uppercase; otherwise, a “KeyError” will occur.
  2. You must use the column name same as the example files; otherwise, the code will not recognize the two columns.

3.2 Peptide Embedding

Peptide embedding, also known as peptide representation, involves converting the peptide sequences in the dataset into numerical vectors or matrices (in our case, we will transform those sequences into vectors). In this protocol, we will use the ESM language model (esm2_t6_8M_UR50D) for the peptide embeddings. Before proceeding, please load Jupyter Notebook in Colab, for example, “Book_chapter_example_binary_classification_320_dimension. ipynb”, upload your dataset file “bitter_single_dataset_example. xlsx” to Google Colab (using the left sidebar) (Fig. 1a–c).

Step 1 ESM language model loading and embedding function construction. The language model can be easily changed to other ESM2 language models (see Note 2).

def esm_embedding(esm2, esm2 Alphabet, peptide_sequence_list): # load the python packages import torch import sm import collections import pandas as pd import gc # Choose CUDA for acceleration if available if torch.cuda.is-available(): device = torchdevice(“CUDA”) else: device = torchdevice(“cpu”) esm2.eval() esm2 = esm2.to(device) batch Converter = esm2 Alphabet.get_batch Converter() # this function is to tokenize your peptide sequences from amino acid sequence into numbers # load the peptide sequence list into the bach Converter batch_labels, batch_strs, batch_tokens = batch Converter(peptide_sequence_list) batch_lens = (batch_tokens != alphabet-paddingidx).sum(1) ## batch tokens are the tokenized results of the whole data set batch_tokens = batch_tokens.to(device) # Extract per-residue representations (on CPU) with torch.no_grad(): # Here we export the last layer of the EMS model output as the representation of the peptides # model’esm2_t6_8M_UR50D’ only has 6 layers, and therefore repr_layers parameters are equal to 6 # If you want to change the esm model, for example: esm2_t12_35M_UR50D, which has 12 layers, they you will need to change the 6 to 12 at the following two lines results = esm2(batch_tokens, repr_layers=[6], return Contacts=False) token Representations = results[“representations”][“6].cpu() # the representation is generated for each amino acid residue; for example, if you have 3 residues, then the output shape is \(3 \times 320\) , 3 is the number of residues and the 320 is the vector for a single residue

Fig. 1 Guidelines for using Google Colab with our protocol from (a) step 1 to (b) step 2, and (c) step 3


(c)
Fig. 1

# Generate per-sequence representations via averaging
# NOTE: token 0 is always a beginning-of-sequence token, so we will discard it and start from token 1
sequence Representations = []
for i, tokens_len in enumerate(batchLens):
    sequence Representations.append(token Representations[i, 1 : tokens_len - 1].mean(0))
# sequence Representations is a list and each element is a tensor
embeddings_results = collections.defaultdict(list)
for i in range(len(sequence Representations)):
    # tensor format can be transformed as numpy format sequence Representations[0].numpy() or
    sequence Representations[0].to_list
    each_seq_rep = sequence Representations[i].tolist()
    for each_element in each_seq_rep:
        embeddings_results[i].append(each_element)
        embeddings_results = pd.DataFrame(embeddings_results).T
    del batch_labels, batch_strs, batch_tokens, results, token Representations # delete those variables to
save GPU memory
gccollect() # release the GPU memory
return embeddings_results

load the esm2_t6_8M_UR50D

model, alphabet = esm.pretrained.esm2_t6_8M_UR50D()

Step 2 Dataset loading and division. This protocol includes two sections: one for using a single dataset (the current example) and the other for using separate train and test datasets. If you are using your own dataset, be sure to update the dataset name in the scripts or adjust the dataset filename accordingly. In this step, you will generate three datasets at the end: train, validation, and test.

One dataset
Dataset \(=\) pd.read_excel(‘bitter_single_dataset_example.xlsx’,header \(= 0\) index_col \(=\) None)
# Two datasets
# train dataset loading
dataset \(=\) pd.read_excel(‘bitter_train.xlsx’,header \(= 0\) index_col \(=\) None)
# test dataset loading
dataset \(=\) pd.read_excel(‘bitter_test.xlsx’,header \(= 0\) index_col \(=\) None)

one dataset: we will split the dataset two times to get the train, validation and test, respectively. X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=123, shuffle= True) X_train, X_valid, y_train, y_valid = train_test_split(X_train, y_train, test_size=0.2, random_state=234, shuffle= True) # two datasets: we only need to split the train and validation dataset # random state is to make sure the dataset division is repeatable X_train, X_valid, y_train, y_valid = train_test_split(X_train, y_train, test_size=0.2, random_state=111, shuffle= True)

Note The ratio for dataset splitting is adjustable. In this protocol, the training dataset is further divided randomly into training and validation datasets. The validation dataset is used to save the model checkpoints. The “random_state” parameter can be adjusted to control the randomness of the split. If you prefer a repeatable dataset division, you should use the same “random_state” value each time you run the protocol.

Step 3 Peptide embeddings. Embeddings are computed only for peptide sequences. To save the GPU memory or RAM consumption, in our demo, we conduct the embedding process one by one. We will load the peptide sequences and make sure the format of the input sequence meets the input format requirement of ESM2. Finally, we save the embedding results into a csv file for further usage (see Note 3).

# training dataset loading  
dataset = pd.read_excel('bitter(single_dataset_example.xlsx', header=0, index_col = None)  
# generate embedding for sequences  
sequence_list = dataset['sequence']  
embeddings_results = pd.DataFrame()  
for seq in sequence_list:  
    # the setting is just following the input format setting in ESM model, [name, sequence]  
    tuple_sequence = tuple([seq, seq])  
    peptide_sequence_list = []  
    peptide_sequence_list.append(tuple_sequence) # build a summarize list variable including all the sequence information  
    # employ ESM model for converting and save the converted data in csv format  
    one_seq_embeddings = esm_embeddings(model, alphabet, peptide_sequence_list)  
    embeddings_results = pd.concat([embeddings_results, one_seq_embeddings])  
    embeddings_results.to_csv(whole_sample_dataset esm2 t6 8M UR50D unified 320 dimension.csv')

Potential Bug This protocol can also be used for protein-related prediction. If your protein sequence is too long (more than 2000 residues), it might surpass the maximum RAM or GPU memory limits of a free Tesla T4 GPU. In such a case, the notebook may crash and automatically restart.

Step 4 Normalization (optional). A min–max normalization is conducted using the training dataset to scale the features in the range (0, 1). In this context, each peptide is represented by a 320-dimension vector, and normalization ensures that each dimension is scaled within this range \(( 0 , 1 )\) . The scaler for normalization learnt from training dataset will be applied to normalize the validation and test datasets as well.

# normalize the X data range  
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler  
scalar = MinMaxScaler()  
scalar.fit(X_train)  
X_train = scorer.transform(X_train) # normalize X to 0-1 range  
X_valid = scorer.transform(X_valid) # normalize X to 0-1 range  
X_test = scorer.transform(X_test)

3.3 Model Training and Performance Evaluation at Test Dataset

Keras library is used for the model architecture design since it is more readable. In the demo notebook, we use the same architecture as that in UniDL4BioPep with a few modifications. The first layer is the input layer, where the peptide sequence information is represented by a numerical vector generated by the ESM model. Then, the next layer is a 1D convolutional layer with filter sizes of 32, kernel size of 3 and ReLU activation, and then a 1D max pooling layer was added to reduce the dimensionality. In addition, batch normalization and dropout (rate \(= 0 . 1 5\) ) are added to avoid

overfitting. Subsequently, the output of the convolutional layer is flattened and followed by a dense layer containing 64 hidden neurons with ReLU activation function and a dropout rate of 0.15. The last layer is also a dense layer with two neurons with a SoftMax activation function for binary classification. The Adam optimizer is chosen to accelerate the model fitting and maximize its performance. The best model will be stored under the consideration of accuracy at the validation dataset. The maximum epoch time is 200, but in practice, the best model is obtained around 100 epochs, which takes around 5 min on the Google Colab platform with T4 GPU.

Step 1 Model architecture construction.

def ESM_CNN(X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test):  
    inputShape=(320,1) # input feature size  
    input = Input(inputShape)  
    x = Conv1D(32,(3),strides = (1),name='layer_conv1',padding='same')(input)  
    x = BatchNormalization().x  
    x = Activation('relu')(x)  
    x = MaxPooling1D((2), name='MaxPool1', padding="same")(x)  
    x = Dropout(0.15)(x)  
    x = Flatten().x  
    x = Dense(64, activation='relu', name='fc1')(x)  
    x = Dropout(0.15)(x)  
    x = Dense(2, activation='softmax', name='fc2')(x)  
    model = Model(input = input, outputs = x, name='Predict')  
    adam = Adam(learning_rate=0.001)  
# compile the model  
model.compile(loss='sparse_categorical.Crossentropy', optimizer=adam, metrics=['accuracy'])  
# set checkpoint and save the best model  
mc = ModelCheckpoint('best_model.keras', monitor='val Accuracy', mode='max', verbose=1, save_best_only=True, saveWeights_only=False)  
# summary the callbacks_list  
callback_list = [mc]  
model_history = model.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_test, y_test), epochs=100, callbacks=callback_list, batch_size=8, verbose=1)  
return model, model_history

Step 2 Train the model and save the best model during training process.

train the model & best model checkpoint will be save as 'best_model.keras'  
model, model_history = ESM_CNN(X_train, y_train, X_valid, y_valid)

Step 3 Load the save model for performance evaluation at test dataset.

# load the saved best model for performance evaluation at test dataset  
saved_model = load_model('best_model.keras')  
# result collection list  
ACC Collector = []  
BACC Collector = []  
Sn Collector = []  
Sp Collector = []  
recall Collector = []  
precision Collector = []  
f1 Collector = []  
MCC Collector = []  
AUC Collector = []  
# confusion matrix  
predicted_class = []  
predictedProbability = saved_model.predict(X_test, batch_size=1)  
for i in range(predictedProbability.shape[0]):  
    index = np.where(predictedProbability[i] == npamax(predictedProbability[i])[0][0] # the class with higher probability was treated as the prediction class  
    predicted_class.append(index)  
    predicted_class = np.array(predicted_class) # transformed into a numpy for performance evaluation  
    y_true = y_test  
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix  
import math  
# np.ravel() return a flatten 1D array  
TN, FP, FN, TP = confusion_matrix(y_true, predicted_class).ravel() # shape [ [True-Positive, False-positive], [False-negative, True-negative] ]  
ACC = (TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)  
ACC Collector.append(ACC)  
Sn Collector.append(TP/(TP+FN))  
Sp Collector.append(TN/(TN+FP))  
recall Collector.append(TP/(TP+FN))  
precision Collector.append(TP/(TP+FP))  
f1 Collector.append(2*TP/(TP+FP)*TP/(TP+FN)/ (TP/(TP+FP)+TP/(TP+FN)))  
MCC = (TP*TN-FP*FN)/math.pow((TP+FP)*(TP+FN)*(TN+FP)*(TN+FN)),0.5)  
MCC Collector.append(MCC)  
BACC Collector.append(0.5*TP/(TP+FN)+0.5*TN/(TN+FP))  
# print the performance in the test dataset  
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score  
AUC = roc_auc_score(y_test, predicted(probability[:,1])  
AUC Collector.append(AUC)  
print(ACC Collector[0])  
print(BACC Collector[0])  
print(Sn Collector[0])  
print(Sp Collector[0])  
print(recall Collector[0])  
print(precision Collector[0])  
print(f1 Collector[0])  
print(MCC Collector[0])  
print(AUC Collector[0])

3.4 Model Application on New Dataset

Once the model is developed, you can use it to make predictions on new data.

Step 1 Create a dataset for your new data similar to the dataset construction process, but leave the label column empty, for example, “bitter_new_data.xlsx.”

Step 2 Generate embeddings for the sequences in the new dataset.

model, alphabet = sm.pretrained.esm2_t6_8M_IR50D()
# new dataset loading
dataset = pd.read_excel('bitter_new_data.xlsx', header=0, index_col = None)
# generate embedding for sequences
sequence_list = dataset['sequence']
embeddings_results = pd.DataFrame()
for seq in sequence_list:
    # the setting is just following the input format setting in ESM model, [name,sequence]
    tuple_sequence = tuple([seq,seq])
    peptide_sequence_list = []
    peptide_sequence_list.append(tuple_sequence) # build a summarize list variable including all the sequence information
    # employ ESM model for converting and save the converted data in csv format
    one_seq_embeddings = sm_embeddings(model, alphabet, peptide_sequence_list)
    embeddings_results = pd.concat([embeddings_results,one_seq_embeddings])
embeddings_results.to_csv('new_data_ism2_t6_8M_IR50D_unified_320 Dimension.csv')

Note Ensure that the filename is updated to match your new dataset file name.

Step 3 (Optional) If you normalize your dataset during the training process, apply the same normalization to the new dataset using the scaler learned from the training dataset.

# read the peptide embeddings  
X_data =  
pd.read_csv('whole_sample_dataset_esem2_t6_8M_UR50D_unified_320 Dimension.csv', header=0, index_col = 0, delimiter=','  
X = np.array(X_data)  
# please make sure the following numbers for random_state is consistent with the above data division section.  
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split  
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=123, shuffle=True, stratify=y)  
X_train, X_valid, y_train, y_valid = train_test_split(X_train, y_train, test_size=0.2, random_state=234, shuffle=True, stratify=y_train)  
# loading the peptide embeddings  
X_new_embeddings =  
pd.read_csv('new_data_esem2_t6_8M_UR50D_unified_320 Dimension.csv', header=0, index_col = 0, delimiter=','  
# normalize the X data range  
from sklearn preprocessing import MinMaxScaler  
scalar = MinMaxScaler()  
scalar.fit(X_train)  
X_new = np.array(X_new_embeddings)  
X_new = scorer.transform(X_new) # normalize X to 0-1 range

Step 4 Load the saved model to make predictions and generate an output file in Microsoft Excel.

# load the saved best model for performance evaluation at test dataset  
saved_model = load_model('best_model.keras')  
# confusion matrix  
predicted_class = []  
predicted(probability = saved_model.predict(X_new, batch_size=1)  
for i in range(predicted(probability.shape[0]):  
    index = np.where(predicted(probability[i] == npamax(predicted(probability[i]))[0][0]  
    predicted_class.append(index)  
predicted_class = np.array(predicted_class)  
for i in range(dataset.shape[0]):  
    datasetiloc[i, 1] = predicted_class[i]  
dataset.to_excel('new_data_prediction_result.xlsx')

4 Notes

  1. There is no specific requirement for the order of samples in the Excel file, as the dataset will be randomized prior to model training.
  2. In this protocol, we use ESM language model (esm2_t6_8- M_UR50D) for peptide embeddings. Five additional ESM models varying in sizes are available at https://github.com/ facebookresearch/esm. Our previous study indicated that larger ESM models generally provide better performance in peptide representation [8]. For dataset containing over 1000 sequences, it is recommended to use a larger ESM model, as it may provide slight performance improvement compared to the esm2_t6_8M_UR50D model. When selecting a different ESM model, hyperparameter adjustments may be necessary to optimize model performance. For guidance on hyperparameter tuning across different ESM2 models, the users may refer to the supplementary documents of pLM4Alg, where we conducted a comprehensive hyperparameter tunning (https:// github.com/dzjxzyd/pLM4Alg) [8].
  3. We separate the embedding and training processes by saving the embedding results first and then loading them for training. This approach improves training efficiency, as embedding is time-intensive. Training, in contrast, is an iterative process, where each peptide sequence is used repeatedly, leading to redundant embedding operations of the same sequence if done concurrently. By directly using the precomputed embeddings for training, we avoid the repetitive time cost of embedding the same peptide sequences multiple times.

Acknowledgments

This is contribution No. 25-037-B from the Kansas Agricultural Experimental Station.

References

  3. Dallago C, Schu¨tze K, Heinzinger M et al (2021) Learned Embeddings from deep learning to visualize and predict protein sets. Curr Protoc 1:e113. https://doi.org/10. 1002/cpz1.113

  4. Rives A, Meier J, Sercu T et al (2021) Biological structure and function emerge from scaling unsupervised learning to 250 million protein sequences. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA 118:

e2016239118. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas. 2016239118
5. Du Z, Ding X, Xu Y, Li Y (2023) UniDL4Bio-Pep: a universal deep learning architecture for binary classification in peptide bioactivity. Brief Bioinform 24:bbad135. https://doi.org/10. 1093/bib/bbad135
6. Elnaggar A, Heinzinger M, Dallago C et al (2022) ProtTrans: toward understanding the language of life through self-supervised learning. IEEE Trans Pattern Anal Mach Intell 44:7112–

  1. https://doi.org/10.1109/TPAMI.2021. 3095381
  2. Lin Z, Akin H, Rao R et al (2023) Evolutionaryscale prediction of atomic-level protein structure with a language model. Science 379:1123– 1130. https://doi.org/10.1126/science. ade2574
  3. Du Z, Xu Y, Liu C, Li Y (2023) pLM4Alg: protein language model-based predictors for allergenic proteins and peptides. J Agric Food Chem:752. https://doi.org/10.1021/ acs.jafc.3c07143

CLAPE: Protein–Ligand Binding Site Prediction via Protein Language Models

Yufan Liu and Boxue Tian

Abstract

Protein–ligand interactions are fundamental to critical biological processes such as transcription, translation, and drug–target interaction. The precise identification of protein–ligand binding residues is essential for accurately modeling these interactions and facilitating subsequent research. However, the development of computational methods that are both efficient and accurate for this purpose remains a significant challenge. Progress in this field holds the potential to drive substantial advancements in biotechnology and drug discovery. In response to this need, we have recently developed a method known as Contrastive Learning And Pretrained Encoder (CLAPE). This approach integrates a pretrained protein language model with contrastive learning to predict ligand-binding residues. In this chapter, we provided a detailed overview of the CLAPE framework, including instructions for using the associated Python package and command-line tools. Additionally, we outline the methods employed for result visualization and describe the comprehensive processes involved in model training.

Key words Protein–ligand interaction, Protein language model, Contrastive learning

1 Introduction

The interaction between proteins and ligands is fundamental to numerous biological processes, including protein–protein, protein– small molecule, and protein–nucleic acid interactions. Protein– DNA interactions, in particular, are essential to fundamental cellular processes such as DNA replication, transcription, signal transduction, and metabolic regulation. DNA-binding proteins, including transcription factors (TFs), are critical regulators of gene expression, influencing processes like immune response and stem cell pluripotency [1, 2]. Aberrations in TF function are associated with the pathogenesis of various diseases, including cancer and neurodegenerative disorders [3]. These proteins recognize and bind to conserved sequence motifs in DNA, which are preserved across evolutionary lineages [4]. Furthermore, other DNA-binding proteins, such as histones, DNA polymerases, and DNA

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_18, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

topoisomerases, are integral to chromatin structure, DNA replication, and transcriptional regulation, with implications for human health and disease. Protein–RNA interactions are primarily involved in post-transcriptional regulation, where RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) play pivotal roles in processes such as RNA splicing, mRNA transport, translation, and degradation [5, 6]. The interaction between proteins and small molecules forms the basis of drug discovery, focusing on drug–target interactions and binding affinities [7]. Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) represent one of the most critical molecular events within organisms. The capacity of proteins to form large complexes is closely associated with various human diseases, and the intricate PPI network is vital for cellular communication, cell cycle control, and signal transduction pathways [8, 9]. Given the significance of protein–ligand interactions, their identification has emerged as a prominent area of research in recent decades.

The identification of ligand-binding sites on a protein represents a crucial initial step in modeling the binding properties of protein–ligand interactions. Various experimental techniques have been developed to elucidate protein–ligand interactions either in vivo or in vitro. For instance, chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) [10] is employed to study protein–DNA interactions, crosslinking, and immunoprecipitation (CLIP) [11] is used for protein-RNA interactions, and the yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) [12] system is a common method for investigating protein–protein interactions. Structural biology approaches, including X-ray crystallography and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, have also been extensively utilized to identify ligand-binding residues and domains within proteins. While these molecular biology-based experimental methods have provided significant insights over the past few decades, they are often time-consuming and require substantial resources. Consequently, the computational prediction of ligand-binding residues using machine learning techniques has emerged as an appealing alternative, offering the potential for faster and more efficient identification of key interaction sites.

The success of these models is largely dependent on the effectiveness of the representation, typically leveraging evolutionary information from protein sequences through methods like PSI-BLAST [13] and HHblits [14]. Additionally, structural features, including protein secondary structure predicted by tools like DSSP [15] and PSIPRED [16], contribute valuable information. Both sequence-based models, such as BindN [17], iDRNA-ITF [18], and PPINet [19], and structure-based models, including NucleicNet [20], HIGH-PPI [21], P2Rank [22], and GraphBind [23], have been developed to enhance the accuracy of protein– ligand interaction predictions. Notably, the application of deep learning techniques, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and Graph Neural

Networks (GNNs), has demonstrated significant promise in improving the predictive capabilities of these models.

In general, structure-based models tend to outperform sequence-based models due to the comprehensive information in protein structures. Nonetheless, accurate protein structures are often required [24], making sequence-based prediction of ligandbinding sites a critical research area. Current sequence-based models still fall short of practical application, often relying on manually designed features that lack refinement. Thus, there is a need for an end-to-end model that bypasses the dependency on handcrafted features. Techniques like pretraining and contrastive learning hold significant promise in this regard, with pretraining leveraging largescale unlabeled data for unsupervised learning and contrastive learning enhancing the discriminative ability of protein embeddings.

To address these challenges, we developed the Contrastive Learning and Pretrained Encoder (CLAPE) in our previous study [25] to predict ligand-binding sites directly from raw protein sequence data. CLAPE presents a versatile framework for predicting ligand-binding sites solely based on protein sequences, contributing to the advancement of feature extraction and model development in this domain.

In this chapter, we overview the architecture, model performance, and datasets utilized in the training of CLAPE. We also offer detailed instructions on how to use the CLAPE Python package and command-line interface for predicting ligand-binding sites from a given protein sequence. Additionally, we demonstrate how to visualize the prediction results based on available protein structures. Finally, we outline the process for training a CLAPE model from scratch, including data preprocessing and model component design, with the aim of benchmarking on provided datasets or applying the CLAPE model to various protein–ligand binding tasks.

2 Materials

2.1 The Model Architecture of CLAPE

To provide a comprehensive framework for protein–ligand binding site prediction, we developed the CLAPE model. The overall architecture of CLAPE, illustrated in Fig. 1, consists of three core modules: the sequence embedding module, the backbone network module, and the loss computation module. In the sequence embedding module, ProtBert [26], a pretrained protein language model, was utilized to encode protein sequences and extract residue-level features. These features were then processed by the backbone network, which in the CLAPE model was composed of a four-layer 1D convolutional neural network (CNN). The loss computation module employed a contrastive loss function guided by a


Fig. 1 Schematic representation of the CLAPE model architecture. The sequence embedding moduleutilizes Schematic representation of the CLAPE model architecture. The sequence embedding module utilizes module processes these embeddings through flexible implementations of various neural networks. The loss module processes these embeddings through flexible implementations of various neural networks. The loss computation module is responsible for calculating classification loss and contrastive loss, enabling backpropagation to refine the ba

binary classification loss. Finally, the prediction scores generated by the backbone network are converted into classification probabilities using a Softmax function in the classification head. The pretrained protein language model was utilized as a feature extractor to eliminate the need for laborious manual feature extraction processes. Additionally, various pretrained protein language models can be employed within the sequence embedding module. In the subsequent sections, the training scheme of CLAPE will be introduced, which has been designed to allow for modifications to different components of the model.

2.2 Prediction Quality of CLAPE

The CLAPE series models, denoted as CLAPE-DB (DNA-binding), CLAPE-RB (RNA-binding), and CLAPE-AB (antibody–antigen binding) in our original research paper, have demonstrated superior performance across all three ligand binding tasks. On the DNA-binding dataset, the CLAPE-DB model exceeded the performance of all sequence-based models and achieved a substantial improvement over the second-best model, with a \(9 . 6 \%\) increase in AUC. Remarkably, CLAPE-DB outperformed most structurebased models despite not incorporating any structural information. Furthermore, it was shown that CLAPE-DB outperformed structure-based models that utilized predicted structures of lower quality, underscoring the advantages of protein language models in the absence of high-quality structural data. Additionally, the CLAPE-DB model exhibited superior generalization ability in a cross-prediction setting. In RNA-binding and antibody–antigen binding tasks, the CLAPE-RB and CLAPE-AB models also achieved leading performances compared to existing models. Notably, the CLAPE-AB model attained an AUC of 0.920, which is

relatively high and suggests its pratical application in accurately predicting the paratope of a given antibody sequence.

2.3 Datasets

In our original research manuscript, the CLAPE model was trained and evaluated across three different ligand datasets, including DNA, RNA, and antibody–antigen, and a detailed analysis was conducted on the task of predicting protein-DNA binding sites.

For the DNA-binding site prediction, two benchmark datasets were employed, which have been widely used in previous studies, specifically from the DBPred [27] and GraphBind [23] models. To mitigate the potential issue of information leakage and enhance the robustness of the models, both datasets underwent similar preprocessing steps, including the reduction of sequence similarity to \(3 0 \%\) using CD-HIT [28]. This preprocessing ensured that the training and testing datasets shared less than \(3 0 \%\) sequence similarity, thereby enabling the model to predict binding sites in dissimilar proteins. Binding residues were defined as those with a distance of less than \(0 . 5 \mathrm { \AA }\) plus the sum of the Van der Waals radii of the nearest atoms between the residue and the nucleic acid molecule, according to the BioLIP [29] database. The two datasets comprised 1219 sequences for training and 175 sequences for testing, with a total of 33,320 binding residues and 489,093 non-binding residues. Additionally, an independent test set generated by the GraphSite [24] model was utilized for further analysis of model performance. All datasets, including those for RNA-binding and antibody–antigen binding tasks, faced issues related to data imbalance. The protein-RNA binding site dataset was collected from GraphBind [23] and pprint2 [30], while the antibody–antigen binding site dataset was sourced from the SAbDaB [31] database. The same data preprocessing procedures were applied to the RNA-binding and antibody– antigen binding site tasks as used for the protein–DNA binding site task.

3 Methods

To facilitate the scientific community in predicting protein–ligand binding sites via CLAPE, the framework has been made publicly available on GitHub, including the model weights, which is hosted at https://github.com/YAndrewL/CLAPE. Additionally, a Python package and a corresponding command-line interface (CLI) have been developed, both of which are accessible for download and installation from the Python Package Index (PyPI) at https://pypi.org/project/clape/.

In this section, we will provide a detailed guide on the usage of the Python package and the command-line interface. Furthermore, we will explain how to visualize results when protein structural data is available and, importantly, outline the process for training a

CLAPE-like model from scratch. Unless otherwise stated, we will primarily use protein-DNA binding site prediction as an example in the following sections as in our original research paper [25].

3.1 Predicting with CLAPE Python Package

To utilize the CLAPE Python package for predicting ligand binding properties within Python code, it is necessary to prepare a coding environment, which can be set up using Conda or Mamba. This step includes installing essential dependencies. Although the CLAPE package offers considerable flexibility, it primarily relies on a pretrained large language model, specifically ProtBert, and its implementation is mainly built on PyTorch and the Transformers library from HuggingFace [32]. The environment can be manually configured according to the provided description to fit the user’s specific system requirements best. Alternatively, a YAML file has been provided to facilitate the creation of a Conda environment.

The CLAPE can be installed using the PyPi command line utility as follows:

$ wget https://github.com/YAndrewL/CLAPE/blob/main/environment.yaml
$ conda env create -f environment.yaml
$ conda activate clape
$ pip install clape

Alternatively, CLAPE can be installed from the source to access the latest version by executing the following commands:

$ git clone https://github.com/YAndrewL/CLAPE.git
$ cd CLAPE
$ pip instal .

After installing, users can utilize CLAPE-related functions within Python code. CLAPE receives protein sequences in FASTA format with extensions as .fasta or .fa, which is convenient for batch predictions. Here, we will use the prediction of DNA-binding sites as an example. A sample file has been provided to demonstrate the use of CLAPE, which can be obtained from the CLAPE GitHub repository. The command line and the format of the sample data file are as follows:

$ wget https://github.com/YAndrewL/CLAPE/blob/main/example.fa

the content of example.fa (DNA-binding proteins)

seq1

TSDLFNEIIPLGRLIHMVNQKKDRLLNEYLSPLDITAAQFKVLCSIRCAACITPV

ELKKV

LSVDLGALTRMLDRLVSKGWVERLPNPNDKRGVLVKLTTGGAAICEQCHQL

VGQDLHQEL

TKNLTADEVATLEYLLKKVLP

seq2

SELKMGELSELLGYALKRAQLRVFEDFLHCVAPVQLTPAQFSVLLLLDANPG

RNQTEIAT

TLGILRPNFVAMLDALEGRGLCVRTRSPSDRRSHILMLTDKGRATLARAKKL

VATRHEDR

LTELLGRDNRDALLSMLATIAR

Each protein sequence is processed by the ProtBert model to extract features, which are then fed into a backbone network to generate binding scores. Below, we present a basic use case in Python and provide an explaination of the code snippets.

from clape import Clape, get_weights

get_weights(“your/model/path”, “DNA”)

clape_mode \(=\) Clape(model_path=“your/model/path”, ligand=“DNA”)

results \(=\) clape_model.predict(input_file \(\mid =\) “example.fa”)

The results variable contains the predicted residue-level binding sites for the given protein sequences, which is a Python list

structured as: [‘0000000111100’, ‘001110001110110000111000’]. In this list, \(^ { \mathfrak { c } } 0 ^ { \mathfrak { s } }\) denotes a binding site, while \(^ { \mathfrak { c } } 1 ^ { \mathfrak { s } }\) represents a non-binding site. To retain the original prediction scores, which range between 0 and 1 and indicate the probability of a residue being a binding site, users can set the keep_score parameter to True in the predict method.

The get_weights function is employed to download the pretrained model weights, which are then loaded by CLAPE (see Notes 1 and 2). Additionally, users have the option to train their own models from scratch using the CLAPE-like framework, with the training loop detailed in the following section.

Moreover, the switch_ligand method, encapsulated within the Clape class, enables users to switch between different ligand prediction tasks. The threshold parameter can be used to specify the classification threshold: residues with scores above this threshold are classified as positive (binding sites), while those with scores below are classified as negative (non-binding sites).

If a feasible protein crystal structure is available, CLAPE also provides an interface that allows users to predict the binding site directly from PDB files. Predictions from PDB files can be made after defining the model, as previously described.

from clape import Clape, get_weights
getweights(“your/model/path”, “DNA”)
clape_model \(=\) Clape(model_path \(\equiv\) “your/model/path”, ligand \(\equiv\) “DNA”)
results \(=\) clape_model.predict_from_pdb(pdb_file, chain)
# or predict by a known PDB file
results \(=\) clape_model.predict_from_pdb(pdb_id, chain)

In the predict_from_pdb method, we offer a method to predict binding sites directly from PDB files. The user should specify the ID of the protein chain to be analyzed. The method first extracts the protein sequences from the PDB file and then generates embeddings using the model. Additionally, this method can be called by providing a known PDB entry in four letters, such as “1A0A,” in which case the corresponding PDB file will be automatically retrieved from the PDB website. As with sequence-based predictions, the keep_score parameter can be set to True to retain the

3.2 Predicting with CLAPE Command-Line Interface

prediction scores, reflecting the probability of each residue being a binding site.

In most scenarios, visualizing the prediction results is crucial for identifying potential binding sites and binding pockets within the 3-dimensional structure of the protein. To facilitate this, we provide a visualize parameter that allows users to generate visualizations directly from the input PDB file. This feature highlights the predicted binding sites within the protein’s 3D structure. For greater flexibility, an independent function is also provided to visualize the prediction results within protein structures, enabling users to customize their visualization needs. This function will be described in detail later in this section.

Apart from performing predictions in a Python console, we further provide a command-line interface, which provides a more straightforward and faster way to generate prediction results in a Unix-like operating system. The command-line interface is installed automatically when the CLAPE package is installed using the pip installer. Below is a simple use case demonstrating the command-line interface of CLAPE:

$ wget https://github.com/YAndrewL/CLAPE/blob/main/weights/DNA.pth

$ clape –input example.fa –output out.txt –lignd DNA –model

/path/to/downloaded/model

The first command line is employed to download pretrained model weights from GitHub. The subsequent command line generates prediction results, which are stored in a predefined file named out.txt. The parameters largely correspond to those used in the CLAPE Python package, as described earlier, with slight differences in the output file format, which are more suitable for terminal usage. Specifically, the parameter—input specifies the input protein sequences in FASTA format,—output defines the path for the output file,—ligand indicates the ligand whose binding is to be predicted, and—model designates the path to the downloaded model weights, as illustrated in the example command line. In addition to the parameters shown in the usage example,—threshold is used to set the classification threshold, and—cache specifies the download path for the pretrained protein language model weights, such as ProtBert for DNA-binding site predictions.

The prediction results generated by the command-line interface are similar to the FASTA format but include an additional line that indicates the classification labels. An example instance is provided below. It is noteworthy that the training data shares the

same format as the output file generated by the command-line interface. The training process will be detailed in the following section. Overall, the CLAPE command-line interface facilitates the rapid identification of potential ligand-binding sites, which can be utilized for downstream biological analyses and experimental applications.

The content of a sample DNA-binding site results from file out.txt

seq1

WVERLPNPNDKRGVLVKLTTGGAAICEQCHQLVGQDLHQELTKNLTADEVA

TLEYLLKKVLP

00010000011111000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000

seq2

RGLCVRTRSPSDRRSHILMLTDKGRATLARAKKLVATRHEDRLTELLGRDN

RDALLSMLATI

00000000000111110000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000

3.3 Visualizing the Results with CLAPE

The protein–ligand binding sites can be determined through the aforementioned steps using various methods, including Python package and command-line interface. However, there is a prevalent need to explore these binding sites from a structural perspective, which is advantageous for pocket discovery and ligand design. To address this, we have provided functions that enable visualization of the results for a given PDB file. As previously discussed, visualization can be generated by setting the visualize parameter to True within the predict_from_pdb method. Additionally, the visualization can be executed via an independent function, allowing for flexible integration with the Python package for analysis. This visualization function relies on PyMOL (https://www.pymol. org/), a widely used molecular visualization software, which can be installed through Conda for use in a Python console (see Note 3).

Need a license for activation

\(\$ 9\) conda install -c conda-forge -c schrodinger pymol-bundle

The installation can be tested by running the command from pymol import cmd in a Python console. Additionally, the CLAPE package offers utilities for visualizing the results. Below, we provide an example of how to predict DNA-binding sites using a PDB structure and subsequently visualize the results. The visualize function will handle all necessary processes to generate the visual representation of the results.

from clape import Clape, visualize
clape_model \(=\) Clape(model_path \(\equiv\) “your/model/path”, ligand \(\coloneqq\) “DNA”)
score, result \(=\) clape_model.predict_from_pdb(pdb_id \(\equiv\) “1A0A”, chain \(\coloneqq\) “A”, keep_score \(\equiv\) True)
result \(=\) list(map( lambda x: int(x), list(result)))
visualize(pdb_file \(\equiv\) “1A0A.pdb”, chain \(\equiv\) “A”, result \(\equiv\) result, out_file \(\equiv\) “out.pse”, select_only \(\equiv\) False, ligand_id \(\equiv\) ‘C,D’)
# or color the PDB structure by predicted scores
visualize(pdb_file \(\equiv\) “1A0A.pdb”, chain \(\equiv\) “A”, result \(\equiv\) score, out_file \(\equiv\) “out.pse”, select_only \(\equiv\) False, ligand_id \(\equiv\) ‘C,D’)

Firstly, the predict_from_pdb method will predict DNA-binding sites using a specified PDB entry, with the resulting PDB file stored in the current directory (see Note 4). This file will be used for subsequent visualization, although this behavior can be adjusted by using the pdb_cache parameter to specify a different path. It is important to note that the prediction results need to be

converted to integer type to meet the visualization requirements, as the original output is in string format.

The visualize function will then color the identified positive residues according to the specified color, with the default being red. This function accepts the predicted results, provided as a Python list, along with the PDB file as its primary inputs. The output will be a PyMOL session file in .pse format, which can be easily loaded into PyMOL. Users can opt to retain only the target protein chain by setting the select_only parameter to True. However, this may conflict with the ligand_id parameter, which is intended to retain ligand chains in the visualization (see Note 5). The default display mode is “surface,” but it can be modified by specifying the shown_as parameter, with options such as “cartoon” and “sticks” being supported.

Additionally, the function can take predicted scores as inputs and color the structure based on the binding probability, offering a more intuitive representation of potential binding sites. This is achieved by altering the B-factor values of the PDB file and applying gradient coloring according to these values.

In summary, the visualize function seamlessly integrates prediction results with structural data. Furthermore, this visualization function is compatible with predicted protein structures generated by advanced tools such as AlphaFold3 [33] and ESMFold [34], thereby enhancing sequence-to-function analysis. Figure 2 illustrates an example of visualizations produced by CLAPE, showcasing both binary results and binding probabilities.

3.4 Training CLAPE Model from Scratch

In the previous sections, we explored the use cases, application scenarios, and visualization tools associated with CLAPE. Furthermore, CLAPE is a highly adaptable framework that integrates a protein language model, a backbone network, and contrastive learning modules, all of which can be replaced with various stateof-the-art methods. In this section, we will outline the training process for CLAPE, which can be utilized to train models on diverse datasets and architectures for protein–ligand binding tasks. We will continue to use the DNA-binding site prediction task as a representative example.

To ensure a fair comparison when benchmarking models on CLAPE, we have provided the original training data, with the data collection process detailed in the previous section. The dataset can be downloaded as follows:


Fig. 2 The prediction results werevisualizeusing he LAPE visualize unction and subsequently ender The prediction results were visualized using the CLAPE visualize function and subsequently rendered in PyMOL. In the left panel, the protein structure and DNA ligand are displayed, with a gradient color scheme indicating binding probability. The right panel presents the structur

$ wget https://github.com/YAndrewL/CLAPE/blob/main/raw_data/DNA.tar.gz
$ tar -zxvf DNA.tar.gz

Alternatively, the dataset can be downloaded directly in a Python console using the following command:

from clape import get_data

get_weights(“your/data/path”, “DNA”)

The DNA directory contains training and testing data in a FASTA-like format, with an additional line indicating binary labels for residues, consistent with the output file format of the CLAPE command-line interface. Given the rapid increase in available protein structure data, the dataset can be expanded using various

database sources, such as BioLIP [29], which is an actively updated resource for protein–ligand binding data. The binding labels can be generated based on specific criteria. In the training process of CLAPE, binding sites are defined as residues that lie within a distance of 0.5 Å plus the sum of the Van der Waals radii of the two nearest atoms between the residue and the nucleic acid molecule.

Initially, protein sequences are passed into a protein language model to extract the initial protein features. In CLAPE’s training, ProtBert [26], a model from the Prottrans protein language model family, was utilized. However, users can employ different pretrained protein feature extractors, such as ESM [34], which was compared in our original research paper, or SaProt [35], which uses a predefined structural vocabulary.

Below, we present two examples of feature extraction using ProtBert and ESM, respectively. In these models, the first and last tokens represent the start and end tokens, while the middle tokens serve as embeddings for the residues in the protein sequences. For batched training, the generated features and labels usually need to be padded to the same length, which is typically the maximum length within a batch. This padding process can be customized in a collation function.

Extract features using ProtBert

from transformers import BertModel, BertTokenizer
import re
tokenizer \(=\) BertTokenizer.from_pretrained(“Rostlab/prot_bert”,
do_lower(case \(\equiv\) False)
pretrain_model \(=\) BertModel.from_pretrained(“Rostlab/prot_bert”)
sequence \(= 1\) .join(seq) # seq is the input protein sequences
sequence \(=\) re.sub(r”[UZOB]“,”X”, sequence)
coded_input \(=\) tokenizer(sequence, return_tensors \(\coloneqq\) ‘pt’)
features \(=\) pretrain_model(**encoded_input).last_hidden_state.squeeze(0)[1:- 1,:]

Extract features using ESM

import esm
model, alphabet \(=\) esm.pretrained.esm2_t33_650M_UR50D()
batchconverter \(=\) alphabet.get_batchconverter()
model.to(‘cuda’).eval()
batch_labels, batch_strs, batch_tokens \(=\) batch Converter(seq)
with torch.no_grad(): results \(=\) model(batch_tokens.to(‘cuda’), repr_layers \(\coloneqq\) [33], return Contacts \(\equiv\) False)
token Representations \(=\) results[“representations”][33]
features \(=\) token Representations.squeeze(0)[1:-1]

Collate sequence embedding into the same length

class BatchCollate(object): def__call__(self,data): #([bz,length,dim],label) # (feature, label) tensors \(=\) pad_sequence([t[0] for t in data], batch_first=True) # ([bz, length,dim]) labels \(=\) pad_sequence([t[1]) for t in data], batch_first=True, padding_value=-1) return tensors,labels

Second, the generated features are passed into a backbone network, which can be chosen flexibly based on comparative analysis. In the case of CLAPE, we found that a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (1DCNN) model outperformed other mainstream backbone models, such as MLPs, RNNs, or Transformerlike models. The training process can either start from randomly initialized weights or utilize pretrained weights. In CLAPE training, backpropagation is typically halted at the feature extractor. However, our experiments indicate that fine-tuning the protein language model can enhance model performance, albeit with a potential risk of overfitting.

The backbone model outputs the binding probability for each residue. The final step involves defining a loss function, which in CLAPE consists of two components: classification loss and contrastive loss. For CLAPE training, we employed a triplet center loss [36], but other loss functions, such as SupCon [37] for supervised settings and InfoNCE [38] for unsupervised settings, can also be applied. We evaluated the performance of different contrastive loss functions in our original research paper. The total loss function is a weighted sum of the contrastive loss and classification loss.

In summary, the CLAPE framework is highly flexible, allowing for customized training at each step. Common training loop practices can be applied once the sequence feature extractor, backbone network, and loss functions are configured. We utilized PyTorch Lightning to structure the training process, making it easy to scale

up to large datasets and model sizes. To provide clarity on the training processes of CLAPE, we offer an implementation hosted at https://github.com/JueWangTHU/CLAPE-SMB, which is a CLAPE model designed for small-molecule binding site prediction tasks [39] and can serve as a general template for other ligand types.

4 Notes

  1. The model_path or a similar parameter should refer to the parent directory of the model weights, excluding the name of the weight file itself. The file name will be matched based on the specified ligand type to ensure the correct model weights are loaded.
  2. If not explicitly specified, the download function for weights and data will retrieve all ligand types mentioned in the original paper, including DNA, RNA, and antibody–antigen datasets.
  3. The PyMOL API in the Python console needs to be activated, and an educational license can be obtained at https://pymol. org/edu/.
  4. When attempting to visualize a customized PDB file, ensure the file name is in lowercase, as this is purposely designed for predictions made from a PDB entry.
  5. The parameters select_only and ligand_id in the visualize function cannot be set to True simultaneously, as this will cause a conflict error.
