Large Language Model (LLM)-Based Advances in Prediction of Post-translational Modification Sites in Proteins

Pawel Pratyush, Suresh Pokharel, Stefan Schulze, Lisa Bramer, Robert H. Newman, and Dukka B. KC

Abstract

Post-translational modifications (PTMs) are vital regulators of protein function, influencing a myriad of cellular processes and disease mechanisms. Traditional experimental methods for PTM identification are both costly and labor-intensive, underlining the pressing need for efficient computational approaches. Early computational strategies predominantly relied on primary amino acid sequences and handcrafted features, which often lacked the contextual and structural understanding necessary for precise PTM site prediction. The emergence of transformer-based large language models (LLMs), particularly protein language models (pLMs), has revolutionized PTM prediction by producing context-aware embeddings that capture functional and structural intra-sequence dependencies. In this chapter, we provide a comprehensive review of recent advancements in leveraging LLMs (or, pLMs) for PTM site prediction, an important residue-level task in protein research. We identify emerging trends in the field, including the application of fine-tuning techniques, the integration of embeddings from multiple pLMs, and the incorporation of multiple modalities such as codon-aware embeddings, 3D structural data, and conventional representations. Additionally, we discuss tools that employ graph-based representations, the mamba architecture, and contrastive learning paradigms to further refine pLM-powered PTM site prediction models. We finally explore the interpretability and explainability aspects of the embeddings used in various tools. Despite the significant progress made, persistent limitations remain, and we outline these challenges while proposing directions for future research.

Key words Post-translational modification, Large language model, Protein language model, Mamba, Graph, GPT, Fine-tuning, AlphaFold, Explainability, Contrastive learning

1 Introduction

The functional complexity of proteomes is driven not only by the number of protein-coding gene sequences, transcriptional regulation, or alternative splicing of mRNA transcripts but also by posttranslational modifications (PTMs) that result in a multitude of proteoforms [1, 2]. These modifications can expand the proteomic complexity by several orders of magnitude and can alter a protein’s

Dukka B. KC (ed.), Large Language Models (LLMs) in Protein Bioinformatics, Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 2941, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-0716-4623-6_19, \(^ { © }\) The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025

structure and function, or serve as signals for interactions, localization, or degradation [3–5]. Since many PTMs can be dynamically added or removed, they serve crucial functions in numerous cellular processes, signaling pathways, and regulatory networks [6– 8]. Thus, PTMs are crucial for understanding cell biology, health, and disease [9, 10].

The addition and removal of PTMs is a non-template-driven process and hence cannot directly be inferred from a protein’s sequence. Existing methods for the experimental identification of PTMs, such as mass spectrometry-driven proteomics, are timeconsuming, expensive, and often yield an incomplete overview of the proteins and sites that can be modified [11, 12]. Therefore, computational approaches are needed to accurately predict PTM sites across whole proteomes.

Computational methods often rely on encoding amino acid sequences into numerical representations known as feature vectors [13]. These vectors act as proxies for the sequences, allowing them to serve as input for various inference models. The accuracy of these models depends heavily on the quality of the feature vectors, among other factors. In residue-level tasks such as PTM site prediction, the focus narrows to the context of specific residues within the overall sequence. For example, in crotonylation site prediction, lysine residues are of particular interest. Within a single protein sequence, some lysines may be crotonylated (positive sites) while others are not (negative sites). Therefore, feature vectors must distinguish between lysines belonging to different classes (i.e., “between” positives and negatives). This is known as inter-class differentiation. Simultaneously, they should differentiate lysines within the same class (i.e., “within” positives and “within” negatives), which is referred to as intra-class differentiation. These concepts lead to the notion of contextualization. Simply put, the feature vector for each residue should reflect its unique context within the sequence.

Based on the approach to contextualization, computational tools can be divided into two groups. The first group represents the pre-contextualization era of tools, where contextualizing vectors was challenging. To address this challenge, researchers introduced the concept of sequence windows. Instead of considering only the residue-of-interest, a predefined window of amino acids around it is selected [14]. The target residue is positioned at the center, with upstream and downstream flanking residues on either side. These window sequences, which are sometimes referred to as peptides due to their shorter length, are extracted for each residueof-interest (i.e., lysines, in the case of crotonylation prediction). Since the local environment of each residue differs, the corresponding window sequences capture different contexts. For instance, the window sequence around a lysine residue at position 4 will differ from that at position 2, as the neighboring residues are different at these positions. This approach imposes a level of

contextualization because the likelihood of two window sequences being identical is relatively low. Furthermore, homology removal tools, such as CD-HIT [15] and MMseqs [16], are applied to these window sequences to eliminate similar sequences, ensuring the uniqueness of the contextual representation for the residues. These window sequences are then encoded into feature vectors. Initially, researchers used the physicochemical properties of amino acids to encode these sequences [14, 17]. The major drawback of this class of features was that the optimal set of properties to use was not known a priori. Researchers had to use some heuristics or feature selection algorithms [18–20] to create the optimal feature set for their tasks. This posed extra overhead and introduced the possibility of missing important properties since it was challenging to exhaustively explore all features. These types of features were commonly known as “handcrafted” features due to the constant interventions required to optimize the feature set. Natural Language Processing (NLP) encoding techniques have greatly streamlined feature extraction by removing the need for manual intervention [13]. With these techniques, features can be automatically obtained by simply inputting window sequences into NLP models, ranging from simple binary encoding to more advanced embeddings (via language models). For a comprehensive review of tools from this era and the encodings used (including various NLP encodings), please refer to the literature by Pakhrin et al [14] and Pokharel et al [13]. However, the tools in this era had limitations. For instance, they primarily used linear sequence data (i.e., the primary amino acid structure), which assumes proteins are linear and fails to capture three-dimensional spatial relationships. Likewise, window sequences represent isolated fragments, limiting the encoding’s scope to local information and omitting important global context. This is significant because, in the 3D structure, distant amino acids in the primary sequence can be spatially close to the residue-of-interest, an aspect not captured by primary sequence-based methods alone. To overcome these limitations, there is a need for encoders that are capable of processing the entire protein sequence and generating global context-aware embeddings. Ideally, such an encoder would represent each residue with a vector embedding that is aware of all other residues in the protein sequence, capturing essential long-range interactions and structural context.

The early advancements in NLP saw a surge in models capable of generating contextualized embeddings, which capture the meaning of words based on their context within a sequence [21]. These embeddings were initially driven by sequential models like Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, which were designed to process sequential data by retaining information across token positions [22, 23]. Recognizing the sequential nature of protein sequences,

researchers began adopting these models for protein-related tasks, enabling contextualized representations of amino acids within protein sequences [24]. However, the inherent sequential nature of RNNs and LSTMs introduced several critical limitations [25, 26]. For instance, these models process tokens one at a time, which makes it challenging to retain information over long sequences. As they progress sequentially, earlier contextual information may gradually diminish, especially in lengthy sequences. As a consequence, RNN and LSTM-based models are often limited in their ability to capture long-range dependencies. Additionally, sequential processing restricts parallel computation, making training and inference slower, especially for large datasets. These limitations highlighted the need for alternative architectures that could handle entire sequences at once to efficiently capture complex dependencies across long sequences and facilitate faster computations. This need eventually catalyzed the development of attentionbased architectures, with the transformer model [27] marking a revolutionary shift. Unlike RNNs and LSTMs, transformers employ self-attention mechanisms, allowing them to capture relationships between words across an entire sequence simultaneously. The self-attention mechanism processes all tokens at once in parallel, efficiently computing the context over a sentence or even a passage “in a single shot.” As a result, transformers excel at capturing long-range dependencies and are highly parallelizable, making them ideal for large-scale training. This initial model architecture laid the foundation for scaling. Researchers rapidly increased both the depth (layers) and width (parameters) of transformers, training these larger models on vast corpora to create what we now call large language models (LLMs). These LLMs, like BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) [28], GPT (Generative Pretrained Transformers) [29], and their successors, which are typically pretrained on a large corpus of data, leveraged immense datasets and billions of parameters that produced models capable of performing a wide array of complex language tasks, often without task-specific fine-tuning. This scalability of the transformer model has enabled state-of-the-art performance across various domains, fundamentally changing the landscape of NLP and driving advancements in fields such as machine translation, text generation, and question-answering [30].

Inspired by the success of NLP in processing text, researchers have applied transformers to protein sequences, where each amino acid (or, group of amino acids or motif in some cases) is treated as a “word” and each protein sequence as a “sentence” [13]. This approach led to the development of protein language models (pLMs), LLMs pretrained on large protein datasets like UniRef50 and UniRef100 using self-supervised techniques usually based on masked language modeling (MLM) or casual language modeling (CLM). These pLMs leverage extensive pretraining datasets to

capture complex relationships between residues (i.e., “grammar” of protein language), creating context-aware embeddings that encode both local and global dependencies essential for understanding protein functions and structural relationships inherent in the sequences. Some prominent examples of existing pLMs include ProtTrans [31] by Rostlab, which incorporates models like Prot-BERT (based on BERT) and ProtT5 (based on T5) trained on UniRef100 for tasks like secondary structure prediction, subcellular localization, and membrane protein classification; ESM (Evolutionary Scale Modeling) [32] series by Meta, which captures evolutionary information for structure prediction, function prediction, and mutation effect prediction; AlphaFold’s Evoformer [33], which integrates sequence and evolutionary data for robust 3D structure predictions; and TAPE [34], a benchmark suite pretrained on the Pfam dataset, which is designed for tasks like secondary structure prediction and homology detection. For an in-depth exploration of various pLMs and their architectures and applications, please refer to Chapter 1.

This brings us to the current generation of tools, which we refer to as the post-contextualization era, where pLMs and LLMs have transformed many downstream tasks including PTM site prediction. New approaches utilizing these models have demonstrated superior performance compared to earlier, pre-contextualized encoding methods [35–40]. With pLMs, we now have encoders that generate embeddings with full sequence context and awareness of protein functions and properties. Building on these advancements, this chapter explores how LLMs and pLMs are being utilized to address various PTM site prediction tasks. We will start with an overview of tools that leverage LLMs/pLMs for PTM site prediction, and then identify trends and advancements in these tools. Finally, we will discuss their limitations and areas for future research directions. This chapter aims to provide readers with a comprehensive understanding of the current landscape of PTM site prediction tools and how these advancements contribute to more accurate and contextually aware predictions. We hope the chapter will be especially useful for researchers looking to apply or develop pLM-based approaches, offering insights into state-of-theart methodologies and highlighting areas where further development is possible.

Subheading 2 provides a brief overview of single PTM and multi-PTM site prediction tools that utilize LLMs/pLMs, and custom transformers. Subheading 3 discusses the key trends in the field and is further divided into seven subsections (Subheadings 3.1, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, and 3.7), each highlighting the key trends observed in these tools. Subheading 4 discusses the limitations of these tools through five subsections (Subheadings 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, and 4.5) and outlines future directions inspired by these shortcomings. We conclude the chapter with a discussion and final remarks in Subheading 5.

2 Brief Review of Tools Utilizing pLMs/Custom Transformer for PTM Site Prediction

While this chapter primarily focuses on discussing trends and advancements in LLM-based approaches for PTM site prediction, this section aims to familiarize readers with existing state-of-the-art LLM/pLM-based tools developed in recent years for some wellstudied PTMs. It is important to note that we provide only a highlevel overview of these tools and encourage readers to consult the original published literature for a more in-depth description of each tool.

Table 1 presents a categorized list of tools designed for the prediction of sites of some well-studied PTM types, separating those that utilize pretrained pLMs from those that incorporate custom transformers into their workflows. The subsequent subsections (Subheadings 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 2.10, and 2.11) provide a brief description of these tools, organized by PTM type.

Table 1 Recently developed tools for predicting PTMs using pretrained LLMs/pLMs and custom transformers

PTM(s) Target residue Pretrained LLM/pLM-based tools Custom transformer-based tools
Phosphorylation S, T, Y Phosformer [41], LMPhosSite [42], CaLMPhosKAN [43] TransPhos [44], DL-SPhos [45]
Glycosylation (N-linked, O-linked, and O-GlcNAcylation) N, S, T Glycosylation (N: N-linked, O: O-Linked): LMGlyPred [46] (N), EMNGLy [47] (N), HOTGpred [48] (O), CoNglyPred [49] (N), Stack-OglyPred-PLM [50] (O) O-GlcNAcylation: LM-OGlcNAc-Site [51] -
Ubiquitination K Work of Pourmirzaei et al. [52] -
Acetylation K MSTL-Kace [53], TransPTM [54] -
S-Nitrosylation C pLMSNOSite [55] -
Succinylation K LMsuccSite [39] -
SUMOylation K SumoPred-PLM [56], iSUMO-RsFPN [57] GPS-SUMO 2.0 [58]
Crotonylation K BERT-Kcr [59], LMCrot [38] -
Glutarylation K Deepro-Glu [60], ProtTrans-Glutar [61] -
Glycation K BERT-Kgly [62] -
Multi-PTM approaches Multiple residues PTMGPT2 [36], PTG-PLM [63], PTM-Mamba [64], UniPTM [65] MIND-S [66], PTM-CMGMS [35]

As can be observed from Table 1, existing tools for PTM site prediction mostly employ pretrained pLMs to extract protein sequence representations, which are then used as input features for downstream PTM site prediction tasks. Additionally, we discuss tools based on custom transformer architectures, highlighting innovations in design and functionality. Generative models, representing a novel paradigm in PTM site prediction, are also reviewed for their unique contributions. Lastly, multi-PTM site prediction approaches are explored, showcasing tools that expand predictive capabilities across diverse modification types and residues.

2.1 Phosphorylation Site Prediction

Phosphorylation is a PTM in which protein kinases catalyze the addition of the gamma phosphate of ATP to specific amino acid residues, primarily serine (S), threonine (T), and tyrosine (Y) [67]. Several custom transformer-based and pretrained protein language model (pLM)-based tools have been developed to predict phosphorylation sites, including Phosformer [41], TransPhos [44], DL-SPhos [45], LMPhosSite [42], and CaLMPhosKAN [43].

Phosformer [41] is a deep learning (DL) model where a domain-specific protein language model is trained specifically for predicting kinase-specific phosphorylation sites in proteins given the kinase domain sequence and the substrate peptide sequence. Phosformer is trained in two phases. In the pretraining phase, a transformer model consisting of an encoder and decoder is trained, which learns from the overall sequence using the masked language modeling (MLM) objective (the goal of training is to predict the masked token). The pretraining phase enables the model to understand the structural and functional properties of proteins. This phase of the model is trained on 295,320 diverse kinase domain sequences and 5538 substrate proteins. The encoder consists of 6 attention layers, with each layer having 12 attention heads. Together, these layers produce an output tensor with 768 embedding dimensions. In the second phase, the model is fine-tuned to predict kinase-specific phosphorylation. The fine-tuning step takes two inputs: (1) an 11-mer window sequence and (2) an unaligned kinase domain sequence. Using this information, it predicts the likelihood of phosphorylation for the middle residues (“S,” “T,” and “Y”) in the peptide by the corresponding kinase.

Similarly, TransPhos [44] and DL-SPhos [45] are other custom transformer-based tools tailored for phosphorylation site prediction. TransPhos focuses on general phosphorylation site prediction, using a transformer encoder integrated with densely connected convolutional neural network blocks. On the other hand, DL-SPhos specializes in serine phosphorylation site prediction that uses five transformer blocks equipped with multi-headed attention and causal mask layers. Interestingly, DL-SPhos not only identifies conserved motifs linked to serine phosphorylation but also excels in kinase-specific predictions.

LMPhosSite [42] takes a different approach by utilizing a pretrained pLM to predict general phosphorylation sites. The architecture of LMPhosSite is stack-based consisting of two components and a meta-classifier. The first component is a supervised embedding layer that encodes local sequence information from a window of amino acids surrounding the site-of-interest. The local encodings are then trained using a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN). Parallelly, the second component first extracts embeddings from ProtT5 pLM using the full sequence as an input and subsequently uses a feed-forward neural network on top of the embedding corresponding to the site-of-interest (“S,” “T,” and “Y”). The prediction probabilities from these two components are fused using a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) as a meta-classifier, allowing the model to incorporate local and global information to enhance prediction accuracy.

CaLMPhosKAN [43] further extends the capabilities of pLM-based models for general phosphorylation site predictor by integrating codon-aware embeddings from CaLM [68] (a pLM pretrained on coding DNA sequences) with amino acid-aware embeddings from ProtT5 (a pLM pretrained on amino acid sequences). The architecture of CaLMPhosKAN consists of three components (or modules) in succession. The first component, the Embedding Extraction and Fusion Module, combines the codonaware and amino acid-aware window embeddings (generated from full sequence) using early fusion strategy. The second component, the Spatiotemporal Feature Extraction Module, utilizes a ConvBi-GRU (Convolutional Bi-directional Gated Recurrent Unit Network) that extracts features from the fused embeddings to capture spatial and sequential correlations among neighboring residues. The last component, the Wav-KAN Module, consists of a classification head based on wavelet-based Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN) [69] which produces the classification inference of the siteof-interest. The novelty of this work lies in the use of complementary information contained in the codon space.

2.2 Glycosylation Site Prediction

In glycosylation, glycotransferases catalyze the attachment of carbohydrate chains (glycans) to specific amino acid residues [70]. It is broadly categorized into N-linked glycosylation, which occurs on asparagine (N) residues within a consensus sequence (N-X-S/T, where X is any amino acid except proline), and O-linked glycosylation, where glycans attach to serine (S) or threonine (T) residues. A specific type of O-linked glycosylation is O-GlcNAcylation, in which a single N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) moiety is added to serine or threonine residues [71].

LM-OGlcNAc-Site [51] is a tool for predicting O-GlcNAcylation sites in proteins by integrating embeddings from multiple pretrained pLMs. Specifically, it utilized embeddings corresponding to the site-of-interest, extracted using full sequence

as an input from three pLMs: Ankh, ESM-2, and ProtT5, which potentially capture complementary sequence representations. Feed-forward neural networks (FFNNs) were trained on embeddings from each pLM independently and integrated via decisionlevel fusion for improved performance. Decision-level fusion, using a hard-voting mechanism, outperformed individual models and other ensemble approaches, demonstrating superior performance on benchmark datasets. The findings emphasize the advantages of combining embeddings from multiple pLMs for PTM site prediction.

LMNglyPred [46] is an N-linked glycosylation prediction tool, trained using two datasets: N-GlyDE and N-GlycositeAtlas. Using full sequence as an input to ProtT5 pLM, embeddings corresponding to the site-of-interest were extracted. Thus obtained per-residue embeddings were trained using the MLP model for classification purposes.

EMNGly [47] further advances N-linked glycosylation prediction by integrating embeddings from ESM-1b [32] pLM with structure-aware embeddings from the Masked Inverse Folding (MIF) model [72]. ESM-1b captures both global and local sequence dependencies, while the MIF model provides local 3D structural information through structure-aware embeddings. For prediction, sequence embeddings are generated as 1024- dimensional vectors specific to the site-of-interest, supplemented by a window-level embedding spanning 41 residues. Structural embeddings, encoded as 256-dimensional vectors, represent the spatial properties of the glycosylation site environment. These combined features are then input into a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for prediction. This work exemplifies the integration of sequence and structure to improve the accuracy of N-linked glycosylation site prediction.

CoNglyPred [49] is another tool designed for the prediction of N-linked glycosylation sites by integrating sequence and structural features using pLMs and graph-based approaches. It uses ESM-2 to extract sequence representations that capture contextual and interactional information across amino acid sequences. Specifically, it uses window embeddings of length 41 to extract sequential representations from ESM-2 using a full-length input sequence. For structural features, CoNglyPred employs AlphaFold2 [33] predicted 3D structures to construct protein graphs, where residues are represented as nodes and their spatial relationships as edges, processed through a Graph Transformer Network (GTN). The GTN dynamically captures spatial relationships between residues using the attention mechanism. To effectively integrate sequence and structural information, CoNglyPred uses a co-attention mechanism, allowing mutual enhancement of features derived from ESM-2 and GTN. The fused features are then processed through an MLP classifier to make the final predictions.

Stack-OglyPred-PLM [50] is a stacked generalization framework designed to predict O-linked glycosylation sites by integrating per-residue embeddings from two pLMs: ProtT5 and Ankh. These embeddings, corresponding to the site-of-interest (“S” or “T”), are used to train two independent base models based on MLP. The outputs of these base models are then combined using a meta-MLP classifier, creating a stacked architecture that leverages complementary features from both pLMs to enhance predictive performance.

Similarly, HOTGpred [48] introduces a novel hybrid approach to predicting O-linked glycosylation sites by leveraging features from 14 pretrained pLMs, such as ESM 1v, FastText, Seq2Vec, ProtTransT5UniRef50, and ProtTransT5XLU50, alongside 11 conventional feature descriptors. The top five pLM-based encodings, selected for their superior discriminative ability, were linearly integrated into high-dimensional hybrid features, multiple tree-based algorithms were trained to generate feature importance scores (FIS), and optimal feature sets were identified using these scores. The final architecture combines the selected 220-dimensional feature set with an extreme gradient boosting (XGBT) classifier.

2.3 Ubiquitination Site Prediction

In Ubiquitination PTM, ubiquitin ligases catalyze the covalent attachment of ubiquitin to lysine residues of target proteins [73]. The work of Pourmirzaei et al. [52] presents a comprehensive study on ubiquitination site prediction in human proteins, comparing three paradigms: feature-based classical ML methods, end-toend sequence-based DL models, and hybrid strategies combining engineered features with raw sequence embeddings. The models utilize architectures such as LSTMs and variants of BERT variants (BERT-small, BERT-tiny, Nystromformer, SqueezeBERT), which effectively capture long-range dependencies and contextual information in protein sequences. Additionally, one-hot encoding and word embeddings were employed for sequence-based inputs, while engineered features like position-specific scoring matrices (PSSM), amino acid composition (AAC), and physicochemical properties (PCP) provided valuable biological insights. Sliding window techniques ensured that fixed-length subsequences were created with the target residue at the center. Among the models tested, the final model, an LSTM-based hybrid approach, outperformed others (even BERT) by combining sequence embeddings and engineered features, such as PSSM and PCP.

2.4 Acetylation Site Prediction

In acetylation, acetyltransferases catalyze the addition of an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to the ε-amino group of lysine residues [74]. Recently, pLM-based computational tools such as MSTL-Kace [53] and TransPTM [54] have been developed to predict acetylation sites, leveraging advances in deep learning and protein sequence embeddings.

MSTL-Kace [53] model architecture is built upon the Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) [28], adapted to encode protein sequences as natural language sentences. Specifically, the model begins with a domain-specific BERT pretrained on protein sequences from six prokaryotic species, ensuring that embeddings capture biological context. Fine-tuning is performed in two stages to address the small sample size issue: the first stage uses larger datasets to create an intermediate model and the second stage further fine-tunes this model using species-specific data. The architecture uses the embeddings of the “CLS” token, which aggregates sequence-level features, and incorporates embeddings from the last six encoder layers for optimal feature fusion followed by a downstream classification.

The TransPTM [54] is a transformer-based model designed for non-histone acetylation site prediction, leveraging the power of pLMs and graph neural networks (GNNs). TransPTM uses ProtT5 pLM to generate contextualized embeddings of amino acid sequences. These embeddings encapsulate residue-level dependencies and serve as node features in a complete graph, where each amino acid is a node and edges represent interactions between residues. The model’s architecture incorporates three TransformerConv layers, which use self-attention enabling the detection of critical relationships among residues. The encoded graph features are followed by a downstream classification MLP that predicts the probability of acetylation at specific lysine residues.

2.5 S-nitrosylation Site Prediction

S-nitrosylation is a PTM in which a nitric oxide (NO) group is covalently attached to the thiol side chain of cysteine \(( ^ { \infty } \mathbf { C } ^ { \mathfrak { N } } )\) residues in proteins [75]. pLMSNOSite [55] is a tool designed to predict S-nitrosylation sites in proteins by stack generalization of two models trained using supervised word embeddings and embeddings from ProtT5 pLM. The word embedding module uses a 2D CNN to capture local amino acid interactions around a window sequence centered on the “C” residue-of-interest. The second module uses the per-residue ProtT5 embeddings, corresponding to the site-of-interest, to capture the global context of the full protein sequence. These two separately trained modules are then fused using a stacked generalization approach, where a metaclassifier combines features from both models to improve prediction performance outperforming existing SNO site prediction tools.

2.6 Succinylation Site Prediction

Succinylation PTM involves covalent attachment of succinyl group to the ε-amino group of lysine (K) residues in proteins [76]. LMSuccSite [39] is a pLM-driven tool designed to predict succinylation sites, utilizing both supervised word embeddings and unsupervised pLM embeddings. Its architecture is built upon a stack generalization of two modules. The first module encodes

protein sequence fragments into vector representations through a supervised word embedding layer with a 2D CNN, which learns local sequence patterns by analyzing interactions within a windowed sequence centered on the target “K” residue. In parallel, the second module employs per-residue ProtT5 embeddings, focusing specifically on the site-of-interest to capture the global context of the full protein sequence. The integrated model effectively combines local and global contexts, making LMSuccSite highly proficient in accurately identifying succinylation sites and outperforming conventional sequence-based prediction methods.

2.7 SUMOylation Site Prediction

SUMOylation is a PTM where Small Ubiquitin-like Modifier (SUMO) proteins are covalently attached to lysine residues on target proteins by ligases [77]. Recent advancements include the development of tools like SumoPred-PLM [56], iSUMO-RsFPN [57], and GPS-SUMO 2.0 [58] (non-LLM/custom transformerbased), which leverage pLM and custom transformer to predict sumoylation sites with improved accuracy and efficiency.

SumoPred-PLM [56] utilizes pLMs for predicting SUMOylation and SUMO2/3 sites in proteins. It uses ProtT5 pLM to extract contextualized embeddings of lysine residues in protein sequences. These embeddings are 1024-dimensional vectors acting as a high-level feature essential for site-specific predictions. The extracted embeddings serve as inputs to the downstream classification model.

iSUMO-RsFPN [57] is another lysine SUMOylation site prediction tool that integrates physicochemical properties features: AAindex and ZScale encodings and ProteinBert, a pretrained protein language model, which generates 128-dimensional embeddings capturing contextual sequence information. Specifically, an input protein sequence fragment of length 39 is fed to Protein-BERT [78] which outputs a representation of size (39, 1024) which is fed to a Residual Pyramid Network (RsFPN). A similar modal is trained using AAindex and ZScale features and results are combined using an ensemble approach.

GPS-SUMO 2.0 [58] is built on the combined architecture of transformers, dense neural networks (DNNs), and penalized logistic regression (PLR) to predict SUMOylation sites and SUMOinteracting motifs (SIMs) with high accuracy. At the core, a custom transformer model is pretrained on 145,545 lysine modification sites, capturing rich contextual information within sequence windows, and is fine-tuned on a focused dataset of 52,404 SUMOylation sites and 163 SIMs. To enhance prediction accuracy, the system integrates seven sequence-based features, such as k-spaced amino acid pairs (CKSAAPs) and position-specific scoring matrices (PSSMs), along with three structural features, including secondary structure (SS), accessible surface area (ASA), and backbone torsion angles (BTAs). Each feature is scored through a DNN model, and

2.8 Crotonylation Site Prediction

the outputs, along with transformer-derived contextual representations, are consolidated using PLR with an L2 penalty for final prediction.

Crotonylation is a PTM wherein a crotonyl group is covalently attached to the ε-amino group of lysine (K) residues in proteins [79]. In this section, we discuss language model-based computation tools: BERT-Kcr [59] and LMCrot [38] to predict crotonylation sites.

BERT-Kcr [59] utilizes a pretrained BERT model, originally developed for NLP tasks, by treating window sequences as “sentences” and individual amino acids as “words.” Raw protein sequences are directly input into the BERT-Mini pretrained model to extract high-dimensional feature representations from the final encoder layer, which are then passed to a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM)-based classifier for lysine crotonylation (Kcr) site prediction. The authors also fine-tuned the BERT model with protein sequences to adapt it specifically for this task. Interestingly, the original natural language BERT outperformed the protein-specific fine-tuned version, highlighting its inherent ability to capture meaningful contextual information applicable to protein sequences.

LMCrot [38] improves predictive performance by integrating embeddings from the ProtT5 pLM with local window-based representations. Its architecture employs intermediate fusion-based stacked generalization across three base models. The first base model, ResConvBiLSTM (Residual Convolutional BiLSTM), is trained on window-level embeddings of length 33, derived from ProtT5 using the full sequence as input, referred to as FSWE (Full Sequence-based Window Embeddings). The second and third base models leverage local representations from a supervised embedding layer and physicochemical properties of window sequences. The second base is a 2D-CNN, while the third is a DNN. Latent representations from these models are merged via stacked generalization and trained on a DNN-based meta-classifier to produce the final output.

2.9 Glutarylation Site Prediction

Glutarylation is a PTM in which a glutaryl group, derived from glutaryl-CoA, is covalently attached to the ε-amino group of lysine residues in proteins [76]. Deepro-Glu [60] and ProtTrans-Glutar [61] are two pLM-based glutarylation site prediction tools.

Deepro-Glu [60] is an ensemble prediction model for glutarylation site prediction developed by integrating features from Prot-BERT [31] with handcrafted biochemical and evolutionary features. The architecture consists of two parallel sub-models: one processes embeddings obtained for a fragment of protein sequence (length 41) generated by ProtBert using a 1D-CNN to capture local sequence semantics, while the other utilizes handcrafted

features including physicochemical properties, evolutionary profiles, and sequence patterns fed into a BiLSTM network to learn sequence dependencies. The two feature sets are combined using an attention mechanism, which assigns weights to fused features to focus on important information for prediction. The final predictions are generated using an MLP model.

ProtTrans-Glutar [61] is another model designed to predict lysine glutarylation sites by leveraging both traditional physicochemical features and pLM-based embeddings. At its core, the model incorporates the per-residue embeddings derived from ProtT5, alongside amino acid composition (EAAC) and Composition/Transition/Distribution (CTDD) features. The combined features are then used to train a downstream classification model consisting of a XGBoost classifier.

2.10 Glycation Site Prediction

Glycation is a non-enzymatic modification in which reducing sugars, such as glucose, covalently attach to lysine or arginine residues in proteins [80]. BERT-Kgly [62] is a pLM-based model to predict lysine glycation (Kgly) sites in proteins. The model combines the representations from three language models: BERT-Base, TAPE (trained on 31 million Pfam protein domains), and BERT-Prot [31] (trained on 556,603 UniProt sequences). Specifically, the “CLS” token embedding of the protein fragment is extracted from the pLMs. It is further processed through the 1D-CNN and BiLSTM to train the downstream classification model. This architecture combines the strengths of the natural language model and protein language models offering an effective approach for lysine glycation prediction.

2.11 Multi-PTM Site Prediction

In this section, we will discuss tools that utilize the same architecture for predicting more than one type of PTM site. These include pretrained LLM/pLM-based tools such as PTMGPT2 [36], PTG-PLM [63], PTM-Mamba [64], and UniPTM [65] and two tools that train their own custom transformer architectures: MIND-S [66] and PTM-CMGMS [35].

PTMGPT2 [36] is a multiple PTM site prediction framework capable of predicting 19 different types of PTMs. It is built upon the ProtGPT2 [81] (a GPT-based decoder-only pLM) and applies a prompt-based fine-tuning method tailored to PTM identification. Here, each prompt is crafted to embed both the protein sequence segment of interest (window sequence) and a classification label, guiding the model to learn patterns associated with PTM sites. To achieve this, PTMGPT2 constructs prompts by framing the input as a sequence paired with a “fill-in-the-blank” label: the prompt includes tokens that specify “SEQUENCE:” followed by the amino acid segment, and “LABEL:” with either a “POSITIVE” or “NEG-ATIVE” indicator for modified or unmodified regions, respectively. During fine-tuning, the ProtGPT2 model, which was originally

trained on large protein sequence data, learns to associate these prompt labels with specific PTM sites. This prompt-based setup allows PTMGPT2 to operate without a specialized classification head; instead, the decoder predicts PTM sites by interpreting each sequence within the structured prompt.

Similarly, PTG-PLM [63] is a CNN-based model developed to predict glycosylation and glycation sites utilizing features from six pLMs: ProtBERT-BFD, ProtBERT, ProtALBERT, ProtXLNet, ESM-1b, and TAPE. The features are extracted using WSWE (Window Sequence Window Embedding), where protein sequence fragments are fed to pLMs to extract window-level embeddings. These representations from the pLMs are combined and fed as input to the CNN classification model.

UniPTM [65] is another tool designed for multi-PTM site prediction, accommodating nine distinct modifications. It uses full-length protein sequences for training and testing, bypassing the limitations of traditional sliding window approaches. It utilizes embeddings generated by state-of-the-art pLMs, including ESM-2, ProtBert, and ProtT5, which capture contextual relationships between amino acids. The model architecture integrates these per-residue embeddings with a convolutional layer and an eighthead transformer encoder for feature extraction. This design enables the model to learn long-range dependencies across the protein sequence, which is essential for understanding the nuanced processes influencing PTM formation.

PTM-Mamba [64] is a PTM-specific pLM designed to explicitly represent PTMs within protein sequences. Existing pLMs such as ESM-2 and ProtT5 effectively capture the structural and functional properties of proteins but often overlook the critical role of PTMs in proteins. To address this gap, PTM-Mamba incorporates a PTM-specific token into its vocabulary, enabling the model to process both unmodified and modified protein sequences. It is built upon the Mamba model architecture, a structured state space model (SSM) known for its efficiency in handling long sequences. By utilizing hardware-aware primitives, Mamba overcomes the quadratic time complexity associated with traditional transformer models [27]. PTM-Mamba employs bidirectional gated Mamba blocks that integrate outputs with ESM-2 embeddings through a novel gating mechanism. This fusion allows the model to effectively incorporate PTM information, enhancing its predictive capabilities. PTM-Mamba exhibits improved performance over ESM-2 in various PTM-specific tasks, including phosphorylation and acetylation site prediction, disease association studies, and druggability assessments. As the first pLM capable of uniquely representing both unmodified and PTM-modified sequences, PTM-Mamba offers a significant advancement in proteomics, facilitating more accurate modeling and design of proteins with PTMs.

MIND-S [66] is designed to predict 26 types of PTM sites leveraging both sequence and structural information. The model processes protein sequences using one-hot encodings, transforming them into dense feature representations through a feedforward neural network. These embeddings are further refined using a BiLSTM layer to encode positional information, which acts as a foundation for two key components: multi-head self-attention and graph attention layers. The multi-head self-attention captures sequential relationships within the protein, while the graph attention layer integrates spatial proximity insights derived from protein contact maps. By combining outputs from these layers, MIND-S generates PTM site prediction scores for each amino acid residue. Its multi-label training strategy enables the simultaneous prediction of multiple PTM types, even for those with limited data, by sharing learned representations across PTMs.

PTM-CMGMS [35] predicts three types of PTM sites (crotonylation, succinylation, and S-nitrosylation) by integrating multiscale sequence data and structural information. Specifically, the model employs a novel architecture that incorporates structureaware representation that captures structural features at the atom, amino acid, and whole protein levels, leveraging AlphaFoldpredicted protein structures. These representations are optimized using contrastive learning, which enhances their discriminative capacity. Additionally, the model extracts multi-scale sequence representations through a combination of BiLSTM, transformer encoders, and multi-scale CNNs, capturing local and contextual sequence patterns. A motif-based approach aligns sequences to identify recurring PTM-related patterns, further refining predictions of crotonylation, succinylation, and S-nitrosylation modifications.

3 Trends in LLM-Based Approaches for PTM Site Prediction

In the previous section, we briefly reviewed existing tools for predicting PTMs that use LLMs, pLMs, or custom transformer-based architectures. In this section, we will outline the key trends identified within these tools. A high-level overview of these trends is depicted in Fig. 1.

3.1 Use of Foundation Models

LLMs/pLMs are often referred to as “foundation” models as they are pretrained on large datasets in an unsupervised manner causing them to be task agnostic [82]. In PTM site prediction, foundation models are either used as a feature extractor (i.e., feature-based approach) or fine-tuned to adapt to a specific task. This section will elucidate these two approaches in detail.


Fig. 1 Schematic diagram of the trends discussed in this chapter

3.1.1 Using Feature-Based Approach (LLM/pLM as a Feature Extractor)

Pretrained embeddings are tensors obtained from the final hidden layer of a foundation model, in our case, a pLM. These embeddings are often referred to as “last hidden states.” The dimension of the embedding is determined by the size of the hidden layer from which they are extracted. For example, the final hidden layer of GPT2-small has 768 dimensions, meaning each token is represented by a tensor of size \(7 6 8 \times 1\) . These embeddings can be finetuned for any downstream protein task by adding task-specific heads while keeping the parameters of the foundation model frozen. This approach is therefore also called Feature-based finetuning [83].

Depending on the task, the input to these prediction heads must be adjusted to provide an optimal representation that addresses the problem. Protein-level tasks (e.g., DNA binding protein prediction) typically employ one of three representation techniques [31, 82]: pooling, attention-based pooling, or using the CLS token. In pooling, either mean pooling is applied, where all residue embeddings across the sequence are averaged, or max pooling, where only the maximum value for each embedding dimension is used. Alternatively, attention-based pooling calculates a weighted average of the embeddings through an attention mechanism [84].

PTM site prediction is a residue-level task, meaning the local environment of the specific residue or site-of-interest should be incorporated to obtain the site-specific representation. Since the advent of pLMs, researchers have adopted several strategies to derive site-specific representations [39, 47, 53, 55, 59]. Notably,

Table 2 Comparison of representation strategies in protein language models for residue-level tasks

Strategy Input type Input dim. Embedding used Representation dim. Comments
WSPE Window sequence W×1 Per-residue corresponding to the site of interest L×1 No global contextLimited capture of association between adjacent residue embeddings
WSWE Window sequence W×1 Window residues L×W(entire window) or L×1 (after pooling or using [CLS] token) No global contextBetter capture of association between adjacent residue embeddings
FSPE Full sequence N×1 Per-residue corresponding to the site of interest L×1 Captures global contextLimited capture of association between adjacent residue embeddings
FSWE Full sequence N×1 Window residues L×W(entire window) or L×1 (after pooling or using [CLS] token) Captures global context and association between adjacent residue embeddings

Each strategy (WSPE, WSWE, FSPE, FSWE) differs by input type, input dimension, embedding usage, and representation dimension. Comments highlight their strengths and limitations in capturing global context and relationships between adjacent residues embedding. W is the length of the window, \(L\) is the embedding dimension, and \(N\) is the length of the full sequence. [CLS] stands for classification token which is prepended in the input sequence in some transformers architecture (e.g., BERT)

the work of Pratyush et al. [ ] categorizes these strategies into38 four main types: Window Sequence-based Per-Residue Embedding (WSPE), Window Sequence-based Window Embedding (WSWE), Full Sequence-based Per-Residue Embedding (FSPE), and Full Sequence-based Window Embedding (FSWE). In WSPE and FSPE, the embedding is derived only for the site-of-interest, but they differ in the input provided to the pLM. WSPE uses a window of residues around the site as input, while FSPE takes the entire sequence. In contrast, WSWE and FSWE consider the embeddings of all residues within a window surrounding the site-of-interest. WSWE uses the window as input to the pLM, while FSWE employs the full sequence. The choice of input, whether a window or the full sequence, determines whether the obtained embeddings capture the global context. Furthermore, the use of per-residue or windowlevel embeddings influences whether the relationships between adjacent embeddings are captured. Table 2 summarizes the

Variants of Feature-Bsed Representation

strategies above, including input sequence type, input dimension, embeddings used for representation, the corresponding representation dimension, and comments highlighting the benefits and drawbacks of these approaches. It should be noted that these four approaches are mentioned because they are among the most commonly adopted in the field, and this is by no means an exhaustive list of all possible methods for representing site-specific representation.

While the prevailing trend in the field is to use the last hidden states (i.e., tensors from the final hidden layer) for obtaining latent representations, the original BERT paper [28] also proposed using hidden states from intermediate layers. This approach has been adopted by MSTL-Kace [53], an acetylation site prediction tool, which uses the mean of embeddings (corresponding to [CLS] token) from the last six layers instead of obtaining the final hidden states of pretrained BERT. Similarly, other variations can be applied, and these approaches generally fall into two main categories listed below [28, 53]. These approaches can be incorporated with any of the aforementioned representation strategies (WSPE, WSWE, FSPE, FSWE).

Single-layer extraction: Embeddings are extracted from either the final hidden layer or intermediate layers. As previously mentioned, extraction from the final hidden layer is the most common approach. However, in certain cases, intermediate layers may prove more useful, depending on the task. For example, earlier layers may capture more syntactic information (higher order arrangement of residues), while later layers tend to capture more semantic information (protein function). If the goal is to focus on a particular aspect of the sequence, embeddings from specific intermediate layers can be extracted.
Multi-layer aggregation: In this approach, tensors from multiple layers are aggregated, either by concatenating or averaging them (e.g., MSTL-Kace). As mentioned in the single-layer extraction approach, each layer captures a different range of information (from syntactic to semantic to task-specific). Aggregating embeddings from multiple layers can help preserve this diverse information, some of which might be lost by the time the final layer is reached.

In Table 3, we report the different PTM site prediction tools, their respective representation strategies, and layer extraction methods, highlighting how each tool is optimized for specific PTMs by leveraging different embedding approaches and layer extraction techniques.

Table 3 Summary of PTM site LLM/pLM-based prediction tools, representation strategies (WSPE, WSWE, FSPE, and FSWE), and layer extraction methods (single-layer and multi-layer aggregation)

Tools PTM Representation strategy for pLM/transformer model Layer extraction Comments
CaLMPhosKAN [43] Phosphorylation FSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) Combines FSWE embeddings from CaLM and ProtTrans
MSTL-Kace [53] Acetylation WSWE Multi-layer aggregation The embeddings are the mean of [CLS] token of the last six layers of BERT
LMCrot [38] Crotonylation FSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) LMCrot also explores WSPE, WSWE, and FSPE
LMSuccSite [39], pLMSNOSite [55], LMPhosSite [42] Succinylation, S-Nitrosylation, and phosphorylation, respectively FSPE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) These tools have similar architectures
LM-O-GlcNAc-Site [51], LMGlyPred [46] N-linked and O-linked glycosylation FSPE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) LM-O-GlcNAc-site is for O-linked while LMGlyPred is for N-linked
EMNGLy [47] N-linked glycosylation WSWE + WSPE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) Combines WSWE (via [CLS] token) and WSPE for sequence embeddings
CoNglyPred [49] N-linked glycosylation FSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) -
SumoPred-PLM [56] SUMOylation FSPE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) -
iSUMO-RsFPN [57] SUMOylation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) -
BERT-Kgly [62] Glycation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) WSWE using [CLS] token was optimal. Also explores WSWE via averaging and WSPE
BERT-Kcr [59] Crotonylation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) -

(continued)

Table 3 (continued)

Tools PTM Representation strategy for pLM/transformer model Layer extraction Comments
ProtTrans-Glutar [61] Glutarylation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) WSWE via averaging
Deepro-Glu [60] Glutarylation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) WSWE via averaging
TransPTM [54] Acetylation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) WSWE via averaging
PTG-PLM [63] N-linked glycosylation and glycation WSWE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) WSWE
UniPTM [65] Multiple PTMs FSPE Single layer (final hidden layer layer) -

3.1.2 Fine-Tuning of Foundation Models

An alternative to the feature-based approach is adapting pretrained foundation models (i.e., LLMs or pLMs) to a downstream task using a labeled dataset. This process involves fine-tuning the model’s weights while using task-specific labels to guide learning. The fine-tuning of pLMs can be simplified into three steps:

Step 1. Pretraining: The model is first pretrained on a large corpus of protein sequences, learning general features of sequences through self-supervised learning, typically using a Masked Language Modeling (MLM) objective.
Step 2. Constructing the labeled dataset: A labeled dataset is then created, represented as pairs \({ < } \mathrm { I }\) , \(\mathrm { O } { > }\) , where I refers to the input (in this case, protein sequences, as used during pretraining), and \(O\) represents task-specific labels (i.e., the outputs).
Step 3. Fine-tuning: The final step involves updating the weights of the pretrained model using the task-specific labels via backpropagation.

It is important to note that not all the fine-tuning approaches need to adhere to these three steps. There are more ways to perform fine-tuning for adapting pretrained models to domain-specific tasks. Raschka [83] classifies these approaches into six categories, one of which is the feature-based approach that we have already discussed in the previous section. In subheadings “Fine-tuning I

(updating output layers only), “Fine-tuning II or full fine-tuning (updating all layers)”, “Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)”, “In-context learning, indexing, and prompt tuning”, and “Reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF)”, we will briefly describe the remaining five categories to equip the readers with foundational knowledge although we recommend going through the excellent book by Raschka [83] for comprehensive explanations. Following this, in subheading “Fine-tuning in PTM site prediction context”, we will focus on fine-tuning approaches specifically in the context of PTM site prediction.
Fine-Tuning I (Updating Output Layers Only) This method updates only the output layer of the pretrained model, leaving the core layers frozen. It is efficient in memory and computation, and suitable when minor task-specific adaptations are sufficient. This approach should not be conflated with the feature-based approach (or, feature-based fine-tuning) that we discussed in the Subheading 3.1.1. The latter involves adding a classification head to the pretrained model without tuning any of its layers, while this method updates the output layers within the existing pretrained model itself.
Fine-Tuning II or Full Fine-Tuning (Updating All Layers) Fine-Tuning II updates all model layers, fully adapting them to the target task. Although computationally demanding, this strategy often achieves higher performance for domain-specific applications than updating output layers only.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) These methods update only select parts of the parameter space of the model, thereby saving resources. PEFT can be further classified into four sub-components: 1. Soft prompt tuning: Soft prompt tuning adds learnable prompts to the input layer, tuning these without altering core model layers. 2. Prefix tuning: Prefix tuning inserts trainable prompts within transformer blocks to enable efficient tuning across layers. 3. Adapters: In this method, small layers are added to transformer blocks, with only adapters being updated while keeping the main layers frozen. 4. Low-rank adaptation (LoRA): LoRA [85] decomposes the weight-updated matrix into trainable low-rank matrices that are injected into the original model. Thus, the number of parameters participating in the fine-tuning is considerably reduced. Popular variants of LoRA include quantization LoRA (QLoRA), which combines LoRA with quantization to reduce memory usage and enable fine-tuning of large models on limited hardware [86]. Similarly, Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation (DoRA) decomposes the pretrained weights into magnitude as well as direction and finetunes these components separately [87].

In-context Learning, Indexing, and Prompt Indexin

These techniques adapt the model via input manipulation rather than parameter updates:

In-context learning: In-context learning embeds examples directly in the prompt to guide the model on the task. This method is ideal for zero-shot and few-shot tasks, where labeled data for training may be limited.
Indexing: Indexing retrieves relevant information from external sources based on query similarity, aiding response generation. This strategy is commonly used in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) applications, which enables LLMs to synthesize responses using both model knowledge and external resources.
Prompt tuning: Prompt tuning optimizes specific prompts for task performance without changing model parameters. Unlike in-context learning, prompt tuning often relies on a small labeled dataset to identify the optimal prompt formulation.

Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback with Hu

RLHF combines human feedback with reinforcement learning, training a reward model to guide model responses. Common in applications like ChatGPT, RLHF aligns the model’s behavior with human preferences through reinforcement techniques.

Fine-Tuning in PTM Site Prediction Context

Various fine-tuning techniques have been adopted for the task of PTM site prediction. For instance, BERT-Kcr [59] utilizes taskadaptive pretraining (TAPT) by fully fine-tuning (Fine-tuning II) the BERT to adapt the model to domain knowledge by pretraining with protein sequences. Similarly, MSTL-Kace [53] fully fine-tunes BERT across multiple species to create species-specific embeddings for acetylation site prediction. Meanwhile, Phosformer follows a two-stage process for phosphosite prediction. Specifically, it first pretrains a custom transformer on broad protein sequence data to capture general protein relationships. The second stage then applies full fine-tuning on kinase-substrate pairs using a multi-level negative sampling strategy that is framed as a context-based questionanswering (CBQA) task. Here, the kinase sequence acts as the “question,” the peptide sequence as the “context,” and the phosphorylation probability as the “answer.”

PTMGPT2 [36], a multi-PTM site prediction tool, fine-tunes the ProtGPT2 [81] pLM (decoder-only model) via prompt tuning by constructing tailored prompts where the window sequence and its inference are embedded within the prompt. The model is then fine-tuned based on these tailored prompts, adapting ProtGPT2 to the specific PTM site prediction task through prompt-based finetuning.

While PEFT methods have not been explored in PTM site prediction, the experiments done by Schreiber [88] show promising results in PTMs using LoRA and QLoRA.

At the time of writing (Oct. 2024), we have not identified other fine-tuning approaches applied in the PTM domain. Moreover, the tools using feature-based fine-tuning (using pLM as a feature extractor) for PTM site prediction have already been addressed in Subheading 3.1.1, so we will refrain from reiterating that discussion here. It is worth noting, however, that this approach is relatively straightforward compared to other fine-tuning methods, as it involves merely adding a classification head on top of the pretrained embeddings without altering the foundation model itself. A study conducted by Sledzieski et al. [82] has observed that this method can even outperform both full fine-tuning and PEFT techniques in the context of protein-protein interaction prediction. This suggests that, for certain tasks, a classification head alone may be sufficient without necessitating further task-specific modifications to the pretrained model.

In Table 4, we summarize the tools that use the aforementioned fine-tuning strategies for PTM site prediction, highlighting each tool’s specific approach and application.

3.2 Training Custom Transformer

While the use of foundation models (e.g., pretrained LLMs) and their fine-tuning has become prevalent in the field, some tools opt

Table 4 Tools utilizing fine-tuning approaches for PTM site prediction, with comments providing further insights into the specific fine-tuning strategies adopted by each tool

Fine-tuning type Tools (target PTM) Comments
Full-fine tuning (fine-tuning II) BERT-Kcr [59] (crotonylation), MSTL-Kace [53] (acetylation), phosformer [41] (phosphorylation) The BERT model undergoes full fine-tuning for BERT-Kcr and MSTL-Kace. Phosformer uses a custom transformer pretrained in the first stage on protein data, followed by full fine-tuning
Feature-based fine-tuning (using pLM as a feature extractor) LMCrot [38] (crotonylation), LMSuccsite [39] (succinylation), EMNGLy [47] (N-linked glycosylation), ProtTrans-Glutar [61] (glutarylation), etc. All the tools mentioned in the previous subsection (i.e., Subheading 3.1.1) belong to this category. Here, the pretrained models are used as a feature extractor
Prompt-based fine-tuning PTMGPT2 [36] (multiple PTMs) Pretrained ProtGPT2 [81] is fine-tuned using customized prompts
Parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) Analysis by Schreiber [88] (PTM type not specified) Shows application of LoRA and QLoRA in PTM site prediction
Other fine-tuning approaches (fine-tuning I, prefix tuning, indexing RLHF, etc.) N/A None found at the time of writing (Oct. 2024)

to train their own transformers on sequences. These transformers are often lightweight compared to LLMs and can adapt to tasks with supervised training using fewer computational resources. Overall, training custom transformers offers several advantages, such as task-specific optimization, reduced computational cost, flexibility in architecture design, better alignment with domainspecific data, and improved adaptability for emerging tasks [89, 90]. In Subsection 2, we briefly reviewed tools that train their own transformers to learn representations for PTM site prediction. These tools were listed in Table 1 as a separate column. Therefore, we will not reiterate the descriptions of these tools again in this subsection.

3.3 Multi-pLM Integration and Multimodal/ Multifacted Approaches

3.3.1 Integrating Embeddings from Multiple pLMs

While sequence-aware pLMs have demonstrated impressive performance across various downstream tasks, their predictive capabilities can be limited when relying solely on a single pLM or unimodal embeddings. To address these limitations, approaches that integrate multiple pLMs, embeddings from other modes, or even conventional representation have been developed.

Each pLM differs in aspects such as architecture, pretraining datasets, and training objectives. These differences enable pLMs to capture unique characteristics of the same sequence. By combining embeddings from different pLMs, we can harness their complementary strengths to capture a richer representation of the sequence. The integration of embeddings from multiple pLMs was first explored in LM-OGlcNAc-Site [51], where embeddings from three pLMs—Ankh [91], ESM-2 [32], and ProtT5 [31] were combined at the decision level to predict O-GlcNAc modification sites. Similarly, Stack-OglyPred-PLM [50] similarly combines embeddings from Ankh and ProtT5 using stacked generalization for O-linked glycosylation prediction. Additionally, HOTGpred [48] employs an early fusion strategy, integrating embeddings from ESM-1 [32], ProtT5 [31], and Seq2Vec [92] for the same task. Likewise, PTM-Mamba [64] integrates embeddings from ESM-2 with its own PTM-specific embeddings, generated using the Mamba architecture, through a gated fusion mechanism, enabling multiple PTM site predictions, including phosphorylation and acetylation.

3.3.2 Integrating Codon-Aware Embeddings

Due to the degeneracy of the genetic code, each amino acid can be encoded by up to six different codons [68]. This suggests that codon sequences (coding DNA) may contain additional information beyond the amino acid sequence alone. Codon usage is known to influence protein synthesis rates and co-translational protein folding [93]. This indicates codon usage can have an influence on the occurrence of PTMs. Recently, a pretrained pLM called CaLM

[68], which was trained on codon alphabets (coding DNA), outperformed amino acid-based pLMs like ProtTrans and the ESM family in several protein-level tasks.

The recently developed phosphosite prediction tool, CaLM-PhosKAN [43], is the first work to utilize codon-aware embeddings in the domain of PTM site prediction and residue-level tasks in general. Interestingly, while CaLM embeddings alone did not outperform ProtTrans embeddings for this specific task, integrating CaLM embeddings (codon-aware) with ProtTrans embeddings (amino acid-aware) yielded the best results. This indicates that although CaLM may not surpass amino acid-based pLMs individually, it provides complementary representations that improve performance when combined.

It should be noted that translating sequences into the most reliable coding sequences, commonly referred to as “reverse translation,” plays a crucial role. One approach is to predict codons using models like LinearDesign [94]. Another method involves mapping protein sequences to actual coding DNAs [95]. For instance, CaLMPhosKAN adopted a process where protein sequences were translated into actual coding DNA by mapping UniProt identifiers to non-redundant RefSeq nucleotide sequences [43]. Specifically, CaLMPhosKAN retrieved coding sequences from the NCBI Nucleotide database [96] and verified the sequence identity with UniProt proteins using pairwise alignment via the Needleman–Wunsch (NW) algorithm. Only sequences with \(1 0 0 \%\) identity were included in the dataset, resulting in a reduced but highly reliable dataset.

3.3.3 Integrating 3D Structure-Aware Embeddings or 3D Structures

Protein function and interactions are intrinsically linked to their three-dimensional (3D) spatial conformations. While sequencebased pretrained pLMs capture linear amino acid sequences, they may overlook critical spatial relationships and structural motifs that are only apparent in the folded protein structure [97]. To address this limitation, integrating embeddings derived from 3D structures or from structure-aware models can provide additional context. The advent of the AlphaFold model has enabled noteworthy advancements in protein bioinformatics by providing highconfidence predicted 3D structures [33]. This has facilitated the development of tools that utilize structural information for PTM site prediction. This chapter discusses four tools that leverage 3D structures for PTM site prediction: MIND-S [66] for predicting 26 types of PTMs, EMNGLy [47], and CoNglyPred [49] for N-linked glycosylation prediction, and PTM-CMGMS [35] for predicting succinylation, S-nitrosylation, and crotonylation. MIND-S and PTM-CMGMS utilize 3D structures by learning latent representations through graph attention layers and contrastive learning, respectively, while EMNGLy employs structure-

aware embeddings from a Masked Inverse Folding (MIF) model [72]. In all three methods, the 3D structural information serves as a complementary representation to sequence-based features rather than relying on 3D structures alone.

3.3.4 Integrating with Conventional Representation

Integrating conventional features such as physicochemical properties and evolutionary information can complement pLM embeddings. Additionally, conventional NLP encoders learned on local window sequences can be integrated. A notable example is combining encodings from the supervised embedding layer (SEL) learned from window sequences with pLM embeddings, providing both global and local views of sites [38, 51]. Tools like pLMSNOSite [55], LMSuccSite [39], and LMPhosSite [42] utilize stacked generalization of two models: one trained on FSPE-based (Full Sequence Per-Residue Embedding) pLM embeddings and another on SEL features from window sequences. Similarly, LMCrot [38] integrates latent representations of FSWE-based (Full Sequence Window Embedding) pLM embeddings with those from SEL and physicochemical properties. Likewise, other tools that combine pLM embeddings with conventional representations are presented in Table 5.

It is worthwhile to note that the integration (or, fusion) of other modalities with sequence modality can be achieved through four primary approaches [37]: early fusion, where sequence and modality-specific features are combined at the input level before being fed into the model; intermediate fusion, where each input type is first processed independently to extract high-level features, which are then combined at a hidden layer within the model; late fusion, where sequence and modality-specific data are processed separately through separate models or network branches, with their outputs (probability or inference) combined at the decision level; and attention based fusion (e.g., cross-attention, co-attention), which utilizes attention mechanisms to dynamically model interactions between sequence and modality-specific features, allowing the model to focus on relevant parts of one modality based on information from the other. While these four fusion methods are the most prevalent in PTM site prediction, other strategies also exist (e.g., fusion via gating mechanisms). In Table 5, we present tools that employ multi-pLM and multimodal strategies, along with the specific fusion methods used.

3.4 Interpretability and Explainability of LLM or LLM-Derived Embeddings

One of the drawbacks of deep learning models is their “black-box” nature [98]. Explaining and interpreting predictions by correlating inputs with outputs can help us understand the behavior of these models. The core mechanism of LLMs, namely the multi-head attention mechanism, computes attention maps that enable interpretation of the parts of the sequence that were focused on by the model. Each value in these maps, called an attention weight,

able 5

Tool PTM Sequence modality (pLM-based) Complementary modality Type of integration Fusion method
LM-OGlcNAc-Site [51] O-GlcNAcylation (Ankh + ESM-2 + ProtT5) embeddings - Multiple pLM integration Late fusion
Stack-OglyPred-PLM [50] O-linked glycosylation (Ankh + ProtT5) embeddings - Multiple pLM integration Late fusion
PTM-Mamba [64] Multiple PTMs (ESM-2 + mamba) embeddings - Multiple pLM integration Gating based
HOTGpred [48] O-linked glycosylation (ESM-1 + ProtT5 + Seq2Vec) embeddings FastText Multiple pLMs as well as conventional representation integration Early fusion
CaLMPhosKAN [43] Phosphorylation ProtT5 embeddings Codon-aware embeddings from CaLM Integration with codons Early fusion
MIND-S [66] 26 PTMs Custom transformer embeddings 3D structure + graph attention layer Integration with 3D structure Intermediate fusion
EMNGLy [47] N-linked glycosylation ESM-1 embeddings 3D-structure aware embeddings from MIF Integration with 3D structure Early fusion
CoNGlyPred [49] N-linked glycosylation ESM-2 3D structure + graph transformer Integration with 3D structure Attention-based fusion
PTM-CMGMS [35] S-Nitrosylation, succinylation, and crotonylation Custom transformer embeddings 3D structure + contrastive learning Integration with 3D structure Intermediate fusion
LMSuccSite [39], pLMSNOSite [55], and LMPhosSite [42] Succinylation, S-nitrosylation, and phosphorylation, respectively ProtT5 embeddings Supervised embedding layer (SEL) Integration with conventional representation Intermediate fusion
iSUMO-RsFPN [57] SUMOylation ProtBERT embeddings Physicochemical properties Integration with conventional representation Late fusion
GPS-SUMO 2.0 [58] SUMOylation Custom transformer embeddings Physicochemical properties and evolutionary information Integration with conventional representation Late fusion
LMCrot [38] Crotonylation ProtT5 embeddings SEL and physicochemical properties Integration with conventional representation Intermediate fusion
Deepro-Glu [60] Glutarylation ProtBERT embeddings Physicochemical properties Integration with conventional representation Attention based
TransPTM [54] Acetylation ProtT5 embeddings + graph transformer One-hot encoding of window Integration with conventional representation Early fusion

chniques and contrastive learning (e.g., 3D structure + contrastive learning) in some places, indicating that latent embeddings are learned from their respective modalities by applying these Note that entries in the sequence modality and complementary modality columns show pairings with methods such as graph transformers (e.g., 3D structure + graph attention)

indicates the degree of correlation between two tokens in the sequence. This can help derive important associations between tokens (amino acids) that the model has learned.

The lysine crotonylation (Kcr) site prediction tool, BERT-Kcr [59], derives average attention weights over the heads from different layers of BERT to obtain the distribution of weights over each position in the input window sequence. This enabled the authors to infer that higher layers capture more complex sequence relationships. Another Kcr prediction tool, LMCrot [38], also examines the attention map from the final encoder layer of ProtT5 to analyze the associations of other amino acids with the site of interest. They also reference the corresponding 3D structure of the sequence to extract insights into the relationship between the attention weights and the actual structure. This analysis enabled them to understand why both full-context and window embeddings are needed, and why FSWE (Full Sequence Window Embedding) provides the optimal result. Similar to BERT-Kcr, MSTL-Kace [53], an acetylation site predictor, examines averaged attention maps from all layers of BERT to observe the interactions between acetylation sites and other residues. Based on these maps, they also inferred why aggregated embeddings across layers are more effective than single-layer embeddings. Additionally, CaLMPhosKAN [43], a phosphorylation site predictor, utilizes attention maps from the final encoder layers of both ProtT5 [31] and CaLM [68]. By contrasting the associations captured by ProtT5 (an amino acid-based pLM) and CaLM (a codon-based pLM) with the site-of-interest, they provided insights into how codon-level information complements amino acid–level information in predicting phosphorylation sites. Similarly, PTMGPT2 [36], a multiple PTM site predictor, extracts attention scores from the last decoder layer averaged across the heads to generate Position Specific Probability Matrices (PSPMs). These PSPMs reveal amino acid patterns and preferences, providing insights into important sequence determinants for protein modifications, such as lysine acetylation and various kinase family motifs. Likewise, Phosformer [41] uses attention mechanisms within its model architecture to identify which positions in peptide sequences are most influential for kinase-specific phosphosite prediction. By examining attention weights from the final encoder layers, Phosformer reveals how the model focuses on substrate specificity determinants; critical amino acid residues for kinase recognition.

Some existing predictors have also utilized established explainable algorithms to interpret their models or derived embeddings. For instance, LMCrot [38] uses the model-agnostic method SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) [99] to determine which residue positions have the most impact on the prediction of crotonylation sites. It was observed that the site-of-interest had the most significant impact, as expected, while adjacent residues also contributed notably. The SHAP values (mean absolute per position in their

case) gradually decreased as the linear distance from the site-ofinterest increased. This observation corroborated the findings from the attention map examination and provided additional support for using FSWE embeddings. Similarly, MIND-S [66] employs the Integrated Gradients [100] method to interpret its predictions. By calculating the contribution of each amino acid residue to the final prediction, MIND-S was able to identify important residues and known motifs associated with PTMs. For example, it successfully highlighted the N-X-S/T motif in N-linked glycosylation and distinguished different kinase-specific patterns in phosphorylation sites. Additionally, HOTGpred [48], which integrates multiple pLM embeddings for O-linked glycosylation, uses SHAP to determine the most influential pLM embeddings impacting the predictions. Tools that train their custom transformers have also applied these algorithms; for instance, DL-SPhos [45], a serine phosphorylation site predictor, employs the LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) [101] method to highlight the amino acid residues most critical to its predictions and to identify conserved motifs associated with serine phosphorylation. Table 6 summarizes the interpretable and explainable approaches utilized by the tools discussed above.

3.5 Use of Graph Representation

While sequence and structural-based representation have been extensively used in the field, graph-based representations offer several advantages by capturing complex, non-linear relationships essential for understanding protein structure and function. Unlike sequence-based models, graphs can represent spatial relationships by treating residues as nodes with edges that capture both close and distant interactions in 3D space.
