This flexibility allows the integration of diverse data types, such as evolutionary conservation and physicochemical properties, enriching the representation. Graphs also enhance interpretability by making residue interactions and dependencies more transparent and allow models to focus on biologically significant connections, such as those involved in binding or regulatory interactions. In this chapter, we will discuss three tools that leverage graph-based representations based on LLMs/ pLMs/custom transformers.

The TransPTM [54] tool utilizes graph representations to predict acetylation sites. In this graph construction, each node represents a residue within a defined window sequence, with each node vectorized using ProtT5 embeddings. Edges are defined based on a complete graph assumption, meaning that every residue is connected to every other residue within the sequence (sequence adjacency). These graphs are then fed to a GNN composed of TransformerConv (TransConv) layers, which apply graph convolution with an attention mechanism to produce a graph-based latent representation of the sequence. Similarly, MIND-S [66], a tool for predicting multiple PTMs, constructs contact maps directly from

Table 6 Tools that perform interpretability and explainability along with the specific strategies they used and inference obtained from it

Tool PTM Interpretable/explainable strategy Inference
BERT-Kcr [59] Crotonylation Attention weights Higher layers capture more complex sequence relationships
LMCrot [38] Crotonylation Attention weights and SHAP Validates the effectiveness of FSWE embeddings
MSTL-Kace [53] Acetylation Attention weights Aggregated embeddings across layers are more effective than single-layer embeddings
CaLMPhosKAN [43] Phosphorylation Attention weights Codon-level information complements amino acid-level information for predicting phosphorylation
PTMGPT2 [36] Multiple PTMs Attention weights PSPMs reveal amino acid patterns and preferences critical for protein modifications
Phosformer [41] Phosphorylation Attention weights Reveals substrate specificity determinants critical for kinase recognition in phosphosite prediction
MIND-S [66] Multiple PTMs Integrated gradients Identifies residues contributing to predictions, highlighting motifs like N-X-S/T and kinase-specific patterns
HOTGpred [48] O-linked glycosylation SHAP Determines most influential embeddings impacting predictions
DL-SPhos [45] Phosphorylation LIME Highlights critical amino acids and conserved motifs for serine phosphorylation

3D structural data. These contact maps serve as the initial graph structure, where each residue is represented as a node, and edges are defined based on physical proximity in the 3D structure rather than sequence adjacency. These contact maps are learned by a graph attention (GAT) [102] layer to create a graph-based representation of the sequence. Another tool, CoNglyPred [49], is an N-linked glycosylation predictor. Similar to MIND-S, it does not use a pLM/transformer directly to obtain the graph representation. Instead, it constructs a graph from contact maps based on 3D structures, where nodes represent residues and edges are defined by spatial proximity. Each residue node includes a combination of structural and sequence features, such as secondary structure information, physicochemical properties, evolutionary PSSM data, and torsion angles. This graph is processed by a Graph Transformer Network (GTN) [103] to create a latent graph representation, which is then fused with ESM-2 embeddings via a co-attention mechanism. These three approaches are summarized in Table 7.

Table 7 Summary of graph-based tools for PTM site prediction, detailing target PTMs, node and edge definitions, and use of pLMs or transformers

Tool PTM Nodes Edges Use of pLMs/transformer
TransPTM [54] Acetylation Each residue in a defined window sequence Complete graph (sequence adjacency) ProtT5 embeddings as node features
MIND-S [66] Multiple PTMs Each residue in a 3D structure Contact map (spatial proximity in 3D space) Graphs are processed by the graph attention layer
CoNglyPred [49] N-linked glycosylation Each residue in a 3D structure Contact map structure (spatial proximity in 3D space) Graphs are processed by graph transformer ESM-2 embeddings fused via co-attention (not used directly in the graph)

3.6 Mamba Meets PTM (PTM-Mamba): Alternative to Transformers

Recent advances in sequence modeling have explored alternatives to transformers to address their inherent computational inefficiencies, particularly their quadratic scaling with sequence length. Mamba [104], a novel state space model (SSM), has emerged as a promising alternative by leveraging hardware-aware primitives that enable efficient modeling of long-range dependencies in sequential data. Unlike transformers, which rely on attention mechanisms to capture relationships across tokens, Mamba employs structured state spaces to achieve sub-quadratic scaling, making it computationally efficient for large-scale datasets without sacrificing modeling capacity. Its streamlined design is especially suited for tasks requiring the analysis of extensive sequences, such as protein modeling.

Building on this innovation, PTM-Mamba’s [64] extends the capabilities of Mamba to explicitly account for PTMs within protein sequences. PTM-Mamba integrates bidirectional Mamba blocks, processing sequences both forward and backward, thereby capturing context and dependencies that span the entire sequence. This is critical for PTMs, which often influence regions distant from the site of modification. Additionally, PTM-Mamba introduces a gated fusion mechanism to combine embeddings from the ESM-2 model with its own PTM-specific embeddings. By explicitly incorporating PTM information, PTM-Mamba has shown to outperform traditional transformer-based models PTM site prediction, disease association, and druggability analysis.

3.7 Use of Contrastive Learning

Contrastive learning refines representations by learning an embedding space where positive samples (e.g., modified sites) are close to each other, while negative samples (e.g., non-modified sites) are pushed further apart [105]. This is achieved using a contrastive loss function that minimizes distances between similar samples (positive pairs) and maximizes distances between dissimilar samples (negative pairs). In PTM site prediction, contrastive learning can be applied by pairing samples based on their modification status, sequence similarity, or shared structural features. For example, samples with the same PTM type, such as phosphorylation sites, are treated as positive pairs, while non-phosphorylated sites serve as negative pairs. This approach allows the model to embed sites with shared modification characteristics closer together, preserving the unique biological signals of different PTM types in the feature space.

An example of this approach is PTM-CMGMS [35], a multi-PTM site prediction tool that applies contrastive learning to optimize structural representations by grouping PTM sites with similar modification characteristics. For instance, crotonylation sites are treated as positive pairs and non-crotonylation sites as negative pairs. This grouping helps the model learn to embed sites with similar PTM types closer together in the representation space while pushing dissimilar PTM types farther apart.

4 Limitations and Future Directions

The following limitations identified in current PTM site prediction tools leveraging LLMs/pLMs/custom transformers highlight specific gaps that could serve as promising directions for future advancements.

4.1 Optimal Use of 3D Structures

While 3D structures are crucial for accurately capturing spatial information in proteins, they are currently underutilized in PTM site prediction. Tools like AlphaFold have enabled access to highconfidence 3D models, but integrating 3D data into PTM models is currently limited. Although we have identified a few tools that incorporate 3D modalities in this chapter, the predictive capability of 3D-based representation alone remains suboptimal. We are likely to observe robust use of 3D structures in PTM site prediction in the future.

4.2 Exploration of Layers

Most models rely on embeddings from the final layer or a simple aggregation across a few layers of pretrained LLMs/pLMs. However, intermediate layers can contain useful information that can aid in capturing different aspects of protein function and structure. Conducting an exhaustive layer exploration, or layer-wise analysis, could provide deeper insights into specific sequence-structure-

function relationships that are useful for PTM site prediction. Additionally, selectively aggregating embeddings from informative layers can enhance the overall representation quality.

4.3 Exploration of Other Fine-Tuning Approaches

The majority of PTM predictors use only limited fine-tuning methods (e.g., full fine-tuning or feature-based fine-tuning), while more approaches like selective layer fine-tuning and prefix fine-tuning remain largely underexplored. Likewise, techniques for parameterefficient fine-tuning, such as LoRA, QLoRA, and DoRA, are also not widely applied. Exploring these approaches could optimize model performance while reducing computational costs, providing notable advancements in PTM prediction.

4.4 Use of Decoders

Most PTM site prediction models rely on encoder-only architectures, with decoders or the decoder components of encoderdecoder models being largely underutilized. In this chapter, we discussed PTMGPT2 [36], which is currently the only tool that employs a decoder-only model and has demonstrated impressive performance across multiple PTMs. This suggests that incorporating decoders can offer advantages in tasks requiring sequential predictions or autoregressive capabilities, and may improve the ability of PTM site prediction models to handle PTMs with dependencies across protein sequences.

4.5 Exploration of Other Modalities

Integrating multiple data types (e.g., sequence, 3D structure, physicochemical properties, and evolutionary information) can offer a more comprehensive view of protein behavior. Although multimodal approaches are gaining interest, few tools successfully combine different data modalities to exploit the diversity of protein characteristics needed for effective PTM site prediction. While multimodal approaches are gaining traction, relatively few tools successfully combine diverse data modalities to utilize the full spectrum of protein characteristics essential for effective PTM site prediction. Expanding the range of explored modalities could amplify predictive accuracy and insights.

In addition to commonly used geometric representations, such as graphs and contact maps, further exploration might include voxel grids and 3D density maps, which offer detailed spatial occupancy and distribution information at finer resolutions. For instance, embeddings derived from multiple sequence alignment (MSA) transformer [106] can be incorporated to reflect evolutionary conservation patterns.

4.6 Improvement in the Predictive Performance

Although significant advancements have been achieved in PTM site prediction with the development of LLMs, there is still ample room for improving predictive capabilities. To illustrate this, we have compiled a selection of existing tools for five well-studied PTMs,

Table 8 Some existing tools for the prediction of five selected PTMs, presented as an example, along with their reported test MCC values (range: \([ - 1 , + 1 ] )\) on the datasets they were trained on

PTM Tools and their reported MCC
Phosphorylation LMPhosSite [42]: 0.39 CaLMPhosKAN [43]: 0.41
Acetylation MSTL-Kace [53]: 0.41 TransPTM [54]: 0.45
Glycosylation EMNGly [47]: 0.80 Stack-OglyPred-PLM [50]: 0.52 LM-OGlcNAc-Site [51]: 0.13
SUMOylation SumoPred-PLM [56]: 0.59 iSUMO-RsFPN [57]: 0.48
Crotonylation BERT-Kcr [59]: 0.64 LMCrot [38]: 0.70

It is important to note that each tool (for its respective PTM) may have been evaluated on different datasets, and the reported MCC reflects performance specific to those datasets. Additionally, for tools that reported MCC values on multiple datasets or species, we have selected the highest-performing MCC observed across those datasets

along with their reported Matthew’s Correlation Coefficient (MCC) values on corresponding datasets in Table 8. A similar trend can be observed across tools for other PTMs as well.

The previous subsections (Subheadings 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, and 4.4) outlined some approaches that could be integrated into current LLM-based methods to enhance their performance. Additionally, improvements can be made at the dataset level, as they directly influence the quality of downstream learning. For instance, efforts should focus on acquiring reliable negative sites to minimize potential false negatives. Similarly, positive sites should be thoroughly vetted to eliminate any false positives.

A high accuracy of predictions is crucial to translate these computational approaches into biological experiments, biomedical treatments, or biotechnological protein design. Even in relatively simple prokaryotic model systems, biological follow-up experiments to study the function of PTMs (e.g., site-directed mutagenesis of PTM sites, followed by phenotypic analyses) often take several months to perform. It is unlikely that biologists would decide to spend significant time and resources on these experiments based on predictions with an \(\mathrm { M C C } < 0 . 9\) . Indeed, so far, we have been unable to find studies that use PTM-predictions as the sole basis for wet-lab experiments. However, reports are emerging that apply prediction tools to databases of clinical variants to assess the impact of genetic mutations on protein modifications and thus protein function [66, 107, 108]. Similarly, PTM-predictions are

becoming increasingly relevant for protein design approaches [109, 110]. With an increase in prediction accuracies, functional studies and applications like these are expected to gain traction.

5 Discussion and Conclusion

The application of LLMs and pLMs has brought significant advances in PTM site prediction. By capturing both local and global contexts within protein sequences, these models outperform traditional sequence-based methods, which often miss key contextual interactions. Sophisticated representation strategies, such as full sequence-based embeddings (e.g., FSPE and FSWE), enable models to incorporate long-range dependencies essential for accurate PTM site prediction. Additionally, aggregating embeddings from multiple layers leverages the hierarchical structure of transformer architectures, enriching the models’ understanding of protein sequences.

Fine-tuning approaches have also evolved. Full fine-tuning adapts models like BERT to specific PTMs, while feature-based fine-tuning applies task-specific classification heads to frozen models, simplifying adaptation. Prompt-based fine-tuning has expanded the use of decoder-only models like ProtGPT2 in PTM site prediction, exploiting their generative capabilities without needing specialized classification heads. However, parameter-efficient finetuning (PEFT) methods remain underexplored and present opportunities for optimizing model adaptation. Incorporating multiple data modalities and combining embeddings from various pLMs further enhances predictive power. Each pLM captures unique characteristics, so combining them yields richer representations. Codon-aware embeddings, for example, add biological nuance, while integrating three-dimensional structural information to address limitations in sequence-only models by capturing spatial relationships critical for protein function. Additionally, blending pLM embeddings with conventional features, like physicochemical properties, broadens the feature space and improves prediction.

Interpretability of these models, however, remains challenging due to their black-box nature. Attention mechanisms in transformer-based models offer a degree of explainability, with attention maps highlighting influential residues in predictions. Model-agnostic techniques such as SHAP values also provide insights into feature importance, but further advancements in interpretability will be essential for broader biological applications.

Recent graph-based approaches model complex protein structures by treating residues as nodes and their relationships as edges, capturing both sequential and spatial information. Contrastive learning further refines these representations by clustering modified sites together while pushing non-modified sites apart, enhancing the model’s ability to distinguish PTM sites accurately.

Despite these advancements, several limitations persist. The integration of 3D structural information is not yet fully optimized, and model layer exploration is limited, potentially overlooking valuable features. Similarly, decoder architectures are underutilized, and multimodal integration could be expanded to include a broader range of biological data. Improving interpretability will be critical to making these models more transparent and broadly useful.

In conclusion, LLMs and pLMs have transformed PTM site prediction by enabling more accurate, contextually rich models. Future research should focus on addressing current limitations by exploring advanced fine-tuning techniques, utilizing decoder architectures, and integrating additional data modalities. Enhancing model transparency will also be crucial for wider acceptance in the biological community. The continued evolution of these models holds great promise for providing deeper insights into protein function and disease mechanisms, driving further innovation in computational biology.

생물학을 위한 딥러닝 (Deep Learning for Biology)
Streamlining Your Research Laboratory with Python
